Noir is a violent and dystopian roleplaying game that deals with humanity's dark sides in a tangible way.
Noir is not recommended for sensitive individuals or children under 15 years of age.

Michael Gullbrandson: 8, 67, 72, 93, 118, 133, 147, 162, 165, 168, 179–180, 184, 187, 190, 194, 206, 208, 219, 249, 254, 265, 282, 286, 288, 295, 297, 302, 307, 319, 328.
Patrik Hultén: 104–117. Alf Svensson: 12, 14, 24, 33, 86, 88, 98–101, 122, 130, 138–139, 144–145, 150–151, 156, 172–173, 196, 250, 278, 312, 346.

"This must be the place I've always wanted to leave."
—Talia Navarre

I died a few minutes ago.
Or at least, that was the plan. As usual in this city, nothing went according to plan. But I'm getting ahead of myself. Let me start from the beginning, about twelve hours ago.

I was eating a late lunch at the Golden
Salon down in Ikonen. A meal consisting of eggs, pork, sausage and bacon, made for a hard-working man. With a Redovs
Fat I washed away the greasy fat taste from my palate. I had no clients in the afternoon and was planning to visit a lady acquaintance for some relaxation for body and soul. It was already here my luck left me, but I didn't realize it then.

Behind me came a two-meter refrigerator lumbering. A strained breath hit my neck and a coarse, hairy hand was placed heavily on my left shoulder.

"Jandaran..." said a hoarse voice, which broke the calm like a pistol shot at midnight.

I knew who was standing next to me. Donner. The man without a first name. One from the old guard, an ox who had survived everything from lead poisoning to being found hanged from the Klemenko Bridge.
You have to respect such a man. The hand remained on my shoulder. I slowly turned my head.

"Gabriel Jandaran, the gumshoe?"

The hoarse voice said it more as a statement than as a question. The breath smelled of cheap booze and cheap whores.

"Yes, that's me. Who's asking?"

I scrutinized Donner. He wore an older, rough suit that didn't fit particularly well, it strained across his frame. The shirt collar had small, barely visible, traces of dried blood.
The knuckles on the hand on my shoulder had fresh bruises and a cut. Donner's face, as if chiseled by an epileptic stonecutter, glared at me.
Hell, he was huge.

"The boss wants to talk to you, outside, now."

The face was completely expressionless when the words came. If I was correctly informed, Donner was employed by one of the city's bigwigs, Klarence Gieger, a magnate in metal and industry, and with contacts within both the clans and the State.
A man it would be foolish to irritate.

I nodded briefly, reached for my hat, laid a twenty on the counter and let myself be escorted out.

On the street I was led to a gigantic
Concran, extended, probably bulletproof. It stood on the other side of the street, all windows were tinted. I saw my reflection in the shiny, black lacquer when the back door was opened by a pale hand. Donner gave me what he surely considered a careful push forward to show that I should get into the car.

The effect was rather that I tumbled in and landed on the floor. Behind me the door slammed shut and the noise from the street abruptly fell silent. I rose as dignified as I could, smiled an awkward smile and sat down on the seat opposite the older, well-dressed man who quietly observed me.

"May I offer you something?" he asked with a cold, strained voice.

"Yes please, a whisky."

A chilly smile flashed across his face. He opened the bar section in the car and took out a bottle, a good brand.

"How do you take it?"

"In a glass."

The professional smile again.
The eyes didn't smile. With slightly trembling movements he poured a double for me. He took nothing himself.

"Mr. Jandaran, I am Klarence
Gieger. I got your name from a mutual acquaintance, Dr. Yannik Reasescu. You helped him a while ago and he said you were quick and discreet."

The gray eyes didn't let go of me with their gaze. He expertly lit a brown cigarette and contentedly blew out the smoke in the cabin.
I took a deep swig of the warming liquid. In the corner of my eye I noted how
Donner remained standing outside the door. There was a chauffeur in the car. The door on the opposite side was locked and the handle was removed. I was trapped in here.

"I remember the case."

It was an old case, one of the easy ones. Find a family member gone astray and return the person to the family. A specialty of mine. I
was lucky that time, picked up the right trail and followed it to the end, to the treasure and the reward. Good money.

"I find myself in a similar situation, Mr. Jandaran."

Gieger leaned forward and lowered his voice. I met him. Felt mint and smoke in the exhaled air. His mouth was small with narrow dried lips, yellow teeth and an almost black tongue.
Under the eyes the skin was stiff and almost grayish-black—like on a smoked corpse. The clothes were of the latest cut, elegant and of high quality. He wore a wedding ring and at least two order rings I couldn't place.

"My daughter is missing. I want you to find her. She is fourteen, turning fifteen this year."

Gieger stubbed out the cigarette and handed over a photograph of a young woman in school uniform.
Bored expression, well-developed for her age, light hair, beautiful, defiant face. She looked older than fourteen, but the city has that effect. If she was even his daughter, if she was even fourteen. It's hard to determine if a man is telling the truth when you yourself would probably lie if the roles were reversed. But it didn't matter.
I didn't care about the motives as long as I got paid in the final act.

"Her name is Derya. I got a call this morning that she hadn't shown up for lessons at Todos."

Todos, one of the Inner City's most prestigious private schools, with boarding school and the whole package. Sure, thanks. Here there was money to be milked.

"According to her bodyguard, a bungler who doesn't work for me anymore, she disappeared without a trace during the night. If she's been kidnapped I'm willing to pay whatever it takes to get her back."

He lit a new cigarette while the gray eyes locked onto mine. I swallowed and nodded briefly.

"There are many in Sandukar who wish me harm, and going through Derya is one way to strike at my heart."

Reality seemed bad enough,
I decided to believe the story for now.

"You'll get two thousand zovrins per day, plus a bonus of five thousand if you solve the case within 48 hours. I don't want this to leak out to the press or the police. Understood?"

I nodded again. He seemed somewhat relieved and drew the smoke into his lungs.

"Keep the photo, on the back is a phone number you can call when you've solved this or need something. I
need to get her back."

The constant problem with wanting something. You always risk failing. Desire makes one unfocused and weak. But I would do my best. That a wealthy man's daughter gets kidnapped is a cliché in this city. But the situation is never a cliché when it strikes you yourself.

The rest of the afternoon went like clockwork.
Already then I should have suspected trouble. Nothing goes that smoothly. The gatekeeper at Todos got his memory back when his wallet was filled and he confirmed my suspicion. Derya

Gieger left the school voluntarily shortly before midnight the night before, got into a car and left the place. No coercion, no gasp, no violence. I cashed in a favor at the archives and traced the car's registration number to an address in Askan, toward the harbor. The State's control over citizens is frighteningly accurate in most cases. The owner of the car, a guy by the name of Janos Kostin, worked as a croupier at a popular nightclub called Chacha Club. A notorious place where more have entered than have come out. The watering hole was also known for its private rooms on the second floor, something that made my stomach knot up.
Chacha Club is by no means a place for a fourteen-year-old schoolgirl.

After maneuvering my way through the subway in rush hour traffic I emerged a block from the club. I found myself in a run-down part of the district, with containers, condemned warehouse premises and worn apartment buildings for bums, fugitives and criminals. It was almost deserted but the music from the club could be heard from far away and several cars, most of older models, stood outside. Suddenly I felt watched. I quickened my pace and entered a doorway and waited.
Nothing was heard. I looked out, backward, but it was empty. Probably it was my own nerves playing tricks.

A light snowfall wrapped the wet streets. I saw my own breath in front of me. Chacha Club was not a place I wanted to return to. A
number of my enemies could very well be there—unfaithful husbands, abusive swine, ex-cons who had served their sentences—all could be someone I put away earlier.

In the darkness of a broken street lamp
I stopped and checked that the revolver was loaded. I returned it to the ankle holster. My automatic pistols were at the office. Five bullets were what I
had if the situation went sour.

The club was housed in a two-story brick building in red. The windows were barred. The building had previously been a police station, so it was freestanding, with parking and alleys all around. A few years ago apparently the police had moved after a scandal when a clan son was found beaten to death and raped in one of the cells. Who was behind the deed never came to light, but according to a source the duty officer, as well as the constables who picked up the son, are in the canal somewhere, in concrete and chains. If you mess with the wrong people in
Sandukar it's often the last thing you do.

Light from a couple of spotlights cut through the snowflakes and passed me.
I couldn't help but let out a low whistle when I recognized the registration number—Kostin's car. The same car that barely a day earlier had picked up young Miss Gieger. I considered running after the car and meeting it when it parked but decided to take it easy. Observe and act based on what happened. Think first and not do anything rash.

The car stopped right outside the club and a slim man in coat and cap got out.
From the passenger side emerged a short, neat figure in a cloak. In the glow from a street lamp her light hair was visible. She walked around the car, the man put his hand around her waist and the two quickly made their way through the snowfall and in through the door to
Chacha Club.

There wasn't a chance I would succeed in getting in without drawing attention. Such miracles don't happen in this city. Opposite the club was a solitary phone booth. I jogged across the empty street and discovered that luck was with me—the phone worked.

I had memorized the phone number and my stiff fingers dialed to reach Mr. Gieger. Two trembling signals, a crackling click and a hoarse voice that answered.

"Yes, Mr. Jandaran?"

I was surprised but collected myself quickly.

"Donner, Miss Gieger is at Chacha
Club in Askan. Are you familiar with it?"

"Yes."

"So where do I pick up the money?"

There was silence for a few moments.

"You'll get a bonus if you fetch her right now. Twenty thousand zovrins."

The hoarse voice was completely without emotion. Immovable as a wrecked diesel locomotive. Greed flared up within me. Although the voice of reason in my mind warned against this, the sum made me feel desire. Desire makes one unfocused and weak. Greed killed the voice of reason in just a second.

"Of course. I'll get her. Have the money ready."
"Yes."
There was a click on the line and it went silent as the grave. The snowfall had intensified. I turned up my coat collar and walked toward my doom.
Under cover of snow and the absence of streetlights, I circled the building and saw the kitchen entrance among overflowing garbage cans and a rusty dumpster. The snow cover crunched softly as I made my way forward as quietly as I could. The door
I reached was metal but proved to be unlocked. The heat and stench of fried food hit me. Quickly I slipped in, closed the door and listened. I found myself in a shabby corridor with bare concrete walls and cardboard boxes along the floor. A few meters down to the left came the clatter of kitchen utensils. Beyond the kitchen a door was visible, from which the harsh music could be heard more clearly.
Two bare light bulbs spread their yellowish-white glow over the corridor as I quickly made my way past the kitchen without being discovered. The door to the establishment had a foggy, round window that I peered out through. It was packed in there. Rough men drank beer, played billiards, made pathetic attempts with harbor whores to get a quickie for free, but neither Kostin nor Miss Gieger were visible anywhere. The staircase up lay a few steps from the door. Quickly I got the door open without a sound. Luck continued to smile my way when with a few quick strides I conquered the solid wooden staircase and came up into a dimly lit corridor with a worn red carpet with burn marks from cigarettes and stains from both one thing and another. From closed rooms along the corridor came grunting and giggling. At one of the doors furthest away stood Kostin. He looked toward me.
"Desire for twenty thousand has made you stupid in the head."
**The voice in the back of my head got air but**
I stifled it. Now it mattered. Show that you can handle this. Take a vacation with the money. Leave the city for a while. Without staring down the thin man I advanced as nonchalantly as I could. No one else was within sight. Sweat pressed forth on my forehead, the hat fortunately covered my eyes. Otherwise Kostin would surely have seen in them that he was in trouble.
My right hand moved down into my pants pocket and closed around a roll of coins. The roll lay perfectly in my hand. The fist clenched. Kostin reacted to me by turning and meeting up. He raised his hand and was just about to say something when my fist struck his nose bone. He blacked out before he hit the floor. I would get a serious bruise over my knuckles, but that was a later problem. The coin roll returned down into my pocket and I got out my lockpicks. Still the corridor was empty. The music rang from downstairs together with indistinct voices and cheerful calls. A quick glance through the keyhole confirmed that the room inside was occupied. The lock only took a couple of seconds to open, I stood up and quietly drew my revolver, grabbed the door handle, breathed in, saw a bag of money in my mind's eye and threw open the door.
Suddenly I realized that all luck was gone with the wind. I felt like a wild dog the second before being hit by an express train.
The room was dim. The bare walls, with their mold stains and their flea market art stared at me. At one wall stood a brass bed. Around the bedposts sat rusty chains with handcuffs. But it was not this that made the air go out of me like a puncture. Two people stood in the middle of the room. Miss Gieger was naked except for a corset and was just in the process of buttoning the shirt of a large, fat, unshaven, slovenly man with a cigarette in the corner of his mouth. Not really a problem if it weren't for the fact that the man was Sergy
Rulov, Sandukar's perhaps most famous and brutal gangster boss. No one was ignorant of his power, connections and ruthless methods. Suddenly twenty thousand felt like two cents. I went cold in my soul.
The situation was beyond bad.
"Who the hell are you, you bastard!?"
roared Rulov and surprisingly quickly pulled out a chrome automatic pistol, a
Calder M2245.
I stood there like a fool. Of all the millions of people in all of Sandukar, why Sergy Rulov? Hell! He's the guy who knocks out your teeth and then kicks you in the stomach because you mumble.
"Well?! You have two seconds to answer before you die."
He raised the pistol. Derya Gieger pulled closer to him and tried to cover herself.
Her eyes looked hatefully at me.
"I am Gabriel Jandaran."
The voice was unexpectedly firm.
"I am here to bring home Miss
Gieger. She is sought by her father."
Rulov's eyes narrowed. He cast a glance at the young woman and lowered the pistol slightly. She looked wide-eyed up at him.
"What the fuck are you saying? Gieger?
The industrialist?"
He pushed away the girl.
"You didn't say your old man sat on the council, you little whore! Are you stupid, what the fuck are you doing here?"

She looked defiantly at Rulov. A look
I myself would not have given in that situation. Blood and composure began to return to my thick skull.
"Papa says he has power and influence."
She clenched her fists. Every muscle in her perfect body tensed, the legs, the back, the buttocks.
"But I wanted to show him who has power! He is weak, he feels strong with me. I hate him. I love you
Sergy!"
She opened her arms in a disarming gesture.
"For fuck's sake, you stupid fucking idiot. I don't want anything to do with you, you're poisoned. If you're connected to me I'll get all the shit in this whole city on me. I've stayed away from messing with people like Gieger for a reason.
Don't you understand that, you ugly whore?"
"But I want to get away from him, his disgusting nibbling teeth, his disgusting dry lips, how disgusting he feels inside me!"
She almost screamed. The hatred took over.
Her breasts heaved. The tears were not far away.
"You're the only one who can stop him,
I'm yours forever if you help me kill him, Sergy, you get me!"
She moved toward him, with open arms, reached toward the man, displaying her young, firm, voluptuous body.
"You, little girl, I can get whoever the fuck
I want. I take who I want. You're too dangerous!"
Rulov spat out the cigarette and turned toward the young woman.
"But Sergy, please, save me f..."
She got no further before Rulov shot.
The sharp bang echoed in the room. The fragile girl's body tumbled over like a rag doll when the lead shattered her chest. Her outstretched hand fell still after her like a farewell.
The rage came like a tap being opened. I reacted an eternity too late, but took aim with my revolver right at the large body mass. Then I only saw stars. My neck bent from the pressure of the baseball bat that struck the back of my head. I don't remember falling.
Just that the pain took over. Somewhere in my mind's eye I saw a bag of money being pulled away from my struggling hands. It went black and cold.
I woke to being wet and shivering. I lay on my back. My skull felt heavy and dull.
Multitudes of small cold spear points hit my face. I lifted my hand and shielded my eyes. It was snowing heavily and the alley I lay in was covered by a centimeter-thick layer of snow. The kitchen entrance to the Chacha club was visible further down in the alley. I rolled laboriously around and saw the silhouette of a thin man a few meters away. Kostin. He had a revolver in one hand and a baseball bat in the other. He grinned, a wide grin, the kind you have when you're a lousy player but have all the aces in hand.
I put a hand down in the snow, took strained support and got up on my knees.
My head screamed like a badly tuned brass orchestra. My heart beat as if it would get out of my chest. I
looked toward Kostin. His broken nose had almost stopped bleeding.
"The girl...?"
"The broad is stone dead, Rulov got on her while she bled out. It's not the first time. Too bad you shot her."
I blinked, unable to comprehend.
Then I felt a weight in my coat pocket. I
moved my hand down, grabbed something and pulled out Rulov's chrome Calder. My fingerprints on the weapon that murdered
Klarence Gieger's fourteen-year-old daughter.
"I haven't heard the boss laugh so much in a long time. What luck that I caught the killer when he tried to flee..."
Kostin's cackling echoed distantly in my skull, despite him standing next to me.
Death would free me from the hell that had just become my life.
He fired, cold as the snow he stood in. The pain that burned in my stomach was enormous. My balance disappeared completely and my body crashed backward, down into the white, pure, innocent snow. This was truly a night when the wolves were silent and the moon howled.
Another shot went off somewhere, someone screamed shrilly but it turned into a quiet rattling. I don't know how long I lay there in the alley before I was dragged away by my coat collar.
In a haze, a world of dream and reality together, I was shoved into the back seat of a car. My belly screamed and leaked, I screamed even more when cheap liquor was poured over the wound. Large, hairy hands began expertly bandaging it. I started shaking.
Then the slide was opened. The unmistakable sound of the loading mechanism from a pump-action shotgun rang out.
While I lay there I heard Donner's voice briefly say that I should lie still, he would go in and get Miss Gieger.
I obeyed the hoarse command and saw the tall figure disappear into the snowstorm, while I welcomed the darkness that began to envelop me.

## Chapter 1 – Introduction

Marx Begel was on his way up again. He felt it in his whole body. Sure, he had been at the bottom for a while, no damn it, below the bottom. As far down as you can get without actually giving up. It had started with Sayah Bonn, his biggest find so far. She had had everything, charisma, grace and that perfect combination of innocence and sexuality that later made her one of the Imperium's biggest stars. And it was he who had found her. He should have gotten a life of luxury, and she a brilliant career. If only it hadn't been for Joachim Lechkov. That bastard had waltzed in and swept away Sayah to Glitterdalen, lured her with a seemingly better contract and some quick steps up the career ladder. Marx had not, however, passively let Lechkov take over his star. With some money in the right pockets and some more or less clean lawyers he had let Lechkov understand that it wouldn't go so easily. Then came the fall.
It was obvious that Lechkov had staged the whole thing, from beginning to end. Accusations from women Marx had never met, and contracts with them that he had never written. Naturally all the evidence was in the office, neatly archived and signed by him himself. Marx hadn't had a chance. And when his reputation was completely sunk the gorillas had come, dragged him out into the night and without a word poured acid in his left eye. They hadn't needed greetings from Lechkov.
But that was then. That was history. When he was at his lowest he had one day lifted his gaze from the liquor glass and found her. Morgana Caravaldi would help him up again. With his help she would become an even bigger star than Sayah. And this time he had learned his lesson, he would be prepared. The revolver he constantly carried was only part of his insurance. He knew that the entertainment industry was as rotten as the underworld, with the big difference that within show business there was no honor whatsoever. He had learned from a master.
And if anyone would try to take Morgana from him they would have to pay. Dearly.

### Introduction

Imagine a cocktail party in the penthouse of a black sky-scraper covered in gothic statues and vaults, to the sounds of a raging rain that frenetically drums against the tall window panes. All present are dressed from top to toe, smiling, conversing. Deals are formed and fortunes lost during the course of a few spoken words or a few sips from frosted drink glasses. The people conceal something ominous, something feverish, within themselves, which makes them uneasy and on edge, it drives them to extremes and misfortune. Welcome to Noir.
**What roleplaying is**
Roleplaying in the Noir vein is to build a story in a group that all group members participate in with immersion. The story is created together and takes participants to another place than our own world, where they act based on the roles they have in the story. This new world contains elements from our own, but much is also different – the sky is darker, people are more nervous and the rain is colder.
In roleplaying everyone wins if the game is exciting, interesting and fun and if the protagonists handle the challenges they face in a good way.
The goal is to cooperate and together build up an engaging atmosphere and story.
One of the participants is the narrator and functions as rules arbiter and director in one, the others are players and each have a protagonist that the game is about.
The narrator portrays other characters and speaks for them in the story. The narrator is not the players' enemy, even though he/she sometimes portrays narrator characters who are hostile toward the protagonists.
The narrator prepares building blocks that the players during the game session help to build together into the story itself. The building blocks consist of narrator characters, with background stories and mutual relationships, places and special events that the protagonists can be faced with. When the protagonists begin to act the narrator characters will react and vice versa, and the story is built up by the events that arise. The goal is for the story after one or several game sessions to conclude – often through the protagonists defeating the villain, freeing the prisoner, finding the missing object or avenging a wrong.
The absolutely most important thing about roleplaying is that it should be fun. It's good to talk outside the game about what is and is not fun, and if something goes wrong during the game's course one must see to doing something about it so it doesn't happen again.

> **Character Sheet** — *This section contains a fillable print character sheet. See the original PDF for the form layout.*

### What Noir Is

Noir is a dystopian roleplaying game of darkness, violence, and death.
The dystopia consists of a worldview that resembles our own during the early 20th century but which is nevertheless not the same. The world is darker and more dangerous—something we recoil from, something we don't want to experience for real.
Noir is a French word meaning simply "black"
and it's a fitting name as it encompasses darkness and light as well as the grayscale between them. The game's setting and mood is dark, bleak and hopeless—where individuals are secondary before the primary state apparatus, where bureaucrats and clans fight for power in the countless corridors of the authorities, where the State's police without cause bring in citizens for interrogation and where insane madmen, strange forces and hidden mysticism lurk in the fog-veiled alleys.
In Noir's world, human life has little value and free will is far from self-evident. Nature is plundered of its wealth and ravaged in a reckless hunt for profit and resources. Vast wars rage against a nameless enemy and many of the Imperium's men perish in the meat grinders of war. All occurs in the State's presence and at its command. Orders shall be obeyed, not questioned. Many who live in the world fight a daily struggle for survival in the vast machinery. If you try to break societal patterns you must be prepared to fight for your cause and for your views. It's a cold and harsh world where many meet grim fates. The rich have enormous fortunes and the poor are left without any social safety nets whatsoever.

### Mood & Setup

The goal with Noir is to create a setting and mood where exciting, dramatic and entertaining stories can be born and driven forward by players and narrator together. What follows is a number of highly stylistic and general thoughts on how Noir can be played, even though it naturally supports other types of stories as well. The below should be seen as a very generalizing description, where multitudes of exceptions from common conventions exist side by side.
The idea is that Noir shall contain elements to create precisely that game world that both players and narrator want to experience adventures in. A melancholic world where fate guides dark doubting heroes in a hopeless struggle against a condescending authority, widespread corruption and brutal criminality—and not least a struggle against themselves, where destructive emotions like violence, vengeance, lust and desire brood beneath the surface.
The players create their protagonists who are often anti-heroes with traumatic experiences in their past. They have weaknesses and fears like everyone else and no one is perfect, but they nevertheless possess the ability to perform small or great wonders in the dystopian world in which they live. Their innate morality is usually questionable but is rarely either completely black or completely white, but lands in a gray zone where egoism and survival instincts decide. Their motivations vary—perhaps someone wants to achieve forgiveness for past sins, another wants to find their childhood sweetheart and finally confess their love while a third wants to avenge the corrupt prosecutor who made him or her lose family, home and honor.
Common phenomena in Noir's world are corruption, violence, cynicism, pessimism, alienation, guilt, desperation and paranoia.
The individuals who move in the stories are often hardboiled private eyes, charming sociopaths, corrupt cops, zealous bureaucrats, cunning intelligence agents, brutal gangsters, pathetic petty criminals, chain-smoking war veterans and cold-hearted killers. Common traits in these are obsession, crude cynicism, resignation, guilt, brooding, inner insecurity and a sordid past, full of dark secrets, tragedies and broken promises.
Often they are morally dubious losers who fight from the underdog position and who struggle to even survive in the harsh and unforgiving world they find themselves in. Not infrequently they fail. Betrayal, murder, vengeance and guilt are very common ingredients in the stories.
The women in the stories are often strongly polarized
—either it's about the maternal, safe, loving and dutiful woman, or about the deadly woman—femme fatale—strong women who by various means control and manipulate their surroundings, especially men. They use charm, sexuality and charisma to seduce, turn the heads of and control their victims, and are often perceived as being mysterious, enigmatic, beautiful, sophisticated, lustful, irresponsible, tough, quick-tongued and/or desperate. Their motives are many times hidden and they use duplicity, flattery and lies to mislead those who seek the truth.
Many times the woman wants to get some person, usually a man, to perform a task. Often it's about something that is illegal and/or life-threatening to carry out, but the woman's enticements with rewards on multiple levels get even the most stubborn of men to give in, regardless of risks or consequences.

Surroundings and environments are characterized by darkness, shadows, rain and bleakness. Rain-wet asphalt, cracked facades in dark alleys and dirty back streets sparsely illuminated by bare light bulbs or weathered neon signs, are classic scenes in a typical Noir story. This shapes the people who live there, many are morally broken, sadistic and selfish, and love, peace and care are as rare as an honest police constable. The individuals are broken, with weaknesses, bad experiences and wounded psyches, and cannot find the way out of their own hell but only repeat their bad patterns time and again in hope of reaching something better, of succeeding with something, even if it's only surviving until morning.
**Sandukar**
The world's center, the world's light, the world's darkness: Sandukar
—the eternal city. Here the dead skyscrapers stretch high up toward the firmament. The streets are shadowed by massive zeppelins that navigate between the air harbors and everywhere can be heard the constant pounding from the machines. Sure, the smoke from the chimneys lies like a sick fog over the city and sure the acid rain falls often, but in the middle of the despotic world's center lies beauty and freedom. Some seek it in the one god, others in spirits. I myself prefer the whores' warm bodies and their soft words in my ear, while they empty my wallet. So I hope you will enjoy it here; oppressed, sullied and forgotten.
—Vautiren Markov, street philosopher, entertainer and agitator wanted by the State
Sandukar is the city. The city where things happen, where raw materials are shipped on endless railways, where the poor journey to find fortune. Sandukar is the city that never sleeps, that is never calm or quiet. Around this tangle of people, iron, stone, concrete and glass are massive industries that spew their sick black smoke over the city's inhabitants, making them live in a gray haze which the sun rarely manages to break through.
As if it were time's hub, everything seems to revolve around Sandukar, by some called the eternal city. Even before the ancient frost had faded and the mighty wind stilled, the industries' smoke rose anew from the city's foundations. The city devours life, the city devours death, the city devours violence, torment and horror.
Sandukar is built on the western coast, by the Oil
Sea, and has millions of registered inhabitants. It is also the capital of the Imperium, a collection of realms which are ruled by the State and its officials from tradition-bound and ancient clans. In Sandukar everything can be found, from basic goods to luxury, from the harmless to the vulgar, from the moderate to the perverse. The city is a breeding ground for human darkness, selfishness and degradation.
Many of the inhabitants are passivized and spend their spare time in their sordid apartments to later journey to dirty and heavy industrial jobs where they spend twelve hours per day. In families they gather around the radio sets in the evenings or they read aloud from books or newspapers.
Despite the harsh world, most have learned to live with the State's oppression and no longer think about it. They uncritically absorb the news the State grants them through censored newspapers and newsreels. Critical thinking

has nearly vanished and people would rather spend time reading about the latest movie stars, their relationships and scandals. Similarly, they read about the brave soldiers who fight at the fronts and about the terrible enemies that threaten the realm. They choose not to see reality in order to cope with living. For even in this harsh world there is hope, love and pleasure. In their free time people enjoy sports, theater, cinema visits or visits to museums and art galleries. Some prefer a cup of coffee at a café, others have a few beers at their favorite tavern. They go with the flow and stay away from trouble.

For others, who have made something of themselves, there are pleasures to partake in such as exquisite food, magnificent operas, exclusive balls and secret order meetings. A third group lives in hiding and makes their living by smuggling in or out contraband, refugees, slaves, weapons, drugs or stolen goods.

The city's architectural appearance fascinates many.
The high-rises are built in stone, with lead-framed windows and grinning stone gargoyles. Marble, copper and steel are also evident. Several architectural styles are mixed but the Gothic spires and arched vaults as well as the Art Nouveau movement's beautiful forms are dominant. In addition to the majestic high-rises there are the overcrowded workers' districts where the hard-toiling laborers live in soot-blackened brick houses. The industries themselves are massive constructions of brick, steel and cogwheels. From their tall chimneys constantly billows a sickly stinking smoke whose ashes like a shroud of mourning settle over the surroundings.

Above the houses the zeppelins glide forward in silence to the airship harbors in the city's center. Sandukar is surrounded by a high wall of black basalt and at its countless gates thorough inspections are conducted by state troops. The circular shape, the canals' unusual construction and the massive towers that reach toward the clouds, higher than any other buildings, reinforce the city's surrealistic feeling.
One can find massive monuments, ancient constructions and decorated halls where the lineages are said to take their pleasure.

Nowhere in the Empire is corruption as widespread as in its stronghold. The bureaucrats, civil servants, authority personnel and police force are all severely afflicted, which causes ordinary citizens to harbor great contempt for all forms of authority. At the same time they know they must have cash on hand to escape the greedy inspectors' wrath and be punished for some obscure crime that the inhuman law book is full of, while the tribunals'
lawyers eagerly lick their lips. One must be careful so that no one reports one's anti-state thoughts or expressions. Members of the secret state police, Stapo, move among the people and monitor, eavesdrop and note what happens. Should one receive a visit from the black-coated Stapo collectors one evening, one knows it is probably the last time one sees one's loved ones.

Within the city's sectors the individual's movement is often restricted and to be able to go to another sector requires passes and correct identification papers. Subway and canal boats are commonly used, but for longer journeys trains are required. To get in or out of the city one must take one of the bridge gates, which are guarded by state inspectors and city guard. The queues at exit and entry are usually long and patience-trying.

Sandukar is both the dream and the nightmare in one—a place where one can succeed in reaching the ultimate achievement, but where one just as easily can die a slow and agonizing death.

### The Empire

The Empire is the gigantic state where the stories in Noir take place. It is said that once upon a time war raged in the Empire. The peoples fought against each other in an unending spiral of violence. Times of peace were short and there was always some realm that fought to conquer its opponents. When everything fell under the State's shadow the realms became provinces under the lineages' rule. Some of the provinces are very rich and have multiple cities, others are wastelands that are plundered for their natural resources but otherwise left alone.

The Empire covers thousands of miles and is connected by poorly maintained roads, canals and airship harbors for the zeppelins. If one wants to travel through the realms one does so most safely by train.
The railway tracks stretch like a spider's web across the Empire and stop at all major stations. This way of traveling is cheap and fast, moreover the State has good control over the passengers and exactly where they travel. The rich use the airships to travel and most places have an airship harbor with at least one ship's capacity.

Beyond a few larger cities, where the capital Sandukar shines brightest, the realm is fairly barren, meager and desolate. Few people in the cities see much of the wilderness, the pine forests or the majestic mountains. In the wilderness there are many hidden things. It is said to be a place for rebels and outlaws. Likewise many areas stand under direct control of the lineages, who dwell there in their massive estates and castles. Here ordinary people are not given access and what transpires behind the walls is kept secret.

Despite the world outside the cities being ill-regarded, here one finds the resources that drive the realms. From the mountains the iron ore and coal are retrieved, from the forests the timber, from the fields the grain and livestock. Here and there rise massive industries where the workers are slaves under the companies.
Usually one only sees the smoke from these in the distance.

Every area within the realm is ruled by a family or clan from one of the great lineages. Intrigue-making, double-dealing and machinations are daily fare among the lineages, as they wish to reach as great success as possible when it comes to riches, power and prestige. It may well happen at the expense of another lineage or in the worst case another family within one's own clan.

In the north the war rages against the enemy. The war has been going on for decades and devours manpower and materiel at a furious pace, all to reach the next trench and conquer another bomb-torn ruined city. But the most important thing is to ensure that the enemy does not succeed in breaking through and destroying the good lifestyle and standard that the State has provided its subjects with in the Empire.

In the east the war rages against a province that broke free from the realm. The province is surrounded but its strong armament and ability for self-sufficiency make it a very difficult target. Nevertheless it is considered only a matter of time before it is reconquered.

### The Lineages

There are a number of lineage houses which under the State's authority watch over the Empire and its people. The lineages follow their own laws and have masses of money, influence and power at their disposal. In recent times it has become increasingly common for lineages to socialize with the rich and powerful from the ordinary population. The older and more conservative lineages, however, prefer to see the people as servants with whom one has no social interaction.

Between the lineages numerous conflicts occur in secret. This rarely reaches the ordinary people's ears. Assassinations, bribes, threats, intrigues and disputes can escalate into major conflicts where many innocent members of the people are used as pawns in the power game.

### The Bureaucrats

The administrative chaos that drives the Empire seems as ingenious as it is incomprehensible.
For an outside observer it is impossible to grasp, and even for those who work within the administration, all parts outside one's own specific area are shrouded in mystery.

Despite its complicated and sometimes seemingly illogical function, the bureaucracy works and maintains the Empire. If one has great influence or money one can take shortcuts within the bureaucracy—but most ordinary mortals must endure its incomprehensible web.

### An Evening with Noir

Here follows an example of a typical roleplaying session in Noir.
Petter is the narrator while Marco, Sarah and Rikard are players. Marco portrays Gabriel, a socially smooth private detective who can handle a pistol. Sarah's role is the efficient criminal inspector Anya Malov, who frantically ensures that the law is followed. Anya and Gabriel have a certain romantic tension between them but Anya has difficulty with Gabriel being, as she sees it, overprotective. Rikard plays Mordekai, an indifferent, large-built ox of a man, with brawling in his blood and masses of dark secrets in his past.

The scene is a rainy Sandukar, the capital of the Empire. It is night and the sky is covered by heavy rain clouds which pour their contents toward the ground below. In the city district
Ikonen the three protagonists struggle to get open a back door to a darkened mechanical workshop. Anya is there because she suspects there should be information about human smuggling among the company's ledgers. Gabriel is there because his client's missing brother was last seen entering the building. Since the workshop is located in one of the really bad quarters Gabriel contacted an acquaintance—Mordekai—to get support if it should be needed.

In the example certain rule terms will be used, which may seem strange if one doesn't know Noir's rules.
Feel no frustration over this but read on—and read the example again later when the rules have also been read through, for increased understanding.

Everything the protagonists say in the scene is written within quotation marks, as opposed to what the players say the protagonists do. All rules and die rolls are written within brackets.

**Petter (Narrator):** The rain pours down over the city and threatens to flood the deserted and dark back alley where you stand. The clock approaches midnight this Monday evening.
At the door stands Gabriel on his knees, determinedly focused on opening the lock in the rusty metal door into the workshop.
What are the rest of you doing?

**Sarah (Anya):** I keep watch backward so we don't get visitors. Also I flip up my coat collar and adjust my hat so I get as little wet as possible.

**Rikard (Mordekai):** I don't give a shit about the rain and tiredly light another smoke.

**Petter (Narrator):** Okay. Gabriel, check how it goes with the lock. Roll a difficulty roll with Dexterity, don't forget the expertise you have in lockpicking.

**Marco (Gabriel):** Yes, I roll. I have skill twelve.
[Marco rolls two ten-sided dice]. Yes, it's well over 20, probably 26, 27 something, do you need to know exactly?

**Petter (Narrator):** Is the roll exceptional?

**Marco (Gabriel):** No.

**Petter (Narrator):** It's okay, as long as it's successful.
You reach the lock's challenge level. With a click you feel how the lock opens to your sensitive fingers.

**Marco (Gabriel):** I whisper and wave to the others and put away the picks.

**Rikard (Mordekai):** I walk forward.

**Sarah (Anya):** I follow, but keep watch backward.

**Petter (Narrator):** You see nothing and hear nothing either. It's dead as the grave here.

### The Church

Like a dog cowed by fear, the church of the one god cowers in the shadow of the State. As time passes it grows ever weaker. Still there are those who pray to the one god, but the oppression of those who visit the church grows ever harsher. Many keep their prayers to themselves and rarely bear the symbol of the cross openly. Yet the church has the power to defy the State, for according to an ancient decree it has the right to spread its teachings. In the church one can find piety and benevolence, but many evildoers hide behind the holy facade, and here it is greed and dark lusts that rule.

Many within the church believe that they now live in the end times. That purgatory will soon sweep across the world. Others believe this has already happened. That the darkness and suffering that surrounds them can only mean that humanity is doomed, and that only through submission before the one god can one be guided to the kingdom of heaven.

Since the uprising in the kingdom of Presvedonia and the church's newfound power there, though the holy scripture is proclaimed in a different interpretation, the State's agents monitor budding revolts within the church's ranks. It would not take much for the State to find justification to crush the church completely.

### The Contagion

The State's apparatus of power knows what exists and what does not exist. To claim anything other than what the State has proclaimed is punishable by death. Sometimes, however, events and mysteries break through the State's control. People see and hear things they shouldn't, and some things cannot be hidden. In Sandukar, a rumor spreads of a soul plague, reaching more and more ears.

The plague is a destructive force that destroys and perverts everything it touches. Within the church, one can hear priests tell their confidants about it and warn them of the afflicted. In the poor quarters, street thugs whisper of strange things living in the city's shadows. The afflicted are driven to madness before the contagion finally extinguishes their life flame. But some of them are said to return from death as something far more malevolent and terrible. The
State claims that all this is the enemy's tactic to create chaos, and those who spread this message do best to do so in secret.

### The Military

The Empire has a mighty military force. The infantry is the main unit, and the massive battles raging in trenches, ruined cities, and during violent offensives consume many lives. Simpler types of armored vehicles have begun to appear on the battlefields. These, however, have trouble advancing in difficult terrain and require constant service. The artillery available is powerful but not particularly precise, and often bombs detonate within friendly territory. Even though aircraft are effective as bombers and fighters, they play a withdrawn role, as most generals believe that battle should be fought on the ground and not in the air.

Recently, all soldiers have been equipped with gas masks, as the eastern enemy commonly uses various types of poison gas in their attacks. At first, it was considered that the loss of soldiers in gas attacks was less severe than the cost of having to manufacture gas masks for the army.

The gas masks, however, do not always provide adequate protection, as the enemy constantly introduces new biological weapons. Soldiers who have spent long periods on the eastern front often suffer from constant nausea, temporary hallucinations, and tremors, as a result of being exposed to poison gas countless times.

Only men are allowed to fight in the Empire's army. Most come from poor circumstances and see a chance for a better future by letting themselves be recruited. Those who survive 20 years within the army receive a pension and can advance as officers. In addition to soldiers who volunteer, there are special army units with former prisoners.
These have been pardoned in exchange for spending time in the army to atone for their crimes. These units have worse

Summary – Roleplaying
• Everyone participating in playing Noir is called a player, except one who is called the narrator.
• Each player has a main character in the game, a hero of the story, and decides for themselves what the main character will do.
• The game proceeds as a told story between the players and the narrator and takes place in each participant's imagination. Neither game pieces nor boards are used.
• The players usually cooperate to achieve the story's goal.
• The narrator is the game's leader
– he/she acts as director, screenwriter, cameraman, and judge, and portrays all supporting roles in the story.
• Each main character in a story is described with game values in a form called the character file. The values are used when the main character's skills in different areas are assessed or need to be checked.
• Game rules exist to represent the game world, create dramatic conditions, and handle the unpredictability that dice rolls bring.

**Marco (Gabriel):** I try to get the door open as quietly as possible.

**Petter (Narrator):** It's stiff and some creaking is impossible to avoid, but you do your best.

**Marco (Gabriel):** I slip inside quietly.

**Rikard (Mordekai):** I follow his example.

**Sarah (Anya):** Me too.

**Petter (Narrator):** Okay, can you all make a resistance roll with Agility to see how quietly you move, as it's quite poorly lit in here, or do you want to turn on the light?
[All three players shake their heads and roll the dice]

**Sarah (Anya):** I get a sum of 24.

**Marco (Gabriel):** And I get 15.

**Rikard (Mordekai):** Yippee, I got a total of 12, exceptional too, with double twos. So that's bound to fail...
[The narrator makes a hidden roll]

**Petter (Narrator):** You slowly enter the premises, among machines and tables with tools. It smells acrid of solvents and oil. The rain drums against the roof. Suddenly
Mordekai bumps into a pile of tools whose handles stick out beyond the table edge. The tools fall onto the concrete floor with a terrible racket.

**Marco (Gabriel):** I stop, crouch down, and try to take cover behind the nearest machine.

**Sarah (Anya):** I sigh and draw my pistol.

**Rikard (Mordekai):** And I curse like hell: "Fucking goddamn cursed shit-bastard, you shall die, for Satan's sake!" Then
I kick, in frustration, a wrench away so it slams into the far wall.

**Sarah (Anya):** "But quiet, for fuck's sake!"

**Rikard (Mordekai):** I give her the look. The look that says more than words.

**Sarah (Anya):** I raise my left hand, disarmingly like that, it's calm, you know.

**Petter (Narrator):** Gabriel, make a difficulty roll.

**Marco (Gabriel):** Really? For what?

**Petter (Narrator):** You don't know.

**Marco (Gabriel):** Okay. [Rolls the dice] The result is
15, an eight and a seven.

**Petter (Narrator):** [Checks Gabriel's skill in Intuition that the narrator has written down and sees that the roll succeeded] Gabriel, even through all the racket and commotion you sense that you're not alone in here.

**Marco (Gabriel):** Oh, I scan the premises and slowly draw my submachine gun with one hand. With the other hand I try to gesture to the others that something is up.

**Petter (Narrator):** You others perceive that Gabriel seems to want something.

**Rikard (Mordekai):** Really?

**Sarah (Anya):** I look at him questioningly.

**Petter (Narrator):** Suddenly the light comes on, the heavy fixtures are awakened with a hum to life over the course of a few seconds. Up on a platform, behind and above you, you see a grated floor with a spiral staircase up to an office behind glass panels. Now you also see how a work lamp seems to have been lit up there. On the grated floor stand three people: a man with a leather jacket and a cigarette in his mouth, another in a suit and glasses, and a third – a giant bruiser

equipment and are usually assigned the most dangerous missions, such as storming machine gun nests, advancing through minefields, or leading mass assaults against the trenches.

The women who wish to fight for freedom must settle for staff positions, maintenance, and hospital service, as the war leadership doesn't want to mix the two sexes unnecessarily.
They would be considered harmful to morale and esprit de corps, diverting focus among individual soldiers. However, one can find female officers within the military. They often come from the families and command just as well as their male counterparts.

Soldiers in the field have few pleasures. After brief leaves, they are forced to return to the war and witness which of their comrades have fallen to the enemy. Alcohol and drugs are used to cope, and the female lifers who are forced into the field are heavily used and usually become breeding grounds for various lice and diseases. Everything contributes to making the soldiers inhuman and cold-hearted. But there are those who find comradeship and those who burn with their struggle to protect the Empire. Then there are always those who begin to enjoy the horrors of battle. They cannot adapt to a normal world but remain in the war because it is only in the bloody clashes that they belong.

### The Enemy

The Empire has two major enemies. These diabolical adversaries threaten constantly, and only through the State's forceful intervention are they kept at bay. There is no communication with the enemy that the soldiers notice. The enemy must only be killed. Prisoners may only be taken on orders from superiors. To communicate with the enemy in any way is seen as treason and carries the death penalty. The State's propaganda fuels the hatred soldiers feel toward their enemies
– only through their extermination can peace be achieved.

The northern enemy: In the north, beyond the Arkady
Mountains lies a wilderness of ruined cities, burned forests, bomb craters, swamps, and mile-long trenches.
Time and again the Empire's armies have clashed with the northern enemy in this hellish place. Millions of corpses lie sunk in the muddy ground along with rusty vehicles and mines. The war has been going on for nearly a century – sometimes in the form of a static trench war, sometimes as bloody offensives.

The eastern enemy: The Todescu family in the eastern province of Presvedonia unexpectedly turned against the State. The province, which is very rich in raw materials and industries, mobilized its troops and crushed the loyal army units that remained. During the years the war has lasted, several cities have been destroyed, and the eastern enemy seems to constantly receive new powerful weapons on their side. The
State's forces now have the upper hand and have slowly begun to close the noose around the eastern enemy's territory. Every meter is costly in terms of lives and capital. The enemy's massive artillery pieces bombard the State's forces day and night, and they have control over the airspace. A further problem is enemy agents who have infiltrated the army and bureaucracy. The eastern enemy, despite being at a disadvantage, is very strong.

The Church has gained great power in the rebellious province, and soldiers fight fanatically in accordance with the word of the one god in a new interpretation of the doctrine. On soot-scorched battlefields rise crosses where prisoners have been crucified to appease the one god. In the provincial capital Marcinko, which is the enemy's stronghold, the Silver
Cathedral has become the seat of the enemy's leadership. From here are proclaimed the commandments of war's cleansing fire that must sweep across the Empire. The people hunger and lack many of the pleasures found in the other cities of the
Empire, but in their hearts burns a supernatural faith. A hope for a better world after purgatory.

### Rules

Rules in roleplaying are primarily used to create a fair balance in those moments where action and result need to be determined during the course of the story. They are used to simulate

with bulging muscles under a dirty undershirt. The man in the suit speaks. "You have no business here, you're trespassing on private land and property. Who are you?"

**Sarah (Anya):** I stand up and keep the pistol hidden behind my back. "We're from the police. We have suspicions of criminal activity here."

**Petter (Narrator):** "Oh, the police. So, where is your search warrant?"

**Sarah (Anya):** Damn, I knew we should have waited.
"It's on its way, sir...?"

**Rikard (Mordekai):** I look for something big and heavy that I could throw at them.

**Petter (Narrator):** Okay, you see a pipe wrench on a bench nearby, just a few steps away. "Young lady, on its way, you say? So you don't actually have it? So right now you're just simple burglars, which means I have full right to defend myself? Good." You see him turn around and nod at the other two, who immediately draw weapons. The muscle guy has a shotgun while the man in the leather jacket has two revolvers. Roll initiative. No one is considered surprised.
[Everyone involved rolls for Initiative.]

**Marco (Gabriel):** Successful roll, 16.

**Rikard (Mordekai):** The dice hate me tonight, I
get seven.

**Sarah (Anya):** I know how it feels, I got nine...

**Petter (Narrator):** The guy with the revolvers is first at 22, he aims at you, Anya.

**Sarah (Anya):** I try to shoot back first. No, my initiative is too low for that. I defend myself.

**Petter (Narrator):** Make a resistance roll – Defense ranged combat.

**Sarah (Anya):** And I don't have any Fate either. [Rolls the dice] Let's see, sum 14, no 15.

**Petter (Narrator):** [Also rolls] Higher. He empties both revolvers into you. [Rolls again]. You take 21 in
Shock, I suspect that's also a Wound?

**Sarah (Anya):** Damn, yes it is. I make the roll for
Will to Live. [Rolls] But, it can't be true! I'm mortally wounded!

**Petter (Narrator):** What's the result of both dice?

**Sarah (Anya):** Eight.

**Petter (Narrator):** Then you die in eight rounds...
Do you lose consciousness?

**Sarah (Anya):** [Rolls dice] No.

**Petter (Narrator):** You others see how Anya reads the situation too late. The guy with the revolvers laughs mockingly while he squeezes the triggers again and again.
The bullets whine around Anya, many ricochet off the machines around her, but several shots hit her in the body and arms. You see her fall backward in a fine mist of blood.

**Marco (Gabriel):** "Nooooo!!" I awaken a drive:
Save Anya's life, level Burning, okay?

**Petter (Narrator):** That feels reasonable given what just happened. Gabriel, it's your turn.

**Marco (Gabriel):** I want to run crouched toward Anya, while I fire snap shots up at the platform and the revolver guy. I spend two Fate points and roll two extra dice, because I must succeed. I add my character trait Loving too, since I want to protect and

different events in the world in a consistent and somewhat predictable way. Even the interaction between parts of the world and its inhabitants is governed by rules for the same reason.

The rules in Noir follow a simple basic concept where consistency and simplicity are guidelines. All dice rolls to determine results are made with two ten-sided dice, to which a fixed value is added. If the result is higher than either an opponent's value or a fixed difficulty level, the roll is successful. If not, it counts as failed. This is the rules in brief—more information about dice rolls can be found on page 199.

### The philosophy behind the rules

The rules in Noir are designed to be easy to understand and consistent to use. They are built on two key words—plausibility and credibility. This means that the purpose of the rules, beyond directly governing interaction with the game world and simulating it, is to emphasize the feeling and atmosphere that Noir is meant to convey. This is done by considering what is plausible and credible based on the conditions in the game world and the story—not based on what is plausible and credible in our own world. It is important to remember this since Noir's world is different compared to our own. In Noir there are forces that affect ordinary people that do not exist in our world.

The players take on the roles of protagonists in a dramatic story where dark heroic deeds, agonizing self-sacrifices and brutal violence are a very tangible part, something most of us are thankfully spared from. Therefore, one cannot base assumptions about plausibility and credibility on our world—instead one must try to consider whether it is plausible and credible in Noir's world and in the story that is ongoing. This may sound difficult, but one quickly realizes at what level one should be for it to feel right.

A very important philosophy is that the rules should inspire the narrator and the players. The rules exist as tools for acting in a fantastic and different world. Therefore, it is crucial that the rules also facilitate and provide ideas for creating new adventures, and that they increase tension without bringing either frustration or pace-killing stops.

### Realism

A very important point is that plausibility and credibility are not the same as realism. Noir is not meant to be a realistic game but one where melancholy heroic deeds, epic showdowns, dramatic twists, action-filled battles and exciting escapes occur without entailing such great risks that the protagonists are more or less guaranteed to die as a result. The goal of any role-playing game is to have fun when participating, and it is easier to achieve this goal when the game allows bolder and more fantastic actions and there is a reasonable chance of succeeding with them. Realism often goes against this goal, since a hit with a firearm usually immediately puts a person out of combat condition and forces them into long hospital stays—and how fun is that, really?

At the same time, it should be mentioned that even though Noir is meant to be an action-filled game where cool maneuvers can be performed, it is not a superhero game where the protagonists can fly, run at a hundred kilometers per hour or throw fireballs at the enemy. Such things are neither plausible nor credible.

### Online

Noir also exists on the Internet, where news, information, background material, tools and similar things are available for viewing and download. The vision is that Noir should be a living game where participants can engage and create a community together. Links to discussion forums and also a constantly updated interactive reference work, a so-called wiki, can be found there.

The address to Noir's home on the Internet is: www.noir.nu

save Anya. [Rolls the dice] I get 23 total with the two best dice results, after deduction for recoil.

**Petter (The Narrator):** Good use of the character trait. [Rolls dice] You get higher than what the opponent gets on their chance defense, so it's you who describes the sequence.

**Marco (Gabriel):** The pistol is emptied anyway—eight shots are used. [Rolls the dice] Damage effect is a bit over 20 so I suspect he's incapacitated, and I don't have to come up with the exact sum?

**Petter (The Narrator):** Correct, so what happens in the scene?

**Marco (Gabriel):** Okay. I desperately lunge toward Anya, with my gaze focused on her bloody body that just reaches the floor, and the blonde hair that flutters. At the same time I shoot as fast as I can with the pistol up at the landing, without really looking in that direction. Most of the bullets go astray, one shatters the glass window, but one hits the revolver man right in the head so the laughter abruptly falls silent. The body slowly slides over the railing and lands with a crash in a wooden crate full of scrap. Since my magazine is empty, I let the pistol fall to the floor and I take Anya's head in my hands, with tears in my eyes.

**Petter (The Narrator):** Nice. Mordekai?

**Rikard (Mordekai):** Easy, I throw the pipe bomb at the guy with the shotgun. [Rolls dice] Yeah well, I get 33, I suspect I get the highest?

**Petter (The Narrator):** [Rolls dice] True. He fails exceptionally too, so he's not in on this.

**Rikard (Mordekai):** [Rolls dice] Damage effect is, wow, that was high, maxed dice, a full 31, stunning admittedly but still.

- Petter (The Narrator): He's incapacitated so you get to tell what happens.

**Rikard (Mordekai):** Okay, the pipe bomb leaves my rough hands, rotates around its own center point in a circular motion and hits the surprised guy in the throat. The force of the impact breaks his neck and he stumbles backward, into the office, gurgling.

**Petter (The Narrator):** Lovely. A door slams somewhere. The glasses guy seems to have removed himself and you hear a car engine roaring to life outside, what do you do?

**Sarah (Anya):** "Help... me... Gabriel..." I cough and spit blood. I seek dimly for contact with Gabriel's gaze.

**Marco (Gabriel):** I stroke Anya soothingly over the bloody hair and at the same time try to find something I can use as a bandage for Anya, rags or an apron or something. I also shout to Mordekai. "Catch that bastard, I'll take care of Anya, get the hell out!"

**Rikard (Mordekai):** I nod to Gabriel, draw my heavy revolver smiling and rush howling with excitement toward the motor sound.

**Petter (The Narrator):** When you wrench open the door you are met by the rain and you see that...

And there we leave the three protagonists to their fates.

## Chapter 2 – The protagonist

Even when he called me that evening I heard that he was onto something big. There was no mistaking the excitement in his voice when he said that name I have carried with me ever since, that name I have on my lips when I wake from my darkest nightmares.

We set a meeting at our regular place, a small beer café a couple of blocks from the canal where he would deliver the evidence, what I needed to finish my article. But he didn't come.

Finally I gave up my wait and left the premises. Listlessly I looked around, lit a cigarette and began to walk down toward the stinking water that runs through our city like a disease-ridden vein. That's where I caught sight of him. He lay next to his car, pierced by several bullets. His chest heaved laboriously and it was a miracle that he was still alive. I threw myself down beside him and when I lifted his head into my lap he briefly focused his gaze and coughed.

"It's bigger... than we thought..."

I could say nothing. The thoughts raced in my head. It was my fault that he was here, it was I who had given him the assignment. I suddenly felt a heavy pressure over my chest and couldn't breathe. The heart. It wasn't the first time. The city's sounds disappeared around me and I was only aware of my own heart's irregular pounding. He said something more, but I couldn't hear what. The heart sounds drowned out everything else.

The attack passed as quickly as it had begun, and the sounds of the surroundings came back. He rattled, coughed up more blood and then the spark in his eyes went out. It took me a few seconds to understand that my brother had just died in my arms. I stared out into the night, and my mind immediately found a thought to focus on. A name he had said when he called. The man who was behind it all. And while the body grew cold in my lap I swore that I would not give up until the Engineer lay dead at my feet.

The game's heroes, those the spotlight greedily craves to capture, are the protagonists. Each player enters the role-playing game through their own protagonist and takes him/her through the story created by the participants, on a journey where no one knows where it ends.

### Creating a protagonist

The people that the players portray in Noir are called protagonists. The rules in this chapter are used to create interesting, deep and multi-layered protagonists who are anchored in the world they operate in.

It may be appropriate to point out that these rules are not intended to simulate all the individuals that exist in the world, but are for creating protagonists specifically, as these are often special people who experience exciting and amazing events.

### Who the protagonists are

Each player has their own protagonist through which they act with and against the game world. The protagonists in Noir's dark, bleak and harsh world are often antiheroes with tragic backgrounds, troubled relationships, destructive behaviors and weaknesses such as guilt, vengefulness, desire, wrath and obsession. Not infrequently they harbor a hunger for justice, an opportunity to set things right, or to get a new chance after life has repeatedly kicked the shit out of them.

That the protagonists are not flawless, good, shining hero types makes them interesting to role-play, as their various dark sides make them encounter the story in unpredictable and exciting ways.

But the protagonists are, despite their human flaws and heavy emotional baggage, the game's heroes and they are the lead role holders in the story that unfolds. This is the reason why this chapter is fairly extensive as it allows a player to find their protagonist and create them as he/she wishes.

Many of the parts covered in this chapter, for example attributes and abilities, are described in depth in the section called "The protagonist's anatomy", on page 212.

**The Protagonist**

How a protagonist is created, step by step
Here follows a step-by-step description of how a protagonist is created. By following it in order, all the parts needed to produce a complete protagonist will be covered. Each step is described in more detail below.

1. Get a character sheet, a pen, and an eraser. The character sheet is available for copying on page 354.
2. Choose an archetype, see page 40, or go directly to step 4.
3. Choose a background package, see page 54, go to step 13.
4. Come up with a concept, an idea about who you want to portray. The concept is described according to the format "Nature + Occupation", see below.
5. Assign attribute values, see page 28.
6. Increase the ability values as desired, see page 28.
7. Calculate the protagonist's skills, see page 28.
8. Assign expertises where applicable, see page 28.
9. Determine the protagonist's limitations, see page 28.
10. Determine the protagonist's traits, see page 28.
11. Define the protagonist's character traits. These are described on page 28.
12. Determine the protagonist's relationships. This is described on page 29.
13. Calculate attributes and attribute values, see page 29.
14. Deepen the protagonist if desired. See page 30.
15. Decide the person's name.
16. Determine the protagonist's liquid funds and acquire equipment, see page 94.

Step 1: The Character Sheet
The protagonist's mechanical values, personal information, background, equipment and the like are recorded on a character sheet. The character sheet is a standardized form that has pre-printed headings which facilitate use during the course of the story. A copyable character sheet can be found on page 354.

The protagonist can also be written down on regular paper, but it requires more work and can become cluttered.

Step 2: Archetypes
A player who has no clear picture of their protagonist's personality and background can choose to use an archetype. The archetype is a typical example and summarizes a protagonist's life before he/she becomes an active part of the story. It concerns both experiences, events, career choices, lessons, upbringing and interests. The archetype also provides the protagonist's current situation, which is stated in the concept.

If this feels like good support in creating the protagonist, continue to page 40 to see the archetypes that are presented. When the archetype is chosen, proceed to Skills on page 28. You can also choose to create the protagonist entirely from your own imagination, and skip the help that an archetype can provide—then go directly to Attributes on page 28.

Step 3: Background Packages
Background packages summarize a protagonist's concept, limitations and traits, as well as character traits and relationships. The idea is that a player who doesn't want to micromanage their protagonist's background should quickly be able to find and use a ready-made package that fits, or at least is close to, the player's idea for the protagonist. Background packages can also be used as a base in which parts can be swapped out if desired.

Step 4: Concept
All protagonists fit into a concept. The concept is a very brief summary of the protagonist's character. The purpose of the concept is to help the player form an internal image of how the protagonist acts and functions and what place he/she takes in the story. The choice of concept has no mechanical impact but is only intended as support.

The concept is always written in the form Nature + Occupation. Nature refers to a summary of the protagonist's current prominent character, temperament and mood. Occupation is what the protagonist primarily does right now—job, assignment or engagement. Each archetype, if one has been chosen, shows a selection of suitable concept constellations. Otherwise, there are suggestions for both nature and occupations on page 28. The concept is noted on the character sheet.

Examples of concepts are hardboiled private eye, war-damaged veteran, constantly intoxicated brawler, cynical whore, determined and rule-breaking cop or curious reporter who stands on the side of the weak.

When developing the concept, the protagonist's general occupation, gender and age should also be considered. Mechanically this doesn't matter, but it provides support to the player during the creative creation process.

If a player has no idea for a concept, this step can be skipped and filled in later, when the protagonist has taken shape.

A concept can very well change as a protagonist gains new experience and experiences new events. Then it's just a matter of updating the concept.

### Better from the start

**Characters created with the presented system all become roughly equal. If a player's new character joins an existing group of experienced protagonists, it can be boring to be much less experienced. Then a narrator can choose to give the newly created character experience directly after the character is created. It may be appropriate to give the character approximately half the life experience that the character with the highest value in the group has, even though this is only a recommendation.**

**More information about experience** can be found on page 220. The narrator must decide for themselves whether this feels like a good solution for their particular group of players—sometimes it may simply fit well that there is a large difference in the characters' experience levels, such as when a beginner joins an older mentor.

**If experience is given from the start,** this improvement occurs after the protagonist has completed the last step in the creation process—acquiring equipment.

### Example of how a protagonist is created

On page 34 there is an example of how the entire creation process of a protagonist works. If anything feels unclear, please look at the example to gain clarity.

### Suggestions for nature

• Irresponsible • Relaxed • Bitter
• Blasé • Charming • Cynical
• Depressed • Desperate • Bold
• Selfish • Mean • Single-minded
• Enthusiastic • False • Cowardly
• Cheerful • Reckless • Idealistic
• Cold-blooded • Cocky • Broken
• Stupid • Manipulative • Condescending
• Nervous • Incompetent • Passionate
• Talkative • Disturbed • Self-loathing
• Sharp • Withdrawn • Dry
• Doubtful • Pompous • Burned out
• Friendly • Ambitious • Adventurous

### Suggestions for occupations

• Lawyer • Worker • Athlete
• Bartender • Boxer • Financier
• Fugitive • Photographer • Freelance reporter
• Forger • Writer • Gangster
• Fence • Burglar • Engineer
• Inspector • Artist • Criminal detective
• Courier • Bodyguard • Fortune seeker
• Doctor • Mechanic • Model
• Musician • Broker • Pawnbroker
• Police constable • Bounty hunter • Private detective
• Priest • Actor • Petty criminal
• Socialite • Con artist • Taxi driver
• Clerk • Investigator • Vagabond

Step 5: Attributes
The attributes are the protagonist's basic prerequisites. There are nine attributes that must each receive an attribute value. Each protagonist gets one attribute value of 6, one of 5, three of 4, three of 3 and one of 2. Table 1:1 summarizes this.

More about what the attributes mean can be found on page 214.

### Number Attribute Value Capacity

1 6 Good
1 5 Good
3 4 Average
3 3 Average
1 2 Poor

Table 1:1

Step 6: Abilities
The abilities are the protagonist's general areas of competence. Each attribute's attribute value is doubled and gives the number of points the player has to allocate to the three abilities that fall under each respective attribute.

Increasing an ability value by one step costs one point. The ability value shows the person's general competence in the ability. The different competence levels are shown in Table 1:2

### Ability Value Competence Level

0 Nonexistent
1–3 Amateur
4–6 Competent
7–9 Professional
10–12 Expert

Table 1:2

Step 7: Skills
When all attribute values and ability values are filled in on the character sheet, they are summed for each ability, in the Skill column. This shows the person's final proficiency in the ability. More about skill can be found on page 213

Step 8: Expertises
Expertise areas are narrower fields within the abilities, which give the protagonist specialized competence. More detailed information about expertise areas can be found on page 58.

In the abilities where the person has ability value four to six (4–6), he/she gets one expertise area. Should the person have ability value seven or higher (7+), he/she may choose two expertise areas within the ability. This is summarized by Table 1:3. Naturally, the requirements for the chosen expertise area must be met.

### Ability Value Number of expertises from start

0–3 0
4–6 1
7+ 2

Table 1:3

Step 9: Limitations
Protagonists in Noir all suffer from various forms of limitations that mean they have a harder time acting independently. This is in line with the idea that the protagonists are dark heroes who not only struggle against the story, but also with themselves and the flaws and inadequacies they possess in soul and heart.
