"The desire was too strong, she wanted too much. That was her weakness, that was where she went wrong. Photo flashes and gala premieres, furs, jewelry and nightclubs. That's why she's dead now. Why wasn't she born ugly?"
— Gabriel Jandaran
**Renting Clothes**
Among common folk it's usual to rent formal wear rather than buy it. The rental for a complete outfit usually runs around 150
zovrins per day, but that includes washing, ironing and handling.

Clothing & Accessories
At galas and premieres, on factory floors and in offices, everywhere fashion is represented. Some people spend enormous sums on having the latest from the most talked-about tailoring houses, all so they can keep their places in society's finest rooms.
**Fashion**
Fashion is very important in the social game. Clothes show which social level one moves in and allow one to determine at a glance how to treat the person standing before you. Thus they have a control function as they help people know their place.
Fashion regarding clothes and accessories varies rapidly with new designers' whims and fashion houses'
caprices. Within the upper social classes, and the families in particular, it's important to be updated about what's in. People usually keep current with the help of stylish fashion magazines, visits to fashionable fashion shows and mingling with fashion designers at high society cocktail parties.
There are thousands of different clothing brands, some families even have their own fashion designers who manufacture garments only for them. Likewise there are influential tailors whose names make their creations incredibly valuable.
**How Clothes Are Bought**
Clothes are acquired in two ways, either as ready-to-wear, or as custom-tailored garments. Ready-to-wear clothes are manufactured in textile factories, in mass editions, and sold to citizens via the fashionable department stores in the city, or in smaller shops that often specialize in one or a few types of garments, for example coats, hats, shoes or ladies' wear.
Custom tailoring is still common but it's more expensive than buying ready-to-wear. The advantage of custom-tailored garments is that the fit becomes much better and the customer can influence the garment's appearance in a hands-on way. Most customers like the personal relationship built up between tailor and customer and many keep the same tailor for many years. Alongside the tailor, the shoemaker, glovemaker and hatmaker are also respected professionals.
The trade in used clothes is extensive. In shops and at market squares used clothes of greatly varying type, style and quality are sold. Among the less well-off it's common for clothes to be used until they literally fall apart. The clothes are patched and mended until the very end.

### Women's Fashion

Ladies' fashion is what varies most from year to year. New ideas are tested and new designers take over. The current fashion favors the feminine and highlights the classic feminine curved form, with hips, narrow waist and emphasized bust.
**Undergarments**
Undergarments are usually made of cotton, silk or satin. Lace is often present, especially among the upper class.
Corsets come in many different types, everything from those worn openly, as a daring garment, to those intended for more festive use and which grant a pronounced neckline. Other models provide generally better and more comfortable support but are then used primarily with blouses or jacket tops. Stockings are often made of nylon and reach mid-thigh and come in different styles — usually held up with the help of garter belts.
Blouse & Sweater
The blouse is usually solid-colored and made of cotton or silk. The collar has soft and rounded tips and the neck is usually v-necked. The sleeves are wide and flowing and reach past the wrist. Among women in certain professions the blouses are very similar to men's shirts with pronounced collars and pointed tips. Sweaters are used more often by women than by men and don't have the same lower-class stigma when worn by a woman. The sweaters are usually thin and form-fitting to show the feminine figure.
Skirt & Trousers
The skirt is a very common garment, the length usually reaches to the knees — wearing shorter skirts than that is considered vulgar and cheap. The styles are usually straight, single-slit or pleated.
If volume is needed a petticoat is used. Trousers are used primarily by working women or in other situations where the skirt gets in the way of the tasks at hand. The trousers are usually made of durable wool, cotton or linen.
**Suit**
The suit consists of a skirt or trousers with matching jacket.
A woman's suit can be said to correspond to a man's suit.
**Dress**
There are many variants of dresses, suitable for different situations from everyday to parties. Like the skirt, the dress rarely ends higher than at the knee but long dresses often have slits. The dress usually buttons in front, or has a zipper in back, while certain models have lacing at the side.
Jacket & Coat
Jackets are form-fitting, discreet and short and end just below the waist. The lapels are pronounced but not as large as those displayed by men's fashion. The coat should cover the skirt and thus reaches approximately mid-calf. Both jackets and coats have discreet shoulder pads and are usually single-breasted. Among high society the fur is common, often made of mink, fox or rabbit. The most exclusive furs are made of wolf fur. At more festive occasions and at premieres a pronounced and elegant cape with high collars is sometimes used.
**Hat**
The fashion-conscious woman wears a wide-brimmed hat with a fairly angular profile. More active working women often use male hat styles such as the fedora. During mourning or other occasions when it's etiquette to conceal the face, the woman wears a veil.
**Shoes**
The standard footwear for women is pumps, a closed shoe with a low heel. A second alternative is the button boot. At more festive occasions shoes with higher heels are used, often between four and eight centimeters, but sometimes even higher.
Boots are also common, especially in poor weather. The boots reach mid-calf or to the knee.
**Women's Accessories**
Jewelry and jewels of various kinds are common among wealthier women. Silver, gold and platinum are valuable metals and rubies, emeralds, sapphires and diamonds are popular settings in rings, bracelets, earrings and necklaces.
Other ways to express oneself are by wearing colored ribbons in the hair, elaborate hairstyles, ankle and waist jewelry or neck chokers with or without bows. At events long gloves are worn as a complement to the long dress. To this often comes a fluffy boa, a shawl or a scarf. Handbags are also in fashion. They are relatively large, seen more as a means of expression than as a practical storage space and can contain everything from makeup to small firearms.
"We used to love each other but now I wonder, are you my friend or my enemy? Before I saw such things in your eyes, now there's nothing there."
— Gabriel Jandaran to Talia Navarre

### Men's Fashion

Men's fashion has been the same for a long time, with the suit as the classic garment. A number of changes occur as trends shift, particularly regarding color, form and accessories, but the basic appearance is the same. Office workers usually wear shirts and trousers while manual laborers, farmers and miners use trousers of coarse fabric and sweaters.
**Undergarments**
Men often wear undergarments of cotton or silk. On the upper body the undershirt is not uncommon, especially among the middle class. Socks are almost always made of cotton and usually match the color of the trousers.
**Suit**
The men of Sandukar usually wear a two- or three-piece suit. The three-piece suit consists of jacket, trousers and vest in matching colors, while the two-piece lacks the vest.
The suits are usually solid-colored, and common shades are muted brown, beige, gray, black and charcoal. A popular pattern is the pinstripe, on gray, blue or charcoal-colored backgrounds.
**Jacket**
The jacket is either single- or double-breasted. The lapels are broad, with pointed tips. Shoulder pads are sewn in to give a more masculine appearance. Two outer pockets and one breast pocket are standard. The materials that dominate are cotton and wool. Linen and tweed also occur.
**Vest**
A vest is worn with a suit and is always in a matching color. It's often part of formal or party attire.
The vest lacks a collar or lapels and is single-breasted. The back panel is either in the same material as the front panel or in distinguishing silk. On regular suit vests two front pockets are something of a standard.
Shirt & Sweater
The shirt is almost always made of cotton, even though silk shirts are preferred in certain upper-class circles. Flannel shirts occur among the working class. Solid-colored, white, shirts are most popular. If they are patterned they are vertically striped. The collar is pronounced and the tips are fairly long and pointed. The shirt usually lacks a breast pocket. The cuffs usually have buttons sewn on, but finer shirts should instead be used with detachable cufflinks. The shirt always buttons all the way up, even if a tie is absent. It's considered unkempt to have the top button unbuttoned. Sweaters are uncommon and are considered lower-class garments. It's primarily outdoor manual laborers who wear thick, sturdy, usually gray, wool sweaters.
Tie & Bow Tie
A tie is almost always worn with the shirt. Manual laborers usually go tieless as the tie gets in the way of their work. The tie should reach just below the trousers' waistband. It's usually solid-colored and almost always darker than the shirt. Discreetly and tastefully patterned ties are acceptable but more vul-

garish expressions are usually viewed with contempt by the conservative upper class – with the exception of the Guiskard family where colorful ties are more the rule than the exception. The tie is held in place with a tie pin in the same metal as the belt buckle, buttons and wristwatch. In the banking and entertainment world, the bow tie often replaces the necktie as neck attire.
**Trousers**
Trousers are primarily made of cotton, wool or linen. They are of straight design with turned-up cuffs and pressed creases. The colors are matte and if a jacket is worn, the trousers are almost always matched.
With trousers, suspenders are worn, but the belt is gaining ground and it's becoming increasingly common for loops to be sewn onto trousers so that a belt can be used. Two front pockets are common.
Manual laborers and country folk often have trousers in coarser fabrics.
Coat & jacket
The overcoat is usually made of wool. It usually comes single-breasted, although double-breasted variants occur among the families and the military. Previously it was forbidden for ordinary citizens to wear double-breasted coats.
The coat usually extends to mid-calf. The lapels are wide and the colors often dark. At the front, the trench coat is very common. It is a beige, gray or dark poplin coat that has good resistance to moisture and rain. The leather coat is also not uncommon. Within the Kriger family, black leather coats made of pigskin, with silver buttons, are very common among both men and women. On special occasions or at receptions, the cape is not entirely uncommon, especially among the upper class.
**Hat**
The hat is a given among the men of the Empire when they are outdoors. All hats are made of durable felt and come in a multitude of different colors and shapes. Usually the hat is in the same shade, or darker, than the suit being worn. At festivities or formal occasions, the tall top hat is sometimes used. The cap is a distinctly working-class headgear and is primarily used by the lower class.
**Shoes**
The majority of all shoes are made of leather and have leather or wooden soles. The colors vary but brown and black shoes dominate. If a belt is worn, its color should match the shoes.
Patent leather shoes are used at parties and holidays and are almost exclusively black, with shiny soles for the best dancing aid.
Manual laborers use solid boots in durable leather that must withstand dirt, moisture and hard work. The boot is usually knee-high, in treated leather, with a sturdy sole. Many officers in the army wear boots as part of their uniform.
**Men's accessories**
Having special buttons, particular tie pins, a handkerchief in the breast pocket, an exclusive pocket watch or perhaps an elegant scarf creates a personal style. The important general rule when it comes to accessories is the rule of matching.
If metal accessories are worn, for example cufflinks, belt buckle, pocket watch, wristwatch, tie pin, tie clip and rings, everything should be of the same metal. Mixing gold

with silver is a grave breach of etiquette, not to mention mixing base metals with precious ones. Cufflinks are often custom-ordered to be of a particular material or have a chosen motif such as a family crest, initials or set gemstones.
Wearing different types of rings is common.
There are countless variants, everything from wedding or engagement rings to graduation rings, order rings or family rings. The families have their own rings that members wear. Those who work at higher levels within the State often wear so-called state rings.
Within the upper class, the walking stick is common. Often it's a black, polished wooden cane with a knob in precious metal, with delicate ornamentation or proper family traditions on it.
On the hands, leather gloves are often worn, which should preferably match the overcoat's color. The gloves are usually lined to warm the hands.
Party & ceremony
When it's a ball, soirée, banquet, cocktail party, wedding, reception, premiere or inauguration, it's important to have the right clothes. Everyone in the upper class and most in the middle class own a tuxedo or an evening gown. If you don't, there are usually good opportunities to rent such items for an occasion.
Women wear either an evening gown, or at more formal events, a ball gown. An evening gown is an elegant type of dress that is often strapless and/or low-cut. The evening gown is usually accompanied by a chic jacket, a stately cape or a tasteful shawl as well as long evening gloves, so-called opera gloves. These reach above the elbow and are often made of satin to signal feminine elegance and sensuality. With the evening gown, jewelry of various kinds is worn such as rings, earrings, bracelets and necklaces.
The ball gown, sometimes called a tailcoat gown, is a floor-length dress that is mainly used at balls and other similar ceremonies where formal dress is recommended. The woman preferably wears it together with a starched petticoat so that the dress skirt can achieve generous width. The ball gown usually lacks sleeves but can be complemented with long gloves. It often has a bare back and can be strapless and low-cut as far as good taste allows.
The male tuxedo consists of several parts: a black tuxedo jacket with velvet or silk lapels, black trousers with braids, a white tuxedo shirt (unstarched) with pleated ruffle, a cummerbund (black at formal occasions, colorful at more boisterous parties) or a vest, a bow tie in the same color as the cummerbund, black suspenders and black patent leather shoes.
At more formal events, it's the tailcoat that applies. The tailcoat is a male formal dress, it is black and has long tails at the back and jacket lapels of silk. The trousers have silk braids and lack cuffs. With tailcoats, a white tailcoat shirt and white bow tie are worn as well as a vest. In the jacket's breast pocket there is usually a white handkerchief carefully placed. Outdoors, a black top hat and cane are worn. The hands are enclosed in white gloves of thin fabric.
The male formal dress can always be replaced with dress uniform if an officer rank within the armed forces, police or security corps is held.

### Cultural differences

There are many cultural differences in fashion and they become particularly noticeable when leaving the capital Sandukar.
The different kingdoms that make up the Empire all have their distinctive traits when it comes to clothing style, although in the cities people try to dress so they fit in as well as possible.
**Darost**
The majority of the population in Darost wears simple, unadorned clothes, but many Celenkers still wear their traditional costumes. Among the latter, white, wide-sleeved shirts and blouses are common, and richly embroidered velvet jackets with slashed sleeves are worn by both men and women. Celenka women often don long, wide skirts.
**Iskarian**
Since the capital Sandukar constitutes Iskarian's shining center, it is from here fashion flows and here all original clothing styles have been absorbed.
**Kovan**
The traditional sleeveless wool coat in black or dark blue is still worn by men in Kovan at ceremonies such as weddings and funerals. The coat extends to the knees and under it a shirt with wide sleeves is worn. A cloth belt is tied around the waist to hold the coat together. The traditional dress is floor-length, lacks sleeves and often comes in dark red or dark green. Married women cover their hair with a shawl.
**Madrostav**
The former high culture of Madrostav doesn't have much left of its clothing tradition but here and there women still wear wide dresses with multitudes of colorful petticoats.
Embroidered headscarves and gold jewelry as well as waist sashes decorated with silver coins are worn at festive occasions.
Men wear baggy linen trousers, a shirt with a high collar, and a vest that is laced together.
**Presvedonia**
Among men, uniform-like garments are common, the outer coats are double-breasted and the trousers are tucked into the high, coarse boots or work boots. The shirts and blouses have lacing in the sleeves and neck and one pulls them over the head to put them on. Embroidery and simpler silver jewelry are common embellishments.
**Sadovia**
Sadovia seems to have stopped in development when it comes to clothes and fashion. Here it's prim Victorian dresses with tightly laced corsets that apply for women and for men strict suits are custom.
**Vadaja**
Up in the northern mountains and the isolated villages, not much has changed over the past centuries but here people still wear traditional clothing. Men wear trousers held together with wide belts and long sheepskin vests and fur coats are common during the cold seasons.
Thick, knitted cardigans and woven dresses, often naturally dyed, are common among women.

For many citizens, violence is omnipresent.
Some choose to shy away and try to avoid problems, but others choose to arm themselves to be able to defend themselves when threatening situations arise
– often with fatal consequences.
**Acquiring weapons**
For an unconvicted citizen with a valid citizenship pass, there are no major problems in obtaining a personal weapon license for an automatic pistol or revolver. The right forms are acquired from the authorities, filled in and stamped at the gun dealer, together with the weapon's serial number. Without a valid license, ammunition may not be purchased. Generally one must obtain a separate permit to be allowed to carry the weapon concealed when present in public places, but if the citizen has a profession that requires this, the State usually doesn't see any problems with it. A current license must always accompany the citizenship pass as long as the weapon is in the person's possession. Automatic weapons and heavier weapons, like machine guns and flamethrowers, may only be possessed by members of the State's forces – the military and police – when these are on duty.
Of course, there is extensive black market trade in illegal weapons where serial numbers are filed off and where the weapons have often been involved in criminal contexts previously. Such illegal weapons circulate on the shadow market until they are completely worn out. Being found with an illegal weapon results in confiscation and prosecution in court.
**Weapon information**
Weapons is a collective term for the different ways and objects a person uses to try to incapacitate another person, whether it be in a boxing match, on the battlefield, in a gang confrontation or in some other context. Since combat and action tend to be commonly occurring in Noir's world, weapon information is fairly thorough. It's not necessary for narrators and players to know everything covered in this chapter, but the information is available for those interested.
Below follows an explanation of various terms found mentioned in the weapon lists on pages 104–117.
Weapons & combat equipment

### Conceal

When a person wants to attempt to detect a concealed weapon on another person, an opposed roll is made (the detector's Perception against the carrier's Dexterity). The carrier receives penalties or bonuses depending on the weapon's Conceal value, as well as depending on how the weapon is carried, see page 119. If the detector frisks instead of observes, he/she receives a bonus (+10) to their roll.

As long as the weapon is not concealed in some way, for example under a jacket or coat, it is naturally automatically visible if someone looks. Objects classified as unwieldy or bulky (see page 91) cannot normally be concealed.

Grip (Melee Weapons)
Most melee weapons, or implements used in melee, can be used with either one or two hands. As long as a person is not using one hand for something else, Noir's rules system assumes he/she is holding the weapon with both hands, in cases where this is reasonable (knives and daggers, for example, are generally not used with two hands). The effect of using a weapon with both hands is that the person may add their Damage Bonus to the damage value one additional time.

Grip (Firearms)
Certain types of firearms can only be handled properly with two hands. If this requirement is not met, the shooter receives a penalty (−5). Firearms that only require one hand (for example automatic pistols and revolvers) benefit from the support of two-handed shooting. If the shooter is not using one hand for something else, the rules assume it is used as support, which gives a bonus (+1) on dice rolls (Firearms).

It is possible to use a one-handed firearm in each hand simultaneously. The description of combat actions that involve two one-handed firearms specifies which rules apply.

### Malfunction

The weapon's construction can increase the risk of misfires and malfunctions, which is indicated by its malfunction value. See page 92 for more information about malfunctions.

### Caliber

The caliber indicates a firearm's ammunition diameter, either in millimeters or in hundredths of an inch. One must have the correct caliber ammunition for it to fit the weapon.

### Magazine

The magazine's size and design determines how many cartridges the weapon can be loaded with and how the cartridges are stored.

### Precision

Precision indicates how much bonus a shooter receives when he/she takes time to aim by, for example, using the combat action Shoot aimed fire.

### Recoil

The recoil is the jerk the weapon gives off when it is fired. How noticeable the recoil becomes depends on two factors, partly on the shooter's inherent nervousness and fear of the recoil, partly on how much the force at firing actually affects the weapon. High recoil is counteracted by high weapon weight, good physique, and handling training.

At the Recoil value, the letters "A" and "E" are also present. They describe the weapon's mechanism – fully automatic mechanism (A), single-shot mechanism (E), or both (AE). For most combat actions involving firearms, it is stated as a requirement that the weapon must have a specific mechanism – if the weapon lacks the correct mechanism, it cannot be used to perform that specific combat action.

### Range

All firearms and throwing weapons have three range limits – short, effective, and maximum range. Attacks against targets within the weapon's short range are not modified, attacks against targets within the weapon's effective range receive a penalty (−2), attacks against targets within the weapon's maximum range receive a penalty (−5), and attacks against targets farther away than the weapon's maximum range receive a penalty (−10). See table 1:31 for a summary. Maximum range is always double the effective range.

The narrator should view the ranges listed for weapons as recommendations. Noir is not a detailed tactical game, and if a target is judged to be exactly on a range limit, it is better to let it fall within the shorter distance.

### Range Penalty

Within short ±0
Beyond short but within effective −2
Beyond effective but within maximum −5
Beyond maximum −10

Table 1:31

### Damage Value

The damage value for different weapons is written, for example, "2D−1". That means the damage effect is rolled with two dice (D stands for die) and one is subtracted from the sum.

Some damage values are designated, for example, "S1D". The code "S" stands for stunning; more information about stunning damage effects can be found on page 263.

### Distance and Melee

**Two terms are used to determine** combat distance. Ranged combat is combat with firearms or throwing weapons against enemies who are usually out of reach. Melee is combat at such short distance that those involved can touch each other without great effort. Melee can be fought either unarmed or armed.

### Melee Weapons

The concept of melee includes all forms of man-to-man combat, both armed and unarmed. Melees are fought when attacker and defender are in proximity to each other, often at contact distance.

Melee weapons include all possible different weapons and implements a person can use to effectively render an enemy unfit for combat. It is common for a person to try to specialize in a specific type of weapon that he/she prefers.

For all melee attacks, Damage Bonus is added to the weapon's damage value. See also the section "Grip" on page 103.

### Knife

The knife, that is, a sharpened blade with handle, comes in many different designs: double-edged, single-edged, with guard, etc. This also includes the stiletto, whose blade is hidden in the handle, as well as other lighter bladed weapons.

### Dagger

This includes, in addition to the common dagger, the commando knife and bayonet, which are substantial double-edged knives intended for the battlefield. The commando knife can be likened to a universal tool for the State's commando soldiers who often carry out sabotage and resistance operations in enemy territory. The bayonet can be used either in hand or on a rifle with bayonet mount. In the latter case, the rifle becomes a spear-like melee weapon.

Army saber (sword)
Heirloom sword (sword)
Stiletto (knife)
Wood axe (axe)
Fire axe (axe)
Folding knife (knife)

### Spear

Rapier (sword)

### Club

A club made of, for example, wood or steel inflicts substantial crushing damage. The designation includes everything from the police baton to the cudgel.

### Spear

The spear is a long stick topped with a double-edged metal blade that is thrust into the enemy.

### Sword

Swords come in countless lengths and designs – with single or double edge, straight, curved, wide or narrow blade, one-handed or two-handed grip – it is only the creator's imagination that sets limits. Rapiers and sabers are also included in the designation. A sword can be camouflaged in, for example, a cane – and thus become a so-called swordstick.

### Axe

The axe consists of a blade with a handle. Common versions are the meat cleaver, hand axe, and machete. The somewhat larger fire axe is also included in the designation, and in the countryside the handy wood axe is often seen.

### Throwing Weapons

Throwing weapons are weapons and objects a person flings away in hopes of hitting a target or position. Nearly all throwing weapons are one-handed weapons. Skilled weapon throwers can perform tricks or inflict great damage with these primitive weapons.

### Throwing Knife

The throwing knife is sharpened and well-balanced, and often made entirely of steel. It comes in countless different designs.

### Shuriken

The shuriken is a metal construction in the form of a star with sharpened edges. The weapon is of Celenkt origin. It is handy and relatively easy to conceal. With a little training, the shuriken can also be used as a melee weapon.

### Hand Grenade

A hand grenade is a small cylindrical metal object with an explosive substance inside. The hand grenade is armed by pulling out a pin and releasing a safety lever – which often occurs when the grenade is thrown. Most hand grenades detonate on impact or within a few seconds from being armed. A hand grenade damages its surroundings through an explosive pressure wave and by the casing breaking up into numerous fragments. In game terms, a hand grenade detonates in direct connection with the throw action.

Molotov Cocktail
A molotov cocktail is a glass bottle filled with gasoline or other flammable liquid (for example spirits) and with a rag in the neck. The rag is ignited and the bottle is thrown against the ground where it breaks so that the rag comes in contact with the liquid which then hopefully catches fire. In game terms, the liquid catches fire in direct connection with the throw action.

### Other Forms of Melee

**Unarmed Combat**
**Mastering unarmed combat** means having the ability to utilize body, fists, feet, head, elbows, and knees in the best way to render the enemy unfit for combat. There are numerous different specialization areas (see page 58) that improve a person's melee ability.

Light, improvised implement
For example screwdriver, scissors, shoe, book, stool, stiletto heel, hockey puck, telephone, billiard cue, chain, flower vase, bottle, or coffee mug.

Heavy, improvised implement
For example crowbar, plank, tool, frying pan, chair, golf club, fire poker, or tire iron.

Very heavy, improvised implement
For example a board, shovel, sledgehammer, tire, or bookshelf.

### Improvised implements' durability

**All melee weapons with the term** "improvised" in the name are not made for fighting with. Therefore, it is easy for them to break or become unusable in some way. If the damage effect from such a weapon in an attack becomes odd, the improvised weapon is counted as unusable for the rest of the fight.

Example: Mordekai hits a shady nightclub owner with a billiard cue that counts as a "light, improvised implement". The damage effect lands on eight, which is not odd, so the cue holds. The next attack Mordekai performs also hits but now the damage effect becomes five – an odd result – so the cue shatters into splinters.

### Other types of throwing weapons

Light, improvised throwing weapon
For example flower vase, bottle, coffee mug, ashtray, plate, automatic pistol, book, stool, hockey puck, attaché case, revolver, tool, a fistful of nuts and bolts, or a substantial stone.

### Heavy improvised throwing weapon

For example coffee table, large painting, wheel, submachine gun, sword, rifle, shovel, axe, heavy furniture, crowbar, sledgehammer, or trash can.

Cudgel (club)
Police baton (club)
Dagger (dagger)

### Throwing knife

Shuriken (dagger)
Commando knife (dagger)
Bayonet (dagger)

**Hand grenade**

### Automatic Pistols

Automatic pistols are small one-handed firearms intended for easy transport and effective defense at short distances. The term automatic pistol refers to the mechanism where the empty casing is ejected and a new cartridge is automatically fed into the barrel each time the weapon is fired. For firing a new shot, a new trigger pull is required – the weapon does not continuously feed in cartridges if the trigger is held down.

Most automatic pistols require slide operation or other manual preparation each time the weapon is reloaded with a new magazine. All automatic pistols are one-handed weapons. Those weapons that are magazine-fed can have an extra cartridge in the chamber, even though this is considered unsafe as it increases the risk of accidental discharge.

Breer z.7
The Breer z.7 is one of Sandukar's most common automatic pistols despite the relatively expensive price tag. The pistol is common on the civilian market and has a good reputation for being durable, safe, and reliable. It is often found with private detectives, spies, and bodyguards. The magazine capacity and the manageable recoil add to its popularity.

Breer z.9
The Breer z.9 is a well-regarded pistol often used as a backup weapon or as a handy and light main weapon. It is powerful in relation to its size and is readily used by those who want to be able to carry a weapon without it being clearly visible, such as criminal police and ministry personnel. The weapon's low mass makes it somewhat harder to control during rapid fire, compared to heavier models.

### Firearms

**Firearms are handheld** mechanical weapons that fire projectiles at the enemy, usually a bullet fired via a cartridge.

**The modern battlefield demonstrates** the superiority of firearms, as a soldier at long distance can eliminate the enemy with minimal risk to their own life and limb.

Kriger Army Pistol-4
Darov 5/2
Breer z.7

Calder M2245
The Calder-45 is the pistol that rules Sandukar's back 
streets. It's popular among criminals as it inflicts 
serious damage, is reliable and accurate. The weapon was 
formerly a status symbol but is now considered by some to be a bit 
simple, a bit brutal. Few weapons have endured as long, however, 
with such an unwavering reputation. The Calder-45's recoil is 
relatively heavy which requires a firm grip. The pistol comes 
in countless variants, from blue-anodized and silver-plated to 
chrome-plated with ivory grips.
Darov 5/2
Darov manufactures distinctive self-defense weapons that are 
very handy and easy to carry. Darov 5-series pistols are technically not autopistols as they 
lack an external magazine and are loaded with two cartridges straight 
into the two stacked barrels. The Darov 5/2 is 
designed for easy transport and is an easily concealed weapon. The 
caliber is considered sufficient to neutralize 
an attacker with two shots.
Darov 6/6
The Darov 6/6 is a very compact and cheap pistol for self-defense. The caliber is quite weak, but the low recoil makes it 
easy to use the pistol for repeated fire when needed. 
The precision is quite acceptable even though the barrel is short. The weapon 
fits perfectly in a handbag, a garter, a 
jacket pocket, or as a backup weapon in an ankle holster.
**Karev Morov**
The Karev Morov is a pure assassin's weapon with a 
barrel that is factory-prepared to accept a silencer. 
The precision is solid and the weapon has good quality 
feel and smooth and pleasant recoil. For a 
factory-made weapon the pistol has very good accuracy. 
Persons caught with such weapons are usually 
viewed with great suspicion, as they are almost exclusively 
used for assassinations.
Kriger Army Pistol–4
House Kriger is the main supplier to the army and their 
AP–4 is used by officers and staff personnel as a 
defensive weapon. Stapo also uses the weapon, but then it 
has a specially decorated grip with Stapo's emblem. The AP-4 
is known for its robustness and resistance to dirt, 
mud and moisture. Few specimens reach beyond State 
ownership.
Darov 6/6
Breer z.9
**Karev Morov**
Calder M2245
Magazine type: Magazine
The magazine is sometimes called a box magazine. It is a small metal box 
with an opening at the top where cartridges 
are fed in by hand, against a spring on 
the bottom. Two magazine lips 
hold the cartridges in place at the opening.
When the reloading mechanism is activated the empty case is ejected from the weapon and 
the topmost cartridge in the box magazine 
is fed by the mechanism into the chamber 
in the weapon's barrel. All weapons that have 
box magazines can have a bullet in the chamber 
and a full magazine. This is shown in 
the weapon lists by "+1" appearing 
after the magazine capacity.
Since the box magazine is considered the standard magazine, weapons 
with box magazines have no code in 
the weapon lists.
"I have a bullet with Rulov's name on it. 
I have ten thousand bullets with his name on them." 
— Gabriel Jandaran

### Revolvers

Revolvers are one-handed firearms that are loaded with cartridges 
individually into a cylinder. The cylinder rotates with each 
firing so that the cartridge chamber comes in line with 
the hammer and barrel.
A revolver is considered safer and more reliable than an 
autopistol. This is because defective ammunition 
can stop the entire feeding process in an autopistol 
while it's enough to pull the trigger again if 
you get a defective cartridge in a revolver, since a new 
cartridge rotates into firing position. Revolvers therefore don't 
suffer from the consequence of misfires (they don't need to be reloaded, 
see page 92), other than that the action counts as 
failed and the cartridge is seen as expended. However, if the 
following action with the revolver shows doubles 
on the dice roll the revolver has suffered a malfunction.
Kai/Dekere Conceal
The Kai/Dekere Conceal challenges Darov's series of small self-defense weapons. It is small and light and designed for 
purses, garters, or jacket sleeves. The short barrel 
and low weight make the pistol unwieldy but its 
small size makes it popular among card players and 
women about town. Detectives and criminal police use 
it as a backup weapon.
Kai/Dekere Wake
Kai and Dekere, two families within House Guiskard, created 
a challenger to the Ruuik and Salimo .38 Police. The reception 
was lukewarm, since Wake is heavier than Police and doesn't 
use its standard caliber, which makes it difficult for the 
individual policeman to get ammunition at a reasonable 
price. However, this very elegant revolver is popular 
among private guards, bouncers and gangsters.

Kai/Dekere Wrath
Wrath was meant as a solid backup weapon but received a 
lukewarm reception as the powerful caliber, short 
barrel and low weight made the weapon quite 
unwieldy during rapid fire. To keep down the size of 
the revolver the cylinder holds only five cartridges.
Ruuik & Salimo .38 Police
The Ruuik & Salimo .38 Police is the standard weapon of 
Sandukar's police force – at least among the patrolling 
constables. The revolver is also popular among criminals. 
It is smooth, effective and very reliable, but completely without 
refinements for the lowest possible production cost. Many 
policemen buy better sights themselves, or a more comfortable 
grip than the rough-hewn wooden one that comes 
as standard.
Ruuik & Salimo .38 Sneak
Sneak is a short-barreled version of Police but with the hammer 
built into the metal body. The size makes the revolver handy 
and the caliber makes it easy to shoot. It's a popular choice 
among criminal investigators and detectives who don't like having 
to lug around extra metal junk. The weapon's rounded form without 
protruding parts makes it smooth to draw.
Ruuik & Salimo .44 Grandeaux
Grandeaux is a macho-revolver for criminals and 
wilderness men. It's a very well-made, large and heavy revolver 
that has gained some popularity among shadow hunters as its 
destructive capability is not to be found on the autopistol side.
Ruuik & Salimo .540 Mardeaux
Mardeaux, this giant monster of a revolver, is mostly a 
status symbol in certain criminal circles. The force generated 
by the very potent .540 cartridge makes the weapon directly 
unpleasant to shoot with unless you possess good strength. 
The revolver is, however, of very good quality, like most 
of R&S's weapons.
Kai/Dekere Conceal
Kai/Dekere Wake
R&S .38 Sneak
R&S .44 Grandeaux
Magazine type: Revolver cylinder (r)
The revolver cylinder is a fixed part of the revolver. In the cylinder there are a 
number of chambers in which cartridges 
are placed. The number of chambers is 
usually five or six, more won't fit 
without stressing the material 
too much. The cylinder rotates with 
each firing so that the cartridge 
chamber comes in line with the hammer 
and barrel. The cylinder can usually be 
released from the weapon body by 
releasing a safety lock 
and swinging the cylinder out to the left. 
This exposes all chambers.
The empty cases must be ejected before new cartridges can be inserted. 
Weapons with revolver cylinders 
are marked in the weapon lists with an "r" 
in the Magazine column.
"Get away from the revolver, or I'll make your 
citizen's pass a worthless piece of paper!" 
— Anya Malov

### Submachine Guns

Kpist is an abbreviation for kulsprutepistol – that is 
a handheld firearm that fully automatically fires pistol 
ammunition. The weapon concept is fairly new but has 
been well received. It's primarily gangsters and the military who 
demand this type of weapon. Since the concept of submachine guns 
is quite newfangled there are quite a few teething problems in the 
construction methods. This makes the weapon type more sensitive to 
misfires than many other weapon types.
**Breer Kalgari**
Breer of House Kriger are known and reliable weapon 
manufacturers even though their models sometimes lack 
inventiveness. Their two-handed weapon Kalgari is a purely 
military instrument of war suited for small reconnaissance 
squads or for assault groups who use it as 
light support weapons. The Kalgari is hard to find on the black 
market which has made it a status symbol among 
gangsters. It can be equipped with an extended drum magazine 
for higher magazine capacity.

Breer Kalgari/Breer Kalgari a.1
**Karev Ulz**

Breer Kalgari a.1
The two-handed weapon Kalgari a.1 resembles the regular Kalgari 
but has a heavier caliber.
**Gault Assault Pistol**
Assault Pistol was intended to be a backup weapon for assault 
soldiers but intrigue within House Kriger has made it 
difficult for Gault to reach out with the creation. Instead it's 
very popular on the streets. Its small size, low price and 
light weight make it easy to carry around, but it 
also means it kicks something terrible when 
fired. Assault Pistol is a one-handed weapon.
**Karev Riekon**
The engineering genius Karev van Dunarmco de Zovrin is 
behind the specialized, rapid-fire one-handed weapon 
Karev Riekon. It resembles a large pistol but has the magazine 
in front of the trigger. The barrel is 
threaded to accept a 
silencer. The weapon has found a place among commando 
soldiers on covert missions, state agents, assault soldiers 
(who are often seen carrying a Riekon in each hand) and 
assassins who need to take out multiple targets in rapid succession.
**Karev Ulz**
Ulz is an older model from Dunarmco's factories. The two-handed weapon has become very popular on the street and is 
known for being extremely reliable and easy to repair. It's 
also relatively easy to get hold of.
Kriger Commissariat Pistol–4
The Commissariat Pistol is a fully automatic one-handed pistol 
specially developed for the State Commissariat. The weapon is of 
utmost quality, finish and precision. Carrying a KP–4 
without proper license is subject to harsh penalties and therefore 
the weapon is very rare in the underworld.
"You, I think I'll die another day, this 
one doesn't feel like the right one." 
— Jessca Navarre
**Gault Assault Pistol**
**Karev Riekon**
Kriger Commissariat Pistol–4

### Rifles

All rifles are two-handed weapons and fire powerful ammunition. 
They are used by hunters and are soldiers' standard 
weapons in the military. It's House Kriger that supplies the State's 
war machine with rifles and carbines. A carbine is a shorter 
rifle, while a sniper rifle is of extra high quality, 
has a heavy precision barrel and is extremely balanced.
Soldiers' rifles are either semi-automatic or 
bolt-action. On a loaded, semi-automatic 
weapon you just need to squeeze the trigger to fire 
shots while a bolt-action weapon must be loaded 
manually with a new cartridge before each firing, using 
an operating handle that ejects the empty case and feeds 
a new cartridge into the chamber. This mechanism has the advantage 
that it increases accuracy for single shots but it makes 
rapid repeated fire virtually impossible, 
since each shot must be chambered manually.
Kriger Combat Rifle–2
Combat Rifle–2, or KG–2 as it's called among soldiers 
in the field, is the State's soldiers' standard weapon. The weapon is well-made, effective and durable, the only thing soldiers usually complain 
about is the weight, the heavy recoil and the long reload 
time that comes from the magazine being built 
into the weapon and having to be loaded cartridge by cartridge. The rifle's 
bolt-action mechanism makes the weapon difficult to use when 
rapid repeated fire is needed. The weapon lacks mounts 
for scope sights but its fixed sights are considered very 
competent. The KG–2 exists in such quantities that many have 
disappeared from State control and are often found as 
souvenirs in the city. The weapon has a bayonet mount.
Rifles Damage Range Precision Recoil Magazine Caliber Conceal Malfunction Price*
Kriger Combat Rifle–2 3D+2 50–400 m +3 5E 8i 8mmR –8 ±0 2,500z
Kriger Sniper Rifle–1 5D–2 50–800 m +5** 8E 3i 13mmR –9 ±0 18,500z
Kriger Sniper Rifle–2 3D+2 50–600 m +5** 5E 4i 8mmR –9 ±0 12,000z

Kriger Combat Rifle–2
Kriger Sniper Rifle–1
Kriger Sniper Rifle–2

Kriger Sniper Rifle—1
The Kriger Sniper Rifle—1 is an almost two-meter-long weapon based on the Sniper Rifle—2, but with a higher weight and a much more powerful caliber. The range and destructive power of the heavy 13mm bullet make the weapon feared and respected. Only extraordinary marksmen within the army are given access to this massive weapon.

Kriger Sniper Rifle—2
The Sniper Rifle—2 is a very high-quality construction.
It is large, long and heavy, but its precision and range are unmatched. The rifle's wooden details are of the finest quality. The weapon can be disassembled into three parts for easier transport. Since it lacks advanced fixed sights, it is assumed that a military-grade and state-approved scope will be used with the weapon. This masterpiece of a weapon is only found in the State's army, with extraordinary marksmen who have been selected to become snipers. A few examples disappear onto the civilian market where assassins pay dearly to acquire a specimen.

Kriger Assault Carbine—3
The Assault Carbine—3 is a carbine developed to improve soldiers' capacity during trench assaults. The weapon is semi-automatic, magazine-fed for quick reloading and has reduced caliber for good handling and low recoil. The disadvantage is that its penetration is lower and the range significantly shorter than the Battle Rifle—2.

The AC—3 is still in limited supply but the first reports from the front demonstrate that its effectiveness during assaults is higher than the BR—2's. Reports of poor quality have emerged, however. The weapon has a bayonet mount.

Rais 8mm Bolt-Action
The Rais 8mm Bolt-Action is a common hunting weapon outside the city's stone facades. Alongside the shotgun, it is used by many farmers to shoot larger game. The weapon is of good quality, and has a polished wooden stock and good fixed sights even though it is intended to be used together with a scope.

Magazine type: Internal magazine (i)
The internal magazine is built into the weapon in some way, like a tube where the cartridges are stored, or in a part of the weapon body next to the barrel.
When the reloading mechanism is activated, the empty case is ejected and a new cartridge is retrieved from the internal magazine. When reloading, each new cartridge must be inserted individually. There are loading clips that contain five cartridges which speed up the reloading process. Weapons with internal magazines are marked in the weapon lists with an "i" in the Magazine column.

"You can't evade the police forever, wouldn't it be smarter to give up and turn yourself in?"
"As smart as cutting your own throat to breathe."
— Mordekai Remus and Stelia Lock

Kriger Assault Carbine—3

Rais 8mm Bolt-Action

Rais H100K

### Shotguns

Shotguns have several advantages and quite a few disadvantages. The advantages are that they have high damage capacity and accuracy at short distance, and that they are mechanically simple to handle and maintain. The disadvantages include the limited range and that the damage capacity decreases radically with distance to the target. The reason for the latter is that the weapon uses buckshot cartridges which, unlike regular cartridges, do not contain a bullet but between 9 and 23 small round lead pellets.
When a buckshot cartridge is fired, these lead pellets are shot through the air. They spread somewhat with distance, and a hit at close range often means that several pellets strike the target, which increases the risk of serious injury.

The problem is that lead pellets quickly lose energy and power, which means that the weapon's damage effect drastically decreases when it comes to distances beyond its short range. Therefore shotguns have two indicated damage values, one for hits within short range and one in parentheses for hits beyond this range. The damage caused is considered stunning since it is relatively superficial and spread over a larger area.

Rais H90 (sawed-off)
The Rais H90 is used as a large firearm that causes massive destruction at close range. It is an H90 Shotgun on which the stock and barrel are sawed off to make the weapon manageable. A limitation is obviously the two buckshot cartridges that are loaded directly into the barrels, which requires more time for reloading during extended firefights. The weapon is used mostly by scum at the bottom of society, or by certain types of gangsters.

Rais H90 (sawed-off)

Rais H90

Rais H90 Shotgun
The Rais H90 Shotgun is very common in the countryside, outside the city's neon lights. Farmers and hunters use it for hunting birds and small game. The two barrels sit parallel to each other and the cartridges are placed directly into each barrel after the weapon is broken open. The shotgun has two triggers in the trigger guard, one for each barrel.

Rais H100 Pump Shotgun
The Rais family, of house Mengal, created the H100 Pump Shotgun for trenches and bunker clearing. Its effectiveness and precision have meant that even the police have taken a liking to it and use it when greater firepower is needed. The cartridges are stored in an internal tube magazine that runs along and under the barrel.
With each shot, the shooter moves a pump handle located under the barrel back and forth with their left hand to eject the empty cartridge and load a new one from the tube magazine.
It is this pumping that gives the weapon its name.

Rais H100K Pump Shotgun
The H100K is a short version of the H100, intended for witness protection and rapid clearing. It lacks a stock and has a shortened barrel for increased maneuverability. The weapon is popular among enforcers. There are various ways to smoothly secure the weapon in a holster-like mounting device under the armpit to quickly draw it from inside, for example, a large overcoat.

Rais H100U Pump Shotgun
Following requests from bunker clearing teams, Rais developed the H100U, an H100 with extended tube magazine and elongated barrel, to give the clearers more magazine capacity before they were forced to reload.

Magazine type: In the chamber (p)
If the weapon is loaded in the chamber, the cartridges are placed by hand directly into the barrel and must after firing be removed to make room for new ones. Weapons that are loaded directly into the chamber are marked in the weapon lists with a "p" in the Magazine column.

"Want a drink?"
"No thanks, I still have a bit of a stomachache since you stabbed me."
— Ilsa and Gabriel Jandaran

Rais H100

Rais H100U

### Machine Guns

Machine guns, or machineguns, are pure military weapons and can be said to be fully automatic heavy rifles created to demoralize the enemy and provide covering fire during assault or retreats. Machine guns are often very heavy and unwieldy since they are intended for longer, continuous fire in fixed installations. They constitute a common armament on various types of vehicles used at the front, not least aircraft. All machineguns directly used by individuals require two hands to operate.

Kriger Machine Gun—1
The Kriger Machine Gun—1 is a heavy machine gun that requires a three-man crew. Assembling the mount takes only one minute, and half that time to disassemble it again. Each team member is responsible for just over fifteen kilograms each, which makes the weapon both hated and loved on the battlefield. Its highly effective ammunition is known for penetrating most protection, which is appreciated. Its rhythmic pounding rattle during automatic fire becomes a morale booster during attacks as it makes the soldiers feel supported.

Kriger Machine Gun—1B
The Machine Gun—1B is only found permanently mounted on vehicles, bunkers or aircraft. It is made to withstand uninterrupted fire for long periods without risk of fire interruption, feeding problems or overheating. The magazine is enclosed for better maneuverability and exclusion of dirt.

Kriger Machine Gun—2
The Machine Gun—2 is a water-cooled machine gun intended for a crew of two men — a gunner and a loader/observer.
The weapon comes with a tripod that is surprisingly quick to set up — it rarely takes more than ten seconds to get the machine gun ready to fire after a regrouping.
The belt feed has received criticism since it has a tendency to jam if dirt or mud gets stuck in it.

Kriger Machine Gun—2B
The Machine Gun—2B is a water-cooled, medium-heavy machine gun that is only used permanently mounted on vehicles or in installations.
The reinforced barrel and mechanism are enclosed in a cast iron block to maximize reliability during extended fire. A large heavy magazine box holds the cartridges and it is a cumbersome procedure to reload the weapon.

Kriger Machine Gun—1

Kriger Machine Gun—3

**Kriger Demolition Lance**

Kriger Machine Gun—3
The light Kriger Machine Gun—3 is intended as a portable support weapon. The weapon is a late addition to the infantry and is not belt-fed but has a large round drum magazine lying on top of the weapon body. Empty cases are fed downward, through the weapon. Soldiers who have fired the weapon like its maneuverability and destructive capacity, but curse its weight and the fact that it is not uncommon for red-hot empty cases from the weapon to land in their boots.

### Heavy Weapons

The term heavy weapons includes specialized weapons primarily used by the military, such as flamethrowers or weapons for building clearing and armored vehicle combat.
No heavy weapons can be fired without using the initiative action Fire aimed shot.

### Kriger Dragonfire

Dragonfire is a portable, back-mounted flamethrower that requires two-handed grip to operate. The two fuel tanks carried on the back can hold enough liquid for eight substantial bursts.

### Kriger Demolition Lance

The Demolition Lance is a cheap and low-quality disposable weapon made for anti-armor and bunker assault. Its high-explosive grenade is created to spread destruction around it through shock wave and shrapnel. The weapon's effectiveness against armored vehicles is limited, but better than nothing. It is not uncommon for a soldier to carry two to three
Demolition Lances just in case. When the weapon is fired, its empty tube is discarded.

### Kriger Steelfist

Steelfist is a heavy, dedicated, portable anti-armor weapon whose powerful grenade sends a very hot fire jet into the target. The weapon is very unwieldy and requires much time to reload, which means that there are often two people who handle a Steelfist — a gunner and a loader.

Heavy Weapons Damage Value Range Accuracy Recoil Magazine Caliber Conceal Malfunction Price*
Kriger Dragonfire High intensity 5—10 m +1 — (8) — Bulky +1 13,000z
Kriger Demolition Lance ** 50—100 m +1 — 1p**** 78mmSS Unwieldy +4 8,000z
Kriger Steelfist *** 50—200 m +1 — 1p 95mmAV Bulky +2 30,000z

Magazine type: Belt-fed (b)
Belt-fed weapons can have almost endless capacity, since multiple ammunition belts can be linked together. It is only machine guns that use this magazine type, which is required for continuous support fire on the battlefield. Belt feeding is one of the reasons why machine gunners often operate in pairs of a gunner and a loader. Cartridges are placed in a belt of fabric or metal links which are pulled through the weapon's chamber and barrel by a feed mechanism. Empty cases and link pieces are spat out on the chamber's other side during firing. The state standard length of military ammunition belts for machine guns holds 100 oiled and inspection-stamped cartridges.
Belt-fed weapons are marked in the weapon lists with a "b" in the Magazine column.

"I can sleep when I'm dead."
— The Sorrowful

Kriger Machine Gun—2

**Kriger Steelfist**

For a weapon to be loaded, ammunition of the correct caliber is required. Various forms of weapon accessories can improve a weapon's area of use.

Ammunition harness, 10 magazines
The ammunition harness fastens over the shoulders and around the waist. It is equipped with adjustable holders for about ten magazines of any type.
**Scope**
The effect of using a scope is that the shooter gets to use the Initiative Action Sharpshooting, see page 256. Scopes can be mounted on all rifles that have mounts for them.
**Suppressor**
The suppressor dampens the sound from a fired bullet. Opponents get a deduction on rolls to detect the shot for every meter of distance (−1/m) between him/her and the shooter. The suppressor increases the weapon's malfunction value by one
(+1) and reduces Conceal by two (−2) as long as it is screwed onto the weapon. It can be attached to all auto-matic pistols and submachine guns that have threaded barrels.
**Box magazine**
A box magazine is an empty magazine for a suitable weapon.
Note that all weapon models have their own magazines which cannot be used for other types of weapons.
Ammo belt, 50 rounds
The ammo belt hangs over one shoulder, diagonally across the body.
It is equipped with a holder for 50 rounds of any type, or 20 shotgun shells.
Weapon maintenance kit (weapon type)
A weapon maintenance kit includes gun grease, cleaning rod, weapon cloth, wood oil, brushes, sandpaper, steel wool and a small screwdriver and file. All packed in a box of wood or metal.

### Holsters

The holster is a carrying device for weapons. Pure firearm holsters are almost always made of hardened and molded leather. Common holster types are the shoulder holster, hip holster, and ankle holster.
**Holster properties**
Each way of carrying a weapon entails requirements and effects, according to table 1:32
Accepts shows which weapon size the holster can handle.
The weapon size is defined by the weapon's Conceal value, and is given in the table as a range. If the weapon's Conceal value does not fall within the range, the weapon cannot be holstered in that type of holster. If a specific weapon type is mentioned instead of a value range, the holster only works for that weapon type.
Draw indicates if the person gets any deduction or bonus when he/she draws a weapon from the holster (with the Initiative Action
Draw weapon).
Conceal shows if the wearer gets any bonus or deduction to the weapon's Conceal when someone tries to find a weapon in the holster, provided that the holster is hidden in some way, for example covered by clothes, otherwise it is automatically visible if anyone looks.
Holster type Accepts Draw Conceal*
Ankle holster (+6) – (+10) −1 +5
Wrist sheath (+7) – (+10) +10 ±0
Shoulder holster (±0) – (+7) +1 +1
Waistband (−2) – (+10) ±0 −2
Sling (−10) – (−3)* −2 −5
Pocket (+6) – (+10) −2 −2
Hip holster (−2) – (+3) +5 −2
Thigh holster (+5) – (+10) −2 +10
Small of back holster (+3) – (+7) +1 +2
Side holster (+2) – (+7) +2 ±0
Combat harness Hand grenade/bayonet** +2 −5
Sword scabbard Sword ±0 −5
Table 1:32 * Some bulky weapons can also have slings, for example Kriger Sprängsfär.
** The bayonet can be replaced with a combat knife.
**Holster descriptions**
The holster descriptions are general and creative players and narrators can use them as inspiration and guidelines to create special holster types for their own weapons.
Among the holsters below, other ways of carrying weapons are also described for comparison. Additionally there are carrying devices for other types of weapons than just firearms.
A holster is made for a specific weapon model so you need to get a new holster if you change weapons.
Item Price (availability) Other

Wrist sheath 500z Unwieldy

**Waistband As clothing**

**Pocket As clothing**
Hip holster 225z
Thigh holster 140z
Small of back holster 125z
Side holster 125z
Combat harness 300z
Sword scabbard 200z Unwieldy
**Ankle holster**
The ankle holster fastens around one ankle with a quick-release mechanism. Some models have support straps that go up over the calf. To be able to draw, the wearer needs to pull up the pant leg.
**Wrist sheath**
The wrist sheath is a metal construction that sits along the wearer's forearm and holds the weapon in a spring cradle.
When the wearer makes a special flick with the arm, usually slightly forward, downward, the spring is released and the weapon is flung forward and into his/her hand. Small firearms or knives are usually carried this way. It requires some training to handle the holster fully.
"When you're lying there, wounded, in a sea of blood and corpses caused by your hand, do you lie there, with closed eyes, short breath, scared as hell and hope to bleed out to avoid continuing?
Or do you pull yourself together, stand up for your own, protect them, make sure nothing further bad happens to them?"
– Anya Malov

### Shoulder holster

The shoulder holster sits under the armpit, and the arms are threaded through it. The holster is often fastened to the belt for increased stability. To compensate for the weight on the side where the firearm is placed, the opposite armpit usually has pockets for one or two extra magazines, in cases where the weapon is an automatic pistol. There are also special shoulder holsters that accept throwing knives on one or both sides.
**Waistband**
The weapon can be stuck into the waistband, with the stock or handle above. This is a cocky but impractical way to carry the weapon, as it is difficult to draw and easy for the weapon to disappear down into the pants.
**Sling**
Slings are common aids for those who want to carry hunting rifles, shotguns, or larger melee weapons on their backs or over their shoulders. They are used, for example, by the army if soldiers need to march longer distances or have special duty.
**Pocket**
Simply carrying the weapon in a pants or jacket pocket is a classic. It is risky, however, as the weapon can easily get caught in the lining.
**Hip holster**
The hip holster fastens on the outside of the shooter's thigh and the lower part is tied just above the knee. The placement makes the weapon easy to draw, but it also makes the holster very easy to see. Hip holsters are worn by police constables and by field officers in the State's army.
**Thigh holster**
The thigh holster is usually used by women wearing skirts. It goes around the thigh and has a hidden fastening device that holds the weapon in place. Variants that fasten to nylon stockings with accompanying garters exist. The shooter must pull up the skirt to draw the weapon.
**Small of back holster**
The small of back holster is placed in the belt or fastened with a metal clip in the waistband at the lower back. The advantage of the holster is that it is difficult to detect if you wear a jacket over it.
**Side holster**
The side holster is threaded through the belt and placed just above the hip bone on either side.
**Combat harness**
The combat harness is worn by the State's soldiers. Hand grenades and a large knife can be attached to it.
**Sword scabbard**
A sword scabbard accepts a sword. It can be placed in a belt or baldric on the hip, or carried on or over the back.
**Body Armor**
Body armor is very rare on the city's streets and the technological development in the area is sadly neglected.
Of the body armor that exists, most are rather heavy, clumsy, and expensive.
**Body armor properties**
Body armor makes the wearer able to endure more before he/she becomes seriously injured. However, it helps little against the stunning that comes from the kinetic energy or force of an attack. As long as the armor is used, the wearer has an increased attribute value for Wound Threshold. See table 1:33 for more information.
Protection shows how much higher the wearer's attribute value for Wound Threshold is when the armor is used.
Restriction shows what deduction the wearer gets on rolls (Awareness, Combat Movement, or Defense) when the armor is used.
**Body armor Protection Restriction**
Tactical gear +3 −4
Police armor +2 −2
Bulletproof vest +1 −2
Storm armor +4 −5
Table 1:33
**Body armor descriptions**
The body armor that exists is used primarily by the State's troops – the police and the military. It is very unusual for civilians to wear body armor.
Item Price (availability) Other
Tactical gear 7,500z (§§) Bulky
Police armor 3,500z (§§) Unwieldy
Bulletproof vest 3,800z (§§) Unwieldy
Storm armor 9,000z (§§) Bulky
**Tactical gear**
The tactical police use tactical gear when they make raids or busts on places where resistance is expected to be hard or opponents armed. The armor consists of heavily padded, hardened leather with thick steel plates on the upper side of the arms, front of the legs, torso, back, groin, and neck. The head is covered by a full-coverage steel helmet with visor of impact-resistant glass, extra protection for ears and neck, and a sturdy leather strap under the chin.
The body armor is very difficult to move in and also quickly becomes very hot. Criminals usually realize they have a good chance of successfully fleeing from a tactically-clad policeman, who due to his heavy armor has difficulty running after someone fleeing.
**Police armor**
The police's standard armor, during foot patrol or in patrol car, consists of heavy reinforced leather gloves and metal-reinforced boots. The chest is covered by a woven silk vest that provides some protection against bullets. Over the police armor is often worn a heavy knee-length police coat in black durable leather, with metal insert plates over the torso.

Police armor is a relatively light armor, intended to give the police constable increased ability to function in brawls and similar situations where heavier weapons are not expected to be used. The armor is not used by criminal police but only by uniformed personnel.
**Bulletproof vest**
The bulletproof vest is a large and extremely clumsy vest in ballistic nylon, steel, and silk, that protects the torso, shoulders, and groin against damage. Since it is relatively quick to take off and on, it is used by criminal police who need protection in threatening situations or during potentially dangerous operations. Aircraft pilots who need protection against machine gun fire also use it since it weighs little enough that it doesn't provide any disadvantages.
**Storm armor**
The storm trooper wears a padded high-hardened molded steel armor with vambraces, cuirass, which protects the torso and groin, as well as gauntlets. In the gaps sits tough leather. The armor is extremely heavy and clumsy to maneuver in but since its protective ability actually means the wearer can be hit by rifle fire and still survive, it is appreciated by the poor bastards sent out on the trench fields.
It is not uncommon for fresh storm troopers to inherit fallen comrades' storm armor. Many consider this to be a great honor and in some units it is common for the wearer to honor the previous user by carving his/her name into the cuirass's hard plate. Others choose not to repair previous damage to the armor as a symbol of luck.
It echoed loudly in the stairwell when Anya Malov with determined steps walked toward the cells. Her eyes were cold and her expression set. She had gone down the stairs many times before, but this time took the cake.
The phone call a minute earlier had ruined an already lousy day, a day lined with idiocies and idiots.
She knew she had done a good job. The arrest was correct down to the last detail. There were no weak points – witness statements, physical evidence, everything was in the report.
She snorted irritably as she approached the end of the stairs and saw the metal door into the holding cells. She threw open the door and saw the duty officer standing with a mug of black, oily coffee in his hand and a cigarette in the corner of his mouth. The fat, sweaty man lit up when he saw Anya come in. She did not return his smile. She positioned herself firmly on the concrete floor and scanned out toward the cells.
"Well, Parkon, what the hell is going on?"
The man took a deep and slow swig from the mug. He swallowed audibly. She saw that he started sweating and avoided her gaze.
"Uh, yes, Anya, you see..."
"Police Inspector Malov."
"Uh, yes, Police Inspector Malov, suspect number 899–53456 is injured and will be transferred to Kuinn
Hospital for care."
"Bullshit, what do you mean injured? How can a person in your custody injure himself in here?"
Anya fixed her gaze on Parkon, who took another swig of coffee.
"Uh, yes, it... seems to be that... that he fell, unfortunately. He must have hit his head on the bunk, or the floor.
He looked like spaghetti in the face. It happened when I was... in the bathroom. I called up to the police captain, according to regulations, and informed about the incident, and then got orders to wait until the prisoner is picked up."
Anya demonstratively took a step closer to the man, who backed into a desk.
"He's mine, no damn hospital staff is coming here and taking him to a hospital where he can escape like a rat. No one!"
A throat clearing interrupted her. She turned around and felt the day getting even worse. She looked into a police badge, of a type she hated. She looked at the owner's face, a pale visage, with eyes hidden behind a pair of sunglasses. The black hat and leather coat revealed his identity faster than the police badge. A hoarse voice drawled the words.
"Police Inspector Malov, I am Doctor Borzakov, Stapo. Your prisoner is now my responsibility. If you have any questions you can take them up with your command. You may leave now."

## Chapter 4 – Imperiet

The roar from the packed foyer was palpable. It was intermission at Beratinalde's production of Fionios' "The Merchant Without Memory," a farcical opera on the edge of what the audience could endure. A major draw, however, was that the production boasted the renowned violinist Darius Pavlov, who was expected to musically illustrate the forgotten son's death scene solo.

The head of House Mengal stood at the bar, gazing out over Sandukar's society over the rim of a vodka. The cold gray eyes searched, registered, and assessed the situation—who was talking to whom, who stood outside, who was there and who was missing. Her bodyguards maintained perfect distance, invisible but ready.

"Madame Archiatros."

The voice was deep, raspy as if it originated in smoky gentlemen's clubs. The older, well-preserved woman responded without turning toward the voice.

"Grand Duke van Gralen-Kriger. I saw you in box three in the hall."

She turned toward the massive, broad-shouldered, gray-haired man whose face seemed carved from granite, scarred by war and battle—the head of House Kriger. His tailored suit fit perfectly and was adorned with the characteristic carnation in his lapel. He bowed stiffly and lightly kissed the archiatros's gloved hand.

"You truly look ravishing this evening, you light up the room, upon my honor."

The man clicked his heels together to reinforce the compliment. She responded with a veiled smile. He lit a cigarette and positioned himself at her side.

"So, Nadya, what do you think of tonight's performance? Surprising that Pavlov is involved, isn't it?"

"No, not at all, if one keeps up with the rumor mills."

She smiled more broadly. He saw in her eyes that it was a genuine smile. One he had seen before, in an entirely different situation.

"Rumors, those are for enlisted men and whores, pardon my language."

He observed the woman's frozen smile. Then she burst into tasteful laughter, laughter that made her sound younger than she was. He took a drag and raised one eyebrow. He saw that his adjutant was vigilant, ready should anything occur, whatever that might be. A brief moment of silence followed.

"I've missed you, Sebastien."

She said it more quietly than necessary. There was no one else around the two of them. No one dared.

"And I you. What say you to a nightcap in my private, and discreet, suite at the Ristoria, say, an hour after the performance concludes?"

"What say you to right now?"

He smiled for the first time that evening and waved over his adjutant.

Sandukar is a vast metropolis containing everything from majestic skyscrapers to workers' quarters with sooty brick buildings as far as the eye can see. The districts' appearance varies depending on origin and who lives there. In this maze of architectural styles, historic buildings, and social classes, the people live under the State's eternally watchful eye.

Sandukar - The Empire's Heart

Sandukar lies on an island at the Copper River's mouth. East of the city are large shipyard constructions and military installations. The island lies closer to the northern shore and three bridges lead from the island to the mainland. One of these is intended only for train traffic. Most of the harbor area lies outside the walls, and here are large garbage dumps and shantytowns where the homeless survive by scavenging through the refuse and eating the remains.

The city's character changes with the year's cycles. The winters are harsh, cold, and severe with massive precipitation and temperatures that creep down toward minus 20°C or colder in the icy winds from the Oil Sea. Many see winter as a time of purification, when the homeless freeze to death and the city's worn facades and garbage are concealed by the gray-white snow. Winter's contrast is the short but very hot summer, when for a few weeks it becomes very hot, with an average temperature over 30°C, which makes the air shimmer in the almost windless city. Between these two poles are the long spring and autumn, both cool and very rainy.

Sandukar resembles no other city in the Empire as it is circular and surrounded by an over twenty-meter-high wall of blackened basaltic stone. Seven gates adapted for cars, trains, and people lead out/in through the wall. Security at these locks is rigorous, with checks of identification papers, freight permits, and passes, and the waiting time to enter or exit the city is often long.

Just inside the wall runs the first canal, called the Moat. It is one hundred meters wide and filled with dark, murky water. Seven bridges span the canal, one to each gate. The Moat is connected to the other canals in the city in a strange geometric pattern that separates the city's areas from each other. The canals are used partly to transport materials within the city on massive barges. Collective transport between districts exists in the form of ferries. The canal system is also where the treatment plants empty wastewater.

**The Capital**

The canals are 100 meters wide and deep, and the water is regulated with dams and locks in areas where the terrain is too hilly. Standing at the canal's edge, one can face a vertical drop of twenty meters before the water's surface begins. The water comes from the Copper River and travels through the various city districts before plunging into the depths at the inner city's center.

Sandukar's architecture is varied and in places almost mad in its execution, as it displays many different wills and a long history. One can find buildings hundreds of years old adjacent to skyscrapers and grand factory halls. In alleys and on squares stand architectural wonders whose origins have long been forgotten, and wherever one wanders, one can find places and things to marvel at. The closer to the city's center, the taller the buildings become. Skyscrapers stretch hundreds of meters up toward the dark clouds, and on tall iron bridges and stone viaducts, trains and cars travel in a steady stream.

Despite new construction, most is worn and blackened, with an aura of antiquity about it. It seems as if the city itself causes the copper statues to patinate, the facades to soil, and the metal to rust. The dark facades are lit up, however, by the electric lights erected by the millions of people who are born, live, and die here. The city is a world unto itself, and the people are parts in its eternal machinery.

### Administration

Each district functions almost as an autonomous city, and citizens rarely need to leave it, except for job commuting if they work in the Machine Cities or in other districts.

The State has branches of the authorities spread throughout each district to give the population greater service in daily activities. Each district's administration is run by its respective city director and his/her subordinates at the city office. Each district administration is controlled by the ministries and cabinet, in a complicated and illogical web of regulations, rules, norms, practices, and laws.

The police stations, run by the police directors, have local precinct offices to be close to the citizens. In each district is a police headquarters that serves as the main office for all police activity in the district and functions as an administrative coordination unit with the other districts and the State.

The State Hospital serves as a hub for more serious care interventions and for administration, which is led by the health director. Under the state hospital are a number of clinics—health centers that should handle most of the daily care citizens need. Without a citizen license, one receives no care.

The districts' courts try, and judge in, all cases not considered enemies of the state. The courts' soldiers are the district justiciars and their deputies, who are responsible for prosecution and act as prosecutors in court.

The State's subjects must be able to manage their work while fulfilling their obligations to friends and family, otherwise many suffer. Therefore, citizens are encouraged to report exhausted or depressed friends, colleagues, and family members to the State, which then assists with counseling and help so the individual shall find the guiding light again. The Ministry of Truth has special offices for this type of case, where accommodating officials can quickly and discreetly help concerned relatives and handle the practicalities regarding retrieval of, and conversation with, the patient.

The Office of Denunciation is a known, hated, and feared place. Seeing a friend or family member enter the building can make even the most cold-blooded person sweat.
