them who possess unique abilities and mystical powers.

Bruno takes care of his staff and pays tribute to 
the Hexagon for protection. People who still come to the 
circus to cause trouble are dealt with efficiently. The knife 
throwers have shown themselves skilled at hitting vital 
areas on several occasions, and the mighty Tamos can easily 
knock down a dozen grown men single-handedly. Violence 
is, however, bad for business and the circus folk try 
to resolve most things through conversation. They know, 
namely, that people need entertainment, they need to 
amuse themselves. At the circus one can laugh, cry, and 
marvel, and see mimes and cunning jesters mock the State 
and the bureaucrats. It is because of this that the circus 
stays afloat. People pay for happiness and for entertainment 
that is something other than mind-numbing, as long as such 
alternatives exist.

### The Underground Market

Adjacent to the square one can find a building whose 
upper floors are burnt out. It previously housed a 
brewery and is still used to some extent as such by 
the Zhavrans, who have a large part of their liquor 
production and sales here. However, it is not this that 
makes the building so special but what lies beneath it. Under 
the vaults and the winding passages and storage halls, 
an underground market is held. Here the most bizarre 
and fantastic things are offered on filthy blankets by 
fawning vendors. The dim space is poorly 
illuminated by oil lanterns and bare light bulbs—sounds are 
distorted in the echoing passages and the air is usually filled 
with incense and mustiness.

Many who visit this market become obsessed with 
it and return time and again. There always seems to be 
new finds to acquire and things to trade in. Greed 
and desire never seem to end but drive people to 
inhuman acts or to the brink of ruin.

### Doctor Kansal

The bodies of people who have been killed usually turn 
up sooner or later. Either the homeless drag their 
rat-eaten corpses up from the sewers in hope of reward, or 
the bloated bodies are fished up from the canal. If one wants 
a body to disappear and never be found again, it can be 
a good idea to visit Doctor Kansal, who operates in an 
old warehouse building in the Fallen City.

Kansal is a chemist and runs a small chemical factory with 
a dozen employees. However, he conducts a business on 
the side to earn some extra money. For 2,000 zovrins 
he takes care of unwanted bodies and lowers them, 
together with any belongings, into an acid bath where 
they dissolve over a few days into an unrecognizable 
sludge that is then emptied into the sewers along with the rest of the 
industrial waste. If one has many bodies, one can get 
a bulk discount, but if one wants to lower living people into 
the acid, Kansal usually doubles the prices.

### The Bazaar

The Bazaar is the part of the Fallen City that can most 
be likened to a normally functioning district. In the south 
lies a power plant that supplies the district, and even 
neighboring districts, with electricity. Several factory areas 
are open and production of basic equipment continues 
around the clock. Some of the factories are run entirely by 
the workers who work there—something that is completely 
unknown in other parts of the city.

**The inhabitants have formed robust and well-armed** 
citizen guards who ensure that criminals and 
violent offenders are repelled—often in a very palpable way. 
Diplomatic contacts with the larger gangs exist, 
agreements that have been reached to satisfy both sides. 
The times gangsters have violated these agreements, blood 
has flowed, and so far the population of the Bazaar has not 
lost such a showdown.

The district's name comes from the large markets that 
are held every weekend at various locations in the area. 
Finds from the Ruins, crafts and tailoring, junk and trinkets 
are haggled between buyers and sellers. Old antique books, 
ornaments, and documents that survived the firestorms in 
the Ruins fetch high sums from wealthy outsiders.

### The Sisterhood

A large group within the citizen guard in the Bazaar 
is the Sisterhood. The group is led by Jea Karev, a good 
rhetorician and charismatic leader who is always seen 
wearing a black veil over her face. The Sisterhood consists 
largely of female prostitutes. It was formed after a group 
of prostitutes had killed their pimps and started their 
own business in Sin's Nest. Soon they gained more and more 
women on their side, but as the business grew, the 
more attention it attracted. The women were forced 
eastward, into the Bazaar, and were eventually forced to 
defend themselves with violence.

Despite lesser firepower, the Sisterhood had other 
advantages, including means of extortion, skilled 
assassins, and seductive talents. The Hexagon, which was the 
greatest opponent, finally gave them the Bazaar to conduct 
their business in, for a smaller share in their earnings.

Recently, several conflicts have arisen within 
the Sisterhood as many in the organization compete for 
power. The company Lustskymning has also begun 
to challenge the Sisterhood's power and tries to take control 
over members within the organization, something that has 
caused Karev to begin motivating members to strike back.

### The Ruins

In a several-square-kilometer area in the Fallen City, 
there is nothing but ruins. Crumbling and burnt-out 
buildings line desolate avenues, and the urban 
landscape is covered with debris, fallen mortar, and twisted 
metal parts. The walls are covered with bullet holes, and in 
poorly covered mass graves rest the bone remains of thousands 
of people who died from gas attacks and fragmentation bombs.

The district is almost always covered by an eerie fog, 
and rumors from treasure hunters say that at night one sees 
strange creatures rummaging through the ruins, and moans 
and cries of anguish are heard distantly wherever one turns. 
Some even claim to have seen fragments of their own inner 
struggles.

In the middle of the ruins lies the remainder of the tower 
that once stretched up toward the firmament and obscured 
the district with its ominous shadow. It is also said to be 
this place that caused the district's violent fall. It 
was here the fiercest battles took place. In places, the ruins 
are nothing but impassable wasteland with fallen 
brick, granite blocks, and twisted metal frames—but in 
many places the burnt-out buildings have been restored and 
house life again.

The people who live here have a very harsh existence but 
live in relative freedom. Likewise, one can still make 
valuable finds in the ruins as the area was once very wealthy. 
Searching for treasure is lucrative, and if one manages to 
find a great discovery, one can move away from this forgotten 
place.

A destructive and dark disease haunts the area. 
Madness, nightmares, and violent outbursts strike 
the inhabitants of the ruins—but at the same time there is a 
dark allure, and people with black souls seek the secrets 
that are embedded in the ground and in the buildings' foundations.

The Varlekus family (see page 149) and its servants are the 
ruling power in the ruined city, but there are plenty of odd 
religious sects, gangs, and leaders with visions and prophecies.

### The Fallen Tower

The fallen tower's base still rises toward the sky, but 
the tower is broken and its shadow no longer haunts 
the surrounding district. The black basalt facade has 
cracked and the shards are scattered across the area. 
Blocks were crushed when the tower broke off and fell with 
a roar that was heard far beyond Sandukar's walls. Many 
strange things have happened in the tower's vicinity, and it 
is still avoided as those who seek it out seldom return with 
their sanity intact—if they return at all.

At night the tower is surrounded by a freezing, pale gray 
fog, and sometimes one can hear echoes, screams, and 
machine sounds from within it. It is said that the tower's 
master still rests in the ruins, and many myths circulate about 
what has happened to people who have sought the tower's 
innermost reaches and been slain by its master. What the 
truth about the tower is, no one knows, but most keep a 
safe distance despite the riches and artifacts one might find 
in its interior.

### Tavol

Sandukar has a maze of tunnels, culverts, and passages 
that wind down into the depths. In the Fallen City, many of 
them are flooded and collapsed and are said to be home to 
horrible things.

If one can navigate through the tunnels, one has the 
advantage of being able to bypass walls, guards, and 
districts and enter otherwise well-guarded areas. Few 
know of this tunnel network, but one of those who has 
mapped it is Tavol, a middle-aged man who lives 
underground and only ventures up to the surface to conduct 
barter trade with the finds he has collected.

Tavol has long, stringy, gray hair and a weathered, 
wrinkled face. He usually goes dressed in a worn, dark 
green suit, raincoat, rubber boots, and headlamp. 
For defense he has a sawed-off shotgun, and for company 
he has a large bloodhound. Usually he travels in the 
water-filled tunnels in a small boat with a brightly glowing 
lantern at the bow. Tavol has been to most places under 
the Fallen City; the only place he avoids is the area 
near the fallen tower where the water is black as tar and 
moves pulsating to the echo of dull heartbeats. Here he has 
seen horrors that have scared him senseless, and no matter 
how much money he is offered, he will not go there again. 
Otherwise, Tavol offers his services for the right amount of 
money and can lead people through the old sewer passages 
to the places they desire.

### Malgrav

At the foot of Kostor Bridge it is close to a square that is 
enclosed between massive buildings, which means it 
always lies in shadow. The square is called Malgrav Square 
and has come to be a hub for the entire area called Malgrav 
in common parlance. During the days there is a large 
marketplace here where one can find everything from fruit 
and old books to smuggled firearms and guides to the city's 
lower parts.

At night, entertainers, artists, and performers gather here 
who try to become known or earn money from their works—
everything from portrait painting to bizarre variety shows 
occurs. Deformed, monstrosities, and disturbed people 
see their chance in this jumble of impressions. This is also 
the hragers' strongest area, and they have their own enclave 
here.

### The Hraghic Enclave

What was previously a walled hraghic ghetto has now become a 
fortress for the hraghic people in the Fallen City. 
The people have united and shown great cooperation in 
the work of restoring the dilapidated houses and 
fortifying the walls. One is only admitted inside the walls 
if one belongs to the hraghic people or if one is invited.

### The Bathhouse Santor

By the canal lies a large bathhouse with Gothic architecture 
and separate sections for men and women. Here one is 
washed and massaged for a small sum of zovrins and can 
also request private bathing facilities. There are plenty of 
different pools, everything from cold baths to steaming hot 
water with exotic herbs. The bathhouse is seen as something 
of a spa for those with joint pain of various kinds.

The bathhouse has, however, a darker side. Madam Santor, 
who runs the bathhouse, conducts slave trade with kidnapped 
people who are caught when they arrive confused at 
Sandukar by train. Gullible people fall into the hands of 
Madam Santor's emissary deceivers, who drug them and

for them to the bathhouse. Once a month, auctions are held where the slaves are herded down into the drained pools and masked bidders haggle over them.

Beneath the bathhouse, a network of tunnels leads to the abandoned and half-flooded subway, and it is through these tunnels the slaves are transported. Here there are also underground dungeons where the slaves are kept imprisoned while awaiting sale. People from the organization Lustskymning often make purchases at Madam Santor's bathhouse and always attend the auctions.

### The Destroyers

A group known as the Destroyers has its base in an abandoned hotel in the southern part of Malgrav. The group consists of a number of the enlightened whose sole purpose is to fight the shadows. No real clarity on what this entails has emerged, but the confirmed members who have been encountered all seem coldly focused and carry long, ornate swords beneath their coats.

### Seburah's Cinema

On a back alley near Malgrav Square lies the notorious cinema owned by Seburah, a member of the organization Lustskymning. According to the yellowed posters, the cinema is showing The Battle at the Front, but it's entirely different films that are on its repertoire. Here come those with odd pleasures, those who revel in the violent and bizarre, once a month and pay 400 zovriner to take a seat in the decrepit salon. Here they get to see shaky silent films where people are stalked and kidnapped. When the film ends, the kidnapped person is brought onto the stage and subjected there to cruel acts by a duo dressed in black and white respectively. These two gentlemen are called Mr. Black and Mr. White and wear theater masks over their faces—it is also they who film and kidnap the victims.

The deeds committed vary from month to month, and so do the victims. It can range from torture, rape and humiliation to outright execution. Sometimes the audience is invited to participate in various ways. All victims are killed at the end of the performance, usually to the sparse audience's cheers.

Those who witness the deeds often become spellbound and addicted to the performances and seek them out time and again. They are often disgusted, certainly, but cannot resist the perversions that unfold.

### The Free Haven

A large area in the northeastern quadrant of the Fallen City is simply called the Free Haven. When the tower in the ruined city collapsed, a firestorm swept across the sky, over what is now the Free Haven where most houses and buildings were constructed of wood. A persistent rain extinguished the flames after a few days, but afterward only the burned skeletons of the houses remained. Nowadays it is mostly the poor, the sick, the mentally disturbed, refugees, escapees and addicts who roam among the ramshackle huts, shelters and dilapidated buildings. Electricity, water and food are scarce commodities.

Rumors of cannibalism occur and bodies tend to disappear only to later be found with considerably less meat on their bones.

A unifying force in the area is the church, which has several well-attended premises. Whether it's the message being preached, the roof over their heads or the warm soup from the soup kitchen that attracts makes no difference—there are few other churches in Sandukar that have so many visitors at sermon and evening prayer.

### The Zoo

Adjacent to the Ruins lies Hauer's Zoological Park, a long-overgrown zoo and home to various mad animals. The zoo was damaged by the firestorms and in an attempt to save the animals, they were set free. Many were confused by the fires and fled deeper into the devastated city. The stronger ate the weaker, the zoo was abandoned and the wild animals fed on the burned corpses they came across.

### The Black Park

The Black Park is a desolate forest area that spreads out in the borderland toward the Ruins. The trees are all damaged by the fire from the firestorms, and shake their naked branches toward the sky. The ground is black and there are plenty of oily puddles and sunken vehicles. A makeshift dump for bulky waste exists in the center of Black Park. Here garbage is dumped from zeppelins that travel over the area. Desperate scavengers rummage around in the area searching for finds and underground caches.

### The Plague Yard

The Plague Yard is one of the few areas where the State has influence within the Fallen City. It is a smaller district, surrounded by high walls with barbed wire at the top. Guards in fortified towers with searchlights ensure that no one enters or leaves.

Something happened in the district that required this intervention. Those who lived there were struck by an insidious, contagious disease that drove them mad, and one can still hear inhuman screams from the center of the district. The State has long waited out those imprisoned in the hope that the entire district will die off on its own—but it doesn't seem to be happening. Moreover, reports come that unmarked trucks arrive now and then and that desperate people are forced into the area by gas-masked soldiers.

At one of the "Gates of Power," as the three passages in and out are called, lies an enormous facility with an associated crematorium. State patrols in protective suits are said to occasionally reconnoiter inside the Plague Yard and bring bodies out for sampling. The bodies are then burned along with the equipment brought in.

### The Mauser Fields

An interesting area deep within the district is the Mauser Fields, a magnificent and now nearly overgrown burial ground with tens of thousands of graves, crypts, statues, parks and memorial groves—all in deplorable condition. Apparently the burial ground is several hundred years old, or even older, and dates from the time when Sandukar was built. Beneath the area, ancient catacombs miles long are said to stretch, filled with legendary personalities, hidden writings and ancient artifacts.

### Groups in the Fallen City

The Fallen City is ruled by criminal gangs that fight for power. There are constant conflicts between them and people who live in the district are more often than not connected to, or under the "protection" of, at least one of them. These organizations not infrequently have branches extending to the rest of Sandukar.

### The Hexagon

The Engineer looked out through the tall windows from his chamber at the top of the Hexagon's skyscraper. The buildings below him were mere dark shadows in the white fog and here and there he could see the light from the boats' lanterns as they made their way through the place that had once had streets and stately monuments. Everything was now submerged. He raised his own hand and looked at it. Rotten, decayed, putrid—far too similar to the building he now resided in. It seemed as if they were bound to each other, he and the building, and shared each other's decay. He turned around and stared at the blackened Hexagon inlaid in the study's marble floor and wondered if he would ever get an answer to the six-pointed star's riddle.

In the center of the Water Moat rises a skyscraper with a worn and algae-covered stone facade. Behind the cracked windows one can see the flickering light of lanterns and figures moving around them like shadows. The building lacks electricity and its lower parts lie completely underwater. The interior is old, worn and moldy with threadbare carpets, moth-eaten drapes and peeling paint. Through the corridors draft cold winds and everything is damp. At the same time, the building is magnificent with its hexagonal facade, the tall leaded windows, the Gothic vaulted arches and the massive halls that bear witness to former wealth and splendor. This is the Hexagon's seat—a feared organization that controls the majority of criminal activity in the district and its surroundings, and which receives large sums of money through the so-called tax it extracts from the people in its territory.

The Hexagon was originally an industrial company and its symbol, a six-pointed star, can be found on stained brass plaques at several factories and buildings in the district. Whether the criminal organization has any connection whatsoever to the former industrial company is unknown, but the members claim the right to control all of the Hexagon's former premises and occupy its seat.

Members of the organization have a six-pointed star tattooed in each palm, which they conceal with gloves when they want to move anonymously. For living in the Fallen City, the members are well-dressed in proper suits and dresses of older cut. Likewise, they usually have an aristocratic and noble manner on the surface, they generally socialize only with each other and avoid the places where "lower standing" individuals frequent.

The organization has thousands of members and branches everywhere in Sandukar. Their influence is deliberately low in the Inner City, as it would otherwise attract bad attention from the truly powerful.

The Hexagon has acquired something of a state-like apparatus in the Fallen City and this is where people come to ask for help, buy information or swear their allegiance. At

"Can you give me another drink?
So, you want to know about her?
She's at the Fallen City, keeping an eye on the riffraff from the shadows. You'll never see her before she knows who you are, what you're doing there and where you live. Well, her face is badly burned, as if by acid or fire or chemicals. Without the damage, the Sorrowful One would be
Sandukar's most beautiful creature."

this damned place, the people see the Hexagon as the only sign of order, albeit a draconian one, and many therefore support the organization's control. It's no secret either that the Hexagon has contacts with the police force in the Free City and that it greases several high-ranking officials to be left alone.

In its immediate area, however, the organization doesn't hesitate to display its power in the most brutal ways—usually by slitting troublemakers open from crotch to throat and leaving them to die in their own blood. Right now this is happening primarily to people believed to have dealings with the new organization the
Apostles of Annihilation, which has begun to challenge the Hexagon's power.

The Hexagon is ruled by the patriarch Hugo Gavilan, though few know his name. He is better known as the Engineer and is a mythical figure whom few have met. According to rumor, he never ventures out into the city but resides high up in the Hexagon building. His right hand Raclia, also known as the Sorrowful One, is the one who relays his orders. This woman would have been beautiful if not for half her face being burned away.

### Stradh and Ilsa

When the Hexagon needs to have some dirty or dangerous job carried out, they usually send out Stradh and Ilsa—a deadly pair. Ilsa uses charm, cold-bloodedness and manipulation to get where she wants and extract information while
Stradh uses violence, torture and threats. Together they perform cruel deeds for the Hexagon's master.

Stradh dresses entirely in white and is a tall man with fine features, his hair is slicked back and he has two automatic pistols under his jacket. His hands are usually covered by leather gloves and outdoors he wears a coat under which he can have several concealed weapons. His indifferent, light gray eyes are widely known in the underworld.

Ilsa is a very beautiful, dark red-haired woman with black clothing, usually corsets and elegant dresses.
Around her neck she always has a choker. She wears tall, black velvet gloves and carries in her handbag an automatic pistol as well as several poisons and drugs hidden in perfume bottles and powder compacts. In her hair there are always several hairpins which she skillfully uses as weapons in close combat when needed.

Both have black, six-pointed stars tattooed in their palms as a sign of their loyalty to the Hexagon.
Their signature is a playing card whose symbol is a six-pointed star and they leave such cards as threats or warnings for what can happen if one defies the Hexagon.

### The Apostles of Annihilation

When rumors began spreading on the streets about a new organization in the Fallen City, not many eyebrows were raised. Various criminal associations come and go, only a few manage to hold together for any length of time. But the Apostles of
Annihilation persisted, and even though the organization at first didn't make much fuss, it gathered more and more members.
The followers were drawn from the poor and outcast, those who had neither power nor hope, people no one cared about. The members' fanaticism, their ability to hold the group together and organize themselves quickly made the organization a power to be reckoned with.

The organization's leader, the Supplicant, is a mythical figure who together with his most faithful companions wanders through the Fallen City and binds more people to his flock. It's said that the Supplicant was once a priest, it's also said that he fought in the army against the enemy in the north and that in this bloody chaos he came to an insight. He received a vision of purgatory, the abomination and eternal torment and it is from this that the Supplicant has promised to free his followers.
How much truth lies in these tales cannot be known, myths about the Supplicant spread through hundreds of mouths and are distorted before they reach the listeners' ears.

Many of the organization's followers are of the Hraghic people and they have found new hope in their master's words.
The followers, who openly show themselves to belong to the Apostles of
Annihilation, wear long, black, priest-like robes. They are long-haired and the men usually have beards according to Hraghic tradition. If you enter the apostles' territory you will encounter their guards. You can also expect a visit from them if you cross the apostles in any way.

Nowadays the apostles control a considerable part of the Fallen City and many smaller gangs obey them out of fear.
Those who don't follow them are seen as enemies and the apostles have quickly gained a reputation as merciless murderers who perform human sacrifices to their master. Their deeds are unusually brutal and they find great honor in killing their opponents in close combat. Thus they are often armed with heavy, curved knives, axes and garrotes. Their access to weapons is for unknown reasons very good, particularly access to military materiel.

The apostles' agenda often seems confused and random as they make attacks and commit acts against seemingly random people and organizations. The Supplicant proclaims a blasphemous darkness that exists in certain individuals—a darkness that must be eradicated. Weapons and resources are gathered and through sermons and cruel deeds the apostles try to open people's eyes to the Supplicant's power. Recently the apostles have defied the agreement with the police force and made raids into the Free City. Their deeds have many times been completely open and they have left masses of corpses in their wake.

On the home front, a war is about to erupt between the Hexagon and the apostles, a war that will claim victims in droves on both sides—and innocents will likely suffer as well.

### The Supplicant

Despite the countless stories that exist about the Supplicant, his life history is known only to a few. He is of the
Hraghic people but grew up in Sandukar and spent his childhood on the streets under miserable conditions.
With forged papers he managed to get permission to join the army at a young age and served there for several years in the northern trenches. His tactical mind and manipulative ability made him an advisor to one of the officers and he learned most things about tactics and strategy. During an unusually bloody offensive where his comrades were killed he received a vision of the world's darkness and of the cruelty that rests in the swirling and destructive abyss.

The Supplicant realized the truth about the enemy in the north and escaped from the war zone to an abandoned monastery up in the mountains. There he spent several years living off nature while writing his own bible based on his dreams and visions. After that he returned to Sandukar. When he left the city he was just a child, when he returned he was an aged man. The city was the same though and he sought out his old quarters where the Hraghic people now lived under oppression. There he began to proclaim his commandments. He wants to awaken people's minds by claiming that the world has been cast into hell and that the life they live is part of purgatory.

To remedy the situation he claims that one must find the people who have darkness in their hearts and kill them. His bible has now begun to be printed and is spreading in secret—in it are said to be answers to who is tainted by the darkness and how they shall be killed. The Supplicant takes no heed of innocents who fall victim to his following.
His struggle is for mankind and without him he considers it doomed.

The Supplicant often wanders disguised in the alleys of the Fallen
City but when he shows himself to his followers he does so clad in a dark red priest's robe and with his long, steel gray hair loose. He always has several weapons on him even though his gaze can be just as imposing and threatening. It's rumored that he has several doppelgangers as his life is constantly in danger.

### The Varlekus Family

Among the ruins, in the fallen tower's shadow, the Varlekus family has its lair. Behind high walls the family has established its seat in what was once a botanical garden and a museum. The buildings have been restored and from the massive greenhouses the artificial light once again illuminates the night. Over the parapet rise slender chimneys and a stench of chemicals and rotted meat hangs over the area. Exactly what goes on in the former halls and under the charred trees no one knows but from here come many of the drugs that spread through the city. The walls are constantly guarded by men with automatic weapons and flamethrowers and the surroundings are patrolled by dogs.

The ruined buildings that lie around the Varlekus seat are all condemned and only one passable road leads up to the wall's massive steel gate. Every week trucks arrive with deliveries and every week precious cargo departs. Who rules the Varlekus family no one knows but it's said to be someone subordinate to the Mengal lineage—perhaps even a family member themselves. That would explain
Varlekus's vast resources and the fact that the authorities do nothing about the family's operations.

Servants of the family can be found throughout the Fallen
City, they often function as drug dealers, slave traders and doctors. The doctors in particular are often respected as they know what they're doing and are known for not asking any questions but charging handsomely in zovrins or favors. Among other things they can perform abortions, plastic surgery and lobotomies. Varlekus doctors often pay a sum of money to anyone who comes with fresh organs to them—exactly how the organs came into the person's possession they never ask. This has led to bodies in the Fallen City often being primitively butchered by desperate individuals to produce a few zovrins for drugs. There are even those who donate parts of themselves, such as eyes, arms or legs, to satisfy their addiction.

Besides drug and organ trafficking, the Varlekus family engages in human trafficking. Young, healthy individuals are bought and taken to the family's seat to never be seen again.

The members of the Varlekus family have managed to stay out of most conflicts as their power is such that one doesn't want to cross them, while they seem content with the power they already have.

### The Zhavrans

The Zhavrans are a collection of Celenkae who keep to and around the Nest of Sin. They have their own business in liquor and drug trafficking. The liquor they usually brew themselves in legal and well-guarded breweries in the area—the drugs are either imported from outside or bought from the Varlekus family.

The Zhavrans have business in the Free City as well but keep to the Celenk quarters as the Hexagon handles smuggling to the rest of the Free City. They have plenty of contacts in both districts and if you need a guide or to arrange something, it's to the Zhavrans you should go. Most members are Celenkae but still blend well into the environment they move in and one can rarely tell if a typical Celenk belongs to the Zhavrans or not.

### Lustshadow

If you're looking for forbidden pleasures, macabre pornography or unusual games to satisfy your lusts, then it's to Lustshadow you should go. The company owns several brothels and porn theaters in the district and also has secret meeting places where customers can have all their wishes fulfilled.

Even among the higher social classes, Lustshadow is popular. Many decadent lineage members order self-produced films or prostitutes who participate in the most bizarre acts. It's even said that members of the Guiskard lineage have connections to the company.

Lustshadow's employees are always well-dressed and skilled at moving socially and understanding their customers' desires.
The company's business card is in black and silver and is handed out to the rich and important... and to those with special needs.

### The Couriers

The Couriers are a loose association of pure muscle—
most members are former military or prisoners led by a man simply called the Captain.
The Couriers are large-built and don't back down from a fight. The Hexagon often uses the gang as a strike force and it also handles the organization's business in the Free City.

Recently the Couriers have begun to grab a larger share of the business for themselves in hope that the Hexagon will be too focused on the Apostles of Annihilation to dare risk yet another conflict.

"In a nightmare, every decision you make is wrong."
—Jessca Navarre

The Empire is everything—the State, the provinces, the peoples, the lineages, war, peace, countryside and cities, the great and the small, in a blessed mixture. Few know anything other than what the Empire boasts of, what the Empire protects and what the Empire stands for.

### Society

Look at the artists on the silver screen, at the magnates who build companies and at those who make careers within the bureaucracy. It's written about them every day in the newspaper.
They get a better and easier life. If they can, so can
I, if I just fight a little harder and strain myself a little more, there's always time to rest later.
—Jako Kovalch, foreman, Krauss Mechanical

Much in the world of Noir is described in grim terms, with words like isolation, desperation, frustration and hopelessness. One might ask how anyone has the energy to do anything at all in the Empire if everything is so thoroughly rotten and damnable?

The thing is that most people don't experience their situation as particularly miserable, as they don't have much to compare with. Sure it's heavy and wearing but one lives and doesn't have to be afraid as the State provides for one's needs and protects one from the enemy.

The vast majority who come to the cities in general or Sandukar in particular from a life in the countryside get things better. The land rains away in acid rain and nature bows under industrialization's yoke.
Crops rot and livestock sicken. Untouched nature is rare, other than in inaccessible areas.

In the cities there is security, you're not alone, there is a governing order that protects you from the enemy, from anarchy. You can get a job, a home, a family if you fight. The opportunity to perform great deeds and fulfill your goals exists there, even if you don't get it tomorrow, or in a month, or even in a year.

Fear of the enemy, of seeing the system waver, of chaos, makes citizens remain passive and accept, albeit reluctantly in some cases, the current situation as the best possible.

The State may be cumbersome and impersonal but the police,
Stapo and the realm's soldiers protect the individual from crime, collapse and doom. Citizens need the State as a counterforce to the enemy and its sadistic rule in the north and east, which is just waiting to destroy and enslave everyone. All this is fact that the Ministry of Truth gladly enlightens downcast or doubting citizens about.

The State & the Empire

Should life become too heavy, there is always the church to comfort and warm. Even though the church is said to be poison for the soul, there is someone there who listens and understands.
Others seek comfort in humanity. They search hope-fully to find the warmth that comes from love, the security that comes from a warm embrace under the covers while rain hammers against the windows. To find someone to confide in, to share one's innermost thoughts, feelings and fears with, is a powerful force that drives many people in an otherwise cold and frightening world.
Love can become the light. Even when the world outside seems large and impersonal, it is there, and through it one can start a family. In love, one's own life becomes valuable and the family, with spouse and perhaps children, is a man-ageable and comprehensible concept for most, something to cherish and protect. Even here the Ministry of Truth can help—
with information brochures about family planning.
**The State**
The slumbering beast that through ordinances, laws, reg-ulations and registrations rules over the citizens of the Empire is best known as the State in common parlance. The State is like its own entity, difficult to meet, harder to understand and hardest to satisfy. Many citizens are reluctant to have any-thing at all to do with the beast, as its attention falls upon the individual if provoked—and no one wants obli-gatory meetings with tax inspectors, visits from truth controllers or visits to Stapo's local office for talks. If one stays out of sight, one avoids having the light on oneself and can operate in relative safety under one's own control.
**State Control**
The State operates through different channels, from paragraph-riding bureaucrats to surly police officers, via local offices, depart-ments, departments, directorates, ministries and further into a very tangled jungle of connections, links and areas of responsibility. Everything done in the Empire in general and in the capital Sandukar in particular, is noted, registered and archived for future needs.
In large office environments sit thousands of people occu-pied with rattling down reports on their heavy type-writers and between the offices documents are distributed via pneumatic tubes or internal couriers on scooters.
All decrees must be conveyed out to every local office, both in the city and to the provinces. The Post Ministry and the telegraphs work hard to convey the authority's will to the chosen champions in the State's forces. Through the
Ministry of Truth's auspices, messages are conveyed to citi-zens via loudspeaker cars, public notices, distributed leaflets, via radio and in newspapers.
In Sandukar there are fixed loudspeakers positioned at important gathering points such as certain squares, public transport hubs and main streets, from which quick announcements can be conveyed when needed.
**The Informers**
Within all parts of the State there are informers and spies for the same. No one knows if "the new person in the department" is placed as an intelligence agent or snitch for one's own administration, for Stapo, or even worse, for the
Ministry of Truth.
At factories and larger private workplaces there are watch-ers and reporters planted to ensure that messages and rules are followed, both by employees and those responsible. Should subversive activities or treasonous thoughts and utter-ances be found, the guilty party quickly receives a visit from Stapo for collection and talks. Sometimes entire families or all staff at a company can be collected during nocturnal raids when a dozen or more dark Stapo vehicles stand ready for transport to bare cells and desolate interrogation rooms.
**The State's Ministries**
The State governs through ministries which have the task of running the Empire. These institutions are from the outside incom-prehensible with their labyrinthine bureaucracy and their tens of thousands of employees. Here follows a selection.
**Ministry of Archives**
One of the seven primary ministries. The guarantor that all documents, permits, deeds, receipts, contracts, pass-ports, citizen passes, agreements and licenses are produced, regis-tered, saved, copied and stored in a safe and permanent way.
**Ministry of Administration**
Responsible for the State's properties, real estate and resour-ces being managed in the best way and also handles other admin-istrative matters. Manages the maintenance of drinking water supply and sewage systems.
**Ministry of Health**
Provides free public healthcare and access to medicines, and ensures that hospitals and clinics function as they should.
**Ministry of Industry**
Responsible for simplifying enterprise and ensuring the labor market functions. Registers all production in the Empire so that there is current information should it become relevant with forced recruitment or rationing.

### Ministry of Justice

One of the seven primary ministries. Manages the courts at all levels and appoints suitable persons as judges.
Oversees prosecutors and defense attorneys so they do not abuse the power and responsibility given them by the State. Enacts new laws and ordinances.
**Ministry of War**
One of the seven primary ministries. Ensures through strategies and resource allocation that the war against the enemy functions as efficiently as possible and ensures that the wars are fought in accordance with the State's set goals. Secures
Sandukar's military defense and is responsible for military research and development.
**Ministry of Culture**
Ensures that citizens' spiritual lives are stimulated in a good way in accordance with the State's recommendations. Responsible for theaters, operas and the like, as well as for paintings, musi-cal compositions and books. The film medium, however, lies under the Ministry of Truth's domain.
**Ministry of Logistics**
One of the seven primary ministries. Responsible for road networks, airports, railways and boat traffic functioning satisfac-torily and for transports occurring as efficiently as possible. Also telegraph and telephone networks are their responsibility.
**Ministry of Order**
One of the seven primary ministries. Responsible through the order police, criminal police and tactical police for order being maintained in Sandukar and the Empire in general and for crimes being combated, investigated and solved.
**Ministry of Post**
Responsible for letters and packages reaching the right destination, and for maintenance of mailboxes and post offices throughout the Empire.
**Ministry of Resources**
Guarantees that the right goods are moved to the right places in the Empire so that resources for industry reach the factories and food for citizens reaches the markets. Responsible for the State's mines, agriculture, livestock management, forests and land.
**Ministry of Coordination**
The organ for coordination and coordination between the minis-tries and/or the Empire's provinces. Ensures that informa-tion is channeled correctly and reaches the right instance or person. Produces standards and templates to simplify work and admin-istration.
**Ministry of Truth**
One of the seven primary ministries. Keeps track of all of the Empire's citizens and ensures they are upda-ted with current ordinances and facts regarding the Empire's current situation. Provides citizens with information and announcements and ensures that printed media, radio and film contain correct information. Handles talks with citi-zens who are lost regarding their place in society and their atti-tude toward the State. Is also home to the Commissariat.
**Ministry of Tax**
Manages the State's finances and oversees tax payments from citizens and businesspeople. Controls the financial world, stock exchanges and banking system so that nothing is done that can harm the State in any way.
**Ministry of Security**
One of the seven primary ministries. Responsible for the state po-lice Stapo, the protection of important persons in society and surveillance of state buildings and institutions. Also responsible for telephone and telegraph interception and for sus-pected subversive letters that must be checked. Also has responsibility for prisoners, prisons, labor camps and such admin-istration.
**Ministry of Education**
Responsible for the five-year elementary school, preparatory education and higher education. Guarantees that the teaching is performed in a suitable and competent way, so that citizens learn the right things and have access to app-roved textbooks.
**Ministry of Science**
Responsible for cataloging research and new inventions being performed and used in accordance with the State's set goals and rules.
**Ministry of the Archive**
Responsible for all other ministries continuously rep-orting what is ongoing and functioning without interruption. These reports are later channeled further up in the State's upper layers.
The Ministry of the Archive stands just below the cabinet, constantly ready to convey information and directives upward and downward in the hierarchy. Their Ari, or "Department for revision and internal investigations" is rightly feared as it through different methods ensures that efficiency in the State's various organs is maintained and that troublesome elements are eliminated.
**The Empire**
The Empire stretches over a thousand miles from east to west.
Untamed and barren wilderness is here and there broken up by mas-sive industrial cities that day and night spread their ash across the firmament. The cities are connected by a modern and constantly expanding railway network and poorly main-tained roads. Many of the transports otherwise occur via the Copper River which like a life-giving artery runs through the realm. The river is in some places so wide that one can only with binoculars spot the opposite side, and the water which in the east is drinkable and crystal clear has blackened, foul-smelling and disease-causing when it runs past Sandukar to poison the Oil Sea. Outside the modern indust-rial cities by the river there are smaller communities and traces of another era before cogwheels and state control dominated.
On this vast expanse there once existed seven great kingdoms but now they are united into one mighty state where dissent is not tolerated. From the capital Sandu-kar the entire vast realm is controlled through complicated ordinances, strict laws and a labyrinthine bureaucracy.
The cities located along the Copper River are in the State's firm

grip but the farther out from the flood zone one goes, the weaker the State's control becomes. To maintain order in the outer regions, the clans and their servants are granted authority to administer the areas and the people who live there. Here rule influential industrial magnates, decadent clan families and religious leaders. It is another world, consid-erably more antiquated and primi-tive, where humanity's struggle against nature is a harsh reality.
Here mysticism, superstition and belief in the supernatural still hold sway.
If the people are the machinery that drives the State forward, the vast natural resources are what feeds it
— and the resources are devoured greedily without any thought what-soever. The brutal exploitation strikes back, however, as the waste from industrialization contaminates nature, makes the water in water-ways undrinkable and causes acid rain to devastate entire regions.
Rural folk find it hard to survive in the wake of crop failures and seek the safety of the cities where they hope for a new and better life.
Instead they become trapped in the state system where they must sub-mit to state decrees and partake in the intense enlightenment that the
Ministry of Truth engages in.
**The War**
No one remembers what it was like before the State took power. Accord-ing to the State's official historio-graphy, which can be found in the vaults of the Ministry of Archives, centuries upon centuries of brutal-ity and conflict between the seven kingdoms followed, and the State was the instrument needed to bring peace, order and an opportunity for the kingdoms to stand united against the terrible enemy in the north.
The enemy in the north swept in like a storm over the lands and brought destruction and death. Their armored cruisers bombarded the coastal cities and the soldiers raped, plundered and killed wherever they advanced. Times were dark for the kingdoms before seven powerful clans, one from each kingdom, united and together fought the threat. Finally they managed to drive away the enemy and established the city of Sandukar at the mouth of the Copper River.
The enemy threatened reinforcements beyond the Arkady
Mountains, but the State's forces made their way through the mountain passes and attacked the enemy on its own ground while defending their own borders. The war has been ongoing north of the mountains for centuries, and there are gigantic forts, minefields and trench complexes which today constitute the Empire's northernmost and most important defensive line.
Certain outspoken researchers have questioned the state historiography, but it is known that the State has banned all other types of historiography and that sources found out in the provinces have been rewritten or banned by state bureaucrats. This is to make the peoples and lands more uniform and preserve peace by consigning old conflicts to oblivion.
**Tensions**
**The state purge and** rewriting has not been able to completely change the kingdoms' culture and history, which means they differ from each other in many ways. One thing that is the same for all provinces, how-ever, is the rural population's mis-trust of big cities in general and of
Sandukar in particular.
Narrow-mindedness and prejudice dominate and outsiders and foreign-ers are regarded with great suspi-cion. This underlying fear of the unknown remains when rural folk move to the cities, as they prefer to build enclaves and quarters around their own people rather than mix with others. National pride applies to the homeland rather than the
Empire as an entity.
This poorly concealed racism cre-ates breeding ground for frustration and conflict, with arguments like
"the others" are stealing "our" jobs,
"our" women, etcetera. Riots and fights based on culture, race and origin are unfortunately not uncom-mon, and little is done by the State to curb it. The prejudices also take hold among employers, who prefer to hire someone "from their own country", and among the police, who on more than one occasion have looked away from crimes com-mitted by countrymen.
**The Peoples of the Empire**
There are plenty of ethnic groups and minorities in the Empire.
Some live relatively isolated in desolate rural areas with their own cultures and skepticism toward the outside world. The majority of the population, however, is assimilated by the State to fit into the society the State provides. There is a
Stradh checked his pistols, felt the reassurance of their weight and closed his eyes for a few seconds. The footsteps of two, maybe three, intruders echoed in the damp cellar corridor. He could smell the scent of cheap aftershave, liquor and cigarettes. There were defi-nitely three, one had wheezing breath-ing, the other two were more controlled.
Typical cops, he thought, always med-dling when they should stay away —
this time they had to disappear.
He quietly put his hand into his coat pocket, fumbled around among the con-tents, felt the cool lighter, some pieces of paper, a condom, and there... a coin.
He flipped the coin away so that it hit the cement wall a few meters to the left of his position, an old trick, but one that almost always worked. The reactions were not long in coming, a beam of light turned on, directed at the wall, and weapons were unsecured. A wheezing voice ordered him to come forward and
Stradh smiled to himself. He couldn't deny that he liked what was about to come, the bangs that would echo in the confined space, the smell of gun-smoke and the quiet clanking of empty shells hitting the cement floor. He was an artist in death and now he would get to create one of his works.
In one motion he threw himself up and fired the first shot in a symphony that would last around three seconds — after this brief time all listeners would lie dead.

widespread racism toward certain ethnic groups and prejudice is common. Especially against those who hold on to their cul-tures and don't blend in the way that is desired.
In Sandukar one can find representatives from all pos-sible peoples. They may be refugees from war-torn areas, desperate rural folk in search of work, fortune seekers hunt-ing for wealth or individuals keeping away from justice among the anonymous masses. The eternal city has room for them all in its embrace. Often they seek out people of the same kind and certain ethnic groups have taken over entire quarters or districts.
**Vagyar**
Vagyar are not really counted as an ethnic group since they are the peo-ple who dominate the Empire by number and are considered to be
"the original people" or "the main people". Vagyar is also not a term that is used except by academics; instead vagyar are called "the peo-ple".
**The Vagyar ethnic group** origi-nates from the former kingdoms in the Empire but has over the cen-turies been brought together eth-nically and socially and united under the propaganda. The part of the people who originate from the northern Arkady Mountains tend to have light hair and blue, gray or green eyes. The majority, those who originate from the vast plains and forests, are somewhat darker in complexion and usually have black or dark brown hair and dark eyes.
Centuries of kinship and marriage have meant that the features of the two ethnic groups are now mixed —
but blond and red hair are seen as unusual and exotic. Unless other-wise stated, all main characters are assumed to belong to this ethnic group. As a vagyar one easily blends into society and into the rest of the population.
**Celenk**
The Celenk ethnic group is the rem-nants of an ancient horse people from the western wilderness. The
Celenk still retain their ancestors'
nomadic spirit and a large part of those who live in the countryside usually use horse and wagon as means of transport and travel between villages and towns to con-duct trade and take temporary employment.
The Celenk who have permanently migrated to the cities usually make their living in the lower strata of society with various odd jobs and sometimes even criminal activity. Celenk often stick together even in the cities and survive by help-ing each other with loans, services and favors. The Celenk people have olive-colored skin and often jet-black, curly hair.
Their slightly almond-shaped eyes are often dark but some-times people are born with green or blue eyes. Celenk are somewhat shorter, and often stronger, than the average vagyar.
**Hragher**
The Hragher are an oppressed people who live under great suspicion from the State since the province in the east, the
Hragher's homeland, turned against the State and the Empire.
A bloody war now rages on their land and the Hragher who live in other places in the Empire find themselves persecuted by both state and citizens. Many crimes and accidents are blamed on them and the law does not do much to follow up on harassment, assaults and murders of Hragher.
**The Hragher seek safety with** each other and live close to each other to protect themselves from the world's hatred. In some cities the State has established ghettos where the Hrag-her are placed. In the breakaway province in the east, the Hragher are the dominant people and those who have all power and all bene-fits. The Hragher people are in many ways like the Vagyar ethnic group but with coarser features, more abundant hair growth, fuller lips and a somewhat grayish skin tone. Long hair indicates status and is therefore common among both men and women — at work or in battle the hair is usually gathered in a braid, which is sometimes taken as a tro-phy by triumphant soldiers from the
Empire.
**Arkarer**
In the eastern mountains and the vast forests that spread out beneath them, the Arkara people have their origin. The Arkarer have managed to preserve a large part of their cul-ture largely thanks to an isolated location and skillful political maneu-vering. In the cities the Arkarer are well regarded although there is some suspicion toward them as they stand out from the crowd. They are notori-ous for engaging in all kinds of mys-tical things and it is whispered that they have natural insight into the occult. Many are also well-read and knowledgeable in academic subjects.
The Arkarer are somewhat taller than the Vagyar people but also generally slimmer. They have well-defined cheekbones and sharp features. They are pale in complexion and have straight, fine hair but otherwise very little body hair.
Ilsa had with a glance gotten the man's attention, through a gesture awakened his desire and with a few words won his trust. She had let his hands caress the clothes off her, his fingers play on her stomach, continue down her thighs and press up into her. Warm and sweet she gave herself to him, felt his pulsat-ing nearness, the heat of his breath against her neck and his lips, so soft for a man's.
**She let him take control and** lured out his deepest lusts, she clawed him pas-sionately and bit his shoulder. When she whispered asking to tie him to the bed-post he smiled and nodded. She tore the bedclothes into strips, tied them into knots and rode him slowly while ensur-ing that he was firmly secured. The cli-ent had asked her to make an example that would serve as a warning to the man's gang, so the death had to be spec-tacular.
In a slow motion she loosened her hair and let it fall in a black cascade over her naked shoulders. The hairpin glit-tered in her grip as she thrust it into his throat just deep enough that only the ability to speak was lost. A work of art takes time to create after all, and this act of blood would be performed to the sound of rattling and faint moans of suffering.

### Mechanical Differences Between Peoples

Just as Noir's rule system does not account for any differences between genders, no modifications are included based on ethnic origin. However, a protagonist can belong to a minority, which is described on page 82.

### Naming Conventions

In general, the Empire's naming traditions are such that names resemble those of our world's Russian, Eastern European (such as Romanian, Czech, and Slovak) and German names. Latin-based (French, Italian, and Latin) names also work well, but English names and pronunciation essentially do not occur. Note that this applies only to pronunciation and melody—not that the names necessarily exist in the aforementioned places in reality.

### Darost

With its vast and fertile plains, Darost is the Empire's breadbasket. The farms are enormous, and on the somewhat barren heaths graze the livestock herds. Everything is about exploitation and production in order to survive. Farmers and wage laborers work themselves to death in the fields, and in the cramped labor camps all manner of diseases rage. Ironically, many workers die of malnutrition and starvation despite Darost being the province richest in food.

Ever-larger portions of the work are industrialized and mechanized; human workers are seen as nothing more than machines and are discarded when they are no longer of any use. Even the livestock is treated without dignity as it is led to the mechanical slaughterhouses or loaded onto overcrowded ships and train cars and transported to the cities to feed the people.

The new oil discoveries have caused large land areas to become poisoned, and machines pump day and night to extract the black gold from the earth's interior.

### History

Darost previously consisted of several small kingdoms that existed in relative harmony with one another. Large parts of the plains were home to the Celenka horse people, who were increasingly displaced until a war finally erupted between the Celenkas and the local population. The Celenka culture was eradicated after centuries of conflict, and those Celenkas who did not flee were enslaved.

When the kingdom of Sadovia in the east began its raids, Darost's kingdoms united to meet the threat. The enemy in the east came to affect daily life for the people of Darost; the inhabitants could no longer feel safe as they could be attacked at any moment. Sadovia's troops were infamous for their cruelty, raping, plundering, and enslaving the people in the conquered areas.

Old chronicles tell that when Dargher of House Orlok came to power, he promised that the people would be kept safe and expelled all the Church's men from the land, as it was well known that many of them supported Sadovia. Thereafter he gathered forces that drove back the invaders, and along the western bank of the Ravike River he had fortifications and defensive works erected, armed with heavy cannons—weapons that Sadovia did not yet possess.

Dargher had himself crowned regent over the new plains kingdom and named it after his son, Darost. Just a few years later, Dargher and his family fell victim to a calculated and macabre assassination; the murderers were suspected to be of Sadovian origin. The kingdom lived on, however, under House Orlok's rule. When the enemy in the north launched its attack, Darost was one of the kingdoms hardest hit. The enemy fleet sailed into the Mirror Sea and bombarded the important coastal cities and landed its troops.

Before reinforcements from the eastern border could be summoned, large parts of the kingdom were destroyed or conquered. When the kingdoms allied under one state, it took a long time before Darost joined them, as their arch-enemy Sadovia was one of the founders of the idea. When the war was over and the Empire formed, Darost slowly came to be transformed into the important natural resource the kingdom is today. Nowadays, much of what is produced is sent to the fronts in the north and east, and oil in particular has become increasingly important for operating the machines of war.

### Natural Resources

Besides cultivation, livestock, and oil, Darost contains large deposits of brown coal which power the power plants. Brown coal is an important export commodity, and long trains fully loaded with coal travel daily toward Sandukar and the other major cities of the Empire. The toxic smoke from the coal power plants and the destruction of the land when the coal is excavated ruin things for the farmers and livestock keepers and have led to a conflict between two important industries.

House Orlok has great control over Darost, but there is still land to stake out on the plains, and fortunes can still be made. There are a number of newly emerged industrial magnates who challenge the Houses' power through bold ventures and innovative thinking. So far, however, the Houses show who is in charge, and it happens that industrial magnates who have made too many enemies are found brutally murdered. Of course, the Houses always have an explanation for who carried out the deeds; usually it is people from the Church or the enemy's agents who are mentioned. Another approach is to force the upstarts into marriages with the Orlok line.

The Church has always been viewed poorly in Darost, and the few churches that have reopened are sparsely attended. Mischief and unrest are often blamed on the Church's men or on members of the Celenka people.

Despite the fertile location, there are plenty of diseases among Darost's people. Some claim it is a remnant from the chemical weapons that Sadovia or the enemy in the north are said to have used against the province—others maintain that House Orlok is culling the weakest workers to reduce the burden on the province. Whatever the case, a large portion of the working population dies before the age of 40. Many try to leave the hard work by either enlisting in the army or fleeing from their employers and seeking their fortune in Sandukar.

### Devastok

Cranes, excavators, digging machines, and thousands of workers are constantly in motion in Devastok, a city expanding along the shores of the Mirror Sea. Harbor areas are built to receive all types of ships, and the railways transport goods that are reloaded to then be transported further into the kingdom.

There was previously another city called Devastok on the Mirror Sea coast, but it and its neighboring cities were all annihilated during a November night when a meteorite struck the frozen Mirror Sea and sent a tremendous tidal wave that brought about the cities' destruction. It took time, but new cities began to be established on the new coast, and the primary one inherited the name Devastok. Few think about the area's past, but on the bottom of the sea there are still remains of the cities and rusty ship hulls. Since the meteorite impact, fishing has also changed—many of the animals have become distorted and unpalatable and can no longer be eaten.

### Liberinsko

Liberinsko is a larger city located inland. Certain parts of the city are abandoned, as a terrible plague broke out there a few years ago and the majority of the city's population perished in terrible pain. New workers are recruited daily, who get to fill the now-sanitized worker housing.

Liberinsko is seen as one of the freer cities where workers have good housing and live under relatively bearable conditions. The State does not have as much control over the city, but House Orlok keeps a close watch on what happens. The House operates the enormous coal power plants east of the city, which supply large parts of the country with electricity.

### The Dome Battlefield

On the rolling Dome Battlefield, the important final battle against the northern enemy's armies took place. Here the united armies of Vadaja, Sadovia, and Darost met the hostile hordes. It became a hard fight that lasted for months as the enemy was slowly but surely driven down toward the Mirror Sea. The area is still filled with overgrown ruined cities, bunkers, and trenches.

### Northern Plainlands

The gray, slightly rolling plainlands seemingly extend endlessly before they meet the kingdom of Vadaja. The acid rain has killed almost all life, and the animals that remain are mad and savage. There are no roads in this wilderness. The Wasteland Railway's lonely steel tracks run through the landscape, but what lies beyond the hills is unknown to most. However, there are places where not all hope has died. Where the grass grows strong and lush and where the trees bear leaves and needles.

### The Coal Fields

In the Coal Fields, valuable brown coal is found just below the muddy ground. Enormous digging machines crawl like insects across the muddy landscape and tear up the coal to the surface with scoops. Everywhere you find the traces of caterpillar tracks, and the coal is transported by smoking trucks or rumbling trains to their destinations.

From the power plants near the Coal Fields rises toxic yellow-green smoke and sweeps the landscape into a fog. The workers here have very short lifespans and are secretly drugged to endure their tasks. They are also equipped with face filters to survive the day at all. Many of the criminals in Darost are sent to labor camps here, under the rule of House Kostash, until their sentences are completed or until they are buried in the muddy ground.

### Iskarian

The Copper River divides Iskarian into a southern and northern part before its poisoned waters spread in the Oil Sea like a plague cloud. The Empire's center, the capital Sandukar, is located on a nameless island at the river's mouth and is a node for humanity's poisoning. On either side of the river mouth are massive forts with enormous steel cannons pointing out to sea, ready to bombard enemy fleets.

The southern parts of Iskarian are virtually uninhabited. They consist of a vast swampland like the neighboring kingdom of Kovan. The northern part is infested with several smaller industrial cities and a network of railways that transport raw materials to Sandukar, the eternally hungry whore. In the few places where fallout has not acidified nature, wealthy industrial magnates have established their estates, usually near one of the many lakes whose muddy bottoms and murky waters can no longer sustain any life.

### History

Once upon a time, Iskarian was a fertile kingdom. The fields were verdant, the lakes and rivers contained abundant fish, and in the forests there was plenty of game. It seemed to be a paradise of nature's abundance. Sea winds brought cool summer rains to the harvests, and in the mountains one could mine precious metals and iron ore. In the coastal cities and the cities along the Copper River, rich trade took place with other kingdoms, and the people were known for their song, their music, and their fiery dances.

Iskarian's soldiers were brave and defended the kingdom against those who tried to plunder its riches—they often managed to stay out of other conflicts. They had close cooperation with Madrostav and borrowed liberally from the high culture in the north, but the kingdoms beyond the river were also explored by Iskarian's traveling merchants. When the ships from the northern enemy appeared on the horizon, the rulers believed that diplomacy could resolve the conflict, but their white flag was met with cannon fire from the armored cruisers.

In desperation, Iskarian's fleet and soldiers tried to hold the river mouth to the Copper River, but their defense was mercilessly ground down and the cannons obliterated the proud coastal cities. The enemy continued up the Copper River and annihilated and plundered wherever they passed. They landed their soldiers who committed cruel deeds.

When the proposal for an alliance between the Houses and the lands south of the mountains came up, Iskarian's leaders were among the first to accept—partly to get rid of the enemy and partly because they saw the power and advantages of unity.

In battle after battle, the enemy's forces were driven from the kingdoms, but they clung tenaciously to Iskarian, and when they finally retreated, the cities were burned-out ruins and the muddy lands covered with corpses. Nothing remained of the proud kingdom except a landmass soaked with blood and destructiveness and the memories of what had once been. These memories came to be reforged into a vision of what would become.

### The Empire

Together, the Empire, now under the State's rule, began to rebuild Iskarian, and it was decided that the capital, Sandukar, would be located on the island at the Copper River's mouth. On the island before the war lay a massive temple city with tall buildings and underground sanctuaries, but the enemy's cannons had transformed it into rubble and ash. On either side of the Copper River's mouth, gigantic forts were built that with their cannons could annihilate the enemy should it ever return. Sandukar was also

### The Fate Cross

In northern Iskarian, near the sea, rises the massive Fate Cross, a construction of rusty steel beams, machine parts and chains. The cross is more than a hundred meters tall and disappears up into the gray-white fog. The workers in the area worship and make offerings to the cross. Even the authorities show it respect. Those who die in labor are usually laid at the cross's feet or hung up in one of the chains.

The cross is said to be able to influence fate and if one believes enough and offers diligently one's prayers may be heard. Likewise the cross is said to be able to proclaim visions for those who seek. This occurs through violent nightmares from which not all awaken.

People from across the realm can sometimes see the cross in their dreams and pilgrimages to its macabre form occur daily.

### The Montroy Quarries

When the black basalt was quarried for Sandukar's walls, towers and foundations, it was from the Montroy quarries the majority of all stone was taken. Nowadays it is desolate wasteland with several large open-pit mines and ponds that make up the landscape. There are plenty of caves and hollows and the quarries have become a place for outlaws and criminals who avoid the State. Now and then state forces are sent in to clear out those who live there but it is nearly impossible to cover all ravines, crevices and hollows. State commissars are said to conduct part of their training in the desolate area.

### Kovan

Kovan is an undulating landscape filled with mist-shrouded marshes, tangled forest patches and cliffs. Several smaller rivers from the Valek mountains run through the ravines to then reach out to the Copper River. The marshlands in the northern parts spread out more and more as a result of rivers being dammed up, and many of the older settlements now lie under water.

The cities have grown up at the rivers' outlets and many are protected by high levees to keep the water away.

In the rocky ravines are countless mines rich in precious metals and on the ravines' slopes rest modern forts that watch over the riches. The land is covered by a gray-white haze that seems to rise from the countless, stinking marshes and virtually all people from the inland have sought out the safe and rapidly growing cities. There are almost no remaining roads and if one must travel one does so by boat, on foot or on horseback.

In the east the marshlands transition into undulating forest terrain and border the breakaway realm Presvedonia and the eastern front. Here are several army camps and in the cities things go downright wild when the soldiers have time off from the battles at the front. Here one can find fortune seekers, prostitutes, battle-weary soldiers and the enemy's agents.

### History

Kovan was once upon a time a rich land whose people conducted trade along the rivers and were known for their skill as seamen and craftsmen. In the undulating inland there was plenty of livestock and the waterways were filled with riches, both gold and noble fish. The people were however moderate and took only what they needed and managed to stay outside most conflicts with the help of diplomatic skill.

The Church came to gain hold of the realm via merchants from Sadovia. Soon it gained through manipulation and foul play total power. It took control over the peoples' industries and gave heavenly grace to those who paid much tax. Massive riches were sent by river boats to Sadovia and gigantic cathedrals were built in the midst of the moderate people's cities.

Soon however doubts began to come, was really the one god's teaching the right one and how could god's greed be allowed to plague the people so? In religion's name thousands died in the mines, greed gained hold of their hearts and soon discontent began to spread. With force of arms Kovan's people annihilated the church, the priests were executed publicly, some had molten gold poured down their throats, others were nailed up on crosses. The churches were burned and their ruins allowed to remain as warnings and reminders.

When religion was driven out the people decided to themselves watch over their riches. The seeds of envy and greed were however already sown and in the following centuries many internal conflicts broke out. The clan that emerged victorious from the bloodbath bore the name Zovrin.

When then the enemy from the north attacked with its massive fleet along the Copper River, Kovan's coastal cities were blown apart by the warships' cannons. The people fled inland and took shelter in ravines and settlements. The enemy never managed to gain foothold in the realm but proceeded further eastward along the river. When the State was formed, the Zovrin clan came to gain great power and also gave name to the currency that is still minted in the realm. Industrialization became increasingly evident and large parts of the countryside were abandoned.

### The Riches

Kovan is a large trading realm, ships run back and forth along the Copper River and the Mirror Sea has several harbors. In the east coal is mined from the porous mountains and the coal then supplies the realm with energy and heat. Along the rivers are masses of mines in the ravines' walls. They are very rich in precious metals, primarily gold and silver but one can also find lead, copper and zinc.

The rivers have plenty of fish, which to some extent constitutes the basic diet, even though it is beginning to become increasingly scarce much thanks to the large overfishing as well as discharges from the mines.

Industry is for the most part focused on steelworks and chemical factories. Here and there one can find abandoned villages and cities where the people were forced to a "better" life in the cities.

Almost all rulers have left the realm and taken themselves to Sandukar. As the wealth leaves the realm the marshlands spread out and the people no longer remember their moderation, their benevolence and culture's origin that lies resting deep beneath stagnant marsh water. People's minds are now bound to machinery and cogwheels and by plundering nature, not living in harmony with it, the people continue their parasitic existence.

### Myta

The stately city Myta lies on a peninsula where the river Khastyr meets the Copper River. It is a place for trade and transshipment. In the massive harbor area warehouse buildings rest beside each other and out on the piers stand rusty cranes. In the city's center is the mint where parts of the state currency are minted. Here the guard is very strict and one must have special certificates and passes to enter the city's inner parts that rise on a mountain ridge and tower up over surrounding districts.

In the harbor area state control is weak and there are plenty of criminal elements, seamen, beggars, fortune seekers, shady bars and prostitutes. The nights in Myta's harbor quarter are widely notorious for their violence and debauchery.

### The Southwestern Marshlands

The southwestern marshlands are an extensive area covered by treacherous swamps, pools and mangrove marshes and border the realm Iskarian. A sickly yellowish-pale fog rests over the region and the stagnant smell causes nausea and sometimes even diseases in throat and eyes. To travel here without a gas mask is dangerous as people have died from the area's poisonous fog banks.

Buildings, machines, vehicles and cadavers of both animals and people stick up from the wetland and create gloomy silhouettes in the fog. From deep below the ground one can sometimes hear the rumble of machines and in the murky water rusty cogwheels still move in unknown tasks. At night one can behold a green-violet phosphorescent glow on the firmament above the marshlands.

The marble floor was cold, rough and inviting, almost soporific. His tremors made the beard hairs sweep rhythmically over the microscopic stone landscape, laboriously built up by a hundred years of madness. He slowly heaved himself up, snorting and drooling. The straitjacket still hung limply around his waist and clumsily hindered his movements. The pen he had written down the so important words with on the back of the Envelope now lay before him—it must have fallen from his grasp when he stumbled. Awkwardly he grasped the pen and stuffed it inside his shirt.
