Some prefer a greater measure of unreasonableness than others — the important thing is that actions feel reasonable in the story that's underway.
**Dice**
Dice are used to produce random numbers which in turn are used to for example answer whether a person succeeds with their action or how much damage an attack does.
In Noir, ten-sided dice are used. On certain types of ten-sided dice the number "0" appears. It should always be interpreted as "ten" which means that the outcome on a die varies from one to ten.
To determine whether a person succeeds or fails with an action, two ten-sided dice are always used. The result of the roll is obtained by adding together the numbers on the rolled dice. Generally it applies to roll as high as possible to increase one's chances of succeeding with the action.
Dice notations are sometimes abbreviated as 2D, where D
means "dice" and the number indicates the number of dice in the roll, in this case two.
Maxed & blocked dice
Dice showing ones or tens in the roll have a special meaning.
For each ten one rolls, one gets to roll another die. This is called the die being maxed. The number on this new die is added to the result. If one has the luck to roll new tens these are also maxed which means one can get very high results in the end. There is no limit to how many maxed dice one can get.
For each one rolled, one prevents a maxing. This is called a block.
**Breakdown**
When two ones are rolled in a situation where objects or equipment are used, and the roll is simultaneously an exceptional failure, the objects/equipment risk suffering breakdown. More information about breakdown can be found on page 92.
Lowest & highest die
In certain cases the individual dice numbers can affect a situation. The terms used for this are lowest, highest and all respectively. Lowest means that it is the lowest number in the roll that is important, highest means that the highest number in
**Glossary**
In Noir, terms and concepts appear that at first glance may seem difficult to handle or extensive. In this section are explanations of such concepts.
ALL: The sum of the rolled value on all dice in a difficulty or resistance roll.
ATTRIBUTE: Secondary informa-tion about a person. Determined often with the help of attribute values.
ATTRIBUTE VALUE: Defines an attribute of a person. Attribute values are often based on Skills.
PENALTY: The opposite of bonus.
If an action is difficult to perform the result of the dice roll for the action can be worsened through penalty.
NARRATOR: The game's director, presenter and rules arbiter.
He/she prepares a larger or smaller situation with the help of a relationship map and a number of bombs, to together with the players create a story. The narrator portrays, describes and interprets what happens in the interaction between players and the game world. In rules disputes the narrator has the final word.
NARRATOR CHARACTERS: Supporting roles, helpers and opponents to the protagonists in a story. They are run by the narrator and live and act in the game world. All persons in a story, except the players'
protagonists, are narrator characters.
STORY: The exciting, dramatic tale the narrator and players create together. A
story consists of a number of scenes which contain places, events, narrator characters, clues and background information that frame the adventures and missions the protagonists encounter.
BONUS: The opposite of penalty.
If an action is easy to perform the result of the dice roll for the action can be improved through bonus.
The lowest die's result is 5.
The highest die's result is 8.
All dice in the roll together give the result 13.
A roll with maxed dice. The result is 30 (7 + 10 + 10 + 3).
Each 10 means that another die is rolled and added to the final result.
The dice roll is exceptional because both dice show the same number.
The result is 12 (6 + 6).
The roll is considered successful if the total sum (the dice roll's result + Skill or Attribute Value) reaches equal to or over 20 (in a difficulty roll)
or is higher than the opponent's (in a resistance roll), otherwise it is failed.
A block with the result 11 (1 + 10). Each 1 in a dice roll blocks a 10, that is, prevents it from being maxed.

PARTICIPANTS: Players and storyteller, that is, those who participate in the roleplaying.
BOMB: An open choice situation where players through their main characters determine how the story and main characters will develop.
Bombs should be formulated so that doing nothing also constitutes taking a stance.
Some bombs are prepared in advance by the storyteller, others arise naturally during the course of play.
DEAD: The person is deceased and is no longer an active part of the story.
A player whose main character dies usually creates a new one who is given space in the story at an appropriate time.
MORTALLY WOUNDED: The person is so severely injured that they will die within one or a couple of minutes. A mortally wounded person can be stabilized by someone with medical knowledge.
ATTRIBUTE: A person's basic aptitude to become skilled in an area.
ATTRIBUTE VALUE:
Indicates a person's maturity/
development for an attribute.
EXPERIENCE: A point value that is given to the main character and which is based on how much they have learned during the course of the journey.
Used to improve attribute values and skill values, acquire expertises, and increase Fate.
EXCEPTIONALLY SUCCESSFUL ROLL: A
successful roll in which the dice also show identical numbers. Grants extra advantages to the action performed.
EXCEPTIONALLY FAILED ROLL:
A failed roll where the dice also show identical numbers. Causes disadvantages to the action performed.
EXPERTISE: Special competence in, or specialization within, a skill.
SKILLS: Everything a person has learned and trained up.
SKILL VALUE: Indicates how much knowledge and/or proficiency the person has in a skill.
DEFENSE SKILLS:
**The collective name for the skills**
Tolerance, Resist and Resilience which are used to withstand physical, social and mental attacks.
DEFENSE ACTION:
Collective name for the actions a person can perform when they are attacked.
roll is important, and all means that the sum of the result on all dice in the roll is important. All possible maxed dice are also counted when reading lowest, highest and all.
Individual results can among other things affect when it comes to determining a certain time, for example "Lasts for the lowest die's result number of minutes", or to get a sum, for example "Strike as many cartridges from the magazine as the value on the highest die".
**Dice Rolls**
When you want to determine whether a desired action succeeds or fails, a dice roll is made. There are two types of dice rolls: difficulty rolls and resistance rolls.
A good general rule for all forms of dice rolls is to always let the rolled dice lie openly on the table so that the result can be read. This is because not only the total value but also the individual dice's numerical values shall be readable and used for various purposes. Sometimes the storyteller may also have hidden bonuses and penalties that are applied to the roll.
When should you use dice rolls?
It's easy to think that you must roll dice in all possible and impossible situations to see if you succeed with what you have set out to do — this can include everything from opening a door to driving a car. This is not the case.
**Dice rolls should be made in the following situations**
• When it is interesting for the scene or story whether the person succeeds or fails
• If the randomness of the roll contributes to creating drama or tension.
• If some form of conflict or challenge exists.
• If a person's health and/or survival is at stake.
Rolls should usually not be made in the following situations
• If the situation is routine, boring or trivial for the person.
• If the randomness would destroy mood and drama.
• If an action's outcome has already been established in the scene, new rolls should not be made for the same thing; if for example a person has succeeded in sneaking past a guard, then they have succeeded and the stealth skill should no longer be questioned in the scene.
**Difficulty Rolls**
Difficulty rolls are made when a person must overcome a fixed difficulty, without direct involvement of any other person.
Driving a car, pushing open a door, climbing over a wall or finding certain information in a book are typical situations where difficulty rolls are made.
Difficulty rolls are always made with two dice (2D) and to this is added a skill's proficiency value or an attribute value. The result is always compared against a fixed difficulty level of 20. If the result equals or exceeds 20, the outcome is counted as successful, if it is below 20 the roll is considered failed.
Which proficiency or attribute value should be used for the roll is stated in parentheses. "Make a difficulty roll
(Manual Dexterity)" means that the person shall roll 2D and add the proficiency value in the skill Manual Dexterity.
If this sum equals or exceeds 20, the action succeeds.
It can happen that a person is so skilled that they reach 20 automatically even before the dice are rolled. This is logical since a very skilled individual does not fail at routine tasks. Rolls can still be made if it is deemed important to check if the outcome becomes exceptional (see below).
**Resistance Rolls**
Resistance rolls are made when two or more people try to win over each other through different forms of influence and conflict. On almost all occasions when a personal skill is directly pitted against another person's skill, resistance rolls are used. Common examples of when resistance rolls are used are during persuasion attempts, gambling, arm wrestling and attacks in combat.
Resistance rolls are always made with two dice (2D) and to this is added a skill's proficiency value or an attribute value. The person who gets the highest result on their roll succeeds in successfully carrying out their intended action. The one who got the lower result therefore fails.
Which proficiency or attribute value should be used for the roll is stated in parentheses. "Make a resistance roll
(Strength)" means that each of those involved shall roll
2D and add the proficiency value in the skill Strength.
The one who gets the highest sum is considered to have succeeded with the roll and the current action.
In cases where two or more parties reach the same sum, the one with the highest proficiency or attribute value wins.
If the value is the same, the one who has the highest value on any of the dice wins. If even this is equal, each party rolls 1D and whoever rolls highest wins over the other
— if equal, the die is rerolled until the result differs.
**Exceptional Rolls**
An exceptional roll results in something special occurring, something extraordinary that affects the person's future in some way. Exceptional rolls can influence either positively or negatively and can occur with both difficulty rolls and resistance rolls.
A roll counts as exceptional if the first two dice in the roll show the same number — any maxed dice don't count.
If a roll counts as successful and is also exceptional, it is called an exceptionally successful roll and something positive occurs beyond the action succeeding.
If a roll counts as failed and is also exceptional, it is called an exceptionally failed roll and something negative occurs beyond the action failing.
Exactly what happens during an exceptional roll depends on what the roll concerns; generally it is up to the storyteller and player to dramatically and engagingly describe an event that is reasonable in the scene. In combat it is defined that it is the person's initiative that is affected, which is described for each combat action.
Example: Anya Malov is on her way home through the rain when she hears the sound of an engine behind her. She turns around and sees how the headlight beam from a sports car approaches at high speed. Her

only choice is to throw herself aside. A difficulty roll (Reactions)
is made and the dice show 9 + 9, which together with Anya's skill gives a result far over 20 — an exceptionally successful roll. With a normally successful roll, Anya would simply have suc-ceeded in avoiding being run over, but since the roll is exceptionally successful she doesn't just jump aside but performs an elegant somersault, comes up on her feet with perfect balance and gets a chance to read the license plate on the fleeing car.
Example: Jessca Navarre hangs in a rope outside a skyscraper in the central parts of Sandukar. She has just cut open a window and is about to enter the building when fate strikes and her flashlight gives out. Since she finds herself in a chal-lenging situation, her player must make a difficulty roll (Dexter-ity) for her to have a chance to replace the batteries.
The roll shows 2 + 2 which together with the skill value in
Dexterity doesn't reach over 20 — the roll becomes exceptionally un-successful. Rather than simply failing to get the new bat-teries in, Jessca drops both flashlight and batteries, which silently fall toward the wet pavement far below.
**Complex Actions**
When an action is so extensive and/or time-con-suming that more than a simple difficulty roll is required to determine success or failure, the action is considered complex. This could for example involve picking a door or a safe, searching through an archive for informa-tion or studying academic research material.
**Performing a Complex Action**
The narrator determines how much time each attempt to perform the complex action shall take and then specifies the chal-lenge level. The sum from each successful difficulty roll is added together and when you reach the challenge level you have completed the action.
Determine a time for each difficulty roll, for example "one roll per minute". Examples of times are given in table 1:34. Each roll represents an attempt during which a certain time pas-ses — a roll might be made every minute or half hour. A
failed roll doesn't mean the entire action fails but rather that it takes longer to perform it, since the failed attempt has taken time.
**Time  Example**
Very fast (one roll per round) Pick a lock, hotwire a car
Fast (one roll per minute) Disarm a bomb, repair a simple machine
Short (one roll per ten minutes) Build a timed bomb, set up a camp in the wilderness
Long (one roll per hour) Search for information in an archive/library, crack a cipher
Very long (one roll per day) Repair a car wreck, compose a piece of music
Extremely long (one roll per week) Translate a book, build a bridge
Table 1:34
Once the time is set, the complex action's challenge level is determined, that is, the sum one must at least reach for the complex action to be considered successfully completed. The challenge level is determined by the complex action's difficulty, according to table 1:35.
**Difficulty Challenge Level Example**
Simple 50 Pick a padlock
Ordinary 100 Pick a door lock
Troublesome 150 Pick a security lock
Significant 200 Pick a safe
Table 1:35
**Outcome**
The outcome of difficulty rolls is calculated differently depending on the roll's suc-cess when it comes to complex actions.
Exceptionally successful: The roll's sum counts double.
Successful: The roll's sum counts.
Unsuccessful: The roll's sum doesn't count.
Exceptionally unsuccessful: The person gives up the com-plex action entirely.
Example: Gabriel is going to pick the rusty lock on a worn back door. He has skill 10 in Dexterity and an expertise in lockpicking that gives him a bonus (+2). Picking the door is considered a complex action.
The time is set to Very fast (one difficulty roll per round) and the difficulty to Simple (challenge level 50).
In the first round, the dice in the difficulty roll show 4 +
4, which gives the sum 20 (10 + 2 + 4 + 4) — an exceptionally successful result. The total sum increases by 40 (20 + 20) so Gab-riel is close to getting the lock open at once. In the next round, a new difficulty roll is made, the dice show 2 + 3, sum 17 — a failure. The total sum is still 40 and the round's six seconds are wasted. The narrator informs that Gabriel hears footsteps further away in the dark alley and that he should expect company in about two rounds so he better hurry!
The following round's difficulty roll is made and the dice show
4 + 7, which gives sum 23, a successful result. The total sum reaches 63 (40 + 23), which is higher than the challenge level of 50 so the picking is successfully completed.
Quick as lightning, Gabriel gets the door open, sneaks in and closes it behind him while he hears the footsteps pass by outside.
**Hidden Rolls**
Generally, there are no problems letting the players make the rolls that concern their protagonists openly. In certain situations it may be that the narrator wants to make rolls for the protago-nists hidden. This could involve events when the players shouldn't get the forewarning that a dice roll implies, for exam-ple if some unknown narrator character is spying on the protag-onists or discovering a cigarette butt with lipstick on it under a bed.
**The Narrator's Rolls**
In most cases the narrator can choose whether his/her rolls should be hidden or open. Many narrators prefer to make all their rolls hidden and focus on the drama and describe the outcome directly rather than let all participants share in the dice roll. At especially dramatic climaxes in a scene where for example a protagonist's life hangs in the balance with a dice roll, it can however become very exciting to make the roll openly.
DEFENSELESS: A person who is not able to defend themselves and who can be subjected to two specific ac-tions — knocked out or executed.
MALFUNCTION: Means that an object stops working. Often occurs if an action fails exceptionally and the dice show low numbers.
PROTAGONIST: The heroes in a story are called protagonists and each protagonist is controlled by a player.
The protagonist is created according to the game's rules and receives different values and characteristics which determine his/her competence and skill in the game world. Once the game session has begun, the protagonist develops in different ways through the choices the player makes.
HIGHEST VALUE: The number on the die in a difficulty or resis-tance roll that shows the highest value.
INITIATIVE: Indicates how composed, coolly and effectively a person acts in combat. The higher the value, the more effective. Also determines the person's turn order in combat.
INITIATIVE ACTION: Collec-tive term for the actions a person can actively perform in combat.
CATATONIC: The term for the lowest level of Wellbeing.
COMPLEX ACTION: A series of difficulty rolls made at regular intervals. When the total sum of the difficulty rolls reaches a determined challenge level, the action is considered successfully completed.
CHRONICLE: A series of stories that are connected, but which usually have concluded parts. Often appear-ing in previously experienced sto-ries are established narrator characters, incidents or other connections in the different parts, but in completely different situations. An example of how the stories can be connected is through the protagonists being given the task of locating three missing persons — when each individual person is found, it's seen as a concluded story, but the chronicle isn't finished until all three persons have been found.
COME TO: A dazed person must come to in order to again act and perform actions.
LIFE EXPERIENCE: The accu-mulated amount of experience a person has gathered in their life, over the course of different stories.

SUCCESSFUL ROLL: When the result of a roll is sufficient to overcome an obstacle or succeed at a challenge, the roll is considered successful.

LOWEST VALUE: The number on the die in a difficulty or opposed roll that shows the lowest value.

MAXED DICE: Each die showing a ten allows an additional die to be rolled and added to the final total. See also locks.

UNCONSCIOUS: An unconscious per-son may not take an active part in the story until he/she has come to.

FAILED ROLL: If the result of a roll is insufficient to over-come the obstacle or succeed at the challenge, the roll is considered failed.

OPPOSED ROLL: Used in situa-tions where two or more people are trying to best each other in some way. Participants roll two dice
(2D) each and add their skill or some other appropriate value.
The one with the highest result wins and the roll is considered successful. The opponent's roll is considered failed.

STUNNED: The person has taken so much of a beating that he/she is so knocked out, confused, or in so much pain that he/she can no longer act independently. A stunned person must come to before he/she may perform any further action.

RELATIONSHIP MAP: Helps the narrator keep track of their narrator characters and how they react to the protagonists'
actions. Both narrator characters and protagonists appear on the relationship map, with the various relationships that bind them together.

SCENE: A building block in a sto-ry. Usually contains a clear element, for example the meeting with an important person, the flight from villains, the bank robbery, et-cetera. Lasts anywhere from a few minutes to days or weeks in game time.

SKILL: The sum of an attribute value and an ability value. Indicates a person's total knowledge in an ability. It is the skill value in an ability that is added when dice rolls are made.

ROLL: A dice throw — ei-ther a difficulty roll or an opposed roll. Can also refer to the outcome of a dice throw.

Bonuses & Penalties

A roll can be affected in two ways. One is through bonuses, the other through penalties. Bonuses are added to a result while penalties are subtracted from it. A roll can be affected by both bonuses and penalties at the same time. A bonus is written in text with a plus sign and a number in parentheses, for exam-ple (+5). A penalty is written in text with a minus sign and a number in parentheses, for example (−2).

A general list of appropriate bonuses and penalties base-d on the action's difficulty is given in table 1:36. The table should be seen as an aid — nothing prevents narrators from using values other than those suggested.

When an action or task is deemed routine, it is automatically considered successful. Examples of this could be that an experienced taxi driver doesn't need to strain themselves to find the city's best hotel and a well-trained athlete automatically manages to do thirty push-ups.

Bonus/
Penalty Complexity Example
+10 Very easy Passing a car on a deserted country road
+5 Easy With a sports car, passing a truck with oncoming traffic
+2 Fairly easy Driving through a busy intersection
±0 Challenging Driving fast through busy streets in pouring rain
−2 Fairly difficult Making repeated overtakes at high speed in rush hour traffic
−5 Difficult At high speed avoiding a motorcycle that suddenly drives out in front of your car
−10 Very difficult At high speed on an icy road turning ninety degrees with the car into a narrow alley
−20 Unreasonable With a motorcycle running a truck off the road
−30 Hopeless While maintaining control at high speed skidding 180 degrees in a truck with trailer and punctures on all wheels, and then driving on

Table 1:36

### Collaboration

Multiple people can collaborate when a roll is to be made, if the task is such that more participants improve the chance of success. Examples of actions where it is advantageous to have multiple people are when trying to break down a door, pull a person up on a rope, disarm a complicated explosive charge or search for information in books. A typical situation where it doesn't help to have multiple people is when driving.

Each person who helps with an action, and who is considered qualified within the action's area, gives the person who will make the roll (usually the person who has the highest skill in the relevant ability) a bonus (+2).
The narrator must determine how many people can reasonably help at the same time — even if there are twenty people available, not all can break down the blocked door simultaneously.

### Actions

The term action is used in all cases where a person wants to attempt to do something. An action can be more or less challenging, but some form of reasonable risk of failure must exist. Actions' outcomes are determined via the result of either an opposed roll or one or more difficulty rolls.

Each protagonist has 9 attributes and 27 abilities.
It is usually with the help of skill in an ability that a roll is made. In some cases rolls are made with the help of attribute values.
In most cases it is obvious which skill or att-ribute value is most appropriate. If it is unclear, the narra-tor and player discuss their way to what applies.

Noir's system is not built for interaction between abilities and/or attributes. All rolls are made with the help of one value
— either a skill value or an attribute value. In spe-cial cases multiple rolls with the help of different skill and/
or attribute values may be needed in sequence to determine a result.

The players and/or narrator may also determine that personal influence can help or hinder an action
— character traits, motivations and other things can influence the action's result positively or negatively.

### Defense Abilities

The abilities Resist, Resilience and Tolerance constitute a person's defense against social, mental and physical attacks respectively. When opposed rolls to withstand influence are made, the victim must add the value in the appropriate defense ability to the result.

Resist is used as defense in social conflicts, for example seduction, interrogation or persuasion attempts. Resil-ience is used as defense against horror experiences, stress or violations. Tolerance is used as defense against adverse climate, drug influence or poisoning.

### Perception Abilities

The abilities Awareness, Intuition and Empathy are typical perception abilities — how well a person perceives things in their surroundings. Awareness is the five common senses
— sight, hearing, touch, taste and smell. Intuition is the sixth sense, a subconscious sense that interprets information intuitively and can provide clues or premonitions about what is to come. Empathy opens a person's emotional channel so that he/she can sense and read other people's general mood — for example sadness or anger.

Often the narrator makes rolls with the protagonists' percep-tion abilities secretly, to avoid unnecessarily giving players insight that they might be missing something.

### Luck

The attribute Luck shows a person's unconscious flow in existence and general luckiness in situations where skill is sub-ordinate. Luck can be used in all situations where chance determines an outcome, without direct influence from a person. For exam-ple, Luck is used in combat in the defensive action Chance De-fense. In a situation where a group of protagonists risk being hit by a falling flowerpot, it is the one who gets the lowest result on their opposed roll (Luck) who gets the pot on the head. Generally Luck is used when the person is not aware of the situation and cannot influence its outcome. Otherwise it is usually more appropriate to use an ability.

Breaking free from rope/straitjacket Mobility

**Dancing Mobility**
**Deciphering Deduction**
**Disarming a bomb Construction**
**Drawing a throwing weapon Throwing Weapons**
**Drawing a melee weapon Melee**
**Drawing a firearm Firearms**
**Drug knowledge Medicine**
**Picking a lock Sleight of Hand**
**Economics Humanities**
**Stubbornness Determination**
**Finding the right person in the city Worldliness**
**Flying an aircraft Vehicles**
**Photography Create**
**Physics Natural Science**
**Forging Create**
**Writing texts Create**
Tailing a person/vehicle Awareness
**Seducing a person Charm**
**Negotiating Convince**
**Interrogating a person Convince**
**Disguising oneself Create**
Faking/imitating voice/mannerisms Charm
**Defending against physical attacks Defense**
**Seeing through lies Resist**
**Hiding Awareness**
Hiding/planting objects on a person Sleight of Hand
Hiding/planting objects at a location Awareness
**Keeping track of the situation Worldliness**
Handling/identifying firearms Firearms
**Gambling Worldliness**
**History Humanities**
**Jumping Mobility**
Threatening a person Strength, Convince or Leadership
**Interviewing a person Convince**
**Law Humanities**
Throwing objects/throwing weapons Throwing Weapons
**Diving for cover Reactions**
**Chemistry Natural Science**
**Handling chaotic situations Composure**
Handling disgusting/repulsive experiences Resilience
**Climbing Mobility**

SOCIAL CONFLICT: When a protagonist and a narrator charac-ter want to achieve different goals through a conversation or discussion.
Social conflicts often have an active and passive party and can escalate if a participant so desires, often at some cost.

PLAYER: Participates in the game via their own protagonist who is their connection to the game world and the story.

GAME SESSION: The term for a game meeting, that is, the real time that passes between game start and game stop. During a game ses-sion one or more scenes unfold and sometimes even multiple stories, depending on the game session's length.

GAME TIME: The time that passes in the story. It can pass at different paces and has no connection to the time that passes in real-ity during the game session.

GAME WORLD: The world where sto-ries in Noir take place, usually in the dark city of Sandukar, but also in other places in the Imperium.

LOCK: Each die showing a one prevents a ten from being rolled again, see maxed dice.

STABILIZE: A medically skilled person can stabilize a mortally wounded person so that they survive.

COMBAT ACTION: Action per-formed during combat; either an initia-tive action or a defense action.

DIFFICULTY ROLL: Used when a person tries to overcome a passive difficulty for example climbing up a steep slope or picking a lock. Two dice
(2D) are rolled and the person adds their skill or some other appropriate value. If the sum is equal to or over 20, the roll is successful. If it is under 20, the roll is failed.

D: Same as die.

TURN ORDER: The order in which people act in combat.

DIE: A ten-sided die with values 1–10 (if the value 0 is represented on the die it should be interpreted as 10). Two dice are used when dice rolls are made.

DICE ROLL: Can be either difficulty or opposed roll.

CHALLENGE LEVEL: The total sum that difficulty rolls must reach for a complex action to be considered successfully performed.

Social Interaction,
Reputation & Relationships
To survive in a cold and harsh world, it's important to have contacts and friends you can turn to for help. Otherwise, you often learn the hard way that alone is weak.

### Social Interaction

The interaction between player characters and narrator characters is a vital part of any roleplaying story. Many scenes consist of various forms of dialogue, whether it be persuasion, threats, or perhaps seduction. These are collectively called social conflicts as they involve two wills struggling to achieve their respective goals.

This section describes the procedures used in social conflicts between player characters, and between player characters and narrator characters. The rules are designed to create drama and to give player characters who are skilled at the social game the advantages they deserve.

### Conducting Social Situations

There are essentially three different ways to act out social interaction, and none is really more right or wrong - different gaming groups prefer different methods.

The first method involves no dice being used at all; all dialogue takes place between the players and/or the narrator - in other words, what a participant says becomes what their player character or narrator character says in the story. This method takes very little notice of the character's numerical values, and the role is replaced, so to speak, by the person behind the character and their social and verbal competence.

The second method is to roll dice for appropriate skills - whoever wins succeeds at what they set out to do and the scene is over. This is how most actions in Noir work, from combat and driving to picking locks or fixing a broken engine, so why shouldn't Persuade and Charm work the same way? However, this method is far from ideal. Social interaction is important in roleplaying - the form of roleplaying is made for conversation and for the entertainment, joy, and drama it brings when participants together build atmospheres, crystallize characters, and move the story forward.

What remains then is the third method: combining methods one and two. This is the approach Noir advocates and that the rules best support. In this case, the social conflict begins with

the players delivering the arguments their player characters would have presented in the story, and the narrator does the same with their narrator characters. When it feels appropriate, resistance rolls are made to determine who gains the upper hand in the conflict.

If one or more parties have succeeded in providing good, exciting, sharp arguments during the opening, this should be rewarded with a bonus to the roll. Factors such as character traits and/or motivations can also have an effect, and naturally any relationship between the parties should also be factored into the whole.

### The Social Conflict

The purpose of social conflicts is that they are indeed conflicts to be overcome. A conflict can vary in scope from small to large, from personal to universal. Almost all social situations in Noir can be seen as social conflicts.

### Parties

In every social conflict there is an active and a passive party. The active party uses some appropriate skill - usually Charm or Persuade - to achieve their goal, see table 1:38. The passive party, that is, the one being targeted, usually uses the skill Resist. In situations where violence, or the threat of violence, is involved, the passive party can use the skill Resilience instead.

If there is any doubt about who is active and who is passive in a social conflict, the rule of thumb is that whoever has the highest attribute value in Impression is the active party.

In the rare situations where there is no passive party - this occurs primarily in pure debate situations - both parties roll with skills as if active.

### Selection of

Social Actions | Skill
Act/perform | Charm
Dupe/manipulate | Charm
Seduce/captivate | Charm
**Negotiate | Persuade**
**Interrogate | Persuade**
Threaten/intimidate/harass | Persuade*
**Interview | Persuade**
**Haggle over price | Persuade**
Mingle/converse | Charm
**Bribe | Persuade**
**Sing | Charm**
Flatter/fawn | Charm
Entertain/amuse | Charm
Convince/plead | Persuade

Table 1:38 * With the right expertise, Strength or Leadership can also be used.

### The Stakes

In certain social conflicts it seems natural that the active party must choose what the goal of the conflict is. Or rather, what happens if the goal is achieved. This is called the stakes. In the same way, the passive party should propose what the effect will be if the active party fails to gain the upper hand and win the conflict. Both stakes should be roughly equally serious - so that an attempt to win big means that a failure also becomes significant.

For example, a failed negotiation at a critical stage might mean the active party is ostracized or dismissed, successful mingling grants an audience with the countess while a failure means dishonor upon the character and their family, and attempting to sing high C can mean anything from fantastic to catastrophic reviews in the culture sections the next day.

Example: Gabriel and Mercurian are both in love with a mysterious femme fatale named Yvonette. Gabriel wants to get Mercurian to back off and not interfere with his continued courtship of Yvonette. The stakes for Gabriel are proposed to be "get Mercurian to stop loving Yvonette." In the discussion between players, however, the stakes are judged to be skewed - it's not something a social conflict can decide, but something deeper. Instead, the stakes are redefined to the clearer "get Mercurian to stay away from Yvonette for now."

The effect if Gabriel loses becomes "Gabriel becomes uncertain of himself regarding his chances with Yvonette compared to Mercurian's." Exactly what that would mean can be judged in future scenes featuring the three involved - perhaps Gabriel gets a penalty on the courtship, or maybe Mercurian can play out the discussion they had to make Gabriel angry, broken, or sad.

### Social Conflicts Between Player Characters

Player characters can also be active and passive in a social conflict. This means that one player character can influence another according to the rules above. A player has no general veto, but if an influence goes strongly against a character's personality, the resistance roll should be modified with bonuses or penalties to symbolize this.

"You don't seem too smart. I like that quality in a man."
"What do you like more? Laziness? Ugliness? Horniness? I possess them all."
"I don't think you seem lazy."
— Stelia Lock and Mordekai Remus

### When Rolls Should Be Made

It should be emphasized that rolls should only be made if there is a conflict where opposing wills are struggling for the upper hand. Does Androv succeed in convincing **Raceu to abandon the idea of revenge? Does Teresia succeed in charming Markeriu into revealing the code to the safe? Does Captain Merkel succeed in getting the broken soldier on his feet through his morale-raising speech about patriotism and comradeship?**

If all parties in a scene are in agreement, no resistance rolls need to be made at all - if Dorian wants to have sex with Julianique, then Julianique doesn't need to exert herself to succeed in seducing him.

### Resolution

Whoever gets the highest result on the resistance roll stands as the victor in the conflict. If the one who didn't want to be persuaded (the passive party) wins in an argument about whether Sayah Bonn is the world's most beautiful woman, it means the arguments didn't bite and the active party must accept that they were wrong. If the one who initiated the conflict (the active party) wins, this means the opponent accepts and agrees that Bonn is indeed the world's most beautiful woman.

Note that a victory doesn't necessarily mean the loser changes their attitude about what the conflict concerned, but only that the winner's arguments trumped the loser's.

A win in a social conflict is lasting for at least the current scene and at most until further notice, depending on the situation and the stakes - people are by nature changeable and can change their minds at the most inopportune times.

A social conflict can escalate, see below, if the losing party doesn't accept the outcome.

If a party during a social conflict gets an exceptional result on the resistance roll, the character gets either a bonus (+5) or a penalty (-5) on all further rolls in the same social conflict - this is important during escalation.

### Escalation

The losing party in a social conflict has two alternatives. The first, and perhaps most common, is to accept the defeat and act according to the winner's goal, based on any stakes. The second is to escalate the situation in hopes of a changed outcome.

Every social conflict has four levels of severity: Conversation, Quarrel/squabble, Brawl/fistfight, and Deadly violence.

Conversation: The parties discuss in normal tones and present their positions, wishes, or wills with respectful and logical arguments.

Quarrel/squabble: The situation is harsher in tone. Loud exchanges of opinion, crude insults, hurtful personal attacks, verbal blows below the belt, shoves, slaps, or blustering gestures close to the opponent's person are included.

Brawl/fistfight: The situation degenerates and the discussion turns into non-lethal violence - such as wild fistfight, sneaky cheap shots, throws and holds, chaotic wrestling match, or storming close combat with all manner of improvised weapons. However, the intent is rather to defeat the opponent than to kill them.

Deadly violence: A raging fight to the death breaks out. The parties don't hesitate to attack each other with merciless violence, with everything from melee weapons to firearms, with the intent to kill. Uninhibited combat is a fact.

Whoever is active in a social conflict determines which escalation level the whole thing starts at - either Conversation or Quarrel/squabble. When the loser decides not to accept but to escalate the conflict, they can go to whichever escalation level is desired, as long as it's more serious than the current one. Then the winner can choose to follow to the new level or abstain and hand over the victory to the loser.

To my admirer Adrian Niemans, thank you for your support and for the beautiful flowers. You know how to make a film star happy.

If the victor follows to the new level, a new opposed roll between the parties shall be made and the loser gets to decide whether to either accept or escalate the conflict further, as far as is possible. Which abilities are used for the roll are judged case by case depending on circumstances and situation.

At the levels Brawl/fight and Lethal violence, a party can at any time choose to give victory to their opponent by giving up and accepting defeat.

The point of the escalation rules is to give the player good opportunities to participate in the conflict and steer it forward if desired. Most often escalation means that some form of cost arises in the form of enmity, lost face, missed opportunity, visit to jail, or in the worst case, that someone dies. Sometimes it can be considered worth paying that cost to win a conflict.

Example: Mordekai has picked up the scent of one of those responsible for his beloved's death. The person is at the opera when the rain-soaked Mordekai throws open the door to the foyer. At the door is a snooty opera guard who informs Mordekai that he must wait until intermission as no admittance is allowed after the performance has begun.

This is a typical social conflict, where Mordekai as the active party tries to persuade the guard to let him in. Mordekai sets the level to "Conversation" as he doesn't want to brawl openly and draw attention from the other opera employees. The opposed rolls are made with Convince versus Resist and the guard gets a higher result than Mordekai.

Mordekai can now choose to accept not being let in, or he can escalate the conflict — which he does! He pulls it straight up to "Brawl/fight" and simply decides he wants to knock down the opera guard, who chooses to follow along in the escalation. This means the social conflict becomes a fight where Mordekai fairly quickly gains the upper hand and wins. The guard, as loser, chooses to accept without escalating to the final level: "Lethal violence".

The cost in the given situation is that other staff have contacted the police to remove Mordekai from the opera.

### Special social situations

Certain forms of social conflicts require a more detailed explanation. See below.

### Interrogate

To interrogate someone means that through psychological attrition, frightening threats, fair promises or with brutal violence one attempts to force information out of someone. The one conducting the interrogation is always active and the one being interrogated is passive. Each opposed roll is calculated to take approximately one hour to complete.

If the active party fails, their Wellbeing decreases (−1) due to frustration. If the active party succeeds, the passive party suffers stress corresponding to a shocking experience, or, if much violence, threats and torture have occurred, a psychological trauma. When the passive party's Wellbeing reaches Tormented (2) or lower, the passive party's defenses break and the active party learns all reasonable information they requested.

The active party can affect their roll. By using torture they get a bonus (+5). If the active party possesses information that affects the passive party on a personal level — for example real threats against family/relatives, threats of revealing something the interrogated doesn't want to come out, or similar — they get a bonus (+5). Each doubling of interrogation time gives a bonus (+2), up to a maximum of (+10).

### Haggle over price

When a buyer tries to haggle down the price of goods, an opposed roll is made as usual for a social conflict — the buyer is always active and the seller always passive.

If the buyer succeeds, the price is lowered by five percentage points times the number on the lowest die in the roll. If the buyer fails, the price is not adjusted. When haggling, usually no direct stake is made, as it is considered a universal phenomenon in Sandukar and the Empire. Those who don't try to haggle are viewed with skepticism. This also means the seller usually sets a higher price on their goods than what they expect to actually get.

### Threaten

By acting threateningly, a person can frighten another person into doing things they don't really want to. The one making the threat is always active while the target is passive. If the opposed roll succeeds, the passive party is exposed to stress, corresponding to a shocking experience, which is described on page 244.

The relationship automatically worsens one step when threats are used, regardless of whether the conflict is escalated or not.

### Inspirational speech

A leader can make a mentally worn subordinate, soldier or henchman feel better through an inspirational speech. The one speaking is always active while the target is passive. If the opposed roll succeeds, the passive party's Wellbeing improves one level, if it fails it worsens one level. The passive party's Wellbeing may at best be Gloomy (6) for the speech to have any effect.

At most one attempt may be made per game session and person.

### Bribe

By bribing a person with money, objects or advantages, a person can reach set goals more easily or escape tragic situations. The one bribing is always active and the recipient of the bribe is passive. If the opposed roll succeeds, the passive party is bribed and performs what the active party requests. The relationship automatically worsens one step when bribes are used, as respect is eroded. If the same person is bribed multiple times, the relationship is not lowered again however.

Generally a bribe should correspond to at least half a monthly salary (for the passive party) for minor activities, and one or more monthly salaries for more serious ones. Situation and circumstances — such as the consequences the passive party perceives they could suffer if the activity is discovered or how great the risk is of being caught — must determine how large the bribe should be. If too low sums are offered, the active party gets a penalty, while higher amounts provide a bonus. In the Empire everyone is corruptible if the sum is right.

### The importance of game flow

Defining the stakes — what happens both with a successful and with a failed result — before the dice are actually rolled can be double-edged. At best it leads to everyone participating in the conflict knowing what's at stake, accepting it and moving forward with what they intended to do.

On the other hand, this defining can initiate a long discussion about what is reasonable and what is not, which in turn leads to players wanting to revise the outcome at for example a success in order to thereby change the result of a possible failure. Such discussions can become very drawn out and destroy the flow of the game and the total experience for everyone involved.

**To avoid unnecessary** game interruptions, the narrator can introduce penalties to the opposed roll for each time a player changes their mind. This can be explained in the story by the protagonist seeming nervous or otherwise uncertain about their case and their arguments. Another tool for the game master to maintain game flow is to only let the players define what they want to happen with a successful outcome and only when the dice are rolled explain what happens with a possible failure.

### Seduce

"If you want someone to love you, open your heart. If you want someone to become obsessed with you, close it."
— Talia Navarre

Seduction is the art of enchanting someone to do something for you. Generally the relationship worsens one step if the opposed roll fails for the active party.

One of the most common stakes is that the active party simply tries to get the passive party in bed, to have sex or for some other reason.

Another common stake is to create an attraction. This means the active party tries to get the victim interested, lured, focused and tempted, through promises, hints and flirtation. This is done through an opposed roll (Charm). If the active party succeeds, they get until further notice a general bonus (+2) on all rolls in such social interactions with the victim where it is judged that the attraction can facilitate. Note that it can be completely non-sexual interactions — once the attraction is awakened, it is.

The active party can try to strengthen an attraction through another successful opposed roll so it becomes a passion. If this succeeds, the bonus becomes (+5) instead.

Both attraction and passion are noted in the character sheet, next to the relationship.

Note that the seduction doesn't need to be of a sexual nature at all — a leader who captivates underlings or an artist who succeeds in charming an admirer follow the same rules.

An attraction or passion needs to be maintained to persist — if the active party ignores or humiliates the victim too much, a passion can drop to become an attraction and an attraction can cease entirely. Often this results in hurt feelings and bitterness on the victim's part.

As usual, if a victim is willing, no rolls need to be made at all. The same applies if the victim lacks capacity to be seduced, for example if a man tries to pick up a homosexual woman, if a woman wants to have sex with an impotent man, etcetera. However, it is entirely possible for attraction or passion to arise even if an anything but benign relationship exists between two individuals — even enemies can be attracted to each other.

### Notoriety

Notoriety shows a person's fame and celebrity status. The normal state is for a person to be Unknown (Notoriety level five). If Notoriety increases to a level above five, it means the person has been noticed for events or statements that by common people are perceived as exemplary, praiseworthy and good. If Notoriety instead decreases to a level below five, then the public's perception is rather that the person is villainous, threatening and evil.

All notoriety levels except Unknown (5) have a modification attached to them, see table 1:39. This modification can be used by the narrator as a bonus or penalty in situations where the person's reputation is judged to be able to affect an action's outcome. When for example people with good reputation speak warmly for a cause, they influence those who listen, and when a person with bad reputation threatens someone it has greater effect as the reputation has preceded the person.

The opposite also applies – a person with a good reputation who acts threateningly might not be taken as seriously, and then the modification might instead give a penalty in the situation.

Reputation Bonus/penalty Spheres of influence
Lauded (10) ±10 5
Honored (9) ±5 4
Respected (8) ±3 3
Known (7) ±2 2
Mentioned (6) ±1 1
Unknown (5) ±0 –
Notorious (4) ±1 1
Infamous (3) ±2 2
Scum (2) ±3 3
Monster (1) ±5 4
Abhorred (0) ±10 5
Table 1:39

### Spheres of influence

Each time a person reaches greater Reputation it must be specified to a sphere of influence or a grouping. The sphere of influence shows where the person is known and where their Reputation can affect and be used. Examples of spheres of influence are The academic world, The general public, Children, Crime syndicates, Bureaucrats, Refugees, The street, Club life, Criminals, The church, Resistance groups, The military, The entertainment industry, The police, The press, The justice system, High society, The sports world, The state, Houses, etcetera.

If a person's reputation decreases this means that a previously established sphere of influence disappears – cross out the one that makes most sense.

Example: Sayah Bonn is a famous film star. Her reputation is Respected (8). The three spheres of influence she has are: The general public, The entertainment industry and The press. This means that she has a bonus or a penalty (±3) within these spheres in situations where her reputation could be thought to improve or worsen her situation.

### Recognition

People with Reputation other than Unknown (5) must be recognized before their modification can be used. To be recognized the observer must succeed at a difficulty roll (Worldliness), with the target's modification from their Reputation as a bonus. A person is automatically recognized within their spheres of influence.

### How reputation changes

There are no concrete rules for how a person's Reputation changes. The guidelines are that a person who does not actively work to increase or maintain their reputation tends to lose it after a time. In the same way sudden upheavals can result in large jumps in Reputation – if for example the respected philanthropist is exposed in the press as a sadistic pedophile their Reputation will plummet, and their spheres of influence will change.

Relationships & Contacts
A protagonist has a defined relationship with all narrator characters he/she knows. Each such narrator character also has an influence that shows his/her power within the field he/she operates in.

When a protagonist is created relationships are established, see page 29. During the course of a story the player can create new relationships themselves by expending Fate to gain contacts (see page 240). Even narrator characters who appear in a story can be entered as relationships if the situation encourages this.

Usually relationships have no other values than Influence/field and Relationship which are described in this section. Should a relationship/contact need to be filled out this is done according to the rules that exist for narrator characters on page 330.

### Impression

Impression shows how a person is perceived and received by others at first meeting. Each time a protagonist enters a social conflict with a new narrator character a difficulty roll (Impression) must be made to see how the protagonist is perceived by the narrator character. The result gives the relationship the protagonist has with the narrator character during the social conflict and until further notice.

Note that even if several protagonists are part of a group it is the one who primarily acts in the social conflict who creates the relationship – the other protagonists do not get a relationship with the narrator character.

Exceptionally successful roll: The relationship is set to Benevolent.
Successful roll: The relationship is set to Neutral.
Failed roll: The relationship is set to Disliking.
Exceptionally failed roll: The relationship is set to Hostile.

The difficulty roll can be modified depending on whether the narrator character has any preconceived opinions that could affect it. A person who for example hates police and who is interrogated by a constable gives a penalty to the constable's difficulty roll. Other things to consider here could be factors such as racism, rivalry, possible threats, prejudices, previous experiences, or general behavior by the person before the difficulty roll (Impression) is made.

A narrator character never makes an Impression roll to create a relationship with a protagonist – the protagonist's player decides for themselves how he/she perceives the narrator character based on the narrator's portrayal.

### Thoughts on seduction

The reason there are special rules for seduction and attraction is that the classic noir ingredient of temptation/
desire for the unattainable should be emphasized. A man who seeks to win the cool woman who has enchanted him with her icy charm and intensity, the woman who can manipulate and tempt him to anything, cannot resist his lust despite the risks it brings.

"I once knew a woman who looked like you."
"Really?"
"She's dead now."
"How sad, what happened?"
"I killed her."
"Why?"
"Otherwise she would have killed me."
"You seem to have bad luck with women."
– Mordekai Remus and Stelia Lock

### Relationship

A person is rarely alone in the darkness of the city. Turning to others and sharing joy, experiences and companionship is a way to resist the heavy and inhumane environment the city represents. In the same way previous meetings may have resulted in conflicts, quarrels and hatred. This is shown through the relationship a narrator character has with a protagonist.

The five levels of relationships are: Friendship, Benevolent, Neutral, Disliking and Hostile.

A relationship's nature generally controls the protagonist's ability to influence the narrator character in social conflicts. It gives bonuses or penalties to the protagonist's rolls (regardless of whether he/she is active or passive) according to table 1:40.

Relationship Bonus/penalty
Friendship +5
Benevolent +2
Neutral ±0
Disliking –2
Hostile –5
Table 1:40

### Friendship

In Noir's world unconditional friendship is something rare. It means that the narrator character has a deep emotional bond with the protagonist and supports almost everything he/she needs help with, even if it is risky or dangerous. This level of relationship usually means that the narrator character loves or is deeply in love with the protagonist, or that they are very close friends since long ago.

### Benevolent

The narrator character is benevolently disposed toward the protagonist and can consider helping out with time, information or material help without major demands for compensation. To get the narrator character to help with something that entails danger to his/her life or that constitutes a great risk however requires very good reasons or proper encouragement.

### Neutral

The narrator character sees the protagonist as an acquaintance, someone he/she knows but has no special relationship with. The narrator character can politely help with simpler tasks or questions, but more advanced or dangerous proposals will probably result in the narrator character either breaking off the conversation or demanding good compensation. The narrator character may very well sell out the protagonist if such a situation should present itself, as there is no loyalty between the two.

### Disliking

The narrator character probably sees the protagonist as an opponent. If this is the case the narrator character does not hesitate to put down, harass or humiliate the protagonist. It can even go so far that the narrator character actively tries to sabotage for the protagonist, not to inflict physical harm but rather to mess with him/her.

At lower influence levels it involves a lone individual or a smaller group of enemies, for example a small street gang or perhaps a couple of corrupt constables. Influential enemies are significantly more well-organized and have good equipment. At very high degrees of influence the opponent has very great power, he/she can for example be gang boss, member of a House, or similar.

### Hostile

The narrator character is hostilely disposed toward the protagonist and does not hesitate to harm, maim or kill him/her if the situation should become serious. The protagonist should see the narrator character as a real threat if their paths should cross.

Sometimes the narrator character may seek out the protagonist themselves to set him/her up in various ways. It is a matter of pure and simple hatred, disgust, emotional bitterness or perhaps cold vindictiveness due to a real or fabricated wrong. An enemy does not hesitate to go after their victim's family, friends and acquaintances so everyone is in danger when the enemy is near.

At lower degrees of influence it involves a lone individual who has no direct significance but who can still be life-threatening. An influential enemy has a collection of henchmen, great resources or is very skilled at what he/she does. At very high influence levels it involves a very influential, rich, or powerful person, who spends time finding their victim and killing him/her.

### Summary of relationships

How a narrator character generally behaves in relation to the protagonist, based on relationship, is described in table 1:41.

### Relationship Means Possible actions

Friendship Takes risks to help Intimate conversation, sacrifices, protect, support, help, defend
Benevolent Wishes well-being Talk relaxed, give advice, offer limited help, advocate
Neutral Doesn't much care Talk superficially, ignore
Disliking Wishes adversity Talk coolly, mislead/deceive, spread rumors, avoid, observe/monitor, threaten, insult, sabotage for
Hostile Takes risks to thwart Attack, harm, kill, degrade/demean, slander, flee from, set up, destroy for
Table 1:41

### Changing an established relationship

An established relationship may very well change. Most common is that such things occur naturally in a story. The narrator must judge whether it is reasonable for the relationship to change based on the experiences the protagonist and narrator character share. If the protagonist for example sets up or makes life harder for the narrator character the relationship can deteriorate.

The relationship can change as many steps as necessary – a scorned lover may very well plummet from "Friendship" to "Hostile" if the protagonist has betrayed and deeply hurt him/her. In the same way a person who initially disliked the protagonist can after a time discover that the first impression was wrong and become "Benevolently" disposed.

A relationship does not change more than once per scene unless special reasons exist.

### Family and kin

When it concerns relationships that are founded in blood or marriage the type of relationship, for example "brother"
or "spouse", should be noted on the character sheet as such things can have impact. Blood is thicker than water and one's beloved may forgive more than a fleeting friend would have done.

### Contact

A contact is a narrator character that the protagonist knows.
**Contacts are acquired with the help of**
Fate. The cost varies depending on the contact's influence. When the narrator character's influence is determined the relationship is decided through a difficulty roll (Impression).

A taxi took him part of the way but he chose to walk the last few blocks. He passed by a smoky bar where he had to stop and ask for directions. The grim, beer-reeking workers glared at him, they noticed he didn't fit in. The bartender explained the way though, and even drew a map on a napkin. He left the bartender a few zovrins and then continued on between the brick facades.

When he stood before the warehouse building he looked carefully around to make sure he wasn't being followed before slipping into the alley and approaching the back door. He knocked and almost immediately a bolt was drawn aside and the door opened. There stood a veiled woman with a candelabra in her hand, gesturing for him to enter. He stepped into the dimness and caught the scent of incense, paint, and turpentine. There was yet another smell making itself known, however, a stench of death, and it came from the woman beside him. The question he asked about whether it was her he sought went unanswered, instead she pointed with a stiff gesture toward a drape where a strip of light was visible. He asked again but the woman before him was locked in the same stiff gesture, with no tendency to move. The veil hiding her face was torture, what if she was the one he was looking for—admittedly impossible but what if... He slowly raised his hand and took hold of the veil's hem and the woman made no attempt to move, in fact not even a breath seemed to come from her body. Slowly he began to lift the veil but interrupted the motion when he heard footsteps behind the drape and a woman's voice asking him to come.

The drape was drawn aside for him and he entered a large studio with tall windows facing the night sky and the canal. In wine bottles and candlesticks, candles lit up the room and its countless easels, sculptures, and tapestries. He turned toward the woman who had called to him, his aunt and newly found mentor. He thanked her for receiving him and she nodded while taking a deep drag from her cigarillo and examining him from top to bottom. His own gaze caught on her long, yellowed nails and on the white hair hanging in stringy strands over her back. She was old, but her face was surprisingly young and free from wrinkles.

In the corner of his eye he saw the veiled woman enter through the drape and position herself in a corner. The aunt bared her yellowed teeth in a bestial grin and said that he was not yet ready to see the veiled one's face but that it was a step in his education. He was told to sit down while she sketched him with charcoal and told him about his coming time as an apprentice, the tasks he would perform and the paths he would walk. She too was of the Hrůzda family and her art drew power from dark and ancient forces—forces he would learn to master. For even though power exists in both wealth and servants, there is also power in images and words and by depicting the perfect image one can gain control not only over oneself but also over life, death, and time. The image of the murdered woman with the haunting face will forever exist as a ghost in his mind, but by drawing it he can also give it life.

The man from the Hrůzda family sat until morning listening to his aunt and when sunlight shone on the portrait of him he fell to his knees in gratitude for this was a gift greater than any other. He now understood the meaning of the name Hrůzda and would in the boundless terror find the truth.

### Influence

Influence shows how much influence the narrator character (the relation) has within their field. There are five degrees of influence: Very great influence, Significant influence, Influential, Moderate influence, and Minor influence.

**At each degree of influence there is a value** called Power that symbolizes the relation's general power, resources, and forcefulness with a game-mechanical value. When needed, rolls (Power) can be made to determine whether the narrator character succeeds with the task he/she has set out to do—for example getting hold of the secret document, persuading the prosecutor to drop the case, fixing the sniper rifle without paperwork, or perhaps gaining access to the film star Sayah Bonn. Naturally this roll can be affected by penalties or bonuses depending on what is to be accomplished.

**A narrator character's Influence and Power** are summarized in table 1:42.

### Degree of Influence Power

Very great influence 18
Significant influence 15
Influential 12
Moderate influence 9
Minor influence 6
Table 1:42

### Field

**To give the narrator character some form** of framing, he/she gets a sphere of influence. This is called the narrator character's field.

Common fields are
• The academic world
• The general public
• Children
• Crime syndicate
• Bureaucrats
• Refugees
• The street
• Club life
• Criminals
• The church
• Resistance groups
• The military
• The entertainment industry
• The police
• The press
• The justice system
• High society
• The sports world
• The state
• Houses

### The protagonist's anatomy

A protagonist is described using a number of attributes and abilities whose values show how accomplished the person is in various fields. Together they provide the person's skills from which a number of attributes are derived.

### Attributes

There are a total of nine different attributes. An attribute is a person's basic prerequisite. There are three categories of attributes: physical, mental, and spiritual, and there are three attributes in each category.

### The physical attributes

• Coordination
• Build
• Motoric

### The mental attributes

• Education
• Creativity
• Will

### The spiritual attributes

• Insight
• Perception
• Charisma

Every attribute has an attribute value that shows how well developed it is. An attribute can at lowest have attribute value one (1). There is no upper limit for how high an attribute value can reach. Table 1:43 summarizes an attribute's capacity.

### Value Capacity

1–2  Poor. The person's capacity within the attribute is either untrained, unutilized, or undeveloped.
3–4 Average. The attribute is used and trained to a normal extent.
5–6 Good. Either the person possesses a natural talent within the attribute or it is regularly trained and used.
7–8 Excellent. The attribute is very well developed and utilized. It is likely one of the person's gifts.
9+ Fantastic. The attribute is developed as far as human capacity allows, thanks to thorough and continuous training, refinement, and/or due to extremely favorable personal prerequisites.
Table 1:43

### Abilities

A person's abilities indicate his/her learned and trained competence, knowledge, and talent within a given field. There are a total of 27 abilities, each of which is linked to a governing attribute. Every attribute governs three abilities. Every ability has an ability value that shows how skilled the person is within the ability's area.

Physical abilities (with respective governing attribute)
• Close combat
• Reactions
• Mobility
• Endurance
• Strength
• Tolerance
• Dexterity
• Throwing weapons
• Firearms

Mental abilities (with respective governing attribute)
• Humanities
• Medicine
• Natural science
• Construction
• Create
• Deduction
• Determination
• Resistance
• Sense

Spiritual abilities (with respective governing attribute)
• Empathy
• Withstand
• Worldliness
• Vehicles
• Intuition
• Awareness
• Charm
• Leadership
• Persuade

Every ability has a value that shows how developed it is. A person who is completely inexperienced within an ability starts with ability value zero (0). There is no upper limit for how high an ability value can reach. Table 1:44 summarizes an ability's level of competence.

### Value Level of competence

0 Nonexistent. The person lacks knowledge or methods for using the ability. Attempting to use the ability results in a greatly increased risk of failure.
1–3 Amateur. The person has basic, unpolished, and untrained knowledge or approaches for performing the ability. This also includes a certain form of general knowledge within the ability's area.
4–6 Competent. The person has proven and reliable knowledge, qualifications, or methods for performing the ability.
7–9 Professional. The person has broad, detailed, and comprehensive knowledge or methods for performing the ability.
10–12 Expert. The person has very great, significant, and deep knowledge or methods for performing the ability.
13–15 Master. The person is among the most skilled within their area and possesses enormously outstanding and recognized knowledge or methods for performing the ability.
16+ Unsurpassed. The person is among the best, most skilled, and respected in the world at performing and understanding the ability.
Table 1:44

### Nonexistent competence

A person who has ability value zero (0) in an ability lacks the basic knowledge he/she needs to be able to use the ability in a competent way. Naturally good capacity in the ability's attribute can facilitate usage but without concrete competence in the ability it becomes difficult anyway. As long as the ability value is zero (0) the person receives a general penalty (–5) on all dice rolls regarding the ability.

### Skill

The sum of an attribute value and an ability value is called skill and is always obtained from an attribute and its underlying ability. Attribute values are never added to an ability's value unless the attribute governs the ability.

It is always the skill that is added to difficulty rolls and resistance rolls, never an individual attribute or ability value. (Formula for skill: attribute value + ability value).

Skill = attribute value + ability value

Example: Jessica has attribute value 8 in Motoric and ability value 15 in Throwing weapons. This gives her a skill of 23 in the use of throwing knives.

}   Coordination
}   Build
}   Motoric
}   Education
}   Creativity
}   Will
}   Insight
}   Perception
}   Charisma

### Description of the Attributes

Here follows overview descriptions of the attributes.
Education (mental attribute)
Education shows the brain's memory capacity and ability to collect, store and categorize learned theoretical information.
It gives a picture of the person's understanding of the stored information and provides subconscious methods for quickly and efficiently remembering facts and app-lying them in arising situations.
Education is important for, for example, students, researchers, teachers, doctors, scientists, profilers, lawyers, bureaucrats, librarians, society people, leaders, con artists and planners.
Insight (spiritual attribute)
Insight is a person's common sense, understanding of events around him/her, ability to interpret and exploit situations, handle quick turns and surprises without being caught off guard as well as to resist temptations, lures and attractions. The attribute also includes being able to understand and empathize with other people's emotional states.
Insight is important for, for example, scouts, spies, fences, fixers, leaders, soldiers, gamblers, con artists, lawyers, crimi-nals and socialites.
Coordination (physical attribute)
Coordination is the person's bodily balance, ability to move harmoniously and in a controlled manner, to react based on sensory impressions and maneuver their body. The attribute also shows the person's reaction ability and physical speed.
Coordination is important for, for example, boxers, brawlers, martial artists and troublemakers, but also athletes, dancers, mountain climbers, burglars, acrobats, gymnasts, magicians and wall climbers.
Creativity (mental attribute)
Creativity shows a person's imagination, creative power and basic curiosity. It also indicates aptitude for putting together disparate parts into wholes and drawing concrete conclusions, as well as showing ingenuity and inventiveness.
Creativity is important for, for example, artists, authors, actors, reporters, profilers, detectives, engineers, mechanics, designers and inventors.
Physique (physical attribute)
Physique shows a person's physical well-being, health status and muscle strength. It indicates in what condition the body is, how strong and how resilient it is as well as how well-toned, defined and aesthetically pleasing the muscle mass is. The attribute does not show the person's physical size
– that is instead described on page 87.
Physique is important for, for example, athletes, boxers, laborers, sailors, brawlers, weightlifters, runners, models, bodybuilders, police, soldiers, hit men and bouncers.
Dexterity (physical attribute)
Dexterity shows a person's manual dexterity, and finesse as well as aptitude for precision work. It gives steady hands and fingertip sensitivity.
Dexterity is important for, for example, shooters, locksmiths, magicians, card players, athletes, criminals, police, soldiers and snipers.
Perception (spiritual attribute)
Perception is a person's ability to observe things or phenomena using sight, hearing, taste, touch and smell, and to be able to draw conclusions from the impressions. It constitutes the aptitude to understand and filter impressions and prioritize what is currently most important. It also indicates the person's sensitivity to the usually imperceptible, such as body language, mannerisms, nuances in speech, nervousness and lies. Even what is usually called instinct is included.
Perception is important for, for example, interrogators, hunters, soldiers, psychologists, therapists, profilers, police, forensic technicians, con artists, drivers, pilots, lawyers and detectives.
Presence (spiritual attribute)
Presence is a person's charisma, charm and aura.
It indicates the person's power of attraction, ability to influence others and convey messages. The attribute affects how the surroundings react and receive what the person says and thinks. The attribute does not include the person's physical appearance
– that is described on page 87 – but a person with strong presence can be anything from fantastically beautiful to repulsively ugly. Presence is more about the ability to influence and convince others through social aggression or shamelessness.
Presence is important for, for example, actors, leaders, con artists, salespeople, interrogators, models, military officers and cult leaders.
Will (mental attribute)
Will is a person's willpower, mental resilience, determination and ability to take action. It gives strength to steel oneself in demanding and stressful situations, to manage to carry out activities under pressure and to summon inner strength to achieve results when everything is against you. The attribute gives personal calm, dignity and self-respect together with the power to resist influence and provocations without reacting emotionally or rashly.
Will is important for, for example, athletes, leaders, celebrities, entrepreneurs, soldiers, police, businesspeople, rescue personnel, doctors, negotiators, scientists and artists.
"In my experience there are three ways to get rid of a blackmailer. Either you pay, and pay, and pay, until you're ruined. Or you call the cops and your secret becomes front-page news. Or you kill him."
— Gabriel Jandaran

### Description of the Skills

Here follows overview descriptions of the skills.
Determination (Will)
Determination shows a person's stubbornness and ability to achieve results, regardless of setbacks and obstacles. The skill helps the person maintain focus and concentrate on the task to be performed and prevents the person from being affected by disturbing elements. It indicates the person's ability to absorb new knowledge, apply old knowledge in new ways, and absorb and store information under pressure.
Determination is an important skill for leaders, elite soldiers, detectives, coaches, athletes, detectives, planners, students, lawyers, researchers, scientists and journalists.
Charm (Presence)
Charm is used to influence, beguile, mislead, manipulate, deflect suspicions as well as lie quickly, effectively and convincingly. The skill also covers flirtation, courtship and seduction. It also includes entertaining other people through performance, acting, drama and role-playing. The skill is useful when it comes to imitating other people.
Charm is an important skill for con artists, swindlers, femme fatales, hustlers, actors, entertainers, singers, spies, lawyers, salespeople and scouts.
Empathy (Insight)
