Empathy indicates a person's compassion, ability to empathize and sensitivity to other people's feelings and moods.
The skill facilitates communication by providing the person with subconscious information and ability to interpret this information in a correct way. A person with Empathy notices if someone else is nervous, worried, upset, sad, depressed, or affected by some other strong emotion. Empathy also gives the ability to understand others'
drives and motives, even if one doesn't share them oneself.
Empathy is an important skill for interrogators, parents, doctors, priests, psychologists, counselors, negotiators, forensic technicians, detectives, entertainers, lawyers and advisors.
Manual Dexterity (Dexterity)
Manual Dexterity controls fine motor skills in precision work with the hands. The skill is used for example in card tricks, magic tricks and other forms of entertainment. Pickpockets need it to steal things from pockets and the like, and it is indispensable in lockpicking.
Manual Dexterity is an important skill for magicians, locksmiths, burglars, pickpockets and car thieves.
Vehicles (Perception)
Vehicles gives knowledge of how to operate different vehicles, which laws and rules apply when operating vehicles and how to perform rudimentary vehicle maintenance. The skill is mainly used when something extraordinary occurs during travel, for example during chases, vehicle failures or sabotage against vehicles.
Vehicles is an important skill for chauffeurs, taxi drivers, aircraft pilots, motorsport drivers, getaway drivers, sea captains, transporters and stuntmen.
Humanities (Education)
Humanities consists of knowledge acquired through studies and learning in various social science and humanistic subjects. The skill gives basic general education in a number of different subjects such as for example economics, history, linguistics, literary and art appreciation, law, political science, sociology, philosophy, psychology as well as religious studies. Note that this concerns a very broad overview – for deeper and more practical applicability the person needs to acquire expertise in the desired subjects.
Humanities is an important skill for students, bureaucrats, researchers, academics, historians, socialites and teachers.
Intuition (Perception)
Intuition is the person's instincts, reflexive actions and sensitivity to their surroundings. It makes the person subconsciously vigilant and attentive to suspicious behavior or strange situations as well as gives the ability to instinctively protect oneself from such things. Intuition helps when it comes to seeing through lies and detecting signs of stress.
Intuition is an important skill for police, detectives, soldiers, psychologists, interrogators, spies, burglars, lawyers, forensic technicians and con artists.
Throwing Weapons (Dexterity)
Throwing Weapons indicates proficiency in throwing objects with precision and hitting what one aims at. The skill also includes things like weapon maintenance and knowledge of what range of throwing weapons is available on the market. The skill applies to all types of thrown objects, from stones and snowballs to throwing knives and hand grenades. Throwing Weapons also gives some understanding of combat and behavior in life-threatening situations.
Throwing Weapons is an important skill for circus performers, knife throwers, soldiers as well as dodgeball and basketball players.
Stamina (Physique)
Stamina shows a person's physical endurance, energy and health status. It gives the person strength to push themselves to the limit and to continue fighting even when every muscle in the body screams for mercy. The skill shows what physical shape a person is in. Stamina also indicates how patiently a person withstands bodily injuries and their effects.
Stamina is an important skill for athletes, bodyguards, soldiers, hunters, survival experts, weightlifters and brawlers.
Construction (Creativity)
Construction is the talent to assemble, understand and repair technically advanced constructed objects or to set up camps, build houses or erect bridges. All forms of basic knowledge in mechanics, electronics, engine knowledge and repair are included, but deeper knowledge in each field is acquired through expertise.
Construction is an important skill for mechanics, installers, electricians, welders, foremen, craftsmen, plumbers, engineers, bomb makers and repairmen.
**Basic knowledge**
A person who has skill value one (1) or more in Humanities can read and write their native language and has basic mathematics knowledge. For deeper knowledge in mathematics the skill Natural Science is required.
All persons are considered able to swim adequately even though there is expertise within Stamina that improves one's performance in water.

Leadership (Charisma)
Leadership is the ability to get people to listen and absorb the message you want to convey. It can involve directing a group of people in a certain direction, getting them to perform a task, or cease a certain activity. Different social strata have different methods for achieving results, but eloquence, rhetoric, charisma, and presence are essential parts of successful leadership. Leadership is also used to appear strong and indomitable in a threatening or stressful discussion. The ability can be used to add weight behind threats and intimidation.
Leadership is important for commanders, interrogators, lawyers, police officers, teachers, managers, con artists, negotiators, and cult leaders.

Medicine (Education)
Medicine is studied and learned knowledge about the human body, everything that can afflict it, and ways to remedy such afflictions. The ability's scope includes elementary competence in making diagnoses, performing surgical procedures and practicing emergency medicine, as well as knowledge of anatomy, psychiatry, toxicology, forensic medicine, and drugs. Even basic knowledge of diseases and poisons is included. For deeper specialist knowledge, expertise is required.
Medicine is an important ability for doctors, therapists, coroners, surgeons, ambulance personnel, military personnel, police officers, and society poisoners.

Resist (Insight)
Resist helps a person withstand external social influence and provocations without reacting rashly or emotionally. It gives the person the capacity to see through what is being said, how it's being said, and why it's being said, and thereby the ability to defend themselves against arguments or attempts at influence. Resist is used to withstand persuasion attempts, handle temptations, enticements, attractions and seductions, as well as interviews and interrogations.
Resist is an important ability for unfaithful husbands, spies, couriers, con artists, interrogators, police officers, bureaucrats, waitresses, strippers, prostitutes, and merchants.

Natural Science (Education)
Natural Science consists of learned and studied knowledge in various technical areas such as mathematics, logic, and physics, and basic familiarity with chemistry, metallurgy, and biology. Additionally, a person with Natural
Science possesses superficial knowledge in zoology, astronomy, geology, and botany. To deepen knowledge, further expertise in different fields is required.
Natural Science is an important ability for students, bureaucrats, researchers, academics, architects, engineers, technicians, scientists, and teachers.

Close Combat (Coordination)
Close Combat is a person's aptitude for attacking and harming enemies in close combat, with or without implements. It provides understanding of how close combat psychology works and how feints, tricks, and deceptions are executed correctly. A person with good close combat ability lacks or can overcome the barriers that exist in most people when it comes to going after an enemy with everything they have. There are numerous expertises that deepen the ability.
Close Combat is an important ability for gang members, bouncers, small-time thugs, brawlers, bartenders, police officers, soldiers, boxers, fighters, gangsters, hitmen, and thugs.

Reactions (Coordination)
Reactions is a person's reflex speed and reaction ability. The ability provides advantages in combat situations where instinctive action, quick reflexes, and the ability to quickly assess and react are decisive.
Reactions is an important ability for boxers, soldiers, police officers, gangsters, martial artists, and athletes.

Resilience (Will)
Resilience is a person's mental toughness and psychological defense as well as the ability to maintain focus and distance despite strong mental stress. It helps the person handle severely degrading and traumatic experiences, such as various forms of threats, stress, torture, violence, humiliation, and abuse. In directly frightening or disturbing situations, it is resilience that shows whether the person breaks down or not.
The ability helps a person see through situations and endure the horrible and twisted, as well as function somewhat normally after trauma.
Resilience is an important ability for commando soldiers, spies, smugglers, waitresses, soldiers, prostitutes, police officers, investigators, and informants.

Mobility (Coordination)
Mobility describes a person's trained movement ability, grace, sense of rhythm, and bodily flexibility, agility, suppleness, and springiness. The concept also includes a certain form of balanced strength required for utilizing the body's sway and power in, for example, long jumps or free climbing. The ability is also used for bodily maneuvers such as acrobatic feats or advanced body contortions. Mobility also provides talent for more shady activities such as moving quietly or sneaking past others without being noticed.
Mobility is an important ability for burglars, gymnasts, athletes, climbers, acrobats, detectives, scouts, commando soldiers, spies, dancers, strippers, and performers.

Composure (Will)
Composure describes a person's self-discipline, dignity, and calm.
A composed person is often methodical, controlled, balanced, and unaffected. Often the person possesses good self-confidence and is fundamentally secure. In stressed or surprising situations, those with good aptitude in the ability enjoy good opportunities to act relaxed, rationally, and calculatedly.
The person can still crack and break down if the mental stress becomes too great, even if they might still manage to hide it from the world by maintaining an external calm and dignity.
Composure is an important ability for martial artists, stressed careerists, wise hermits, lifestyle personalities, soldiers, shadow hunters, cult leaders, slick criminals, and con artists.

Create (Creativity)
Create indicates a person's aesthetic development and talent.
The ability is used when a person wants to write, compose music, paint, sculpt, photograph, or design and sew clothes. Create can also be used for more shady activities such as forging licenses, papers, and documents. Within the ability there is also knowledge of makeup, cosmetics, wigs, and disguise.
Create is an important ability for artists, journalists, writers, actors, makeup artists, poets, musicians, photographers, entertainers, and designers.

Firearms (Motor Skills)
Firearms is a person's ability to correctly handle, control, and fire firearms of various kinds, from light handguns to heavy sniper rifles and machine guns, but for deepening knowledge there are numerous different expertises.
This includes familiarity with how to load, unsecure, perform bolt action, etc. Knowledge of weapon models, basic weapon mechanism theory, ammunition knowledge, and weapon care is also included in the ability. Firearms provides certain insight into how to act in combat situations.
Firearms is an important ability for soldiers, police officers, snipers, assassins, hitmen, gangsters, gang members, detectives, bodyguards, security personnel, and guards.

Deduction (Creativity)
Deduction is the brain's ability to see connections and patterns and figure out seemingly nonexistent links through inference, reasoning, and logical thinking. It also indicates a person's ability to formulate and develop thoughts and ideas. The ability is used to draw conclusions, solve puzzles, crack ciphers and codes, interpret dreams and texts, or to progress in various experiments.
Deduction is very common in the police force, especially among forensic technicians in evidence analysis at crime scenes.
Deduction is an important ability for forensic technicians, criminal investigators, detectives, scientists, researchers, seers, criminals, doctors, and tacticians.

Strength (Build)
Strength is a person's muscle mass and physical development, and a measure of both raw strength and strength technique. The higher value a person has in this ability, the more well-built and muscular they are. The ability is important for breaking down barriers, lifting heavy things, dragging objects, or smashing something or someone.
Strength is an important ability for boxers, weightlifters, models, bodybuilders, bouncers, soldiers, fighters, hitmen, and laborers.

Tolerance (Build)
Tolerance is the body's ability to resist foreign organisms, poisons, and drugs, as well as influence from cold and heat.
Tolerance includes immune defense, metabolism, and general resistance—and of course tolerance to alcohol.
Tolerance is an important ability for athletes, survival experts, soldiers, alcoholics, and vagrants.

Awareness (Perception)
Awareness is a person's observational ability and vigilance and is used both actively and passively. It gives the person an aptitude for discovering and noting things by using and interpreting sensory impressions in the best way in every situation. The ability primarily concerns sight and hearing, but also touch, taste, and smell. It can also be used when searching persons, as well as to stay hidden and hide oneself.
Awareness is an important ability for scouts, spies, bouncers, bodyguards, soldiers, police officers, snipers, detectives, forensic technicians, burglars, guards, and hunters.

Streetwise (Insight)
Streetwise indicates the ability to function in social contexts. It constitutes a person's social agility and understanding of how the norms and culture that prevail should be exploited for best results. A person with Streetwise doesn't feel lost or out of place in social situations, regardless of circumstances or social class, but finds ways to fit in. The ability provides a sense of how to interact with people of various kinds, how to adapt language use, jargon, gestures, and conversation topics so as not to offend or show lack of respect for the person you're speaking with. The ability also includes various specialized areas within social contexts, for example gambling. Streetwise is also used to pick up and exploit information "on the street,"
knowing who to talk to about a certain question, or which bar is the hangout for a certain gang, etc.
Streetwise is an important ability for gossip journalists, socialites, celebrities, spies, luxury prostitutes, intelligence agents, fixers, fences, undercover cops, con artists, gamblers, and models.

Persuade (Charisma)
Persuade is the ability to verbally convey a message or desire to another person and try to get that person to follow your will. The ability covers social competence and allows the person to overcome inhibiting factors such as shyness or awkwardness. There are countless ways to achieve results, and the ability includes diplomacy, rhetoric, and negotiation technique as well as pure persuasive ability. The ability also covers interview and interrogation technique.
Persuade is an important ability for businessmen, reporters, leaders, mediators, negotiators, interrogators, prostitutes, salespeople, hitmen, spies, businessmen, detectives, and informants.

"The greatest of all conspiracies is that there isn't one! No one is after you. No one cares about you. It doesn't matter if you live or die. So, does that feel better now?"
— Franko Millovy

### Will to Live

Will to Live is a person's ability to withstand serious injuries without dying. More information can be found on page 264.
The attribute value for Will to Live is obtained by adding together the skill in Determination, Fitness and Sense and dividing the sum by three (/3), rounded down, and then adding five to the sum.
**Notoriety**
Notoriety indicates how well-known a person is and how they are perceived by others. The reputation can be good or bad. Most people have Notoriety Unknown (5) — that is, they are completely unknown. A person's notoriety can be changed during the creation of traits, see page 86.
**Damage Modifier**
Damage Modifier is a person's ability to inflict extra damage on the enemy in close combat. The attribute value is added to all close combat attacks' damage value.
The attribute value for Damage Modifier is obtained by adding together the person's skill in Strength with their Size and dividing the sum by ten (/10), rounded down.
**Size**
Size measures a person's physical bulk, presence, height and weight. The attribute value in Size affects a person's capacity to withstand damage. It is extremely rare that rolls are made directly for Size, instead the value is primarily used in calculations of other values. Once size is determined, it is very rare for it to change — certain expertises can, however, affect it over time.
The attribute value for Size is normally 15, when the person is considered to be medium-sized. The value can be changed during the creation of the person's traits, see page 87.
**Combat Movement**
Combat Movement indicates the person's aptitude for maneuvering their body in a pressing situation such as combat. The concept includes things like not hesitating, moving in a zigzag, keeping the head low and running in a crouched position. In cases where distance measurement is needed in combat, the attribute value in Combat Movement counts as the number of meters the person reaches. This occurs primarily in connection with the Initiative actions Move, Fight and Fury Attack (see page 255).
The attribute value for Combat Movement is obtained by adding together the skill in Determination, Fitness and Agility and dividing the sum by three (/3), rounded down.
**Wound Threshold**
Wound Threshold indicates how resilient a person is and their capacity to take damage without receiving Wounds. If an attack's damage effect exceeds the victim's Wound Threshold they receive a Wound, see page 262 for more information. No rolls are made against the attribute value for Wound Threshold, it is only used as a comparison value.
The attribute value for Wound Threshold is obtained by adding together the skill in Fitness and Strength with the person's Size and dividing the sum by three (/3), rounded down.
**Description of Attributes**
A person has a number of attributes and attribute values. Attributes are a kind of secondary information about the person. Some of the attributes are used for rolls while others are used as references for other circumstances.
The 16 attributes are
• Defense • Learning
• Impression • Standard of Living
• Will to Live • Notoriety
• Damage Modifier • Size
• Combat Movement • Wound Threshold
• Luck • Endurance
• Appearance • Wellbeing
• Fate • Awareness
**Defense**
Defense is a person's survival instinct and ability to keep themselves out of danger in combat. Defense is considered the basic instinctive disposition while trained thugs, police or soldiers have other trained methods to escape harm in various forms of combat. The attribute value for Defense is entered in the box marked "Defense" as well as in the two boxes marked "Ranged" and "Close Combat" respectively in the character sheet. For rolls (Defense), always use the attribute value that best fits the situation — either Ranged or Close Combat.
The attribute value for Defense is obtained by adding together the skill in Intuition, Reactions and Vigilance and dividing the sum by three (/3), rounded down.
**Learning**
Learning shows a person's general aptitude, learning ability and wisdom. It shows how the person primarily learns things — the hard and physical way, by reasoning logically and rationally, or perhaps by having an innate cunning and a certain measure of guile. Learning is used in the calculation of Experience, see page 220.
The attribute value for Learning is obtained by adding together the skill in Determination, Reasoning and Worldliness and dividing the sum by three (/3), rounded down.
**Impression**
Impression shows how a person is perceived and treated by others when they meet for the first time. At meetings with significant narrator characters, a difficulty roll (Impression) is made. More information about impression can be found on page 209.
The attribute value is obtained by adding together the skill in Charm or Leadership with the skill in Sense and Worldliness and dividing the sum by three (/3), rounded down. Charm/Leadership shows what impression the person gives — one of style and allure or one of respect and authority.
**Standard of Living**
A person's standard of living refers to their place in society's hierarchy. It shows birth, wealth, success and status. It is a simplified way of indicating what resources, what style and what housing a person normally has.
A protagonist's standard of living is normally 4 but can be changed during the creation of traits, see page 86.

### Luck

Luck shows the degree of good fortune in life and the person's general luckiness in situations where skill is worth nothing. Being lucky is granted to very few in the world of Noir.
The attribute value for Luck is normally 0. The value can be changed during the creation of traits, see page 87.
**Endurance**
Endurance indicates how much beating, pain and stunning a person can withstand before they risk being knocked out, fainting or becoming unconscious. See page 263 for more information.
The attribute value for Endurance is obtained by adding together the skill in Strength and Resistance with the person's Size and dividing the sum by three (/3), rounded down, and then adding five to the sum.
**Appearance**
Appearance is a person's physical exterior, features, bearing and beauty — or lack thereof. A good appearance helps the person in most cases of social interaction, debate and seduction. A frightening exterior and appearance defects can, on the other hand, make social interaction more difficult as others instinctively distance themselves from a person with such characteristics. Rolls are never made for Appearance but the attribute can give bonuses or penalties to social actions.
The attribute value for Appearance is normally 0, which corresponds to an ordinary appearance. Appearance can be changed during the creation of traits, see page 87.
**Wellbeing**
Wellbeing shows a person's general health, energy level, freshness, mental soundness and general capacity for action and initiative. Various phenomena, such as hard work, taxing stress, shocking events and traumatic experiences lower a person's Wellbeing while sleep, food, shelter and security increase it. Love, drugs and therapy can also have an effect. More about Wellbeing can be found on page 242.
If nothing special has emerged during the creation of limitations or traits, all persons start with Wellbeing Relaxed (10).
**Fate**
Fate shows the person's relationship to destiny and its whims. The person can manipulate the odds in life and sometimes succeed in performing things that seem unlikely or escape misfortune with a few scratches and mere fright.
All protagonists begin with 10 points in Fate, unless something else emerged during the creation of traits. More about Fate can be found on page 236.
**Awareness**
Awareness is the person's talent for acting in a controlled manner in combat, their combat experience, quick thinking and ability to quickly assess the situation. Awareness is used to determine a person's Initiative, and gives the person the aptitude to act in an efficient, planned and decisive way in combat. For more information, see page 254.
The attribute value for Awareness is obtained by adding together the skill in Reactions, Sense and Vigilance and dividing the sum by three (/3), rounded down.
"I promise, I saw it, he was lying here. Three shots he took while lying down. First she fired one shot so he fell like a pine tree. Unprovoked, they talked a bit then she just shot. So fucking sick, she seemed to kiss him after he died. When she had left I ran out of hiding and called you, as soon as I could, on my word of honor. When I came back he was gone! Poof gone. As if the Reaper had taken him! I may be drunk but it's completely true, I promise!"

Protagonists learn from experiences, maturity, adventures and events. This is called gaining Experience. Experience is used to improve a protagonist's qualities.
Experience & Life Experience
After each story that is concluded, each protagonist who has participated receives a sum of Experience. This sum is added to any previously recorded Experience in the character sheet. The same sum is also added to Life Experience.
**The total sum of Life Experience shows** how much Experience the protagonist has accumulated in total during all their adventures, and can be seen as a general indication of wisdom and knowledge, see table 1:45. It is a good sum for the narrator to use if the protagonists are at very different development levels and a narrator wants a newly arrived protagonist to catch up, then the narrator can award the inexperienced protagonist a certain sum of Experience directly after the protagonist's creation process has been completed.
Experience shows how many available "points" a protagonist has to improve themselves. When a protagonist improves, the sum in Experience is reduced by the cost of the improvement. Life Experience does not decrease when Experience is used, nor can one use Life Experience to improve oneself.
**Life Experience Maturity Level**
0–100 Untested
100–400 Tested
400–1,000 Practiced
1,000–2,000 Experienced
2,000–3,500 Hardened
3,500–6,000 Veteran
6,000–9,000 Authority
9,000–15,000 Excellent
15,000–25,000 Peerless
25,000+ Legendary
Table 1:45
Example: Convarian has 120 points in Experience and 425 points in Life Experience. He is awarded 360 points in Experience, which increases Experience to 480 points and Life Experience to 785 points. Convarian spends 295 points of Experience on improving various abilities. In the character sheet it is noted that he has 65 points remaining in Experience, while Life Experience remains at 785 points.
**Experience**

### Learning

When a protagonist uses an ability and gets an exceptional result (whether successful or failed), they may place a learning mark next to the ability on the character sheet. This is also called marking the ability.
A learning mark makes it easier for a protagonist to increase the ability's ability value by one level using Experience, see page 222.
Only exceptional rolls for abilities grant learning marks. Exceptional rolls for attributes grant no marks.
Learning marks remain with the ability until the ability value is raised, regardless of how long this takes. An ability can only have one learning mark at a time.

### How a protagonist gains experience

After a completed story, the narrator should distribute Experience to the participants. This is preferably done when the protagonists have an opportunity to take it easy and reflect on what has occurred in their lives and what they have learned.
Experience is distributed based on the story's Challenge. If the story has been challenging, difficult, and given the protagonists opportunity to grow and become an active part of the environment, this gives more Experience than if it had been simple, routine, and isolated. The points for Challenge are then multiplied by the protagonist's attribute value in Learning.
Beyond this sum, extra experience can be awarded based on Ego – if a player has portrayed their protagonist in accordance with their personality, character traits, and motivations, and if they have actively supported and created exciting and dramatic situations during the story's course, they can be rewarded with extra points.
The sum of all this becomes the number of Experience Points distributed to the protagonist. (Formula for Experience:
(Learning x Challenge) + Ego).

### Experience from motivations

A protagonist can receive Experience upon fulfilling motivations. The Experience is then distributed directly when the motivation is fulfilled and used in the same way as other Experience.
For more information about Experience from motivations, see page 233.

### Experience pacing

If Experience is distributed rarely, the protagonists should receive extra much when it does happen. However, it's recommended to distribute Experience fairly often since the protagonists'
development then becomes more consistent and not so jerky. Additionally, this provides room to improve Fate continuously, something that can be crucial for the protagonists'
lives and health.

### When may experience be used?

Experience may be used when a protagonist is rested, is in an undisturbed place and doesn't feel stressed, for example after having slept through an uneventful night.
The protagonist should feel calm and reflective and have opportunity to gather their thoughts. This can occur multiple times during one and the same story. Every time Experience is distributed, it can also be used immediately.
However, Experience may not be used for things like in the middle of a scene in the story increasing an ability the player just discovered they needed, or improving Fate just when the need presents itself.
Note that there are no limitations on how much unused Experience a protagonist may have.
The player decides themselves when they want to use the protagonist's
Experience for improvements.

### Learning

The attribute Learning shows a person's general aptitude, learning ability, and wisdom. It shows how the person primarily learns things – the hard and physical way, by reasoning logically and rationally, or by having an innate cunning and a good deal of guile. More about Learning can be found on page 218.

### Challenge

The Challenge is the story's difficulty level set in relation to the protagonists' knowledge, resources, and opportunities.
Table 1:46 shows how many points a challenge should give depending on its difficulty level.

**Rating** | **The Challenge was...**
10 | Child's play – the protagonists danced through the story's problems without any difficulties whatsoever.
15 | Uncomplicated – sure, some resistance appeared, but on the whole the protagonists had no major difficulties solving the problems.
20 | Troublesome – the resistance was there and the protagonists had to exert themselves to overcome it with the right combination of cunning, luck, and skill.
25 | Tough – the resistance was tangible and purposeful, the protagonists had to fight hard to reach the goal and it was close a few times that someone bought it or nearly fumbled away.
30 | Enormous – virtually no protagonist has Fate left, their lives hung by a fragile thread on multiple occasions, the opponents' resources were enormous and the protagonists were truly exposed, outmaneuvered, and manipulated.

Table 1:46

### Ego

Ego encompasses the player's roleplaying – how the player's protagonist expresses themselves in the world. What affects the rating in this category is how well the player lives out the character traits and background the protagonist possesses, how consistent they are in the role, in what way they use the bonds and contacts the protagonist has in the world and how they let their protagonist develop from experiences and knowledge. That the player is concentrated and doesn't clown around and disturb the atmosphere is also to be considered.
Table 1:47 shows how many experience points from Ego that should be distributed depending on the player's participation.

**Rating** | **Participation was...**
10 | Ordinary – the player participated but didn't exert themselves notably and didn't contribute much to building up atmospheres or conveying the protagonist's personality.
40 | Active and engaged – the player succeeded well in conveying the protagonist's character, personality, and goals as well as being active in the story in a positive way.
100 | An exceptional performance – the player immersed themselves in the protagonist to a very high degree, was strong in the portrayal and independently created exciting, dramatic, and interesting events through the protagonist's actions, limitations, and choices.

Table 1:47

Example: Convarian has Learning 9. The just completed story was incredibly difficult, with tricky traps, cunning enemies, and several setbacks which gives him 25 points in Challenge. Multiplied by Learning this becomes 9 x 25 = 225 points Experience.
During the story's course, Convarian's player was very involved in the story and roleplayed his character in a very convincing and consistent manner. Therefore the narrator gives him the highest possible points for Ego, that is 100 points.
This means that Convarian is awarded a total sum of
325 (225 + 100) points Experience, which is noted in the character sheet under both Experience and Life Experience.

### Challenge

**Learning** | **10** | **15** | **20** | **25** | **30**
1 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30
2 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60
3 | 30 | 45 | 60 | 75 | 90
4 | 40 | 60 | 80 | 100 | 120
5 | 50 | 75 | 100 | 125 | 150
6 | 60 | 90 | 120 | 150 | 180
7 | 70 | 105 | 140 | 175 | 210
8 | 80 | 120 | 160 | 200 | 240
9 | 90 | 135 | 180 | 225 | 270
10 | 100 | 150 | 200 | 250 | 300
11 | 110 | 165 | 220 | 275 | 330
12 | 120 | 180 | 240 | 300 | 360
13 | 130 | 195 | 260 | 325 | 390
14 | 140 | 210 | 280 | 350 | 420
15 | 150 | 225 | 300 | 375 | 450
16 | 160 | 240 | 320 | 400 | 480
17 | 170 | 255 | 340 | 425 | 510
18 | 180 | 270 | 360 | 450 | 540
19 | 190 | 285 | 380 | 475 | 570
20 | 200 | 300 | 400 | 500 | 600

Table 1:48

### Improving a protagonist

Improving values costs Experience. A protagonist can be improved in four areas: increase attribute values, increase ability values, acquire expertise areas for an ability, or improve their odds with Fate.

### With Experience the following activities can be performed

• Increase attribute value
• Increase ability value
• Acquire expertise area
• Improve Fate

### Increase attribute value

Increasing an attribute value costs 50 x the new level in the attribute value points Experience. Note that one must increase a value one step at a time.

Example: Convarian has attribute value 4 in Build.
Increasing the value to 6 costs 550 points Experience (first 50 x 5
= 250, thereafter 50 x 6 = 300, sum 550).

### Increase ability value

Increasing an ability value costs 10 x the new level in the ability value points Experience. Note that one may only increase a value one step at a time.
If one wants to increase an ability value to over double the level in the governing attribute value, the Experience cost is doubled. This symbolizes that it requires enormous training to reach ultimate success in a field one lacks basic aptitude for.
If a protagonist has a learning mark next to an ability that is about to be improved, the cost to reach the next level is halved. Note that this only applies to increasing one level.

Example: Convarian wants to increase his ability value in Strength, which falls under the attribute Build where he now has attribute value 6. Currently Convarian has ability value 8 in Strength, but he wants to increase this as much as he can without getting the double cost. The highest ability value he can then reach is 12 (double the controlling attribute value, in this case 6).
Increasing to ability value 12 costs 420 points Experience
(first 10 x 9, then 10 x 10, followed by 10 x 11 and finally 10 x 12:
sum 420).
Convarian discovers he has Experience left so he decides to after all increase Strength one more step.
Ability value 13 is over his double controlling attribute value, which means the cost is doubled, so it costs him 10
x 13 x 2, or 260 points.
Thereafter Convarian wants to increase Awareness. He has ability value 3, but also has an improvement mark noted after an exceptional failure that occurred during the story's course. He wants to increase Awareness to level 5. This costs him
70 points Experience (10 x 4 x 0.5, followed by 10 x 5, sum 70).

### Acquire expertise area

Acquiring a new expertise area for an ability costs
10 x Complexity points Experience.
Note that an expertise area's Requirements must be fulfilled for a protagonist to be allowed to acquire it.
The protagonist may also not have a higher total sum in

### Quick reference for Experience

In table 1:48 experience based on Learning and Challenge is calculated in advance. Learning is shown on the vertical axis and the different challenge levels on the horizontal axis.

Complexity than ability value in all of an ability's expertise 
areas, see page 58.
A protagonist can erase learned expertise areas, but does not get back the Experience points that the exper-tise areas cost to acquire. Naturally, the erased expertise 
areas' values do not count toward Complexity, since they no longer exist or can be used.
Removing expertise areas can be interesting if a protagonist wants to completely change direction within an ability, for example perhaps a rifleman who has lost his arm wants to "forget" his expert knowledge in rifle handling and instead concentrate on becoming a skilled revolver shooter.
Example: Convarian wants to become a specialist in shooting with his revolver, an R&S .38 Stealth. He has ability value 4 in
Firearms so he can at most have expertise with a total complexity value of exactly four. He already has the expertise "Take cover" which has Complexity 2. Acquiring "Weapon han-dling: Revolvers" and "Weapon expert: R&S .38 Stealth" costs a total of 3 (2 + 1) in Complexity, which is one too many. Conva-rian can choose to increase the ability value in Firearms one step but he will then not have enough Experience to acquire the two expertises he wants.
The solution is that he forgets "Take cover" and acquires the two new expertises for a total of 30 Experience points ((2
+ 1) x 10). "Take cover" is erased from the character sheet and
"Weapon handling: Revolvers" and "Weapon expert: R&S .38
Stealth" are added. Convarian is satisfied as he now gets a bonus of a full five (+5) when he uses his revolver.
**Improving Fate**
A protagonist can improve their standing with Fate through Experience. For a cost of 2 Experience points the protagonist gets to increase Fate one step. A protagonist can have a maximum of 25 in Fate.

Example: Convarian wants to improve Fate by seven steps. This costs 14 points (2 x 7) Experience.
**Extreme values**
A protagonist who reaches extreme attribute or abil-ity values finds it much harder to improve fur-ther. This means that it is usually much more econom-ical to become reasonably skilled in several fields than to become hyperskilled in a single one. There is no upper limit for how high a protagonist's values can reach.
When an attribute value is increased to 10 or higher, each new level costs five times more Experience than usual.
Similarly, the cost increases five times when an ability value is increased to level 20 or above. This is summarized in table 1:49.
Example: Increasing attribute value 9 to 10 costs 2,500 (10 x 50
x 5) Experience points. Similarly, it costs a full 1,200 (24 x
10 x 5) Experience points to increase ability value 23 to 24.
**Attribute value Ability value Increase multiple**
10 or higher 20 or higher x 5
Table 1:49
**Skills that affect attributes**
When a person's skill increases, this can affect the per-son's attribute values. This effect is listed below.
**Ability Attribute**
Determination Learning, Willpower, Combat Movement
**Charm Impression**
**Intuition Defense**
Endurance Willpower, Combat Movement, Injury Threshold
**Leadership Impression**
Reactions Defense, Overview
**Resistance Stamina**
**Mobility Combat Movement**
Sense Impression, Willpower, Overview
**Deduction Learning**
Strength Damage Bonus, Injury Threshold, Stamina
Awareness Defense, Overview
Worldliness Learning, Impression
Training & studies
A protagonist can engage in studies to learn things and acquire knowledge. However, this method is nowhere near as instructive as gaining experience through real experiences and insights.
**Study ability**
The protagonist can choose to study an ability. After 40
hours of study in a suitable place and with appropriate study material, the player rolls a difficulty check (Learning) to see what result the studies produced. Attributes cannot be trained in this way.
Exceptionally successful roll: The protagonist receives as much Experience as the sum of the results on all dice. In addition, the studied ability counts as learning-checked until it is improved.
Successful roll: The protagonist receives as much Experi-ence as the sum of the results on all dice.
Failure: The protagonist receives as much Experi-ence as the result on the lowest die.
Exceptional failure: The protagonist learns nothing during the period.
**Teaching**
A teacher who helps and guides a student can increase the student's understanding of the study material. The teacher rolls after 40 hours of teaching a difficulty check (Leadership), before the student rolls their check. The result gives the modification the student gets on their difficulty check (Learning) for studying an ability.
Exceptionally successful roll: The student gets a bonus (+5) on the difficulty check.
Successful roll: The student gets a bonus (+2) on the difficulty check.
Failure: The student gets a penalty (–2) on the difficulty check.
Exceptional failure: The student gets a penalty (–5) on the difficulty check.
**Daily grind**
A protagonist who is not active in a story or who is not actively studying still learns things just by being in the world. After each month in the game world, the protagonist rolls a difficulty check as if they had studied an ability. The player and narrator should agree on which ability is most appropriate to check if an exceptional success occurs.

All people are different, some remain cold and cal-culating while others are ruled by their emo-tions. Character traits show passions and distinctive traits in an individual's personality.
**Character traits**
Character traits show how a person is. It is the per-son's inner disposition and fundamental behavior in the world they operate in. Character traits affect the choices a person makes. Possessing a character trait does not automatically entail an advantage or disadvantage, as the same character trait can work both positively and negatively, depending on the situation.
The 13 character traits included in Noir are
• Aggressive
• Arrogant
• Cautious
• Greedy
• Honorable
• Vengeful
• Loving
• Indifferent
• Lustful
• Suspicious
• Deceitful
• Risk-taking
• Convinced
**Aggressive**
Aggression can manifest in many ways – from every-thing from senseless rage to more restrained sadistic satisfaction. An aggressive person is often tempera-mental, irritable, violent, sadistic, brutal, combat-ive, hot-tempered and angry. Aggressive people easily become angry at provocation and do not shy away from violence. They also often lack the moral barriers that most people have.
Aggressiveness is a common character trait among box-ers, police officers, brawlers, hitmen, debt collectors, gangsters, gang members, drunks, party animals and soldiers.
**Arrogant**
A person who is arrogant usually considers them-selves superior to everyone else and likes to point out their own importance and competence. The person is often cocky, superior, smug, ambitious, nonchalant and contemptuous. Not infrequently, the person is vain, egotistical, attention-seeking, conceited, snob-
**Character traits**

back. The revenge can happen immediately through violent actions or through careful planning and protracted action that bit by bit destroys another person's life. The vengeful person usually magnifies the extent and signifi-cance of any insults.
Vengefulness is a common character trait among crime victims, gangsters, betrayed wives, fired people, cor-porate leaders and dynastic personalities.
**Loving**
Love is a powerful force in human life and even though it can be twisted into both jealousy and pure lust, it main-tains hope. There are those who have an open and peaceful attitude toward their fellow humans and these lov-ing people are often compassionate, sensitive, humble, friendly, understanding, sympathetic, forgiving, helpful and kind-hearted. A loving person listens to their feel-ings and rarely resorts to violence unless it is to defend friends, family or an ideal. The loving person usually lets offenses go quickly and feels no need to dwell on old events or keep old grudges alive. They believe it is better to move on and seize life and seek new experiences instead.
Lovingness is a common character trait among priests, good parents, spouses, teenagers, teachers, aid workers, philan-thropists and doctors.
**Lustful**
Lust and passion are powerful emotions that exist within all people. In some they are so strong that they constantly dominate the mind and sometimes become twisted into perversions and bizarre acts. A lustful person is often easily aroused, horny, single-minded, sexual, erotic, amorous, pleasure-seeking and/or passionate. It is not uncommon for the lust-ful to have odd inclinations, perversions or obsessions due to eager curiosity or past traumas. The lust that drives the person, however it manifests, often becomes the dominant part of the person's life even if it is not always evident on the surface.
Lustfulness is a common character trait among addicts, broken souls, prostitutes, war veterans, crime vic-tims, party animals, socialites, gangster bosses, jaded people, seducers, femme fatales and casanovas.
**Indifferent**
The chaos and suffering surrounding a person can make them become jaded and indifferent to what happens. An indifferent person is often world-weary, unengaged, uninter-ested, cynical, scornful, ironic, sarcastic, lazy, slack and blasé.
The person rarely feels any joy in experiencing new bish, thoughtless, narcissistic and shallow. Many arrogant peo-ple easily tire of people around them and have great difficulty cooperating with others.
Arrogance is a common character trait among bosses, movie stars, gangster bosses, ruthless scientists, domi-nant people, socialites, models and bureaucrats.
**Cautious**
Caution can be due to insecurity, mild paranoia or excessive planning. A cautious person is often discreet, modest, shy, unassuming, simple and forward-thinking. It is not uncommon for the person to be insecure in themselves. Cautious people often avoid getting into combat or dangerous situations – some would call them cowardly or timid but often it is the cautious who survive longest in Noir's harsh world.
Caution is a common character trait among hermits, cowed people, academics, priests, scientists, par-ents, servants and submissive people.
**Greedy**
The fire of greed burns in many hearts. Without money and power one is defenseless and the pursuit of influence, status and wealth often makes the greedy ignore morality and laws. Even though greed can ultimately pay off, many greedy bastards lose their friends along the way and instead gain hordes of enemies. Greed often leads to spiritual loneliness.
Greed is a common character trait among gangster boss-es, executives, bankers, financiers, socialites and gamblers.
**Honorable**
Honor is a virtue that is all too rare in the harsh and cruel world Noir takes place in. The honorable often become exploited and over time bitter and withdrawn but there are those who hold ideals high throughout life.
An honorable person is often righteous, loyal, just, faith-ful, noble, upright, reliable, honest, steadfast, dependable, selfless, solidary and/or comradely. Only in emergencies or for a higher purpose does the honorable break a promise or abandon a friend.
Honor is a common character trait among auditors, forensic scientists, police officers, prosecutors and key witnesses.
**Vengeful**
The thirst for revenge exists within all people but some trans-form their dark thoughts into reality. A vengeful person is often resentful, bitter, unforgiving, touchy, relent-less and quick to take offense and believes that wrongs must be paid

things. The condescending and joyless attitude that often accompanies such a person is quite enervating for outsiders.
Or the person is simply lazy and listless and chooses to waste their life by loafing around and doing nothing at all during the day.
Indifference is a common trait among war vete-rans, seasoned police officers, jaded doctors, ambulance per-sonnel, divorced people, and spoiled teenagers.
**Suspicious**
In a world of shadows, violence, and desire, a certain degree of suspicion is required to survive. However, there are those who are completely ruled by this trait. A suspicious person is insecure, distrustful, critical, doubting, question-ing, and skeptical. Even envy and jealousy belong here. In more extreme cases, suspicion takes the form of paranoia. The person is suspicious of strangers, and tries to keep to themselves when out among people.
Suspicion is a common trait among street dwellers, criminals, police officers, bureaucrats, lonely people, and betrayed individuals.
**Deceitful**
Deceit is a dark and destructive force that is deeply rooted in every person. If it takes complete hold of a person, they often become selfish, ruthless, lying, false, unreliable, cunning, treacherous, sly, smug, unscru-pulous, hardened, and cold. Deceitful people are everywhere in the dark world of Noir. People who first and foremost look out for their own gain and who wouldn't blink at selling their children as slaves if the price were right. Deceitful-ness usually stems from selfishness — granted, the deceitful themselves may see it as the only way to survive, but it also ensures they remain alone, as deceitfulness almost unfail-ingly crushes every relationship with other people.
Deceitfulness is a common trait among con artists, swindlers, gamblers, gangsters, unfaithful swine, spies, femme fatales, traitors, war criminals, smugglers, human traffickers, and criminals.
**Risk-Prone**
There are those who don't shy away from danger but rather seek it out. A risk-prone person is often brave, bold, wild, daring, fearless, crazy, proud, self-confident, and often has a great deal of integrity and courage. Virtually nothing scares the risk-prone, and they dare perform insane heroic deeds to achieve results, without much thought for their own safety. No odds are too impossible and no challenge too difficult. The risk-prone aren't afraid to argu-e for their case, even if their opinions will create conflicts and friction with others. Of course, the risk is great that the person gets in over their head and ends up in greater difficulties than they can handle.
Risk-proneness is a common trait among pilots, heroes, firefighters, police officers, adventurers, desperate crimi-nals, businessmen, lawyers, and managers.
**Convicted**
Nothing strengthens the soul and will as much as personal con-viction. A convicted person is certain, assured, believing, determined, rigid, and inflexible when it comes to the conviction's nature. Not infrequently, the person is hard to persuade, rather fana-tical, and relentless when it comes to argumentation or attacks against what the conviction concerns. Being convicted means having a deep belief in something, a mantra, a path in life, or a view of how the world works and looks. It could be a police officer who sees the law as the only right thing, a priest who has seen the One's glory, a soldier who has the fatherland's well-being in their heart, or an agitator who hates the State's oppression.
It can also involve superstition — there is much unknown in the dark alleys, the sewers' damp tunnels, and the vast wastelands that spread out beyond the cities.
The conviction helps the person move forward in life, it constitutes a fixed point in the chaos and a clear goal to strive for. Attacks on the conviction usually fall flat or are dismissed as idiotic or uninformed statements.
Conviction is a common trait among reporters, police officers, soldiers, pilots, lone wolves, homeless people, athletes, self-proclaimed prophets, and rural folk.
**Using Traits**
In situations where a trait could influence a person's choices and reasoning, the narrator can let the trait provide a bonus or penalty to relevant dice rolls.
Of course, players can also suggest when traits should be used in this way.
If a person finds themselves in a situation where the player thinks the person's trait should be able to influence
— positively or negatively — the narrator must judge whether this is the case. It is always the narrator who ultimately decides whether a trait can be used or not. If the trait is used, the affected rolls receive a penalty or bonus of one (±1). In cases where multiple traits affect the same situation, the total bonus or penalty can at most be five (±5). This also applies to Stronger influence, see page 229.
Using traits during the story should be encouraged, and a player who portrays their character based on their traits should be rewarded when experience is distributed.
The difference between traits and drives
It can be difficult to see the difference between traits and drives.
The difference is that traits show how a person is, while a drive shows what a person wants.
"So, what do you want to drink?"
"I told you I have a husband."
"He can have a drink too."
"He's not here, he's away on business."
"Perfect. We can toast to his honor?"
"He's only home on weekends."
"You know, I like him better and better."
— Gabriel Jandaran and Marlena Illianescu

### Trait Negative Influence

Aggressive Can make a bad first impression, may have difficulty charming someone and creating personal or business contacts, people become anxious and insecure in the person's presence and don't trust them.
Arrogant Gives the impression of being condescending and snide, may have difficulty charming someone and initiating personal or business contacts.
Cautious Is afraid of combat or of being hurt, avoids conflicts
Greedy Can be perceived as unreliable and/or disloyal, can easily be bribed or lured with riches
Honorable Cannot consider bending moral and ethical rules, meets disapproval in interaction with dishonorable people
Vengeful Can make a bad first impression, can be perceived as emotion-ally cold, is easily misled when a chance for revenge presents itself
Loving Stands up for themselves, has limited freedom of action when loved ones are threatened, has a strong desire to care for the vulnerable
Indifferent Perceived as apathetic and cynical, often seen as unpleas-ant and emotionally cold and may have difficulty functioning normally in social situations
Lust-driven Easily tempted with sex, can be persuaded and manipulated by a sexual or passionate individual
Suspicious Can make a bad first impression, has difficulty building relationships, has difficulty perceiving things beyond what occupies their attention
Deceitful Has difficulty maintaining contacts and friends, easily tempted to switch sides or sell information
Risk-Prone Doesn't hesitate before dangerous situations, has difficulty resisting bets or challenges, can easily drag others into danger
Convicted Is strong in thought and conviction
Table 1:51
**Suggested Influences**
Traits can influence positively or negatively, tables 1:50 and 1:51 provide suggestions.
**Trait Positive Influence**
Aggressive Is threatening, good at fighting and hurting others, doesn't back down in threatening situations
Arrogant Creates insecurity and inspires respect/fear in others, refuses to admit when hope is lost
Cautious Inspires confidence in some, has patience in stressful situations, is thoughtful when planning
Greedy Often sees opportunity for profit in all situations, doesn't hesitate to bend rules and regulations to reach their goals
Honorable Makes a positive impression, inspires confidence, is easy to trust
Vengeful Is determined, has a good sense of planning, can find extra willpower and inspiration when a revenge opportunity presents itself.
Loving Makes a good first impression, has an easy time creating and maintaining relationships, finds it easy to get people to open up, is a safe embrace to cry in
Indifferent Resists surprises, is not affected by shocking events, doesn't feel ill when loved ones are harmed
Lust-driven Dares to hit on "impossible" targets, has an easy time creating new contacts, makes a good impression on certain partners
Suspicious Sees through lies and bullshit, assumes traps and ambushes are the rule rather than the exception, is prepared for dangers
Deceitful Has no qualms of conscience, takes all chances, turns their coat with the wind.
Risk-Prone Doesn't give up in seemingly impossible situations, dares to perform crazy maneuvers, stays cool in danger
Convicted Is strong in thought and conviction
Table 1:50
**Changing Traits**
It can happen that a player doesn't roleplay their main character based on their traits.
The narrator should then make the player aware of traits that no longer feel relevant so they can be changed. In the same way, new traits can be established. This is motivated by the main character having developed in a new direction and gained new values or behaviors
— something that isn't at all unusual in
Noir's tangible world. The most important thing is still that the main character's traits feel right for the player so they can be portrayed in a good and constructive way.

### Adding New Traits

The traits included in the book should be seen as a selection from the infinite number of traits that exist. The traits have been chosen because they fit the mood the game wishes to convey — a harsh, dark world where desire, lies, sex, violence, obsession, betrayal, and revenge rule.
Players and narrators are free to add more traits themselves. However, one should try to create traits that are grounded in the world and are easy to use. One suggestion is to start from the following five requirements when creating a new trait:
• The trait should be value-neutral, that is, it should not in itself be positive or negative but should be able to influence a person's actions in both directions.
• The trait should be clear to the player, who should understand at a glance what the trait means.
• The trait should have a mechanical impact on the rules that is understandable and easy to use.
• The trait should be part of the story. The narrator should be able to create stories in which the main characters' traits can take place and be used as a starting point for new stories or substories.
• The trait should fit the game and its mood.

### Character Traits in Combat

Personal conviction can move mountains. A strong conviction, a burning love, or a seething hatred can drive people to perform actions that would otherwise be seen as impossible. A father can run through a burning house to save his daughter, a woman can drag her injured husband for miles to reach a hospital, and a friend can throw themselves into icy water to pull up their comrade who just fell in.
Both protagonists and prominent narrator characters have character traits that help define their personalities, motivations and life goals, but also their weaknesses and desires. In combat, these character traits can help or hinder the execution of a plan. If a player's protagonist has, for example, the character trait Loving with expression toward his beloved, he can in a situation where his fiancée is directly threatened choose to add his value in the character trait as a bonus to a dice roll that results in the threat becoming less.

A narrator must decide how much the character traits factor into combat. Ideally, bonuses from character traits should only be used when something truly matters in a person's life that they care about. It should not be used in every other combat situation, but saved for more climactic events so that the result becomes incredibly spectacular — like when the antagonist lets their hatred pour out over the hero, when the protagonist saves their beloved from a hail of bullets with the help of their strong faith in their love, or when a companion's loyalty is so strong that it affects the outcome of a firefight.

Suggestions for use in general combat situations
It's not unthinkable that a player wants to use a person's character trait in just about every combat — especially if the protagonist is Aggressive, Risk-prone or Cautious.
A simple approach is to let the character trait be
"active" throughout the entire combat. If, for example, Aggressive or
Risk-prone is used, bonuses are given on all Initiative actions and penalties on all Defense actions, while Cautious works the opposite way.

### Expression

The character traits' expressions provide increased depth and an extra dimension to the person. They specify how and in which situations the character traits primarily manifest, as well as how they affect the person, and thereby make it easier for the player to immerse themselves in and portray their protagonist.

All chosen character traits shall have at least one expression but they can have several, usually between one and three.
Character traits can of course also affect situations to which they lack specified expressions, if it is deemed reasonable and in the person's nature.

### Suggested Expressions

To facilitate the choice of expressions, here follows a selection. Note that the following are only suggestions and that an enterprising player or narrator can surely come up with more expressions.
In cases where one must choose a specific alternative, it refers to something very limited that often stems from a distinct event or experience in the person's life. If the person has the trait "Suspicious (of the Police)" then they have most likely had bad experiences from previous contacts with the police, and this has given rise to the expression.

The specific expressions that are appropriate (of course depending on situation and character trait) are:
• Specific activity
• Specific object
• Specific company
• Specific phenomenon
• Specific group
• Specific individual
• Specific circle
• Specific organization

### Examples of Expressions

Examples of what expressions character traits can take and how they can be interpreted follow here:
• Vengeful (loathes wife-beaters, painful punishments) — The person loathes people who prove to be wife-beaters, and when opportunity for revenge presents itself, it will likely be brutal, harsh and painful.
• Very aggressive (murderer, brutal) — The person enjoys killing and has no problem injuring or killing others in excessive and bestial ways. Often little provocation is needed for deadly situations to arise.
• Very loving (their wife, their children) — The person is passionate about their family and does everything in their power to make things easier for and support them.
• Extremely convinced (immovable, the State's excellence) — The person refuses to accept criticism of the State and attacks with strong frenzy those who attack their conviction.
• Extremely risk-prone (death-defying, likes to fly, prefers bad odds) — The person enters without hesitation into violent situations where they risk being maimed or killed. The person is moreover strongly attracted to piloting aircraft and thrives like a fish in water when the odds speak against them.
The person is probably happiest when they alone face six enemy aircraft just as they have low fuel and nearly empty machine guns.

### Stronger Impact

The character traits can be made more pronounced and take up even greater space in the person's life and personality. By giving a character trait the prefix very or extremely, one raises its degree of impact. For example, a person instead of Honorable and Aggressive can be Very honorable and
Extremely aggressive.

A person should not have more than two character traits with the prefix very or one with the prefix extremely, unless exceptionally good reasons exist.

A character trait that has the prefix very gives a penalty or bonus of two (±2). In the same way, a character trait that has the prefix extreme gives a penalty or bonus of four
(±4).

Keep in mind that character traits that are extreme have a very large impact on how the person acts, so they should be used sparingly and with consideration. People with several extreme character traits tend to be difficult to play and exaggerated, and this can easily destroy the story. The narrator must approve all character traits that have prefixes.

Example: Mordekai is "Very lust-driven (prefers very young women)". When a challenging girl comes up to him at the smoky bar counter inside the nightclub Needle's
Eye, where he's watching a suspected child murderer, he quickly becomes aware that the woman is trying to seduce him in a rather forceful way.

The narrator judges that Mordekai must make a resistance roll (Resist) to resist the tempting seductress, but because the woman is pushy, young and obvious, the narrator decides that Mordekai's roll gets a penalty of two (−2).
This is because the prefix "very" before Mordekai's character trait
Lust-driven gives value two and the narrator considers that the trait very clearly works against Mordekai in the situation — it is after all a young woman who's hitting on him.

The woman wins the resistance roll thanks to this and Mordekai, wildly entwined by a young woman in a dark booth far back in the venue, misses that the suspect leaves the nightclub with a rattling medical bag in hand.

Everyone is driven by something, an inner desire that must be satisfied, personal goals that must be met, or a personal agenda that must be followed. The path toward fulfillment is often selfish and full of danger and violence as different people's drives clash with each other — often with fatal outcome.

### Drives

A person can possess one or several drives — goals the person wants to fulfill in their life. The drives must be fulfilled for the person to become satisfied and be able to move on. The purpose of drives is to give the characters their own goals to strive toward so that they become a more active part in the world they act in. When a drive is fulfilled, this grants Experience.

### Measurable Drives

It's important to remember that there are tons of drives a person can be thought to have, but what's crucial from a rules perspective is that the drives are measurable. More generally held drives like for example making friends or becoming popular are difficult to measure. Therefore they are not included in the listing below. Narrators and players can of course jointly work around this by trying to set up measurable goals within hard-to-measure drives and bring them among the choices.

### Secret Drives

Many times players will want to keep their protagonists' drives secret from other players and their protagonists. Such drives should be discussed with the narrator in private and possibly not recorded in the character sheet. It could be about schemes and intrigues that are directed at people within one's own group, or that the player simply doesn't want their protagonist's innermost thoughts exposed. Many times it can be exciting to try to figure out what drives the people in one's surroundings, and as a player knowing what one's protagonist doesn't know can kill most of the mystique.

### Drive in a Group

Many of the presented drives can be seen as individualistic. But nothing prevents the entire group from having a shared drive — it's just for each person to note the same drive in their character sheet. Everyone then gets to share in the fulfillment and effects when such occur.

**Drives**

Gain Forgiveness/Attain Redemption
"It wasn't supposed to happen, by the End, no, it can't have happened, can't, no, NO!"
The person suffers from guilt over an event or action and seeks atonement. He/she has difficulty living with what happened and therefore tries to gain forgiveness from someone affected or perhaps from themselves. Others wish to attain redemption through the higher power's grace.

Find Love/Feel Security
"Do you, Mattius Rein, take this Fransceska Segeda, to be your wife until you die, regardless of circum-stances or experiences, in the End's name?"
The person wants to find someone to grow old with or find security, romance, and companionship in another way. It can also involve achieving harmony in life and no longer having any enemies.

Destroy/Kill
"Doctor Bavory has hereby forfeited his right to live and I am his judge, jury, and executioner."
Someone or something has wronged the person so badly that blood must be spilled for the person to gain satisfaction. It can involve an individual or a group of people, but it can also be an organization or a company that symbolizes the person's hate and violation.

Attain Position/Prove/Achieve Goal
"And with these words I confirm your promotion.
Congratulations, Police Captain Tristarian!"
The person needs to prove to someone or to themselves that he/
she is capable of reaching a goal in life. It can be attaining a posi-tion within one's profession, showing that one can handle life as a soldier, or achieving a scientific breakthrough with research one has been mocked for. It can be becoming a famous artist, making the front page, or perhaps recording an album. It can also involve things like having sex with a new partner every day or helping the poor by working in soup kitchens.

### Self-Actualization

"If you keep training your strength at this pace you'll soon be unrecognizable."
The person wishes to reach perfection within some area or field. This is symbolized by him/her reaching the level of out-standing in a chosen attribute or master in an ability.

### Suggested Drives

Here various drives a person can possess are described. Feel free to use them as inspiration to come up with more drives that can fit into the story.

Expose/Combat/Oppose
"If it's the last thing I do, I will expose to the world what you are!"
For some reason, such as personal conviction or pathos, the person wants to reveal what someone or something is or stands for, with clear evidence supporting the exposure. It can involve a person, a group, a company, a phenomenon, criminality, poor working conditions, or something completely different. A public exposure usually occurs as a scoop in newspapers or radio or via a public trial, while a private exposure occurs before a person or group.

Defeat/Gain Vindication
"And therefore, Mr. Konstantin, I am right and you are, and have always been, wrong, are you not?"
An injustice has been committed and the person has sworn to repay it. The person wants at any cost to defeat an enemy and thereby gain vindication and clear their own or someone else's name, reputation, and honor. The enemy can be an individual, a group, or an organization. The factors surrounding it all vary but it can involve something as simple as getting an apology from the one who insulted, or larger conditions can be involved, where debts from several generations must be paid. Without victory, life is not worth living.

Protect/Help/Save
"I can't leave her, not now, not like this."
The person has decided to defend someone or something for one reason or another. It can be as a bodyguard for someone, rescuing someone who has been kidnapped, generally protecting women from rapists, or children from slavery, or perhaps pro-tecting an illegal operation from the police with all available means.

Bring/Return/Deliver
"A statuette to the Ministry of Truth, Chief Inspector Kamarov.
Okay, understood, I'll be done by Thursday, have the money ready."
The person is driven by returning or delivering an object, a person, or knowledge to a client or to its rightful family, owner, or place. Until it is done, no peace can be found.

### Vision and Abuse of the Rules

**The idea with drives is to let** the players' characters have their own, clear goals in the stories. The drives exist to give rise to increased drama and pres-ence in the game. There are certainly ways to abuse the rules and the narrator must be prepared to put their foot down and stop such attempts. Drives are not something one should fulfill as many of as possible just to get rewards.

**The narrator must keep watch** so that group drives become reasonable and support the story and that the players don't try to trick with the rules to maximize the benefit. Be prepared to deny a person a drive if it doesn't feel right from the story's perspective that the person has it.

Harm/Disfigure
"He gave Emilia irreparable scars on her soul through his vile actions. It is a mild justice that I give him scars all over his body. Can you pass me the jug of acid?"
For some reason the victim wishes to harm and/or disfigure the target of the drive. It can be according to the classic maxim "An eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth," or it can involve some other form of justice, such as cutting off the genitals of a rapist.

Search/Seek/Find
"Have you seen the woman in the photo? No?
Sure? Look again, please, look again!"
The person is searching for someone or something. A lost love from the past, a brother presumed missing at the front, or a colleague who has vanished without a trace. Perhaps it's a coveted object that eludes the person – a special wine, a film reel, a stamp, a peregrine falcon, or perhaps the last component in an experiment. It can also involve a missing heirloom, such as grandmother's pearl brooch. Special knowledge can also be included, such as obtaining an address, the solution to a puzzle, or perhaps one's own adoption papers. Until the object, person, or knowledge is found, the person is unsatisfied.

Experience
"Is this your first time flying in an airplane, Professor?"
The person craves something special, it can be romance with a certain person or a number of people for a certain time.
Perhaps it involves rising in the hierarchy within the crime family one belongs to. It can involve a special experience, such as climbing the highest peak of the Arkady massif, doing a loop with an airplane, firing an artillery piece, meeting a clan head, making a pilgrimage to Georgior's monastery, visiting the northern trenches, being present at the New Year's ball at
Marchalk Square, or perhaps seeing the innermost vaults of the ministry archives.

Dishonor/Humiliate
"When I'm done your wife will have left you, your children will hate you, and your par-ents will regret the day they conceived you."
For justice to be served, the target of the injustice must be humiliated, either before themselves, the avenger, or a third party – usually someone the original violation was about, for example a lover in a love triangle. Or the violated one wants to humiliate their victim before his/her friends, acquaintances, or associates.

Repay/Pay Back
"Here are thirty of the fifty I owe, you'll get the rest in five days, I promise. This time it can't go wrong."
The person owes someone or something a favor, a promise, money, or their life, and wants nothing more than to repay this debt.

### A Drive's Birth

During a story's course, situations arise that give birth to drives. When such a situation reveals itself, a person may choose to take up the drive as their own, provided that he/she has room for it – see Impact. If not, the person can attempt to abandon an existing drive. This is described under
Abandon Drive.

When a drive is born, this should be roleplayed by the player, so that the drive becomes part of the story and something worth striving for. Everyone should understand why a drive comes into being and understand the person's motivation for the drive.

All new drives are written down in the character sheet and should also be explained. Generally, a maximum of one drive is born per scene, but variations can occur in special cases.

When a person is created, he/she gets one drive, see page 31.

Example: Mordekai Remus has two drives, namely "Find
Stelia Lock" and "Destroy everyone who was involved in the mur-der of Janya." As the drives are written, their goals are clear –
when Miss Lock is found, the first drive is fulfilled, and when everyone who was part of the conspiracy that led to Mordekai's youthful love Janya's death is dead, the second drive is fulfilled.

### Scope

A drive's scope can be either limited or continuous. Most are limited, which means they disappear when they are fulfilled.
Continuous drives remain even after they are fulfilled; this mainly applies to drives whose goal is long-term or can be affected over a long time, for example protecting a person.

Example: Mordekai Remus has two drives, according to the exam-ple above. The drives are both considered limited since there is no reason to keep them once they have been fulfilled. If the second drive were such that the goal itself was each individual's death, then it would have been a continuous drive, but now the wording is such that it is at the point when all involved are dead that the goal is fulfilled and thus the drive is limited.

### Impact

A drive can have one of three degrees of impact. These are, from mildest to most pronounced: subdued, burning, and manic. A person has room to at most possess drives that total five points.

• A subdued drive takes up one point
• A burning drive takes up two points
• A manic drive takes up three points

### Strengthen Impact

When a drive is born, the person must set its impact to either subdued, burning, or manic. The impact must be chosen directly and cannot be changed later. The only exception is if the person already has a drive and during a story ends up in a situation, or gains access to new knowledge, that directly affects the drive – then its impact can be increased if there is room for it (in terms of points).

If the drive is continuous, it remains in the character sheet. For example, Walian Kardoni's drive to kill all shadow walkers is a typical continuous drive, as there is really no end to it but each time a shadow walker dies by Kardoni's hands it counts as fulfilled. A continuous drive can be fulfilled at most once per scene.

### Long and tough road to fulfillment

A narrator who believes that the path a main character has walked to finally reach fulfillment was both long, perilous and character-defining can without hesitation give up to five times the amount of points at the moment of fulfillment. A burning limited drive would then give 75 points instead of 15 points.

### Multiple fulfillments

Even if several different drives would be fulfilled at one and the same time, the person can only receive fulfillment from one of them simultaneously. A person can for example have the drives "Seek out Anannia's jewels" and "Bring the one who stole Anannia's jewels to justice".
