It is not impossible for these two drives to be fulfilled simultaneously, but then the person only receives points from the one that gives the most.

### Fulfillment through roleplaying

The narrator may encounter players who play their main characters as if they had drives but who for some reason don't spend any time specifying these. Perhaps they feel it would take them out of their role to do so, they may have difficulty choosing a limited number of drives, they may have forgotten to do it or they may simply be uninterested in the system for how to use drives. The game master can then choose to give these players points for portrayed fulfilled drives even if it is not specified when the drives are fulfilled.

### Abandon drive

A defined drive can only disappear in two ways. Either through fate willing it – "it was not written in the stars that it should happen" (this can happen at most once per game session and to get rid of the drive the person then consumes as much Experience as the fulfillment of the drive could have given). Or through the drive's goal suddenly becoming impossible to reach (if the drive for example is to return Irvan Lesarov's famous painting Moonseye to Felix van Kordar and the painting in question burns up) or if the drive is deemed unreasonable or unnecessary (if for example one has as a drive to protect someone from a person and that person is killed or imprisoned).

When a drive disappears without the person wanting it to, the person suffers stress equivalent to a psychological trauma, see page 244.

Example: Walian Kardoni received the drive "Kill all shadow walkers you encounter" when his family was mutilated by a group of shadow walkers. The drive was then subdued. Later in life, after Kardoni had succeeded in killing several shadow walkers, he found love again, but the happiness was short-lived. A shadow walker violated his new love and ate her up. When Kardoni discovered this, his drive grew to manic.

### Degree of influence

A subdued drive lies simmering in the back of the person's mind and unconsciously or consciously guides his/her choices and decisions. There are no problems temporarily breaking the thoughts or focusing on something else, but sooner or later the thoughts will return to the drive.

A burning drive is often present with the person and influences his/her everyday life in a tangible way. The person is still free to disconnect its influence, but has difficulty concentrating on other things for longer periods without falling under its influence.

A manic drive takes over the person's ego and steers him/her toward the goal day after day, night after night, hour after hour. Few things can disturb the thought of fulfilling the drive, of getting an outlet for it, of reaching the end and feeling the victory. Other phenomena are seen as secondary or insignificant in the context, especially when the end can be sensed.

Example: Mordekai Remus has two drives, as shown above. It is determined that it is clearly more important for Mordekai to find the murderers than to locate the assassin Lock. The drives are noted as follows: "Find Stelia Lock (subdued)" and "Destroy everyone who was involved in the murder of Janya (burning)".

### Fulfillment

When a person receives a drive, its goal shall be clearly stated. Therefore the formulation is important. When the goal is fulfilled, the drive has given the person a revelation, a satisfaction and a calm.

This is symbolized by the person receiving points that can be used to increase Experience or Well-being, according to table 1:52. Each point can either be translated into one point of Experience or for ten points, Well-being can be improved one level (this means that for subdued or burning continuous drives, Well-being cannot be improved, as the points are not sufficient – this comes naturally as continuous drives are usually not emotionally sound).

### Limited Continuous

Subdued 5 points 3 points
Burning 15 points 6 points
Manic 50 points 20 points
Table 1:52

When a limited drive is fulfilled it disappears and can be struck from the character sheet. If the fulfilled drive is continuous, the situation and the narrator decide whether it is still active or if it is definitively fulfilled and can thus be struck. When a limited drive is fulfilled, an identical drive cannot be born again in the person, unless very special reasons exist.

"What are you going to do?"
"I will kill them all. Everyone who was involved in it. Everyone who profited from it. Everyone who tries to stop me. Everyone who talks back."
"My god. You're going to kill everyone..."
– Sandrine Makovy and Mordekai Remus

Conviction & frustration
In situations that have a direct connection to a drive, the narrator can let the person receive a penalty or bonus on dramatic or reasonable activities in the scene that promote or threaten the drive's fulfillment. The modification is linked to the drive's influence, so a subdued drive gives penalty/bonus (–1/+1), a burning (–2/+2) and a manic (–4/+4).

The modification applies to direct connections to fulfillment, preferably in such a way that the person can really imagine that the goal is within reach. This symbolizes the person's inner strength and will to complete something that has long been driving in his/her life.

Such a connection can also create frustration and a feeling of powerlessness in the scene if a person feels that the drive is close to being fulfilled but that something is hindering or disturbing. An example could be wanting to kill a person but when reaching him/her there are 300 witnesses nearby which makes it a physical or at least social suicide to fulfill the drive in a reasonable and credible way.

Example: Gabriel has the continuous drive "Protect Anya Malov (subdued)". In situations where he actually protects her from various misfortunes he receives a bonus (+1). In the same way he receives a penalty (–1) in situations where he experiences that he should protect Anya but cannot do so.

### Find strength

A dazed or mortally wounded person, or one suffering from low Well-being, can in a scene where a drive is close to being fulfilled use this motivation to accomplish dramatic feats. It means that the person finds new strength in desperation, rises above the pain and anxiety and continues to fight to reach the goal of the drive.

The person can choose between rising and doing what he/she must do and for a short time focus on a single final task, or resignedly giving up under the injuries, bleeding, pain, exhaustion, poisoning, drug influence, hopelessness, despair or terror and just withdrawing, collapsing, admitting defeat and wasting away.

If the person chooses to pull themselves together to manage the final task, he/she temporarily counts their Well-being as Relaxed (10). Additionally, the person gets to act even if he/she would be dazed or mortally wounded. All other rules still apply as usual: the person dies from an additional Wound if he/she is already mortally wounded, the person dies when the rounds before death arrives have passed, and the total penalty the person has from their injuries applies.

Note that Find strength can only be activated for actions performed in direct purpose of fulfilling the drive. Should the person deviate from the goal the slightest bit, the effect disappears and the person can no longer act. When the person mentally releases the drive's focus, he/she can no longer resist the pain and fatigue or manage to push their body to extreme levels for any less noble purpose than the fulfillment of the drive. If the person in any way tries to leave the scene or perform any action that doesn't directly work for the fulfillment of the drive, the effects cease immediately.

Usually it only works to find strength from drives that were born before the current scene arose. This is to prevent someone from birthing a new drive as a response to an action that just occurred.

Example: The reporter Tremaro has the drive "Expose Franko Davidov", a shady businessman that Tremaro has written tons of articles about. Tremaro is now close to the big scoop. In Davidov's fancy office there is a dossier with detailed information about how Davidov records illegal films with drugged refugees who are subjected to various forms of humiliation and abuse.

Tremaro is in the process of photographing the contents of the dossier when the door behind him opens. There stands Davidov with a revolver in hand, which immediately blinds the reporter with a flash of light and a dull bang is heard as he is hurled across the desk and tears down most of what stood there onto the floor, including a telephone. The bullet hits badly in the middle of his chest and Tremaro, who is not a physically strong person, becomes mortally wounded by the attack and thus cannot act at all, except to lie whimpering on the floor.

The large-built Davidov steps in, turns on the lamp and grins triumphantly. Tremaro lies still and feels that he will soon die. Davidov starts talking to him and tells with a smug grin about all the dirty businesses he is behind – since Tremaro won't get any use from the knowledge anyway. Davidov continues talking while he goes to the bar counter on the other side of the room to pour liquor. Tremaro now wants to pull himself together and find strength – he hates Davidov and wants to expose him to everyone in the whole world.

The narrator wonders what Tremaro has in mind and the plan is that while Davidov has his back to him, he wants to call his editor-in-chief, who he knows records all calls, to let the receiver lie off while Davidov reveals everything about everything. The narrator thinks it's dramatic, fateful and a really good idea so Tremaro finds strength in the scene.

A weak hand grabs the telephone receiver and bloody fingers dial a phone number without being discovered by Davidov. Tremaro collapses, coughing, and the receiver falls silently onto the thick carpet. The editor-in-chief is met with a surprise when one of Sandukar's most notorious businessmen reveals both this and that, all while a stiffly smiling Tremaro is slowly received by death's cold darkness, with the knowledge that he got his nemesis in the end.

### The drama in finding strength

Find strength allows main characters (and also central narrator characters, if the narrator so wishes) to act even when the odds are against them. The mortally wounded policeman who has as a drive to protect a witness can pull himself together and shoot the murderer with his last strength. The psychologically broken mother who has seen her husband rape their daughter can get the opportunity to avenge the crime if this is her drive.

Since the effect is activated when the person is already knocked out and dying (mortally wounded or dazed persons normally do not get to act) or if the person is broken for some reason (low Well-being), he/she has nothing to lose anyway when the situation arises. Instead it becomes a memorable and personal ending – regardless of whether it succeeds or fails.

### Abuse around Find strength

The rules around Find strength are made to allow drama at special and fateful occasions. They are not made to be a lifeline away from danger and if the narrator notices a player trying to exploit the rules in this way, it's just to directly let the found strength disappear and let the person lie crawling, unable to act.

The headlights from a passing car illuminated the alley where Anya Malov kept hidden. She pressed herself closer to the damp brick wall and kept her gaze fixed on Hotel Black Lantern, one of Hexagon's infamous opium dens.

Something told her that the man she was looking for,
Fredrick Hesser, would be here. He had slipped through her fingers time and again but opium's iron grip he could not escape. Anya waited until the car had passed before she silently ran out of the alley and across the street to the hotel's fire escape. The thick fog that had rolled in from the sea helped conceal her and the night sky's clouds hung low and threatening.

She grabbed hold of the rusty ladder and began climbing upward. The bolts holding it fastened to the brick wall creaked. The windows beside the ladder were all boarded up but through the cracks she could see into filthy rooms where pale men and women inhaled opium through long pipes. Most of them slept, others moved in stupor, they all wanted to get away, they all wanted to forget.

Anya climbed further to the hotel's top floor which housed the private rooms and the brothel.

She opened a small window at the top of the ladder with the help of her pocket knife. She crawled into the dark room and the stench of urine and stale sewage immediately hit her. She had ended up in a small bathroom with a filthy toilet and a cracked bathtub, the cockroaches moved like black spots on the dirty tiles. Silently she made her way out into the corridor.

The hotel's top floor was poorly lit, most of the light bulbs were broken, the others flickered, the walls were covered with damp stains and torn wallpaper. The doors to the various rooms were peeling and cracked
— from some rooms came cries of delirium and from others moans and creaking beds. Anya peeked around the corner where the corridor split. She realized immediately that her intuition had led her right. Outside one of the doors sat Fredrick Hesser's short, stocky body-guard on a wooden chair reading a dog-eared porno mag.
His underbite, bull neck and shaved head made him resemble a bulldog. A handful of empty beer bottles stood at his feet and he was just downing the last of a lukewarm Redov.

Anya kept still and waited, going after him in the middle of the corridor would have been too risky —
gunfire would attract Hexagon's people. She didn't need to wait long, though, soon the bodyguard put down the porno mag and stood up. Anya retreated to the toilet and picked up a rusty fire extinguisher from the corridor.

When the bodyguard had entered the stinking space and reached out his hand to turn on the light, Anya struck him in the back of the head with the fire extinguisher — a metallic clang echoed in the room and the bodyguard fell to his knees. He didn't pass out though but let out a muffled scream and began drawing a revolver from his shoulder holster. The second blow hit him in the neck, the force made him knock out his teeth against the toilet rim and he collapsed unconscious. She locked his hands behind his back with handcuffs and picked up his revolver. Quickly she moved out into the corridor and to the door the body-guard had been watching. She opened it carefully and slipped in.

The room was dim, only lit by a few thick candles, and the sweet opium smoke hung so thick that one could cut through it with a knife. On a divan among blood-red cushions and thick tapestries lay Fredrick
Hesser's sickly figure. She approached him and looked down at his gaunt face, the half-open mouth and the closed eyes. He was covered in sweat and breathed raspingly and as if he sensed her presence in his opium dreams he began to stir.
Anya took a step back and raised the revolver toward him but Fredrick's eyes were still closed.

The ash-gray hand with its yellowed nails fumbled for the opium pipe on the table by his side and
Anya let the barrel of the bodyguard's revolver take its place.
As if in a trance, Hesser brought the oily revolver barrel to his mouth and closed his cracked lips around the muzzle to draw smoke into his lungs — even in this sleeping state opium held him in its grip.

The click when Anya cocked the hammer made him open his eyes and the hazy gaze transformed into surprise when she coldly informed him that he was under arrest.

The goddess of fate may choose to smile or turn away from those she fleetingly rules over. Her will is that little extra that can make the scales tip over. That which makes the coin land on edge, that one manages to catch with fingertips on the roof gutter when falling from a roof or that one takes the stairs and it later turns out that the elevator was sabotaged.

### Fate

In Noir, Fate plays a major role as the protagonists are in various ways chosen in the story. Not infrequently, Fate's whims play a decisive role in critical situations. Fate guides and influences more or less discreetly the protagonists and the story's significant narrator characters. Ordinary people in the world are rarely affected by her notions.

Fate can be seen as a kind of currency that players have available to actively influence and interact in a story. It gives them opportunities to handle fantastic challenges, perform spectacular maneuvers or simply have incredible luck in critical situations.

Fate should be seen as a tool for building drama and tension in the story in a way that has mechanical connections, to increase fairness and balance between narrator and players.

### Use

All situations where Fate is involved must be motivated in the story in some dramatic way. Additionally it must occur with reasonableness and within the story's framework. If these criteria are not met, the narrator can decide that Fate may not be used.

Generally, Fate may never be used to directly counteract or circumvent an event or situation that is created with the help of Fate. This is described further on page 239.

### The protagonists' fate

Each protagonist can at most possess 25 points of Fate. More than that, events cannot be influenced. All protagonists start with 10 points of Fate when they are created, see page 87. A protagonist's Fate is personal and cannot be used to influence other protagonists'
situations, unless this is explicitly mentioned — see Rescue on page 241.

One's Fate can be improved by using Experience, this is described on page 223. There are also other

**Fate**

situations where a protagonist's Fate improves, primarily when the narrator activates the protagonist's limitations or lures them into ruin.

The phenomena a protagonist can use Fate for are:

• Protect • Grit
• Change situation • Inspiration
• Knows a contact • Play the odds
• Rescue • Survive

### The narrator's fate pool

The narrator has access to a pool of Fate, called the Fate pool. All narrator-initiated Fate influence comes from the Fate pool. Narrator characters have no personal points of Fate but use the shared Fate pool's points.

Common narrator characters often lack the ability to influence their Fate and therefore rarely expend points from the Fate pool.

### The fate pool's size

At each story's start, the narrator has a Fate pool consisting of the same number of points as the protagonist who has the most in Fate, added with the number of protagonists.

Each time any of the protagonists improve their
Fate by one or more points, the narrator gets equally many new points to add to the Fate pool.

The Fate pool can never consist of more than 25 points.

Example: At a story start, three protagonists participate.
Kallous is the one who has the most Fate, 24 points. This means that the narrator gets 27 points of Fate (24 + 3 for the number of protagonists), but since the maximum Fate pool is 25, the sum is adjusted to 25.

After a number of scenes in the story, the narrator has 12 Fate points left in the Fate pool. Kallous improves his Fate through
Experience and gets five Fate points — the narrator then gets equally many and increases the Fate pool to 17.

The phenomena the narrator's Fate pool can be used for are

• Setback • Protect
• Grit • Change situation
• Knows a contact* • Play the odds
• Survive

* Special use, see "Force a contact on a protagonist" on page 241.

### How the narrator can use Fate

The narrator advantageously uses the Fate pool's points to guide a story in the desired direction, through divine interventions. Often it is an advantage to begin such situations with words like "Fate plays you a trick..." for then the players realize that the narrator has used their Fate pool to ensure that a situation that may seem a bit strange or contrived occurs, and this creates better understanding than when the narrator arbitrarily takes over a scene.

Example: The narrator has a Fate pool of 25 points available.
Jessca is chasing an assassin through a ministry archive. The assassin runs into an office and knocks down a bookshelf that blocks the door. This means that the narrator uses
Fate points for "Change situation, minor influence" (as the bookshelf was excellently placed on the narrator's map), see page 239.

The narrator lowers their Fate pool by three, to 22. The reason the narrator uses Fate points is that the story becomes more dramatic if Jessca doesn't catch the assassin at this stage.

The narrator says to Jessca: "When you reach it you hear a crash and dust swirls up between the door and the threshold.
The door, which opens inward, is blocked and can at most be opened a few centimeters. From inside comes sounds of furniture moving. You realize it will take time to push aside what's blocking the door.
You feel how fate grins mockingly at the back of your neck."

### What Fate does

It is possible to influence a variety of situations by playing Fate's game and hoping that she smiles upon you. Here various things that Fate can be used for are described, as well as the amount of Fate that is expended upon use.

Setback (3 points of Fate)

A protagonist suffers a setback when the narrator activates one of the protagonist's limitations.
A protagonist can at most suffer one setback per scene, regardless of where it comes from. As usual, the setback should fit into the scene and story and be reasonable. Each time a setback strikes a protagonist, regardless of whether it's activated or not, their Fate improves by one point.
This point does not, however, give any points to the Fate pool.

Example: Jon Hargartian has an ailment — a bad heart. In a scene he chases his nemesis, the child murderer Rarkov, through a warehouse. Suddenly Jon sees how Rarkov is about to escape in a car. Jon raises his revolver and just then he suffers a setback. The narrator crosses out three points and Jon must roll to see if the ailment kicks in, according to the rules on page 243. Additionally Jon gets to improve his Fate by one point.

The whole thing is reasonable and dramatic — Jon has been running after his nemesis in the scene and now, when he has the chance to take him, fate laughs in his face...

### Fate rewards

If a protagonist behaves or acts in an exciting, dramatic or simply fitting way in a scene, the narrator can immediately reward them by improving their Fate by one point.

The clock was a few minutes before midnight and the time for the meeting was close at hand.
On the closed bridge between the Icon and the Fallen
City waited Sergy Rulov and his entourage. The cars'
headlights illuminated the rusty steel construction and the chilly north wind made cables and beams creak and groan. The wind carried with it a smell of sulfur from the Machine Cities and against the dark cloud masses the silhouette of a zeppelin on its way out towards the wastelands stood out.
Rulov drew his Karev Riekon and checked the magazine before returning it to the shoulder holster.
His men were on edge and they were all armed to the teeth in case something should go wrong—or if an opportunity should present itself.
Finally several car headlights became visible, slowly approaching from the other side of the bridge, the cars slowed down and stopped a few dozen meters away. Rulov nodded briefly to his bodyguards, opened the car doors and stepped out into the biting wind. The crew in the other cars followed his example and positioned themselves in a semicircle with submachine guns and shotguns hidden under their heavy coats.
Rulov told his men to stay alert. He let his gaze sweep over his closest bodyguards. Petrov whose light gray coat fluttered in the wind, Rudel who kept a firm grip on his Calder under the brown jacket and the suit-clad Milo who openly held the pump-action shotgun.
They were good men and they were ready to follow his orders to the last.
Then finally movement appeared at the vehicles on the opposite side, the Hexagon's people stepped out, archaically dressed men and women in suits, high shirt collars and long dresses—all dressed in black and all with gloves on their hands. They carried silver lanterns whose flames were protected by lead-set glass and the light cast surreal shadows on their faces. They stood there and waited. A white-clad man stepped confidently out of one of the cars, the Hexagon's foremost assassin, he carried a submachine gun in his hands and let his vigilant gaze sweep over the Rulov gang.
Sergy suddenly became impatient and called out that he was there to meet the Hexagon's leader and not his henchmen. Silence met him—the black-clad individuals on the other side stood motionless—not even the wind seemed to tug at their clothes. Sergy looked nervously at the clock, three minutes to twelve. If the leader didn't show up he would assume it was a trap and he certainly didn't intend to negotiate with simple lackeys. Admittedly the Hexagon's people were as numerous as they were but Sergy didn't doubt that his crew was better armed and had better combat experience. The Hexagon's members would pay in blood if they had lured him to a meeting with their mythical leader, the Engineer, and he didn't deign to show up.
Then he heard it, the sound of horse hooves clattering against the asphalt, a sound he hadn't heard since childhood in Darost. The cold wind subsided and the night fell into an uncomfortable stillness. The shadows seemed to grow and deepen while the car headlights'
artificial glow became ever weaker. Sergy's men looked around uneasily and some irresolutely drew their weapons.
The lights slowly faded only to then go out completely and the only glow came from the silver lanterns that the black-clad figures from the Hexagon carried. Everything was shrouded in darkness.
The clattering came closer and behind the Hexagon's vehicles appeared a dark carriage drawn by a team of four pitch-black horses. The carriage swung up in front of the vehicles and stopped. A hunched person sat on the driver's box with a mask over their face.
Sergy swallowed, without having thought it through he had drawn the submachine gun and now held it in a firm grip in front of him. His men looked tensely at him and he curtly told them to stay calm. He returned the weapon to the holster and stepped forward a few steps, flanked by his three bodyguards.
The door to the carriage opened and out stepped a platinum blonde woman in an old-fashioned dress with a corset. When she turned her gaze toward Sergy he saw that half her face was youthful and beautiful while the other half was scarred and grotesque. After her stepped out an older man whom Sergy realized must be the
Engineer. The man was tall and thin, his bony hand rested on a cane whose knob was shaped like a silver skull and his face had almost the same appearance.
The worst part, however, were the lifeless eyes that firmly met Sergy Rulov's gaze.
Somewhere far away a church bell could be heard tolling for midnight.

Protect (3 Fate points)
A person can take on an attack or a harmful situation that another person suffers. This is done after the situation has been established but before damage has been determined. It costs the protector three Fate points to receive the damage. The original target escapes completely without harm.
Note that in the narrative it must be reasonable that the protection is carried out, for example the person should be in proximity (within Combat Movement) of the one to be protected, and be aware of what is about to happen. Protecting someone in this way is a noble act and should be described in the narrative in a dramatic and inspiring way.
If the protecting person should die as a direct consequence of the situation, a protagonist created in his/her place automatically receives 25 Fate points from the start as a gift from the smiling goddess.
Example: Gabriel and Anya are investigating a warehouse in the harbor. When they open the door to the office they hear a metallic sound. Gabriel is the only one who succeeds with a difficulty roll (Awareness) and thus the only one to perceive an unsecured hand grenade in the doorway. He reacts lightning fast by firmly and forcefully shoving Anya away over a couple of crates when the explosion sweeps the area in fire, heat and shockwave.
Gabriel crosses off three Fate points for the protection and takes damage from the detonating grenade twice—once because he himself stood in its blast radius and once more because he took the hit that Anya would have received.
Anya rises unharmed thanks to Gabriel's protection and finds him shortly thereafter severely burned, with his smoldering overcoat smeared against a container wall, and barely alive. She smiles warmly at him, kisses his cracked lips, lifts him up and supports him out of the premises in search of a taxi.
Grit Your Teeth (1 Fate point)
The person can use one Fate point to be able to act in a round even though he/she is mortally wounded or dazed. All penalties for Wounds and Dazed apply as usual. The person can continue to expend Fate to act in more rounds while mortally wounded, as long as he/she has Fate to use. Fate cannot be used in the same round that a mortally wounded person dies. Nor can it be used in the same round that the person becomes stabilized.
When the person grits their teeth it should be evident from the portrayal how agonizing it is and how battered the person is.
The person is after all still mortally wounded and near death or alternatively near unconsciousness.
Example: Makasty was stabbed by his lover Keya when he refused to pay for her abortion. The stab went badly and made
Makasty mortally wounded and he will die in twelve rounds. His Initiative is automatically set to one. During the second round he grits his teeth, crosses off one Fate point and tries to assess the situation. His Initiative increases to 8 and he sees how Keya is throwing her belongings into a bag, while she curses at him. In round three Makasty continues to grit his teeth and painfully draws his revolver from the back holster.
In round four he shoots the very surprised Keya in the head with clenched teeth so that she tumbles into a closet, stone dead.
When round five begins Makasty has no Fate points left but collapses and heavily drops the short-barreled revolver on the thick bloody carpet. He looks unfocused up at the window and feels how death slowly arrives, all the while the rain relentlessly lashes against the panes.
Alter Situation (3 or 12 Fate points)
The person can noticeably change or affect a situation in a scene through Fate's providence. The situation should be such that it is obvious that it is Fate that determines that the situation changes. The narrator should ensure to keep the balance in the narrative at the right level. An altered situation consumes three Fate points.
A general rule is that a person cannot use Fate to counteract or combat an effect that has already been created with the help of Fate.
Example: Since Karim Darkov has used Fate to barricade the door with a crossbar, Darkov's enemy may not use Fate to knock away the crossbar or affect the door in a way that would negate the reasonable effect of the crossbar. However, of course the door may be attacked in the usual way with gunfire, kicks or axe blows, but then without Fate's involvement.
Furthermore, Fate cannot change situations or events that have already occurred or that have been established, thus those that are already clearly established in the narrative. If it is established that a safe deposit box is empty, then Fate cannot be used to make it full of money. However, Fate can be used to let someone find money in a safe deposit box that is not yet established in the narrative—if it is deemed reasonable in the scene.
Fate very rarely causes direct damage to, or attacks on, a person so such proposals should be assessed carefully, and their effect should fit very well into the scene and narrative for them to be allowed to be created. Influence that leads to direct damage to a narrator character costs twelve Fate points to activate instead of the three required for non-damaging influence.
A narrator can always veto when a player wishes to use Fate to change a situation if the influence is deemed unreasonable or falls outside the scene's framework. The Fate points are then not expended.
**Examples of alterations**
As always, the alteration must be reasonable and fall within the narrative's framework. Players and narrator are encouraged to come up with situations and types of influence themselves, but some suggestions follow here.
Introduce an unimportant narrator character:
• When the protagonists desperately watch for an individual by a house in the slums that the individual has a vague connection to, the individual actually comes out through the gate.
• When the protagonists are threatened by the villain's gorillas inside an office, a night watchman just happens to pass by to see it all.
• When the protagonists are chased by enemies one early morning they are saved by a newspaper delivery person who suddenly comes around the corner and happens to trip up the enemy.
**Reasonableness**
All situations where Fate is mixed in must be motivated in the narrative in some dramatic way. Furthermore it must occur with reasonableness and within the scene's and narrative's framework. If this is not fulfilled the narrator can without hesitation decide that Fate may not be used.

• When the villains are searching for the protagonists in a warehouse, police sirens suddenly sound outside the window — it's a patrol car that received an alert from some unknown person who observed suspicious activity.
Introduce object/location:
• The person discovers a secret passage between two already known locations in a fortress.
• The person finds a spare key to a suspect's apartment in the wall lamp by the front door.
• A car happens to pass in the night rain and blinds an individual who is watching toward the person's hiding place.
• A person stranded in the wilderness after a plane crash remembers that his/her captain from the army actually has a cabin nearby.
Manipulate an object's function:
• The person manages to position a shotgun as a bar for a door so the enemy is prevented from getting through.
• The person manages to shake the flashlight to life when it goes out due to dead batteries.
• An enemy's magazine happens to fall out of the weapon when he/she aims at the person.
• The person gets a broken telephone to work tolerably for a brief desperate call.
Escape indirect harm or death:
• The person finds a branch to try to grab hold of when he/she is thrown over a cliff.
• The person manages to find a piece of furniture to use as cover when the bomb detonates.
• The person lands in a swimming pool after a jump from the seventh floor of a hotel.
• A person who has been hanged by the enemy manages to smash a nearby window and with the help of a glass shard between the feet cut through the ropes.
Example: Anya comes home after a shift, takes off her clothes and steps into the shower. She hears the front door creak.
The scene is already defined such that Anya has removed her clothes, and with them her weapons. She now sacrifices three Fate points so that her shoulder holster hangs by the sink next to the shower so she can easily get hold of her automatic pistol. The narrator has no problem with this as it doesn't take focus from the scene.
Had Anya not sacrificed any points, the narrator would have thought that all clothes, including the shoulder holster, would lie on her bed, which is where they reasonably ought to be. But Fate apparently wanted it otherwise.
Example: Gabriel is being chased by the enemy up in the Arkady massif.
He has no ammunition left and is becoming desperate. He finds an old hunting cabin and breaks in through a window.
Gabriel sacrifices three Fate points to find an old hunting rifle in the cabin. The narrator judges it as reasonable and within the framework of the story and has no objections.
Example: Talia Navarre is driving home in her car when she is pursued by a motorcyclist. She tries to escape but no matter what she does the pursuer stays on her tail. Since she must meet a contact at a certain time she doesn't have time for more cat-and-mouse games but wants to use Fate for help along the way. She asks the narrator if it's okay that the motorcyclist suddenly drives into a curb and gets a flat tire on the front wheel? The narrator judges that it's not impossible, but fairly unlikely, but approves it anyway as it makes the scene dramatic.
Since the reasonable effect is that the pursuer will injure himself, at risk of death, from the change in the scene, Talia must strike off twelve Fate points. She then vividly describes how the motorcyclist tries to shoot her back in a rain-soaked intersection but misjudges the situation, rips the tire off the wheel, slides down on the side and with full force crashes into a bakery on the other side of the street.
Inspiration (1 Fate point per person in the group)
Situations may arise in a story when the protagonists have gotten stuck. They may have missed an essential clue, forgotten a detail from a conversation, or not achieved the success expected. Then inspiration can be the solution to move forward. If the protagonist group sacrifices as many Fate points as the number of people in the group, the narrator can give a relatively clear clue, preferably in the form of an inspiring and dramatic aha-moment that describes how the case is cracked, how the solution reveals itself, or how the puzzle piece suddenly falls into place.
Note that each group member doesn't have to pay one point each, but that the group as a whole must sacrifice a certain sum — this means someone can sacrifice two points while someone else doesn't need to sacrifice any points.
Example: The three investigators Kallous, Irvan and Jesimea have completely gotten stuck in their criminal investigation. They ask for inspiration and the narrator says it requires three Fate points. Irvan sacrifices two and Jesimea sacrifices one for the group to reach the three points. The narrator says that the perfume-soaked handkerchief with sewn-on monogram that was found at the murder scene might be identified by someone whose profession is to manufacture or sell perfumes. Jesimea immediately starts looking in the phone directory to follow up the lead.
Knows a contact (5, 10 or 15 Fate points)
The protagonists know a narrator character who is considered significant to the scene and the story. Once per scene a protagonist can establish a narrator character as a contact — it could be an old friend, a companion from before, an army buddy, an ex, a relative, a business contact, an acquaintance, a rival, an enemy, or similar. The most important thing is that the contact, when established, must not feel unreasonable or misplaced in the story. That a criminal detective knows the police director isn't strange, but if a homeless vagrant claims he is best friends with a clan patriarch this should be questioned by the narrator. A good explanation can of course make the contact real.
A protagonist may at most establish one contact per scene. When the contact is established, this must occur in a way that fits the story and must be motivated by the player, preferably in the form of a monologue or a flashback.
This introduction of the contact should include name, location, function/occupation, and how he/she knows the protagonist. It should preferably create a new dramatic situation in the ongoing story.

Rescue (2-10 Fate points)
Situations can arise in which a person stands without
Fate's blessing when it really matters. Another person can then sacrifice their own Fate to assist the unfortunate one. For every other Fate point sacrificed in this way, the unfortunate one immediately gets to use one Fate point. The one who rescues the unfortunate one may at most sacrifice ten points in this way. The one who is rescued must not have their own Fate remaining.
Note that in the story it must be reasonable that rescue can occur. For example, the person should be nearby and notice the unfortunate one's predicament. The rescue should occur in a dramatic and reasonable way in the story, perhaps through an exchange of weary glances, an understanding nod, a thumbs up, or something else that shows that a rescue actually occurs.
Example: Nevei stands on a rooftop in the rain and observes the mortally wounded Gabriel, who slowly feels his will to live draining from him. Before Nevei stands Anya on her knees with Nevei's sword blade against the side of her neck. Gabriel has no Fate left after the wild battle that has unfolded and cannot act. Anya meets
Gabriel's dim gaze tearfully and forms a soundless "I love you" with trembling lips. This is the signal for Anya to rescue
Gabriel (and hopefully herself) by expending two Fate points so Gabriel can immediately use one. He uses it to grit his teeth and manages to raise the automatic pistol toward Nevei, who is just lifting the blade. In the rain, with the pain piercing his head and with a trembling hand, he presses the trigger and the pistol's thunder cuts through the rain's drumming as Nevei jerks and drops the sword when the bullet strikes its blade.
Survive (3-15 Fate points)
A person who knows he/she will probably be injured can lower the number of Wounds the situation inflicts by one per three Fate points (–1 Wound/3 Fate points). A maximum of 15
points can be expended in this way. The number of points must be determined before the damage effect is confirmed, and even if the number of Fate points would lower the number of Wounds to zero or less, the victim always receives at least one Wound. If the situation results in no Wounds being caused, the expended Fate is wasted.
How this occurs in the story must be described dramatically.
Survive can be used during executions (see page 271).
Example: Anya takes off with one of the witnesses to a bestial murder with connections to one of the clans. The rain pours down over the two and when they come around a corner they are met by an assassin who fires a shot at Anya. The shot hits and Anya, who feels she must survive, sacrifices
9 Fate points and is lucky — the shot turns out to inflict exactly three Wounds on her, which is reduced to zero. But since she must suffer at least one Wound if Wounds arise, she still receives one Wound.
In the story this is described as the bullet taking her right in the chest but her police badge she has hanging around her neck manages to stop it enough that its destructive effect becomes clearly less than one would have thought. She manages to kick the weapon from the assassin's hand and flees headlong down an alley with the witness in a firm grip.
The amount of Fate expended determines the contact's Influence.
Five points gives Minor influence, ten points give
Moderate influence, and 15 points show that the contact is
Influential. A difficulty roll (Impression) is then rolled to determine the person's relationship to the contact. This involves a certain risk-taking as the relationship doesn't become known until the contact has been established — an old friend may very well turn out to be hostile. For more information on this, see page 209.
The player can choose whether the contact has blood ties with the protagonist or if he/she is a family member.
The contact is now written into the character sheet. Once a contact has been established, it is maintained, together with relationship and influence, and can be used in other stories or scenes without problem, by both player and narrator.
Example: Mordekai is in trouble and needs to hide. He suddenly remembers an old flame he had when young, Klarysa
Hagi — a theater girl who hung around the loft above the nightclub
"The High Hat". She and Mordekai had a fiery affair for a couple of months many years ago. This is sufficient to establish Klarysa as a contact in the story.
Mordekai strikes off five Fate points and gives Klarysa Minor influence which feels reasonable given the backstory.
Then the difficulty roll (Impression) is made. The result becomes a failure whereby Klarysa stands Disapproving toward Mordekai.
Mordekai shrugs and mumbles something about "not everyone can love you", while he figures out arguments to convince her to let him hide at her place for a couple of days.
Play the odds (1-5 points, alt. 4 Fate points)
A person may expend Fate to increase their chances of succeeding at difficulty and resistance rolls. This is usually called
"betting the odds". For each Fate point used, the person gets to roll an extra die in the roll. A maximum of five points may be used in a single roll. The player then chooses which two dice results to use, other dice are ignored.
A player can also choose to spend four Fate points to roll an extra die after a roll has been made.
This die can then be used to replace one of the dice in the result, if desired.
Example: Mordekai fires snapshots at an axe-wielding onrushing maniac. He must hit on the first shot as he must make it to a meeting place to get information about
Janya's killer. A maximum of five Fate points are sacrificed. Seven dice are rolled and the results are 1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 7, 10.
There are two sensible choices to make in this situation.
Either Mordekai chooses the two sevens and hopes that it's enough to hit, in which case it becomes an exceptionally successful roll (exceptionally failed if the result isn't sufficient).
Or he chooses one of the sevens and the ten which are maxed so another die gets to be rolled. The five dice that aren't chosen are ignored and removed from the roll.
Example: Jessca makes a difficulty roll (Stamina) to manage to keep running. The dice show 1 and 1 — an exceptional failure! This cannot happen as it's critical that she reaches the goal in time. She sacrifices four Fate points and rolls one more die, which shows 7. She removes one of the ones and replaces it with the seven which means she succeeds with the roll.
**Forcing a contact**
**on a protagonist**
Troublesome situations can arise where the narrator wants a protagonist to know a narrator character, even though the protagonist hadn't intended this. If a narrator establishes a relationship in this forceful way, the customary amount of Fate is struck from the Fate pool and functions thereafter as if the protagonist Knows a contact. The protagonist's Fate also improves by one point due to the intervention.

A human body can become sick, injured or killed and likewise the human psyche can be broken down and destroyed. Many cannot handle existence's strains but sink into depression and destructiveness where suicide seems to be the easiest way out.
**Wellbeing**
A person's well-being, degree of relaxation, general condition and state of mind are collectively called
Wellbeing. Wellbeing can vary between eleven different levels, from Relaxed to Catatonic.
Stressful experiences, physically exhausting work or other influence worsens Wellbeing while calm and quiet, security and sleep improve it.
When Wellbeing worsens, this can give penalties to all possible, reasonable actions. The penalty symbolizes stress, nervousness, exhaustion, fatigue, confusion, sleep deprivation or similar. See table 1:53.
**Wellbeing Penalty**
Relaxed (10) —
Composed (9) —
Troubled (8) —
Tense (7) —
Uneasy (6) —
Stressed (5) –1*
Strained (4) –2*
Weary (3) –3*
Tormented (2) –4*
Desperate (1) –5*
Catatonic (0) –10*
Table 1:53 * The person only suffers the penalty that applies at the current level of Wellbeing — the penalties are not cumulative.
**Maximum Wellbeing**
Certain events can lower a person's Maximum Wellbeing.
A person's Wellbeing can never be higher than Maximum Wellbeing.
Usually a person has both Wellbeing and Maximum
Wellbeing at Relaxed (10). For a person's
Maximum Wellbeing to fall often requires extreme experiences and the afflicted person is then not seldom depressed, traumatized, disturbed or otherwise broken.
Wellbeing zero (Catatonic)
A person who feels so bad that he/she has Wellbeing level zero must once per day, usually right after any recovery of Wellbeing has been checked,
**Wellbeing**

rats, make an unmodified difficulty check (Willpower). If the check fails, the person dies due to the condition, for exam-ple from starvation, from the psyche or body giving up, or from the person simply taking their own life.
**Wellbeing Recovery**
Wellbeing can at best be at the same level as Maxi-mum Wellbeing, and at lowest at the Catatonic level (0). If
Maximum Wellbeing is lower than Relaxed (10), a difficulty check (Sanity) must be made once per day, usually directly after a longer sleep or rest period. This check is modified by the penalty for current Wellbeing as well as depending on how the rest period looked, according to table 1:54.
The result from the difficulty check indicates how much Well-being is affected.
Exceptionally successful: The person's Wellbeing improves by as many levels as the value on the lowest die, to at most the person's Maximum Wellbeing. If the person's
Maximum Wellbeing is under ten, it improves one level.
Successful: The person's Wellbeing improves one level.
Failed: The person's Wellbeing does not change.
Exceptionally failed: The person's Wellbeing dete-riorates one level.
**Penalty Circumstances**
±0 Rested for approximately eight hours in a calm and safe environment
−2 Rested for approximately six hours, or in a disturbing environment
−5 Rested for approximately four hours, or in an unsafe environment
−10 Rested for less than four hours, or in a chaotic environment
Table 1:54
**Therapy**
A person can get help raising their wellbeing through therapy with a therapist. At least one hour of conversation is required. The therapist makes a difficulty check (Medicine) to see if the person's
Wellbeing is affected.
Exceptionally successful check: The patient gets a bonus (+10)
on the next difficulty check for Wellbeing recovery.
Successful check: The patient gets a bonus (+5) on the next difficulty check for Wellbeing recovery.
Failed check: The patient is not helped by the session.
Exceptionally failed check: The patient gets a penalty
(−5) on the next difficulty check for Wellbeing recovery.
**Love**
A person who is happily in love or beloved gets a bonus (+5)
on difficulty checks for Wellbeing recovery. Unhappy or unrequited love gives a penalty (−2).
**Physical Exhaustion**
A person who struggles with heavy physical labor, marches at high pace, suffers from limiting illnesses or handi-caps or is exposed to inhospitable climate, weather and wind, risks being struck by exhaustion. Physical exhaustion is rarely as dangerous to health in the long term as stress is, but can cause temporary obstacles in action.
Note that physical exhaustion primarily applies to rela-tively prolonged activity — even if it's tough to run a kilometer, it doesn't cause any mechanical exhaustion. A guideline is that an activity should be ongoing for at least four hours for the rules to apply.
**Exhaustion Procedure**
When a person is struck by exhaustion, a difficulty check must be made. At most, the check is made once per four hours.
Depending on the situation or event, different abilities are best suited for the check.
Stamina is appropriate for fatigue, exertion and bodily endurance.
Tolerance is appropriate for illness, poisoning and exposure to the elements.
When the appropriate ability has been determined, the check is made, pos-sibly modified depending on how serious or extensive the exhausting situation is judged to have been. The per-son's Wellbeing also plays a role. The result of the difficulty check can cause the person to be struck by an impairment for a certain time. More about different types of impairment and their effects can be found on page 244.
Exceptionally successful check: The person withstands the exhaus-tion and does not need to roll again for similar exhaustion for the rest of the game session.
Successful check: The person withstands the exhaustion.
Failed check: The person is struck by a minor impair-ment.
Exceptionally failed check: The person is struck by a serious impairment.
**Impairment**
There are two different levels of impairment — minor and serious. Minor impairment usually passes with time.
Serious impairment can cause long-lasting consequences with long recovery periods. Often it also causes the person's Maximum Wellbeing to deteriorate.
There are different categories of exhaustion, depending on source — freezing in a snowstorm is different from being forced to march in harsh terrain for a full day. The narrator and player must judge which category seems reasonable in each individual situation, based on the information given in table 1:55.
Sex & pleasure
Just like love, sex can also improve a person's Wellbeing. By feeding a drive that is fulfilled through intercourse or similar sexual activities, the person is given the opportunity to improve their Wellbeing. More about drives can be found on page 230.

### Category Minor Serious Example

Physically drained Worn Out Exhausted Marching, long-distance running, swimming, hard riding, mountain climbing, heavy physical labor
(for example building houses or digging ditches), carrying/dragging heavy loads.
Exposure to elements Troubled Beset Heavy rain, snow or sunshine, extreme humidity, cold or heat.
Personal ailment Hindering Life-threatening When an ailment is activated.
Table 1:55
**Effects of Impairment**
The person is struck by damage through a damage value. This can represent overexertion, fainting, disorienta-tion, confusion, hyperventilation, muscle or joint injuries or perhaps sprains.
The person's Wellbeing deteriorates as balance and calm are disrupted. In serious impairment, the person's Maxi-mum Wellbeing can also drop — it symbolizes the more long-term effects of the exhaustion. When it is mentioned in the impairment that Wellbeing deteriorates by values from a die, this refers to the difficulty check that was made to check if the person was affected by the exhaustion. All types of impair-ment can lower Wellbeing to Catatonic (0) and the person risks dying, see page 242.
**Different Types of Impairment**
Description of impairments.
**Beset**
The person's Maximum Wellbeing deteriorates (−1). The physical strain represents damage value B1T.
**Troubled**
The person's Wellbeing deteriorates (−1).
**Hindering**
An ailment is activated and the person is struck by the ailment's penalty (see page 82) in all reasonable activities for the rest of the scene.
**Life-threatening**
An ailment is activated and the person is struck by the ailment's penalty (see page 82) in all reasonable activities for the rest of the scene. Additionally, the person receives a Wound.
**Worn Out**
The person's Wellbeing deteriorates (−1). The physical strain represents damage value B1T.
**Exhausted**
The person's Maximum Wellbeing deteriorates by as much as the value on the lowest die in the check. The physical strain represents damage value B2T.
**Psychological Exhaustion**
"I if anyone have seen into evil people and felt their destructive power. I am a victim and a victor, for without the insight I would still have been small and lost."
— Jessica Navarre, after a heated dispute with Mordekai Remus
A person who is exposed to pressing situations, terror-filled experiences, nasty surprises, horrific abus-es, brutal violence or deep violations risks being struck by negative and limiting psychological effects from this. Such events or situations are collectively called stress.
**Stress Procedure**
When a person is struck by stress, a difficulty check must be made.
Depending on the situation or event, different abilities are best suited for the check.
Resistance is appropriate for withstanding mental strain, such as experiencing violence, terror or abuse.
Sanity is appropriate for withstanding prolonged stress and panic situations as well as for managing to keep one's head cool under pressure.
When the appropriate ability has been determined, the check is made, pos-sibly modified depending on how serious or extensive the stressful situation is judged to be. The person's
Wellbeing also plays a role. The result of the difficulty check can cause the person to be struck by a stress condition for a certain time. More about different stress conditions and their effects can be found on page 245.
Exceptionally successful check: The person withstands the stress and does not need to roll for similar stress again for the rest of the game session.
Successful check: The person withstands the stress.
Failed check: The person is struck by a minor stress condition.
Exceptionally failed check: The person is struck by a serious stress condition.
**Stress Conditions**
There are two different levels of stress conditions — minor and serious. Minor stress conditions usually pass with time when the stress-inducing event has disappeared. Se-rious stress conditions can cause long-lasting effects that require outside help to overcome. Often they also cause the person's Maximum Wellbeing to deteriorate.
There are different categories of stress, depending on the type of impact — being tortured long-term by the enemy gives different types of harm than being forced to face one's severe fear of heights.
The narrator and player must judge which category feels reasonable in each individual situation, based on the infor-mation given in table 1:56.
It may be important to note that the narrator must be subjective in the assessment. If for example two main charac-ters, a war veteran and an accountant, together kill a person, the accountant will most likely be struck by significantly more stress than the desensitized war veteran. Base the assessment on reasonableness from the person's background, previous experiences and psycho-logical profile.

"I have no problem with a reasonable amount of trouble."
— Mordekai Remus

### Category Minor Severe Example

Psychological trauma Shocked Collapse Being violated, subjected to assault/violence/rape, tortured, humiliated, suffering abuse/
degradation
Shocking experience Shaken Terrified Violent shocking event, social shock experience, subjected to threats/harassment, being involved in combat/violence/death/mutilation, injuring/killing/murdering someone, seeing visions/apparitions, sudden awakenings from deep sleep/trance/hypnosis.
Prolonged stress Depression Melancholy Involuntary isolation, being in a war, close friend/relative ill, bullying/ostracism, debt, living in a destructive relationship, being awake for a long time
Personal fear Frightened Panic When a fear is activated
Personal weakness Distraction Obsession When a weakness is activated
Table 1:56
**Effects of being afflicted by stress conditions**
A person's Wellbeing deteriorates when balance, calm and inner harmony are disturbed. In severe stress conditions, the person's Maximum Wellbeing can also decrease — this symbolizes the more long-term effects of the trauma.
When it is mentioned in a stress condition that Wellbeing deteriorates by values from a die, this refers to the difficulty roll made to check whether the person was affected by the stress. All types of stress conditions can lower Wellbeing to Catatonic (0). If this occurs, the person risks dying, see page 242.
The person risks dying or breaking down completely from the experience. This can involve extreme constraint, cerebral infarction, respiratory arrest, stroke/cerebral hemorrhage, palpitations/cardiac arrest/heart attack, coma or permanent apathy.
Ongoing effects, for example daily control rolls to avoid further harm from the experience.
**Different types of stress conditions**
Description of stress conditions.
**Obsession**
See Distraction for general information. The difference is that if the weakness is not satisfied in the scene, the person's
Wellbeing is lowered by as much as the value on the lowest die in the difficulty roll. Additionally, Wellbeing will deteriorate (−1) each day that the obsession is not satisfied, until Wellbeing reaches zero.
**Shocked**
The person's Wellbeing deteriorates by as much as the value on the highest die in the roll.
**Depression**
The person's Maximum Wellbeing deteriorates (−1). Each day the person must make a difficulty roll (Sense) with a bonus
(+5) and if it fails, Wellbeing deteriorates (−1). On an exceptional failure, both Wellbeing and Maximum Wellbeing deteriorate (−1). The stress condition releases when
Maximum Wellbeing reaches Relaxed (10).
**Distraction**
The person stops in thought and action to meet, indulge in, converse with, admire, or otherwise give the target of the weakness their attention. This is a subconscious impulse and the prevailing situation plays no role (except in mortal danger). During the scene where the distraction occurs, the person suffers a penalty equal to the weakness's intensity on all actions that do not directly involve the weakness's target. When the distraction can be judged to be satisfied, this penalty releases. Should the person completely fail to achieve satisfaction in the situation, his/her Wellbeing deteriorates (−1).
**Collapse**
The person's Wellbeing and Maximum Wellbeing deteriorate by as much as the combined value on all dice in the roll. The person is exhausted and must make a difficulty roll (Will to Live) with a penalty from Wellbeing to avoid dying or becoming incurably insane.
**Melancholy**
The person's Maximum Wellbeing deteriorates by as much as the value on the lowest die in the roll. Each day the person must make a difficulty roll (Sense) and if it fails, Wellbeing deteriorates (−1). On an exceptional failure, both Wellbeing and
Maximum Wellbeing deteriorate (−1). The stress condition releases when
Maximum Wellbeing reaches Relaxed (10).
**Panic**
The person's Wellbeing and Maximum Wellbeing deteriorate each by as much as the sum of the value on all dice in the roll. The person must make a difficulty roll (Will to Live) with a penalty from Wellbeing to avoid dying or becoming incurably insane. If the person is not exhausted, one of the usual defense reactions may appear, for example hysteria, flight, paralysis or outward aggression.
**Shaken**
The person's Wellbeing deteriorates by as much as the value on the lowest die in the roll.
**Frightened**
The person's Wellbeing and Maximum Wellbeing deteriorate by as much as the value on the lowest die in the roll.
**Terrified**
The person's Wellbeing deteriorates by as many steps as the value on the highest die in the roll. The person is exhausted and must make a difficulty roll (Will to Live) with a penalty from
Wellbeing to avoid dying or becoming incurably insane.
**Dying from an ailment**
Situations may arise that at first glance may seem strange when it comes to ailments, as an exceptionally failed roll means a risk that a person dies from their ailment. If it is narratively defined that the ailment is not of such nature that it is deadly to the person, such as back problems, a troublesome knee, rheumatism, migraine, etc., it may seem odd that the person receives a Wound from it.
See it then as circumstances outside the ailment's effect itself in the scene giving rise to the Wound. The knee may give way on stairs or cause the person to stumble out in front of a car. A migraine attack may cause the person's vision to blacken so that he/she falls and hits their head on a table edge.
In other words, see it as a life-threatening effect arising from the ailment. In this way, all ailments are dangerous, and not just heart problems or a bad liver are potentially deadly.

While the affluent warm themselves by fires in marble halls, the homeless huddle under filthy blankets to survive the winter nights. A person's standard of living is often the same as his/her social level and the respect he/she receives from society.
**Standard of Living**
A person's standard of living refers to his/her place in society's hierarchy. It indicates birth, wealth, prestige and status. Standard of living is a simplified and deliberately abstract way of indicating a person's lifestyle, everyday life and economic possibilities.
If major upheavals occur during the story's course, the narrator can adjust a person's standard of living based on the information given below. Examples of such events are inheritance, catastrophes, fires, bankruptcies, scandals and prison sentences.
Standard of living is described with a value between zero and ten, where a higher value refers to better status. All new persons created are considered to have standard of living four unless otherwise modified in their background.
Both the players and the narrator are encouraged to use standard of living as a basis when bringing their characters to life and creating places for them in society.
Standard of Living 0
The person is a washed-up street bum without a fixed address.
He/she seeks shelter in sewers, under bridges and in tunnels and runs a great risk of being assaulted, mocked and scorned. A few good souls donate a coin when the person begs for survival while the State cares little about the person and the police beat him/her up for fun. Many of those who have standard of living 0 are mentally ill, insane, handicapped, addicts and/or criminals.
Residence: The street, the sewers.
Assets: One set of tattered, torn clothes in miserable condition.
Cash: 50 zovrins.
Creditworthiness: Cannot borrow cash from anyone.
Advancement: One cannot be moved up to standard of living 0.
Standard of Living 1
The person lacks permanent housing and lives most of their life on the street but has certain connections that mean he/she can sometimes lodge with some better
**Standard of Living**

off unfortunate or at a shelter run by volunteers or the church. Sometimes the person manages to get some miserable odd job that makes everyday life a bit more bearable.
Residence: The street, lodging, shelter.
Assets: One set of worn, shabby clothes in poor condition.
Cash: 250 zovrins.
Creditworthiness: 2,500 zovrins.
Advancement: 5,000 zovrins in cash.
Standard of Living 2
The person is probably temporarily unemployed and still has a roof over their head. Everyday life is tough and grueling and the person often goes hungry but can, thanks to helpful friends and relatives, keep their housing. He/she gets by on shady odd jobs or grueling part-time work.
Residence: Rents a room or a small, miserable apartment in a bad area.
Assets: Two sets of clothes of older style in poor condition. Furniture in miserable condition.
Cash: 1,000 zovrins.
Creditworthiness: 10,000 zovrins.
Advancement: 20,000 zovrins in cash.
Standard of Living 3
The person is probably one of the countless anonymous, unqualified workers struggling with heavy and dirty work in the factories, or perhaps one of the lifetime employees working for slave wages until death relieves them. Perhaps he/she works part-time in a shop or at a small company, as a taxi driver or as a bartender at a slum bar.
Residence: Dingy rental apartment in poor condition.
Assets: A personal car in miserable condition. A wardrobe consisting of half a dozen garments and shoes in poor condition. Furniture in poor condition.
Cash: 2,500 zovrins.
Creditworthiness: 25,000 zovrins.
Advancement: 50,000 zovrins in cash.
Standard of Living 4
The person is counted as low-income but has a reasonably steady job where he/she is considered to have basic qualifications, for example factory work with higher demands or a government job such as police officer. The Empire's soldiers also fit here as compensation for field battles is not high, but lodging and food are included at least. Within this standard of living there are loads of opportunities regarding odd jobs, temporary work, criminal activities and other things where one has shown to be trustworthy and good at what one does. In their free time, the person often hangs out at shady bars to wind down.
Residence: Worn rental apartment in a half-decent area.
Assets: A personal car in poor condition. A wardrobe consisting of half a dozen garments and shoes in worn condition.
Furniture in worn condition.
Cash: 5,000 zovrins.
Creditworthiness: 50,000 zovrins.
Advancement: 100,000 zovrins in cash.
Standard of Living 5
The person is a normal income earner living a bearable life without the immediate struggle to survive or the stress of not being able to pay their bills. Perhaps he/she is self-employed or an office worker within companies or the State. Even experienced police officers can fall into this category. The person amuses themselves at dance clubs, pubs and bars.
Residence: A normal rental apartment in a decent area.
Assets: A personal car in worn condition. A wardrobe consisting of half a dozen garments, shoes and accessories of normal quality. Furniture of normal quality.
Cash: 10,000 zovrins.
Creditworthiness: 100,000 zovrins.
Advancement: 200,000 zovrins in cash.
Standard of Living 6
The person has a stable economy and a secure work situation with room for some luxury in the daily grind, such as restaurant visits and theater tickets. The work, which is full-time, is demanding and includes some responsibility, for example over a group of individuals or for an activity.
Residence: One's own normal apartment.
Assets: A personal car of normal quality. A wardrobe consisting of half a dozen garments, shoes and accessories of normal quality. Furniture of normal quality.
Cash: 25,000 zovrins.
Creditworthiness: 250,000 zovrins.
Advancement: 500,000 zovrins in cash.
Standard of Living 7
The person is well-paid and possesses a good position at their work such as doctor, lawyer, higher officer or successful small business owner. The person is almost counted among the upper class but not quite yet.
Residence: One's own, large, normal apartment in a good area.
Assets: A personal car of excellent quality. A wardrobe consisting of a dozen garments, shoes and accessories of excellent quality. Furniture of normal quality.
Cash: 50,000 zovrins.
Creditworthiness: 500,000 zovrins.
Advancement: 1,000,000 zovrins in cash.

Living Standard 8
The person belongs to the upper class where money and luxury are commonplace. Here you'll find directors, managers and higher officials within the State, together with lesser-known family members. Very successful entrepreneurs and freelancers also fit into this company.
Residence: A very large, excellent apartment in a respectable and quiet area.
Assets: A luxury car of excellent quality. A ward-robe consisting of dozens of garments, shoes and accessories of excellent quality. Furniture and art of excellent quality.
Cash: 100,000 zovrins.
Credit rating: 1,000,000 zovrins.
Advancement: 2,000,000 zovrins in cash.
Living Standard 9
The person belongs to, or mingles among, family personalities, senior executives and state ministry directors. He/she might be a successful film personality or musician who rubs shoulders with the wealthy at elegant parties, receptions and inaugurations. Here you'll also find the elite of high finance in the form of brokers, financiers and bankers.
Residence: An excellent villa or estate, alternatively a luxury apartment in one of the finest areas.
Assets: Two cars (a luxury car and a sports car) of excep-tional quality. A wardrobe consisting of dozens of tailor-made clothes, shoes and accessories of exceptional quali-ty. Furniture and art of excellent quality.
Cash: 250,000 zovrins.
Credit rating: 2,500,000 zovrins.
Advancement: 5,000,000 zovrins in cash.
Living Standard 10
The person belongs to the social elite consisting of promi-nent individuals from the families, the State's highest directors, top diplomats and foremost investigators. They are seen together with business magnates, wealthy heirs and the most prominent bankers, in a constant struggle to achieve ultimate success. One single mistake and the person can only fall downward.
Residence: An exceptional villa, a penthouse in the city, or an estate or castle outside Sandukar. Three excellent overnight apartments in different locations in Sandukar or in some other city. Alternatively a luxury yacht or private island.
Naturally the person owns at least one excellent summer house in the country.
Assets: Three cars (a luxury car, a sports car, a limousine)
of exceptional quality. A wardrobe consisting of two dozen tailor-made garments, shoes and accessories of exceptional quality. Furniture and art of exceptional quality.
Cash: 1,000,000 zovrins.
Credit rating: 10,000,000 zovrins.
Advancement: 20,000,000 zovrins in cash.
**The Concepts**
What the various terms in the living standard template mean.
**Residence**
Indicates where/how the person lives.
**Assets**
Tells what assets are included in the person's household. Upon advancement to a new standard, the person automatically receives the assets specified in the new standard — they are considered included in the advancement cost. The previous assets disappear as they are considered disposed of. Upon a downgrade, all old assets are considered sold or scrapped.
A wardrobe is always included in every living standard.
It is up to the player to define this in greater detail based on the main character's background and occupation.
**Cash**
Shows how much ready money the person has available in the form of cash or quickly liquidated securities. All personal belongings in chapter 3 are acquired with cash.
**Credit rating**
Shows how much the person can borrow without risking losing a living standard. The loans come from family, relatives, friends, banks, credit institutions, pawnbrokers, usurers and/or criminal loan sharks. A person who has borrowed has debts.
**Advancement**
The advancement value indicates how much cash must be invested to move up to the level from the underlying level. It takes the value in the new living standard in number of weeks to reach a new standard level.
Upon advancement, the person discards their old assets and their old residence and records the new residence, the new assets that come with it and the new credit rat-ing in the character sheet instead. However, the person does not receive the cash specified in the new living standard but retains the cash he/she had previously, after the cash invested in the standard is deducted.
Debts, if any exist, are also retained.
A person may only move their living standard one level at a time and may not skip any level.
Loans & debts
A person can go into debt by borrowing money. When a person borrows cash, his/her amount of avail-able cash increases by as much as his/her debt. A person may never go into debt beyond their credit rating — however, interest can drive the debt beyond the credit rating limit.
Interest & repayment
The interest on the debt amount is typically 20% per month (game time), which is added to the debt. Inter-est can bring the debt amount above the person's credit rating limit, which is very risky — see Down-grade.
"If you want to be certain of keeping your position of power, then make sure your citizens and servants are dependent on you every second of their lives."
— Prince Malkom Harakat to Vincent Cateur

A person can at any time convert cash to reduce their debt but must then pay a surcharge of
10% on the transferred amount.
Interest rates other than those stated may apply but this is in that case something that must be determined during the game.
Example: Talia incurs a debt of 20,000 zovrins. After one month it has increased to 24,000 zovrins (20,000 x 1.2) and another month later it is up to 28,800 zovrins
(24,000 x 1.2). Talia now wants to reduce the debt by 10,000 zovrins which costs her 11,000 zovrins (10,000 x 1.1) in cash.
The remaining debt amount totals 18,800 zovrins and increases the following month to 22,560 zovrins (18,800 x 1.2).
Now Talia wants to pay back her entire debt which costs her a total of 24,816 zovrins (22,560 x 1.1).
**Downgrade**
If a person's debt exceeds his/her cre-dit rating limit, the person must, very quickly, usually within a couple of days, reduce their debt to the right side of the limit. Otherwise the person's assets are liquidated.
**Liquidation**
If a person is forced, or chooses to, liquidate their as-sets, his/her living standard is lowered one level. The resi-dence and all assets from the abandoned standard level are struck and considered sold, and the person receives ten times
(10x) the cash sum specified at the abandoned living standard in proceeds.
The new living standard's assets, residence and cre-dit rating should immediately be recorded in the character sheet. The as-sets are considered to have been acquired in the liquidation process. If the liquidation occurred due to the person's debt exceeding the credit rating limit, the per-son must convert enough cash to reduce the debt so it falls below the new limit for credit rating. Additionally, the person should review their finan-cial situation and sort out their personal finances.
Example: Mordekai Remus has a debt of 120,000 zovrins after having gambled quite a bit in the city's seedier gambling dens and the interest has just hit. The debt is over his cre-dit rating and he only has 20 zovrins in cash which makes him realize that he must liquidate his assets to escape the devil's grip of debt.
Mordekai lowers his living standard from 5 to 4. He receives cash equivalent to ten times the cash value at living standard 5, the level he is leaving. This gives him
100,000 zovrins (10,000 x 10). The credit rating at liv-ing standard 4 is 50,000 so his debt already lies
70,000 above the limit.
With his new cash, Mordekai wants to reduce the debt by 90,000 zovrins. This costs him 99,000 zov-rins (90,000 x 1.1). After the transaction he has 1,020 zovrins in cash and a debt of 30,000 zovrins. Additionally he has a worse car, worse residence and worse assets than before the liquidation. If he wants to again reach living standard 5 he must gather 200,000 zovrins in cash and invest them in an advancement.
In the game
The rules for living standard, advancement, downgrade, loans and debt are kept deliberately abstract as it is not intended that
Noir's rules in detail should simulate personal finance and play accountant.
However, the rules presented here can certainly be expanded to become interesting scenes in a story — difficult conversations with shady loan sharks or stern bank clerks who lack understanding when the family home must be foreclosed.
In all major events concerning a main character's financial situation there is naturally loads of bureaucracy — in the form of licenses, contracts, broker fees, lawyers, tax returns, etc.
This is however filtered out in the simple game mechanic that handles financial events.

## Chapter 6 – Combat

The rapid footsteps echoed among the dark alleys.
"Halt, stop, I'll shoot!"
Criminal Inspector Anya Malov pursued single-mindedly after the two men from Rulov's orga-nization. This time they wouldn't get away. She had witnessed the deed with her own eyes. Her partner was shot down when the men tried to escape. She had wounded one of them and in grim triumph she saw the blood trail between the puddles in the alley.
