She saw how her target turned off between two tall tenements. Otherwise the alleys lay deserted. The moonlight gave the area an almost black and white appearance.
She quickly turned into the alley and ducked behind a dumpster when an echoing shot struck the metal. She drew a deep breath and smoothly rose with an automatic pistol in each hand. The larger of them was her partner's, he had asked her to take it. She hoped he would make it, she had left him in the apartment with a gunshot wound to the stomach. She had to catch the criminals. She had sworn that it would happen. Now. Tonight.
"Stop, it's the police, put down your weap..."
She was interrupted by a long burst from an automatic weapon and dove behind the container again. The bullets echoed and bounced against the groaning metal. She heard mutedly how someone shouted a command further ahead. She didn't hesitate when she rolled forward and up on her knees, in the middle of the alley. She saw two men, the wounded one was laboriously trying to get over a fence while the other, a young slim youth, had a submachine gun in his hand. He saw her and raised the weapon but she was faster — both pistols echoed surely a dozen times and the man tumbled lifeless into the fence. The other man looked at her in horror and slowly swung around with a revolver in his hand. Anya rose with the weapons aimed at him.
"Don't even try, Kristo, you don't have a chance, you're coming with me to the Cogwheel and then to the Fort."
Kristo gave her a hazy, contemptuous look. He was bleeding heavily from a wound in his side, the coat was dripping black in the dim light. He seemed to assess his chances. The revolver was still in his hand.
"I've got you in my sights, don't be as stupid as your buddy, drop the weapon and come down."
Anya had her sights on his chest with one pistol while she holstered the other, took out hand-cuffs and tossed them on the ground beneath him.
Kristo sighed deeply and dropped the revolver which landed heavily in a puddle.
"Put them on, and do it now, you scum!"
The man jumped down with a groan and obeyed the command, and the two figures made their way back out toward the main street where ambulance and reinforcements had arrived.

Bullets & hand-to-hand
T he world is hard and cold. Both external and internal darkness are confronted daily. It is an environment where violence lies close at hand and another corpse in an alley just becomes forgotten statistics. Firefights, brawls, assault and rape are part of everyday life when the darkness of night conceals the desperate perpetrators. These criminals are on a blind hunt to find an outlet for their pent-up aggressions and their spiritual anguish.
Outlets that often bring innocent people suffering, pain and death. A person who lacks the ability to effectively defend themselves should consider staying indoors after nightfall.
**To fight**
Since Noir is an action-oriented game, the rules that deal with combat between persons follow the same idea.
The rules are made to be fast and support dramatic confrontations between the main charac-ters and their enemies. The goal is to give very great room for players and narrator to describe what the fights look like and paint the scenes based on what they want to experience together. Therefore the rules are made to be general and open.
The most important thing is that fights feel like excit-ing and dramatic elements in the storytelling and that they move the story forward. One soon sees that the rules for combat follow certain simple and recurring moments precisely so they will be easy to learn and see the consequence of.
The amount of rules for combat is greater than for other moments in Noir. This is because combat is more defini-tive than many other situations and can bring a person to death — which is often a very strong experience narratively and emotionally. Even though the scope of combat rules can be seen as large, the actual rule core is very compact and smooth. This is so it will be simple to learn.
Shootouts & throwing knives
Firefights are common as firearms are easy to come by in Sandukar's alleys. The objec-tive is to make firefights feel tempo-filled and adrenaline-soaked experiences where much lead flies through the air, where devastation is great, where gunsmoke hangs heavy and where persons perform daring maneuvers of spectacular proportions.
Details like how the automatic fire's shell casings feed through the air like a string of pearls in the moonlight, how a blood-red

muzzle flash brutally chases away the shadows in the dark-ened corridor, or how the casing from the fatal shot 
metallically clinks against the polished marble floor, can 
all become memorable moments that those involved remember. 
Details like sounds and scents can make situations even more 
vivid — such as muffled bangs or gunpowder smell.

Don't be afraid to bring to life scenes where people shoot 
at each other — describing misses can be at least as 
dramatic as describing hits. How the bullets slam into 
the damp bricks next to a person so that stone dust 
swirls, how the café's window shatters in a cascade of glass shards 
that whirl through the air, or how the shotgun blasts tear up 
large holes in the plaster walls while the target desperately throws themselves 
aside — such experiences and actions make combat become 
something more than just dice rolls and recording ammunition 
expenditure.

How a person moves during combat is also part of 
the event. Feel free to let the players describe how their main 
characters crawl around behind the worn leather sofa, 
how they hurl themselves through the terrace door to execute a som-ersault and land on their feet, or perhaps how they leap 
up on the bar counter and slide forward while their two heavy 
submachine guns pump lead toward the enemy.

**The protagonists have special talents they** 
can use, even as they struggle against their inner darkness. 
They are the heroes, or at least the antiheroes, in the story 
they are an important part of.

Brawls & knife fights
When the ammunition runs out, fists take over. Close combat of 
various kinds is a common feature in the city's dark embrace. Gen-erally there are two types of close combat — with or without 
weapons. Fistfights are common even though it's 
rarely just fists being used. Most things can be used 
as weapons, so even if you don't carry a knife or a club 
with you, a garbage can lid, a drainpipe, 
a frying pan or why not a telephone can be used to 
put opponents out of fighting condition.

The rules describing close combat are very open 
to allow room for improvisation and portrayal. If 
a wild melee takes place in a dingy hotel room, each attack 
can be executed in numerous ways — a directed jaw punch might 
be followed up by the protagonist grabbing a chair that the play-er says is in the room and smashing it against the enemy 
so that splinters fly around. After this the protagonist 
can grab the enemy and drive them through a vanity mirror. 
Then the enemy is thrown over the bed and into the plaster wall on 
the other side so that dust swirls, followed by them being shoved 
into the bathroom where their head is slammed repeatedly 
against the filthy toilet bowl. Mechanically, 
this is just repeated Brawl actions, but the descriptions 
and improvisations make it much more interesting 
and exciting than just saying "I attack". Therefore encourage 
the players to describe what and how their protag-onists do while they fight in close combat.

One thing to remember is that close combat according to the rules 
doesn't bind people to each other, and even those who 
have fought in close combat can do other things 
in their turn order, such as shooting with weapons. Since it's 
in their turn order, they have initiative at that moment and thus 
get to try to do what they wish.

Brawl is a versatile action as it can be used for 
many things related to close combat. It's princi-pally only the player's or narrator's imagination that sets the limit. 
The Brawl action encompasses everything from holds, 
strangleholds, throwing sand in someone's eyes, tackles, 
pushing aside people trying to block a doorway, 
tripping, sticking the stiletto into someone's side, smash-ing an enemy's skull with a flower vase, etc. Creative 
action should be rewarded and encouraged.

### Success in combat

In most cases when one person wants to attack another person, 
both parties involved will make opposed rolls to 
determine what happens. The general principle is that who-ever succeeds in rolling highest with their roll wins and succeeds in carry-ing out their chosen action, while the opponent fails.

The terms ranged combat and close combat 
are sometimes mentioned. Ranged combat means combat executed with 
the abilities Firearms and Thrown Weapons, while close combat 
is executed with the ability Close Combat.

The term Defense Roll refers to an opposed roll where the attri-bute value from Defense (Ranged) or Defense (Close Combat) 
is used.

### Combat structure

To bring some order to the chaos that combat is, two 
terms are used: round and combat action. A round is a time 
period during which everyone involved in a combat gets the opportunity 
to act. A round is approximately six seconds long, but the 
exact time isn't particularly important. What's important is that everyone 
has time to try to do what they wish.

Combat actions are what a combatant can perform. 
There are two types of combat actions: initiative actions 
and defense actions. When it's your turn to act, you 
perform an initiative action. If a person subjects you to 
an initiative action intended to harm you, you 
get to perform a defense action in response to this. The de-fense action's purpose is to try to make the initiative 
action fail.

### Flow and precise fairness

**The rules presented here** 
are intended to be fast and 
action-packed and shouldn't require 
floor plans and miniatures. 
**Therefore, as narrator, don't be** 
strict about all distances 
and movements — if it seems like half a meter 
is missing when a person wants to attack, let the person reach 
anyway. It's much better 
to maintain flow and pace than 
to start bickering about whether you 
were actually standing one or two meters 
from the desired position.

### Dramatic combat

**A player who creatively and** 
dramatically aids the narrative 
through vivid descriptions can 
be given increased damage output, if 
the narrator so wishes. This is something 
the narrator should be restrictive 
with since otherwise players will expect 
it after routine 
activities.

### General bonuses and penalties in combat

**Special circumstances provide penalties** 
or bonuses in different combat situations. 
It's up to the narrator to determine 
the conditions for this through the 
standard system for bonuses & penalties 
on page 202. Shooting when it's 
dim or foggy might be 
quite difficult while a heavy 
downpour in pitch darkness might actually 
make it very difficult.

### Initiative

Initiative is the term that shows a person's ability to act in a controlled, conscious and decisive manner during 
combat. Initiative determines each involved person's turn order.

People in combat act in descending turn order — the 
one with the highest Initiative begins by choosing and performing their 
initiative action. When the initiative action is completed, 
the next person in turn order performs their initiative action, 
and so on until everyone involved has acted. Then 
the round ends and the next round begins, whereupon 
the one with the highest Initiative acts first again, etc.

When a person performs combat actions, their Ini-tiative can decrease. This symbolizes that the person's 
decisiveness, situational control and combat awareness are reduc-ed, while stress, confusion, nervousness and exhaustion take 
over. Especially being forced to perform defense actions 
reduces one's own ability to control the combat. Injuries 
can also lower a person's Initiative.

### Initial Initiative

At the start of combat, each combatant's Initiative is determined. This 
is done by all participants automatically making a difficulty 
roll (Awareness) simultaneously, and reading the result below.

Exceptional success: The person's Initiative is set to 
double the sum of the total dice result.

Success: The person's Initiative is set to the sum of the 
total dice result.

Failure: The person's Initiative is set to the highest 
die's result.

Exceptional failure: The person's Initiative is set to 
one (1).

The person retains this Initiative for the time being during 
combat but it can change due to special situa-tions, such as action outcomes, or injuries. A person's 
Initiative can never drop below 1. Initiative can be as 
high as anything. Either each person keeps track of their 
own Initiative or you can advantageously download an 
Initiative board from Noir's website.

If several participants end up at the same value in 
Initiative, the one with the highest attribute value in Awareness acts, 
followed by Alertness, Reactions and finally Luck.

### Surprise

If a person is judged to be surprised when combat starts, 
for example during an ambush, they receive a penalty (–10) on the difficulty 
roll (Awareness).

### Low Initiative

If a person's Initiative is five or lower when the person is to 
perform a combat action, the person receives a penalty (–2) on 
it. At Initiative one, the person suffers a penalty (–5) on 
all combat actions, see table 1:57 for a summary. 
This symbolizes among other things that the person doesn't feel they 
have control or overview of the chaos, and becomes 
extremely stressed and/or confused by this.

If Initiative is... the person receives...
6 or higher No penalty
2 to 5 –2
1 –5
Table 1:57

### The Initiative board

**The Initiative board is an administrative** 
tool for keeping track of all 
participants' Initiative. Get as 
many markers as parties and 
place the markers at each 
party's Initiative. When a party's Initiative 
changes, the marker is moved to the 
new Initiative.

**The narrator can advantageously** 
use the same Initiative board 
as the players, and can let one of 
the players be responsible for it 
as relief since the narrator has 
many other tasks during combat.

**The Initiative board is available for download** 
on Noir's website: www.noir.nu.

When are you surprised?
It's up to the narrator to determine 
whether a person is surprised 
by an event or not. There are 
expertises that make it easier for 
people to detect potential 
ambushes and other situations that 
might seem surprising.

### Combat Actions

Combat actions are what a person can perform during combat. There are two types of combat actions: initiative actions and defensive actions.

What actions mean & what they include
As players and narrator, you're meant to portray combat actions and bring them to life. Combat actions are described in the rules in fairly general terms. The intent is that the participant controlling the person who successfully performs a combat action describes what happens within the framework of the scene, story, and action.

Since combat actions often affect people other than oneself, it's also fair game to, within reasonable limits, affect other people during your own action. If a player's character shoots a narrator character, the narrator shouldn't prevent the player's description of what happens to the narrator character, even if it means they're moved or knocked down.

The fact that you can "take over" other people this way without problems works because they always come back on their Initiative in the turn order. The impact is purely dramatic and doesn't affect the mechanical scope for action in combat.

A combat action shouldn't be seen as a single individual maneuver but rather as an attitude toward what you want to accomplish and how. Many performed actions using firearms involve multiple rounds fired—this doesn't mean you go through how each individual bullet hits or misses but means the person has chosen to solve the task in a certain way, for example through lots of lead in the air.

### Movement during combat

Most actions automatically include movement up to the person's Combat Movement, measured in meters, even if this isn't written out. This movement doesn't allow any advanced movement maneuvers. For those, use the initiative action Move.

A person may move around within their immediate area during their action to create atmosphere—for example, perhaps a person choosing to Fight runs around and around a conference table while chasing their target who's trying to escape (Defend). The total running distance becomes quite long, but the person doesn't move particularly far from their own starting point.

### Procedure

Each time a combat action is performed, it follows a certain procedure, described here step by step.

1. The person with Initiative chooses an Initiative action.
2. If it's a difficulty roll to be made, do so. If it's an opposed roll to be made, the initiator determines who the target is. The target then chooses a defensive action in response. Both parties make rolls to determine who wins the opposed roll.
3. Initiative is modified based on the combat action's outcome and all other mechanics are noted immediately (such as ammunition expenditure or damage effects).
4. The person who succeeded with the combat action describes in a few sentences for the other participants what happens in the scene, within what's reasonable for the combat action.

### Initiative Actions

Initiative actions are the combat actions a person whose turn it is can choose to perform. A person may only perform one initiative action per round. The available initiative actions are summarized in table 1:58.

Initiative Action | Summary | Initiative Change
Aimed fire | Firearms (S). Ammo: Lowest | +5 | - | - | −10
Snap shots | Firearms (S). Ammo: Highest | +5 | - | - | −10
 | Penalty: Recoil
 | Damage Effect: +1D per 2D
Targeted fire | Firearms (S). Ammo 1 | +5 | −5 | −5 | −10
 | Bonus: Precision
 | Minimum Initiative: 10
Marksmanship | Firearms (S). Ammo 1 | +5 | −10 | −10 | −20
 | Bonus: Precision x 2
 | Minimum Initiative: 10
Fire with two weapons | Firearms (S). Ammo: All | +5 | −5 | −5 | −10
 | Penalty: Recoil + Recoil
 | Damage Effect: Damage Value + Damage Value
Concentrated automatic fire | Firearms (A). Ammo: All | +5 | - | - | −10
 | Penalty: Recoil
 | Damage Effect: +1D per 2D
Spray with automatic fire | Firearms (A). Ammo: All x 2 | +5 | −5 | −5 | −10
 | Penalty: Recoil
 | All targets within 90 degrees make defense rolls
Automatic fire with | Firearms (A). Ammo: All x 2 | +5 | −5 | −5 | −10
two weapons | Penalty: Recoil + Recoil
 | Damage Effect: +1D per 2D (total)
Fire blind | Firearms. Ammo: All x 2 | +5 | −5 | −5 | −10
 | Penalty: −10. Bonus: Luck
 | (Defender: Bonus: Luck)
Throw object at target | Throwing | +5 | - | - | −10
Throw object to location | Throwing. Minimum Initiative 10 | +5 | −5 | −5 | −10
Fight | Melee. Damage, Hold,  | +5 | - | - | −10
 | Disarm (Penalty: −10),
 | Break hold, Shove away
Berserk attack | Melee. Bonus: +5 | +5 | −5 | −5 | −10
 | Damage. Damage Effect +1
 | Minimum Initiative: 10
Move | Combat Movement | +5 | - | - | −10
 | Move Combat Movement x 2 meters
 | Bonus/Penalty: +10 to −10
 | depending on complexity.
 | Each repeated attempt grants Bonus: +10
Draw weapon | Throwing or Firearms | +5 | - | - | −10
 | If two weapons drawn simultaneously:
 | Penalty: −2. Each successive
 | repeated attempt grants Bonus: +10
Reload | Firearms. Each successive | +5 | - | - | −10
 | repeated attempt grants Bonus: +10
 | Box magazine: Full reload
 | Belt-fed: Complex action
 | Other types: Minimum number of rounds
Survey the situation | Overview. Maximum Initiative: 20
 | +All x 2 | +All | +Highest | +1
Wait | No initiative action performed | - | - | - | -
Other action | See page 259 | =1 | =1 | =1 | =1
Await target | See page 259 | +5 | - | - | −10

Table 1:58

### The Combat Action

**All combat actions follow a given template:**

(Its name) ((type of roll))
**Brief description of the combat action.**

Requirement: The requirements or conditions that must be met to perform the combat action.

Rule: The rules encompassed by the combat action and how they should be handled.

Initiative: How the combat action's outcome affects the person's Initiative, in order from left to right, for: Exceptionally successful, Successful, Failed, Exceptionally failed.

For example, the code:
+5 | - | - | −10

...means that the person's Initiative increases by five (+5) if they succeed exceptionally with the combat action, remains unchanged if the person succeeds or fails, and that Initiative decreases by ten (−10) on an exceptional failure.

### General about movement

During combat, where there are people who want to harm you, you never stand still like a statue, unless there are special reasons. The people involved in combat all have some form of self-preservation instinct that makes them try to stay out of harm's way by instinctively protecting themselves, staying low, trying to use available cover, etcetera. But this is something the rules automatically assume.

**The defensive action Defend includes many variants of ways to avoid harm, see page 260.**

### Aimed Fire

The person wants to fire roughly aimed limited fire with a firearm at a selected target.

Requirement: The person has a loaded and ready-to-fire firearm that can fire single shots.

Rule: An opposed roll (Firearms) is made. The target makes a defense roll. Strike as many rounds from the weapon's magazine as the value on the lowest die. If the value is higher than the number of remaining rounds, the magazine is empty. If the shooter wins, the target has been hit. Roll the weapon's damage effect.

Initiative: +5 | - | - | −10

### Snap Shots

The person wants to fire repeated single shots in rapid succession with a firearm at a selected target.

Requirement: The person has a loaded and ready-to-fire firearm that can fire single shots. The weapon has at least two rounds in the magazine.

Rule: An opposed roll (Firearms) is made. The shooter receives a penalty equal to the weapon's Recoil. The target makes a defense roll. Strike as many rounds from the weapon's magazine as the value on the highest die in the opposed roll. If the sum is higher than the number of remaining rounds, the magazine is empty. If the shooter wins, the target has been hit. The weapon's damage effect increases by 1D per 2D in the weapon's damage value (minimum +1D).

Initiative: +5 | - | - | −10

### Targeted Fire

The person wants to take time and fire a carefully aimed shot with a firearm at a selected target.

Requirement: The person has a loaded and ready-to-fire firearm that can fire single shots. The person has Initiative 10 or higher.

Rule: An opposed roll (Firearms) is made and the shooter receives a bonus equal to the weapon's Precision. The target makes a defense roll. Strike one round from the weapon's magazine. If the shooter wins, the bullet has hit and then roll the weapon's damage effect.

Initiative: +5 | −5 | −5 | −10

### Marksmanship

The person wants to fire a very well-aimed shot with a firearm equipped with a scope at a selected target.

Requirement: The person has a loaded and ready-to-fire firearm that can fire single shots. The weapon has a scope that the person is using. The person has Initiative 10 or higher. The target is at least 20 meters away.

Rule: An opposed roll (Firearms) is made and the shooter receives a bonus equal to the weapon's double Precision. The target makes a defense roll. Strike one round from the weapon's magazine. If the shooter wins, the bullet has hit and then roll the weapon's damage effect.

Initiative: +5 | −10 | −10 | −20

### Fire with Two Weapons

The person wants to fire multiple shots in rapid succession with two firearms simultaneously at a selected target.

Requirement: The person has two loaded and ready-to-fire firearms that can fire single shots. The weapons have at least two rounds each in the magazines.

Rule: An opposed roll (Firearms) is made. The shooter receives a penalty equal to both weapons' total Recoil. The target makes a defense roll. Strike as many rounds from both weapons' magazines as the sum of the value on all dice (this includes any maxed dice as well) in the opposed roll. If the sum is higher than the number of remaining rounds, the magazine is empty. If the shooter wins, the target has been hit. The two weapons' damage values are added together to determine damage effect.

Initiative: +5 | −5 | −5 | −10

### Concentrated Automatic Fire

The person wants to fire a burst of automatic fire with a firearm at a selected target.

Requirement: The person has a loaded and ready-to-fire firearm that can fire automatic fire. The weapon has at least two rounds in the magazine.

Rule: An opposed roll (Firearms) is made. The shooter receives a penalty equal to the weapon's Recoil. The target makes a defense roll. Strike as many rounds from the weapon's magazine as the sum of the value on all dice (this includes any maxed dice as well) in the opposed roll. If the sum is higher than the number of remaining rounds, the magazine is empty. If the shooter wins, the target has been hit. The weapon's damage effect increases by 1D per 2D in the weapon's damage value (minimum +1D).

Initiative: +5 | - | - | −10

### Spray with Automatic Fire

The person wants to fire a long burst of automatic fire with a firearm over an area, hoping to hit multiple targets.

Requirement: The person has a loaded and ready-to-fire firearm that can fire automatic fire. The weapon has at least four rounds in the magazine.

Rule: An opposed roll (Firearms) is made. The shooter receives a penalty equal to the weapon's Recoil. Strike as many rounds from the weapon's magazine as the sum of the value on all dice times two (x 2) in the opposed roll (this includes any maxed dice as well). If the sum is higher than the number of remaining rounds, the magazine is empty. All people, friends and foes, judged to be within a cone of approximately 90 degrees in front of the target each make a defense roll. Those the shooter wins against are hit. The roll counts as successful if at least one person is hit. The weapon's damage effect is rolled separately per target hit.

Initiative: +5 | −5 | −5 | −10

### Automatic Fire with Two Weapons

The person wants to fire automatic fire with two firearms simultaneously at a selected target.

Requirement: The person has two loaded and ready-to-fire firearms that can fire automatic fire. The weapons have at least four rounds each in the magazines.

### Ammunition Expenditure

**Note that regardless of a combat action's outcome regarding firearms, the indicated amount of rounds is always consumed from the weapon's magazine.**

### It may seem like a lot of rounds are consumed during a firefight. That's correctly perceived, and entirely intentional. Most actions with firearms involve a greater or lesser amount of discharges. They're not intended to symbolize a systematic firing ("bang-bang-bang-bang-bang") but rather a stressed firing ("bangbangbang-bang-bangbang-bang-bang") because the actions themselves are so open to interpretation. For example, perhaps the combat action Snap Shots consumes nine shots in one action—this just means the situation for the shooter that round has involved multiple opportunities to fire. Most of the shots have probably missed the target. An automatic burst of perhaps 15 rounds can mean a longer burst in sequence or several shorter bursts—the shooter, scene, and situation determine this.

**This is where the beauty of the open actions comes in—they're created to generate drama and action in a way that suits the story and scene. Both players and narrator are encouraged to take the opportunity to paint intense combat situations.**

"Nothing says goodbye better than a bullet."
— Mordekai Remus

Rule: An opposed roll (Firearms) is made. The shooter takes a penalty equal to both weapons' total Recoil. The target makes a defense roll. Cross off as many rounds from both weapons' magazines as the sum of all dice values times two (x 2) in the opposed roll (this includes any maxed dice as well). If the sum is higher than the number of remaining rounds in either magazine, that magazine is empty. If the shooter wins, the target has been hit. The weapon's damage increases by 1D per 2D in the weapons' total damage value (the weapons' damage values are thus added together and not counted separately).
Initiative: +5 | −5 | −5 | −10
**Blind Fire**
The person wants to shoot and attempt to hit a concealed target, for example someone behind a table, a bar, a wall, in complete darkness, or if the shooter is blinded, through a combination of many bullets, skill, intuition, and a large helping of luck.
Requirement: The person has a loaded and ready-to-fire firearm. The weapon has at least four rounds in the magazine. The target area is somewhat limited.
Rule: An opposed roll (Firearms) is made. The shooter takes a penalty (−10), but may add their attribute value in Luck as a bonus. The target makes a defense roll and adds their attribute value in Luck as an additional bonus. Cross off as many rounds from the magazine as the value of all dice times two (x 2) in the opposed roll (this includes any maxed dice as well). If the sum is higher than the number of remaining rounds, the magazine is empty. If the shooter wins, the target has been hit. Roll damage.
Initiative: +5 | −5 | −5 | −10
**Throw Object at Target**
The person wants to throw an object to hit a chosen target.
Requirement: The person has a throwing weapon or a throwable object ready.
Rule: An opposed roll (Throwing Weapons) is made. The target makes a defense roll. If the thrower wins, the target has been hit. Roll damage.
Initiative: +5 | - | - | −10
**Throw Object to Location**
The person wants to throw an object in a chosen direction or to a location, for example through a window or to a spot on the ground. Grenades and similar are always thrown with this initiative action.
Requirement: The person has a throwable object ready and sees the position (it's possible to throw blind, for example over a wall, but then it's harder to hit the right spot). The person has Initiative 10 or higher.
Rule: A difficulty roll (Throwing Weapons) is made, modified depending on circumstances. If the thrower succeeds, the location has been hit. If the throw fails, the object lands somewhere it does no good, or the thrower simply fails to throw the object.
If the thrower doesn't see the impact location (if this matters, the narrator decides), they receive a penalty (−5).
Initiative: +5 | −5 | −5 | −10
**Fight**
The person wants to attack a target in close combat, either unarmed or with the help of a weapon or makeshift weapon of some kind.
Requirement: The person can perform the action.
Rule: The attacker must choose whether they want to damage the target (the default option), hold the target, disarm the target, break a hold, or push/tackle away the target. To hold the target, the attacker must have at least one hand free. Attempting to disarm the target results in the attacker receiving a penalty (−10) on their opposed roll.
Once the choice is made, an opposed roll (Close Combat) is made. The target makes a defense roll. If the attacker wins, they have succeeded in carrying out the chosen attack. If the intent was to damage the target, roll damage.
Initiative: +5 | - | - | −10

### Disarming

One variant is to attempt to disarm an opponent, that is, attempt to strike or wrench away, or twist loose, an object that the target has in their hands. If the attacker wants to attempt to disarm, they receive a penalty (−10) on their opposed roll.
The effect of a successful disarming is that the object is counted as unavailable to the opponent during the current and following round. This should be portrayed in the narrative in a creative way, for example by the attacker kicking away the revolver and the opponent not seeing where it lies, or not being able to reach it because the attacker is blocking it. The attacker may, however, pick up the object if desired and if the narrative supports it.
Should the disarming occur in such a place that the object disappears from the immediate vicinity, for example during a wild brawl on a narrow bridge or on a rushing train roof, the narrator may judge that the object cannot be picked up until a proper search is conducted.
**Berserk Attack**
The person truly wants with heart and soul to attack a target in close combat, either unarmed or with the help of a weapon or makeshift weapon of some kind, without thought to what consequences it might have later in the fight.
Requirement: The person can perform the action. The person has Initiative 10 or higher.
Rule: An opposed roll (Close Combat) is made with a bonus of five (+5). The target makes a defense roll. If the attacker wins, they have succeeded in carrying out the attack. The attacker then damages the target and adds one (+1) to the damage.
Initiative: +5 | −5 | −5 | −10
On being grasped or held
One of the selectable outcomes in a successful Fight action is to hold the target. It is up to the narrator to judge the reasonableness and consequence of various grappling activities. Certain defined grips may also give bonuses or penalties to other activities — a narrator might judge that a person with a low value in Size has more difficulty maintaining a grip on a target with a high value in Size. But these are situations the narrator must decide on a case-by-case basis.
A person who is held cannot perform certain actions, such as Move.
Which actions are impossible depends on how the grappler defined what they did when the Fight action was carried out.
If the attacker said the purpose was to hold the arm and hand in which the defender had a revolver, then the defender cannot perform actions involving the revolver. The narrator must be flexible and try to interpret the grip's nature and intent so that it becomes reasonable in the scene.
Other types of holds are partly the so-called police grip, with one arm behind the back pulled up toward the shoulder blade, and partly restraint by firmly holding or lying over the held person so they cannot move.
Breaking free from a grip is a Fight action, where both the initiative holder and the opponent may choose between using Close Combat or Strength in the opposed roll. A
successful roll for the held person means the grip is broken, otherwise they are still held.

### Move

The person wants to move in some way that requires a greater effort. The action can be used to flee from someone or to get to cover or to an object a good distance away. It is the narrator who determines whether the person must use this action or if the purpose can be achieved as a sub-element in another action.
Requirement: The person can move. A person can at most move the equivalent of their double Combat Movement meters per round.
Rule: A difficulty roll (Combat Movement) is made. For more difficult or easier maneuvers, the narrator may give bonuses or penalties on the roll, see table 1:59. If the difficulty roll fails, the person does not succeed in achieving the goal of the action — perhaps because the person is frozen in fear, the situation feels too risky, the person is too injured to succeed with the maneuver, or it may simply be too difficult to perform the maneuver. Regardless of reason, the person is in the same place and in the same situation as before the roll was made, or something else has occurred during the action, the narrator decides.
A person who fails the action and who chooses Move again as their next initiative action without choosing another initiative action in between receives a bonus (+10) on the subsequent attempt. This applies incrementally, given the above conditions.
Initiative: +5 | - | - | −10
Bonus/penalty Type of movement
+10 The person wants to run straight ahead through an alley toward an enemy, flee away, or similar.
+5 The person wants to run at full speed in furnished rooms or among cars on a busy road, etcetera.
+2 The person wants to jump up to a fire escape, grab hold of a hatch in the ceiling and haul themselves up, run in cramped spaces with much debris, cross a heavily trafficked highway, etcetera.
−2 The person wants to quickly move by swinging down through a hatch, jump out from the second floor and maintain speed, quickly get out of a trench, etcetera.
−5 The person wants to jump between balconies or rooftops, jump from the third floor without being hindered, maneuver in rooms with extremely limited visibility or where stressful circumstances such as heavy fire, grenade detonations, etcetera prevail.
−10 The person wants to throw themselves from the fifth floor, roll around and get on their feet with control of the situation, chase a car on foot, run through completely dark or smoke-filled spaces, etcetera.
Table 1:59
**Draw Weapon**
The person wants to draw a weapon from their person, from a holster, a sheath, a pocket, or similar.
Requirement: The weapon is available and can be drawn.
Rule: A difficulty roll (Firearms, Close Combat Weapons, or Throwing Weapons depending on which weapon is drawn) is made. Depending on how the weapon is carried, the person receives penalties or bonuses, see table on page 119. If the difficulty roll fails, the person does not succeed in drawing the weapon for some reason.
If the person wants to draw two one-handed weapons simultaneously, a penalty (−2) is given.
A person who holds a weapon with their hand while it is holstered, for example in a pocket or as standard procedure when a police officer is in a potentially dangerous situation, is given a bonus (+5).
A person who fails the action and who chooses Draw Weapon again as their next initiative action without choosing another initiative action in between receives a bonus (+10) on the subsequent attempt. This applies incrementally, given the above conditions.
Initiative: +5 | - | - | −10
**Reload**
The person reloads a firearm.
Requirement: The person has a loadable firearm and extra ammunition available.
Rule: A difficulty roll (Firearms) is made. If the narrator judges that the ammunition is difficult to access, this may give a penalty on the roll; if it's very easily accessible, it may give a bonus. If the difficulty roll fails, the person does not succeed in reloading the weapon.
A person who fails the action and who chooses Reload again without choosing another initiative action in between receives a bonus (+10) on the subsequent attempt. This applies incrementally, given the above conditions.
How many rounds are loaded per successful action depends on the weapon type.
• Weapons with box magazines are reloaded fully.
• Weapons with revolver cylinders, with internal magazines, or where the rounds are loaded directly into the chamber are reloaded with as many rounds as the lowest die's value, up to the weapon's maximum magazine capacity.
• Reloading a belt-fed weapon is an uncomplicated complex action (Challenge Level 50). One attempt may be made per round and person.
Most reloadable heavy weapons also function according to this rule.
Initiative: +5 | - | - | −10
Why rolls are made for movement, reloading, and weapon drawing
Difficulty rolls are made for movement, reloading, and weapon drawing to increase the drama in the scene and create uncertainty in the chaos combat is meant to be.
For most uninjured participants, these rolls don't matter much, and should they fail, they will most likely succeed in the next
Initiative action, thanks to the high bonus that is granted.
However, injured persons will have greater problems with action due to the penalties wounds give.
This is in line with the philosophy —
injuries and suffering hurt and make one's combat capacity severely suffer.
Attacking someone at a distance in close combat
If the situation is such that an intended target is a good distance away from the attacker who wants to attack in close combat, then the attacker must advance toward the target before the attack occurs. The target must be within the attacker's
Combat Movement, otherwise the
Fight or Berserk Attack action may not be used, but then Move should instead be used to symbolize that the attacker doesn't have time to reach before the target has time to get into action.
"What are you after Anya? My life story? Okay, here it comes. Four words: grand ideas, small results."
— Gabriel Jandaran

### Draw improvised weapons

The initiative action Draw weapon is only used for drawing weapons carried on your person. No Draw weapon action is required to use improvised weapons in melee or to throw one—if the narrator judges that the chosen improvised weapon is available and can be used, you just use it. See page 105 for more information on improvised weapons.

The same applies to firearms in the vicinity that are not holstered but are, for example, lying on a nightstand or on a shelf. They are drawn automatically unless the narrator judges otherwise.

### Survey the situation

The person attempts to regain Initiative and get control of, and an overview of, the situation so that he/she can act on his/her own terms.

Requirement: This is the only initiative action this round.
Current Initiative is 20 or lower.

Rule: A difficulty roll (Overview) is made. The roll's outcome determines how much the person's Initiative increases (the result is added to the Initiative the person had before the action's outcome).

Initiative:
Exceptionally successful: +(Sum from all dice x 2)
Successful: +(Sum from all dice)
Failed: +(Value of the highest die)
Exceptionally failed: +1

### Wait

The person chooses not to perform any initiative actions this round.

Requirement: The person is conscious.

Rule: The person chooses not to perform any initiative action during the round. No roll is made, and the person retains their Initiative.

Initiative: - | - | - | -

### Other action

The person wants to perform some form of activity during combat that has nothing to do with combat or direct dice rolls, for example using an ability like Medicine.

Requirement: The person can perform the action. The narrator must judge on a case-by-case basis.

Rule: As soon as a person chooses this initiative action, the person's Initiative is immediately set to one (1) because the person is no longer trying to maintain overview or control in combat but wants to dedicate themselves to something else. The narrator must be prepared to judge each individual situation. If the ability is not directly combat-related, the roll is not affected by the general penalty for low Initiative (see page 254).

Initiative: =1 | =1 | =1 | =1

### Wait for target

The person wants to wait for a specific target to appear at a specific location and react to this.

Requirement: The person has overview of the target area.

Rule: A person waiting for a target cannot perform any other combat actions (except Chance defense) without breaking the wait. A waiting person can, each time it's their turn order, choose to break Wait for target by performing another initiative action.

If a waiting person chooses to perform a defense action other than Chance defense, the wait is also immediately broken.

Each time the waiter chooses to wait, the person may slowly move up to half their Combat movement. One can, for example, cautiously advance toward a door where one suspects an enemy might be—which is, incidentally, a very common police tactic during operations.

It is only when a target appears that both the waiter and the target must make an opposed roll (Overview). See table 1:60 for bonuses and penalties for this opposed roll.
It is also this roll that provides any Initiative impact (this impact applies first in the following round, as those involved in a wait act on the same turn order in the current round, see the paragraph below).

If the waiter wins, he/she gets to perform an initiative action just before the target performs their planned initiative action. If the target wins, he/she gets to perform their initiative action and the waiter gets to perform their initiative action just after (on the same turn order) the target performed theirs.

Initiative: +5 | - | - | −10

Bonus/penalty for the waiter Condition
+5 Waiting for target that might appear within a small, defined area, for example in a doorway, in a window, behind a car, and so on.
−5 Waiting for target that might appear somewhere within a larger, wider area, for example outdoors in uneven terrain, in a large warehouse, hidden somewhere in a furnished room, among cars in a parking garage, and so on.
+2 Has higher Initiative than the target.

Bonus/penalty for the target being waited for Condition
+5 The target knows he/she is being observed and knows fairly well (within an area equivalent to a window, a door, behind a car, and so on) where the waiter is located.
−5 The target suspects he/she is being observed but doesn't know from where.
−10 The target is surprised/doesn't know he/she is being observed.
+2 Has higher Initiative than the waiter.

Table 1:60

### Increased damage effect

Certain initiative actions cause an attack's damage effect to increase.
Usually this happens according to "The weapon's damage effect increases by 1D
per 2D in the weapon's damage value
(minimum +1D)."
Table 1:61 summarizes this.

### Increased

**Damage Value Damage Value**
1D 2D
2D 3D
3D 4D
4D 6D
5D 7D
6D 9D
etc. etc.

Table 1:61

### Combat example

Examples of how combat actions are used begin on page 272.

### Shoot back

The person wants to meet an attack by rather uncontrollably shooting return fire in desperate hope of hitting first.

Requirement: The person is being shot at or subjected to a thrown weapon attack, and is aware of the attack and sees the attacker. The person has a loaded and ready-to-fire firearm ready. The weapon has at least two rounds in the magazine. The person has Initiative 10 or higher.

Rule: An opposed roll (Firearms) is made, with a penalty equal to the weapon's triple (x 3) Recoil. Strike off as many rounds from the weapon's magazine as the value of all dice
(this includes all any maxed dice) in the opposed roll. If the sum is higher than the number of remaining rounds, the magazine is empty. If the defender wins, the attack failed and the defender succeeded in hitting the attacker instead.
Roll for the weapon's damage effect.

Initiative: +5 | −10 | −10 | −20

### Strike back

The person wants to perform desperate counterattacks in hope of successfully outmaneuvering and damaging the opponent, despite their disadvantage in the situation.

Requirement: The person is attacked in melee (or shot at from close range) and is aware of the attack. The person has
Initiative 10 or higher.

Rule: An opposed roll (Melee) is made, with a penalty
(−10). If the defender wins, the attack failed and the defender succeeded in hitting the attacker instead. The defender then gets to choose whether he/she wants to damage, hold, or push away the attacker. To hold the attacker, the defender must have at least one hand free.

Initiative: +5 | −10 | −10 | −20

### Being in cover

If a target is in cover, this grants him/her a bonus to Defend yourself and Chance defense when he/she is subjected to a ranged attack.

There are several levels of cover, and these symbolize both concealing cover or cover that creates difficult circumstances for being hit. Choose the level of cover that feels appropriate for a given situation.

### Bonus Degree of cover

+1 Barely protected. The target stands behind a telephone pole, hides behind an armchair, sits in a bush, etcetera. The target is considered covered by the protection to approximately 25 percent.
+2 Lightly protected. The target shoots around a corner, stands behind an overturned dining table, lies over a hood, peers out from behind a substantial tree, etcetera. The target is considered covered by the protection to approximately 50 percent.
+5 Substantially protected. The target is visible but is in cover of sandbags, ducks behind a car, stands in cover of a door, kneels behind an overturned table, etcetera. The target is considered covered by the protection to approximately 75 percent.
+10 Maximally protected. The target is barely visible and lies cowering in a foxhole, ducks behind a stone wall, peeks out from behind a wall, etcetera. The target is considered covered by the protection to at least
90 percent.

### Defense actions

Defense actions are what attacked persons get to perform as a reaction or response to the attack. The standard defense action is Defend yourself unless otherwise stated. What a successful or failed defense looks like in the narrative is determined by the protagonist's player and/or the narrator.

Most defense actions lower the person's Initiative. Note that if Initiative reaches low values, the person's actions suffer penalties, see page 254.

The available defense actions are summarized in table 1:62.

### Defense action Initiative Comment

Defend yourself +5 | −5 | −5 | −10 Defense: Ranged or
Defense: Melee.
**Opposed roll**
Chance defense +5 | ­ | ­ | −10 Luck. Opposed roll.
Shoot back +5 | −10 | −10 | −20 Firearms. Ammo: A.
Penalty: Recoil x 3.
Minimum Initiative: 10.
Strike back +5 | −10 | −10 | −20 Melee. Penalty: −10.
Damage, Hold,
Push away.
Minimum Initiative: 10.

Table 1:62

### Defend yourself

The person wants to defend themselves against attacks in various ways, with different methods and all available means depending on the attack's nature and the circumstances surrounding it. This includes everything from staying in cover, blocking, parrying and avoiding to rolling away, throwing themselves to the ground, moving behind cover, or ducking away from attacks.

Defend yourself is the standard defense action, used when nothing else is said when a person is attacked.

Requirement: The person is being attacked in some way and is aware of it.

Rule: An opposed roll (Defense: Ranged or Defense:
Melee) is made, based on the attack's type. If the defender wins, the attack has been deflected and is then counted as a failure.

Initiative: +5 | −5 | −5 | −10

### Chance defense

The person instinctively performs a passive defense, if he/she is, for example, unaware of or surprised by the attack, or chooses not to actively defend. The person walks straight toward the attacker, doesn't bother to block, or is nonchalant in general. This usually means the person is hit, unless providence favors him/her very well.

Requirement: The person is attacked and is surprised or overwhelmed by the attack, or the person voluntarily chooses not to actively defend themselves.

Rule: An opposed roll (Luck) is made. If the defender wins, the attack has been deflected and is then counted as a failure.

Initiative: +5 | - | - | −10

"Sleep is death's brother."
— Talia Navarre

What Does "Defend" Actually Mean?
Here are a number of interpretations of how it might look in the story when the defensive action Defend is used.
• An assassin fires burst shots from a machine pistol at Anya as she leaves police headquarters. She chooses to Defend, and wins against the attacker with her resistance roll. **Anya's player describes how Anya instinctively** senses the shooter coming up the stairs. As shots thunder in rapid succession, Anya rolls behind one of the large stone gargoyles standing at the top of the stairs. The statue is damaged and stone fragments break loose in the hail of bullets while Anya seeks cover.
• Gabriel creeps across a skyscraper rooftop with a pistol in hand. Through the rain he sees a figure approaching. It's Nevei, a Destroyer, who has been hunting Gabriel for several days. The confrontation is inevitable and Nevei attacks with a large ornate two-handed sword. Gabriel defends against the woman's thrust and manages to win the resistance roll. It's portrayed in the story as Nevei coming rushing forward screaming and Gabriel turning around in surprise. With rain lashing his face, Gabriel ducks just under the sword swing, rolls around, and comes up in a kneeling position with the pistol aimed at Nevei. Since Gabriel is next in turn order, it's dramatic that he gets the pistol up this way, and in his turn fires repeatedly at Nevei...
• Anya wakes with a start to someone kicking down her hotel door. In the hazy morning sunlight she sees hired scum that someone in the Gault family has sent to ensure her secret dies with her. Unabashed, the naked woman gets out of bed and meets the thug in close combat. She fights but the thug manages to defend. This is depicted by the narrator and player as her savagely delivering a kick but the thug getting his hand up, stopping the foot and twisting so that Anya is flung over and lands on a coffee table that breaks apart.
• Film star Sayah Bonn steps out of a taxi for the premiere of her new film. A crazed Creator in the crowd pulls out a shotgun. Sayah has time to perceive what's happening and defends. She manages to roll better than the shooter. In the story nothing more happens than Sayah having time to twist enough that the shotgun blast only tears apart her wide red ball gown and cape with a boom.
• Mordekai is assaulted by a junkie who turns out to be one of the Afflicted. The junkie strikes with a cobra's speed and begins pummeling Mordekai, who defends and succeeds. The situation is portrayed in the story as Mordekai perceiving what's happening just in time, ducking under the punch, circling around, avoiding a kick by jumping over some old crates and parrying a flurry of strikes (remember that the initiative action fight doesn't show a single blow, but only that the person is fighting in close combat to defeat their opponent) before the event is over.
• Three Rulov gangsters are enjoying their dinner at restaurant Dorsia. A vengeful Gabriel enters, sees them and draws his Kalgari submachine gun from his coat. He opens fire on all three, and only one manages to defend. In the story this is described as when Gabriel begins sweeping with his chattering SMG, the first gangster takes a bullet right in the back and falls onto the table, the second has time to stand but is hit in the chest and falls backward into a decorative aquarium that shatters so water gushes out onto the floor, and the third miraculously manages to throw himself backward over his chair, stumble behind the bar and disappear from sight.
• Jessca encounters a patrolling guard when she breaks into a factory building where slaver Kastor maintains his trucks. Jessca stylishly throws a throwing knife at the guard but he rolls better on his resistance and manages to defend. In the story it's determined that the guard perceives what's happening and just barely gets a trash can lid up as a shield against the knife, which is deflected and lands some distance away.

None of the situations above are described in the rules, beyond what can be found under the defensive action Defend. It's the players and/or narrator who together determine how combat situations manifest in the world. The intention is to create dynamics and tension in the scene. That participants around the gaming table use their imagination collectively and partake in the rich gaming experience Noir is meant to be.

Gladly let tough, bold and spectacular events occur, let there be tension in the story and don't worry if someone else determines something that affects your protagonist or narrator character — after all, Initiative determines when a person gets to act and there are essentially no situations where this is prevented. Thus almost anything can happen in the world — even if it's breakneck or life-threatening things.

Physical Injuries & Death
It's more or less inevitable that a person suffers violence in Sandukar's dark alleys and abandoned basement spaces. The injuries caused vary from minor scrapes, scratches and bruises, to serious physical injuries that in the worst case are fatal but give the soul a peace that cannot be found in the world of the living.

### Physical Injuries

When a person is injured in Noir, they become either lightly or seriously injured.

Light injuries result in the victim receiving Disorientation but are not life-threatening and one recovers from them fairly quickly. However, Disorientation is debilitating and can cause the person to become temporarily knocked out or unconscious.

**Serious injuries give the victim both Disorientation** and Wounds. Wounds can kill the victim and it takes a long time to recover from them. Moreover, Wounds are severely debilitating.

Damage & Wound Threshold
All dangerous situations that risk injuring a person cause damage, based on the situation's, or weapon's, damage value.

Damage is obtained by rolling the number of dice specified as the attack's damage value. The result of the roll is added to the victim's Disorientation, which shows the immediate disorientation, pain experience, stress and shock that the injury gives rise to.

If damage is also higher than the victim's attribute value in Wound Threshold, the victim receives a Wound — the damage was so serious that the victim risks dying from it.

If damage is higher than the victim's double attribute value in Wound Threshold, the victim instead receives as many Wounds as the number the lowest die in the damage roll shows. If damage is higher than the victim's quadruple attribute value in Wound Threshold, the victim instead receives as many Wounds as the value the highest die in the damage roll shows. Table 1:63 summarizes this.

If damage is higher than the person's... then the person receives...
...attribute value in Wound Threshold ...one Wound.
...double attribute value in Wound Threshold ...as many Wounds as the value on the lowest die.
...quadruple attribute value in Wound Threshold ...as many Wounds as the value on the highest die.
Table 1:63

Note that the number of Wounds does not in any way show how many individual injuries a person has suffered. A single instance of damage can result in the victim receiving loads of Wounds. Rather, see the number of Wounds as an indicator of how seriously injured the victim is.

### Subduing Damage

There are certain attacks or situations that inflict so-called subduing damage. The difference between subduing and regular, lethal, damage is that the former has a harder time inflicting Wounds on the victim — the victim temporarily doubles their value in Wound Threshold at the moment of injury.

What causes subduing damage is primarily brawls, blunt force, shockwaves, attacks with weapons lacking a cutting edge or penetrating point, as well as fall and collision damage.

Weapons or attacks that deal subduing damage are marked by a "B" in front of the damage value, for example "B1D+1".

### Total Penalty

Injuries have negative effects on a person's ability to effectively perform actions in and out of combat.

Every ten steps of Disorientation gives a penalty (−1) on all actions. Each inflicted Wound immediately lowers the victim's Initiative by five (−5) and gives a penalty (−2) on all actions. The sum of these penalties is recorded on the character sheet in the section marked Total Penalty. How Disorientation and Wounds affect is summarized in table 1:64.

### Physical Injury Effect

Disorientation Every ten steps of Disorientation gives a penalty (−1) on all activities.
Wound Each inflicted Wound immediately lowers the victim's Initiative by five (−5) and gives a penalty (−2) on all activities.
Table 1:64

Endurance & Disorientation
A victim who receives increased Disorientation must make a difficulty roll (Endurance). The roll is only affected by the penalties from the total amount of Disorientation the victim has.

Exceptionally successful: The victim can continue to act, despite their injuries.
Successful: The victim can continue to act, despite their injuries.
Failed: The victim is disoriented and may attempt to recover in their turn each round. Initiative is set to one (1).
Exceptionally failed: The victim is unconscious for as many minutes as the total amount of Disorientation they have.

### Disoriented

If the victim becomes disoriented, this means in the scene that the victim is temporarily knocked out, stunned, or severely confused.

While the victim is disoriented, they may not perform any actions whatsoever, not even scream or attempt to call attention.

A disoriented person automatically receives Initiative one (1) and is counted as defenseless, see page 271. A disoriented person who receives additional Disorientation automatically becomes unconscious.

### Recover

A disoriented person may make a difficulty roll (Endurance) on their turn each round to recover. Penalties come only from any Disorientation.

Exceptionally successful: The victim recovers and immediately gets to raise their Initiative as many steps as the value from all dice. In the next round they may act as normal.
Successful: The victim recovers. In the next round they may act as normal.
Failed: The victim is still disoriented and may attempt to recover again next round.
Exceptionally failed: The victim becomes unconscious for as many minutes as the total amount of Disorientation they have.

### Unconscious

An unconscious victim may not perform any actions whatsoever. The victim wakes on their own after as many minutes as the value in Disorientation was at the moment unconsciousness took effect.

An unconscious person is always counted as defenseless, see page 271.

Wake the Unconscious
A medically trained person can attempt to wake an unconscious person. If the medically trained person succeeds at a difficulty roll (Medicine), the unconscious person is instead counted as disoriented. Each such attempt takes one round. Appropriate methods include slaps, cold water or chemical smelling salts.

### Summary of Being Injured

1. Determine damage from the attack (from weapon, attack type or event) by rolling the dice indicated at the weapon's damage value. Let the dice remain visible on the table.
2. Increase the victim's amount of **Disorientation by as much as** the damage dice show.
3. If damage is higher than the victim's Wound Threshold, the victim receives one Wound. If damage is higher than the victim's double Wound Threshold, the victim receives as many wounds as the lowest die in the damage roll shows. If damage is higher than the victim's quadruple Wound Threshold, the victim receives as many wounds as the highest die in the damage roll shows.
4. The victim makes a difficulty roll (Endurance). If the roll fails, the victim is disoriented and must recover to be able to act again. A disoriented victim's Initiative is set to 1.
5. If the victim received one or more Wounds, they must make a difficulty roll (Will to Live). If the roll fails, the victim is counted as mortally wounded and dies after as many rounds as the sum of the difficulty roll's result, and initiative is set to 1. **If the difficulty roll failed** exceptionally, the victim dies immediately. A mortally wounded victim must be stabilized to survive.

"The bullet holes glittered like rubies in her chest, the blood gleamed against the perfect white skin. She was so beautiful. I was satisfied with the work of art."
— Stelia Lock

Why damage is so random
One might question how victims of weapon attacks suffer such random damage?
It's important to remember that targets in combat don't stand still and take perfect hits. Even if a defender fails their defense, they still try to avoid harm in every way, by moving, instinctively ducking that centimeter required to transform a lethal hit into a scratch—the bullet missed the lung by a hair's breadth.
See also the rule for hits on defenseless persons, described on page 271. There, guidelines are given for how to act in situations where a person has total control of a situation and violence is to be used.
**Painkillers**
One way to mitigate the effects of being injured is to pop painkiller pills or inject syringes. This is described on page 270.
Willpower & Wounds
If a victim is dealt one or more Wounds, they must make a difficulty roll (Willpower). The roll is only affected by the penalties from the total number of Wounds the victim has.
Exceptional success: The victim can continue to act, despite their injuries.
Success: The victim can continue to act, despite their injuries.
Failure: The victim is mortally wounded, and dies after as many rounds as the combined total of all the dice shows. The victim gets Initiative 1.
Exceptional failure: The victim dies immediately from the injuries.
**Mortally wounded**
A person is considered mortally wounded when they fail a difficulty roll (Willpower). When this happens, the vic-tim dies after as many rounds as the combined total on all dice in the difficulty roll. The victim's Initiative is set to 1. In the character sheet, note that the victim is mortally wounded.
While the victim is mortally wounded, they may not perform any actions, other than possibly crawling, groaning, rattling, coughing, whispering final words, or similar. The victim may not scream or call attention from others than those who have already noticed their status.
A mortally wounded victim is not automatically unconscious
(this is controlled by Confusion).
A person who is mortally wounded is considered defenseless
(see page 271), and dies immediately if they are inflicted with additional Wounds while they are defenseless.
The only possibilities a mortally wounded person has to act are if they are either in a scene where a drive can be fulfilled (see page 233) or if they use Fate and grit their teeth (see page 239).
A mortally wounded person who has been stabilized (see below)
is no longer considered mortally wounded, so the marking in the character sheet is erased.
**Stabilization of the mortally wounded**
A medically skilled person can attempt to stabilize a mor-tally wounded person in hopes of saving their life. Stabiliza-tion is counted per Wound and the person may make a difficulty roll (Medicine) each round to hopefully stabilize a Wound. Each successful stabilization is noted in the character sheet at the Wound. Note that the Wound still remains—sta-bilization doesn't heal any Wounds.
The outcome of the difficulty roll (Medicine) is read here:
Exceptional success: As many Wounds as the result on the lowest die are considered stabilized.
Success: One Wound is considered stabilized.
Failure: No effect.
Exceptional failure: A previously stabilized Wound is no longer considered stabilized. If there is no stabilized
Wound, nothing further happens.
When all Wounds are stabilized, the victim is no longer con-sidered mortally wounded, has Initiative 1 and can act as normal.
With this, all noted successful stabiliza-tions are erased from the character sheet.
Note that each time a person becomes mortally wounded, all their Wounds must be stabilized, even those that have been stabilized previously. This shows that the body is a whole and that too much violence against it causes blood pressure drop, shock, and respiratory distress. Therefore, it's often a good idea to try to break off or avoid combat if one has suffered multiple Wounds.
9   17   28
Here you fill in the total amount of Confusion the per-son has. In this example, the person has accumulated
Confusion 28.
**Here you mark the person's**
Confusion to determine which penalty applies at the current level; in the exam-ple, Confusion 28
gives a penalty of (–2). Should the amount of Confusion be
130 or higher, you must calculate the penalty manually, by taking and striking the last digit in the total amount of Confusion.
3 11 10 9
Here the number of Wounds a person has is noted; in the example, 3 Wounds.
Here you see the penalty the person gets from the Wounds they have; in the example, the 3 Wounds give a penalty of (–6).
The circle is marked if the person is mortally wounded, which is the case in the example.
Here is noted how many rounds a mortally wounded person has left before they die from the injuries. In the example, the person has 9 rounds left.
The crosses are used when stabilizing Wounds, to note which Wounds have been tended to. Here you can see that two of the 3 Wounds are stabilized.
**Here it shows that the person's**
Initiative is lowered by five steps for each Wound they've received. In the example, the person's Initiative has been lowered by 15 steps.
The sum of the penalties from these two columns is the person's Total penalty and shows how much the injuries impair their continued actions. In the example, the Total penalty is (–8)

### Recovery

A person's bodily injuries heal with time. Confusion decreases gradually while Wounds take longer to heal.
**Reduction of Confusion**
Confusion automatically decreases by the person's attri-bute value in Endurance per hour in the story.
**Healing Wounds**
After each longer rest period, usually at dawn, and at most once per day, the wounded must make a difficulty roll (Willpower) to see if they succeed in healing Wounds. The roll suf-fers a penalty if the person has not been in a hospital the day before. The degree of the penalty is determined according to table 1:65.
The outcome of the roll is read below.
Exceptional success: The person heals as many Wounds as the lowest die shows.
Success: The person heals one Wound.
Failure: No Wound is healed.
Exceptional failure: No Wound is healed and the person suffers an additional Wound due to unforeseen conse-quences of the injury, such as an infection or internal bleeding.
**Penalty Circumstances**
±0 Perfect—the person is in a hospital and receives competent care and rest
–2 Good—the person rests in a protected environment, for example a room at home, is undisturbed and eats regularly, has warmth and peace and quiet, wounds are bandaged and treated
–5 Poor—the person does not rest, engages in physical and/or strenuous activity such as traveling around or fighting, wounds are poorly treated
–10 Terrible—exposed situation, the person is in a trench with dirt and mud, where diseases and infections rage, in moisture and cold, unprotected from weather and wind, with wounds completely un-treated
Table 1:65
**Operations**
One way to heal Wounds faster is to be patched up and oper-ated on—a skilled surgeon can work wonders. The surgeon may make a difficulty roll (Medicine) for each Wound the patient has.
The outcome is read here:
Exceptional success: The Wound is restored but the patient's
Confusion increases by the result on the lowest die.
Success: The Wound is restored but the patient's Confusion increases by the result on the highest die.
Failure: The Wound remains and cannot be restored through operation. The patient's Confusion increases by the sum of all dice results.
Exceptional failure: The Wound remains and cannot be restored through operation. The patient's Confusion increases by the lowest die's result ×10. Complications arise and the patient receives an additional Wound with all that entails, and thus risks dying on the operating table.
Each attempt to restore a Wound takes as many hours to complete as the lowest die's value. A patient's last Wound cannot be restored through surgery.
**Realistic injuries**
Noir is built to create drama, tension in the world that is presented, based on that world's grounding in reality and natural laws. Relentless realism has had to yield to atmosphere, flow, and entertainment. Parts of Noir, both in world and in rules, lie fairly close to realistic thinking, others lie far from it. However, Noir has deliberately been made credible, that is, rules and world should feel reasonable and well anchored within their internal logic.
The damage system in Noir is therefore also deliberately kept abstract to avoid getting bogged down in details that most resemble an autopsy report.
In our reality, almost all types of injuries cause a person to immediately cease the activity they were engaged in, thanks to pain and shock. But since the protagonists in the game are indeed protagonists in the ongoing story, they can take more punishment than real people. They still suffer the negative effects of injuries and risk being killed at each instance of damage, but in most cases they manage to continue their endeavor, even if they have bruises, flesh wounds, and pain.
For those who want some form of connection to reality, the bodily injuries can be seen as follows:
Light injuries include flesh wounds, scratches, small wounds, scrapes, abrasions, bruises, bumps, sprains, light bleeding, swelling, but are also pain, confusion, agony, stress, and disorientation.
Serious injuries encompass everything from deep wound channels from bullets, severe cuts, extensive crush injuries, internal injuries, organ damage, broken bones, torn muscles, damaged tendons/joints to severe external and internal bleeding.

### Other ways to be injured

There are other situations that can arise during a story where injuries result, which are described here.
**Strangulation**
Strangulation occurs when a person with their hands or with an implement prevents oxygen supply to the brain of their victim, or damages the throat so severely that continued breathing becomes impossible. The damage value is based on the strangulation method; see table 1:66. Once the victim is knocked out due to high Confu-sion, it's easy to execute them through continued strangulation for another round (see "Defenseless victims" and
"Execution" on page 271).
A person subjected to strangulation may not use the defense action Defend oneself, but must use
Luck defense, Push back, or Strike back.
The narrator can judge that the strangler has the upper hand if the strangler, for example, stands in a position so that it becomes difficult for the victim to defend themselves. The strangler then gets a bonus (+5) on all continued strangulations of the victim made in succession. It can also be judged that the strangulation causes problems for the strangled person's freedom of action, and the narrator can then assign penalties to the strangled person.
**Method Damage Value**
Strangulation without implement B3D–3
Strangulation with implement* B4D–4
Table 1:66 * For example garrote, piano wire, cord, or rope.
**Explosions**
Explosions cause damage through pressure waves and with shrapnel if such is created. There are three distances when it comes to explo-sions: contact, close, and far.
Contact means that one more or less physically touch-es the explosion, or is very close to it (within about
1 meter). Close means that one is within a few meters from the detonation, while far means that one stands outside the primary damage sphere but is affected by secon-dary effects—usually lighter pressure and burn injuries. The dam-age value is given per distance; see table 1:67.
If the damage effect a person suffers from an explosion exceeds their Endurance value, the person is knocked down by the pressure wave. Initiative is then set to 1.
If a detonation occurs in an enclosed space, for example in a room or a car interior, the distances for close and far are doubled.
**Explosion Contact Close Far**
Artillery shell B10D (0–1m) B5D (1–10m) B2D (10–25m)
Gasoline barrel B5D (0–1m) B3D (1–2m) B1D (2–5m)
Fuel tank B10D (0–1m) B5D (1–5m) B2D (5–10m)
Dynamite, stick B5D (0–1m) B3D (1–2m) B1D (2–5m)
Dynamite, charge B10D (0–1m) B5D (1–5m) B2D (5–10m)
Dynamite, full crate B20D (0–1m) B10D (1–10m) B5D (10–25m)
Dynamite, truck bed B30D (0–1m) B15D (1–20m) B5D (20–50m)
Aerial bomb, small B20D (0–1m) B10D (1–10m) B5D (10–25m)
Aerial bomb, large B30D (0–1m) B15D (1–20m) B5D (20–50m)
Gas tank B5D (0–1m) B3D (1–2m) B1D (2–5m)
Mortar shell B10D (0–1m) B5D (1–2m) B2D (2–5m)
Hand grenade B10D (0–1m) B5D (1–2m) B2D (2–5m)
Explosive fence B20D (0–1m) B10D (1–5m) B5D (5–10m)
Table 1:67
The blood-stained hands still trembled as he convulsively held the bundle of newspaper against his chest. He had been lucky; no one had seen him drag away the unconscious woman, and he got to perform his task undisturbed on the basement floor. A flask of liquor he had needed to do it, less than last time; that was good—less money for liquor, more money for a fix.
He looked around several times before half-running across the street toward the steel door. That there was a clinic behind it one couldn't guess—a friend of his had tipped him off about it. The warmth from the bundle spread across his chest; the soiled shirt was now soaked with blood that had leaked through the newspapers.
He pounded with a bloody fist on the door. The fresher the organs were and the younger and healthier the donor was, the more zovrins one got—and the woman had been very young—almost not a woman. The discomfort began to well up over him, and the lump in his throat made it hard to breathe, but he put his thoughts on the fix he could get for the money.
The door opened and he looked into the gray-clad guard's gun muzzle. He could barely stammer that he had things to sell; the guard nodded and let him into the room with the antiseptic air and the tile-clad walls. He was shown to the doctor—the bald, sickly thin man whose eyes were always hidden by black glass. The doctor pointed to a bench of stainless steel and he laid down the bundle there. While the doc-tor unfolded the newspaper, the man took the opportunity to look around. On shelves of stainless steel stood quantities of glass jars with organs preserved in medical alcohol; by the floor drain the cockroaches crawled, and on the wall hung a yellowed anatomical poster of the human body.
The doctor smacked and licked his lips; he studied the red heart, the kidneys and liver; some of the more mangled intestines he poked aside. The man watched with disgust as the doctor bent down, put his nose right next to the organs and breathed in the smell. Then he turned around and wiped the blood from his fingers. He smiled and walked up to a metal cabinet from which he took a handful of zovrins and placed them in the man's hand. After that, the guard showed him the door, and the man ran out into the night toward the nearest dealer who could give him what he needed to feel good and to get rid of his guilt.
The fix, the wonderful high that would remove the image of the girl's butchered body on the basement floor, the rats that had started eating her while he was still working. What won't one do to survive, he thought as he slowly died inside.

### Where attacks hit

It doesn't matter where an attack hits an enemy. The player may improvise this based on damage dealt and effect. High damage and/or many
Wounds can symbolize very serious hits to the chest or head, while minor damage can be scratches across the forehead, shot-off earlobes, or flesh wounds in arms or legs, but the opposite hit location relationships can also apply.
